```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> In your mind, perhaps. :-)

The question was "Does that make it right?" where "it" refers to "Another 
God (Allah) told his faithful to spread his word -- with force if 
necessary." In order to NOT avoid the question, you should either respond 
with "yes" or "no". And if you respond with "no", expect to get asked why 
this religion is not "right" but Christianity is. Presumably, "right" in 
this context is in the sense of morally good, but you'll have to ask 
Alistair to be sure.

>
> It depends on who/what one makes their 'god'. The real God, the one who 
> created everything, and before Whom every being will stand and be judged, 
> chose not to force people to believe. Had He wanted to do that, he could 
> have avoided being blasphemed and cursed by those who hate Him.

    So far, this is (logically) correct, but there is a bit of laxness here 
(the term "The real God" implies that it is known that there is a real God, 
which hasn't been established yet), and the part about "chose not to force 
people to believe" is true but misleading, but I'll get into that later.

> If this 'Allah' is all powerful and pre-determines every act, then calls 
> upon some to destroy others for not believing, that is logically 
> inconsistent.

    Not nescessarily. You can compare this to a child playing with toy 
soldiers. From the point of view of those soldiers, that child is 
essentially God, in that he has control over the soldier's entire 
"universe", and knows well in advance everything that will ever happen in 
that universe. However, the child may *INTENTIONALLY* create enemies for his 
"chosen people". He will, of course, ensure that his chosen people will win, 
but he may take delight if the battle is particularly bloody.

> The real God permits people a choice, calls upon them to do right, 
> provides a way to be reconcilled to Him even after they fail to do this, 
> then judges them based on what they choose to do with their own hearts.

    This is not nescessarily true. Perhaps the "real God" (if such a being 
exists), gave us no choice at all. Maybe he is forcing you to believe in 
him, and forcing me to not believe. So while the statement "God is not 
forcing everybody to believe" is true (i.e. he's not forcing ME to believe), 
the statement "God give people a choice to believe in what they want" is not 
nescessarily true (and as far as I can tell, cannot logically be shown to be 
true nor false).

>
>> In contrast, Christianity requires turning the other cheek. However,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> The Pope isn't God, he is just a man.

    This may be another source of Christianity-bashing. From your view point 
(I'm assuming), the "boss" of Christianity is God. But from an Atheist's 
point of view, there is no such thing as God, and so the "real" boss of 
Christianity is the Pope. From the point of view of the Atheist, it's like 
"the man behind the curtain" and the Wizard of Oz. Some people are fooled, 
and some aren't, but the person who's truly in control is the Pope.

    Given this viewpoint, it's understandable why some people strongly 
dislike Christianity as it exists now: The Pope is the head of the 
organization, and the Pope has caused lots of death. People don't want to 
have anything to do with that organization now because of it.

    - Oliver
```

>God (Allah) told his faithful to spread his word -- with force if 
>necessary." In order to NOT avoid the question, you should either respond

The answer is obvious.   Whatever the True God wants is right.   

>And if you respond with "no", expect to get asked why 
>this religion is not "right" but Christianity is. Presumably, "right" in 
>this context is in the sense of morally good, but you'll have to ask

those other religions are wrong".

which by our standards are wrong.   But by *our* standards, God
Himself did stuff which are wrong - so our standards are not good
guides here.   And why should Muhammed (or Moses for that matter) have
to be perfect to be a carrier for the word of God?   (Did Jesus claim
to be perfect?)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 41)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-14T12:21:07-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2mZnf.8425$kP5.2999@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <GYXnf.256524$ir4.74044@edtnps90> <86j0q11q2ss75j3b3hb4fh32b99qse3o5q@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> (Did Jesus claim to be perfect?)

No. But the Bible clearly declares Him to be perfect and sinless. In fact, 
the whole principle of His death as a substitute sacrifice for our sins is 
based on the fact of His sinless perfection. If Jesus had any sin, he would 
have had to die for them Himself, and been unqualified to become our 
substitute. Under Mosaic law, where animal sacrifices were made as a picture 
of the future coming Messiah's sacrifice, it was made very clear that the 
animals had to be completely perfect and without blemish of any kind. In 
certain cases, even a single discolored hair made the animal unfit for 
sacrifice.
```

"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> later.

But the question of God being "The Real God" is absolutely central to this 
question. There is no good in following a false god, no matter what the 
follower thinks.

If God does not exist, then it makes little difference what we do, for we 
will all cease to exist at death, and that's the end of it. If one chooses 
to believe there isn't a God, then that settles it for that person. If God 
doesn't exist, they were right, though it doesn't benefit them in the end, 
and if God does exist, He will judge their unbelief if and as He sees fit. 
The only issue then, is what about people who believe there may be a God, 
are perhaps not sure, but would like to know if and which?

Believe me, the Real God knows Who He is. I am personally convinced that the 
God of the Bible is the Real Deal, because of my relationship with Him. And 
if the God of the Bible is real, then He desires a relationship with each of 
us. If this is the case, then we should be able to expect Him to respond to 
us if we actually and honestly seek Him. I believe that anyone who truly and 
honestly seeks The Real God will find Him, and can know that He is The Real 
Deal. The Bible says this is true.

>> If this 'Allah' is all powerful and pre-determines every act, then calls 
>> upon some to destroy others for not believing, that is logically 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> will win, but he may take delight if the battle is particularly bloody.

Toy soldiers do not have free will, or any other kind of will, either. 
Therefore, they make no decisions, take no actions. Humans, on the other 
hand, frequently do all of the above. :-)

>> The real God permits people a choice, calls upon them to do right, 
>> provides a way to be reconcilled to Him even after they fail to do this, 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> be shown to be true nor false).

Would that kind of god, who cared nothing for the wants and desires of his 
creatures, create such things as love and beauty, joy and happiness? Doesn't 
seem likely to me.

>>> In contrast, Christianity requires turning the other cheek. However,
>>> Papal action allows the killing of heathens and pagans (in crusades)
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pope.

Well, as far as I know, even the Pope doesn't claim to lead all Christians, 
only Roman Catholics. Perhaps the Pope doesn't consider non-Catholics as 
Christians, or considers all Christians to be Roman Catholics, but I don't 
recall hearing such a thing from the Vatican. :-)

>    Given this viewpoint, it's understandable why some people strongly 
> dislike Christianity as it exists now: The Pope is the head of the 
> organization, and the Pope has caused lots of death. People don't want to 
> have anything to do with that organization now because of it.

Have you ever heard of the Protestant Reformation and the Roman Inquisition? 
The Roman Catholic Church tortured and killed literally millions of 
Protestants. Many Jews despise Christians because they say the Roman 
Catholic Church was complicit in rounding up the Jews during WWII, and Roman 
Catholics have been accused of calling Jews "Christ killers". I don't know 
about the accuracy of these claims, but I do know that many Jews view Roman 
Catholicism as the face of Christianity. Catholics are approximately half of 
all Christians, worldwide. In the U.S. Protestants outnumber Catholics by 
more than 2:1. Christians as a whole represent about 33% of the world's 
population, by far the most predominant religion.

> Judaism and Christianity believe in the same God.

Patently false. It is true that Jesus was a Jew and that christianity
took over some of the Jewish mythology and historical stories* as a
base belief system, but the way that you understand your 'god' is quite
different from how jews in general think of theirs.

In any case many christians believe that Jesus is 'god' or is part of a
'trinity' that was invented to avoid no longer being monotheist.

* _some_ of the jewish bible is undoubedly based on historical events,
more so in the more recent stories especially after it was written down
starting around 800 BCE. Of course even these are elaborated and
mythologised, as was the style with all writings of the times.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 44)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-14T15:42:26-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <GYXnf.256524$ir4.74044@edtnps90> <J7Znf.8421$kP5.5458@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <uoo0q1hnnok7hbutqrh3o58nv4sk5uqbhp@4ax.com> <JNZnf.8430$kP5.4376@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <1134590230.286602.182240@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>> Judaism and Christianity believe in the same God.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> different from how jews in general think of theirs.

That is no argument, Richard. It is not how we think of God that makes Him 
Who He is. And you clearly speak from ignorance. Even the Jews acknowledge 
we worship the same God, we simply believe somewhat differently about some 
aspects. Jews who become Christians call themselves "Completed Jews." You 
may be unaware of this, but there is quite a lot of cooperation and goodwill 
these days between Jews and Christians. Christians, particularly 
Protestants, have a great interest in seeing Israel prosper, and there has 
always been a great interest in tourism to Israel among Christians. In my 
experience, modern Jews and Christians have mutual respect for each other's 
beliefs, and generally get along quite well. Jews are very sensitive about 
being proselytized, though I haven't found them to be reluctant to discuss 
our various beliefs.

> In any case many christians believe that Jesus is 'god' or is part of a
> 'trinity' that was invented to avoid no longer being monotheist.

You are unfamiliar with the Old testament, I see. From the beginning, many 
of the names and words for God in the Old Testament are plural. "Then God 
said, "Let Us make man in Our image, according to Our likeness;..." (Genesis 
1:26). The "central creed" of Judaism is "Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, 
the Lord is one!" (Deuteronomy 6:4). The actual Hebrew here says "Our God 
(plural) is One (singular)." The plurality that we now know as the Trinity 
is deeply embedded in these Scriptures kept by Jews since Moses. The Jews 
have known all along that there was a mysterious plurality about God, but 
only after the appearance of Jesus was the nature of this plurality made 
clear (to Christians). The Jews won't accept that, of course, before 
accepting Jesus. The Jews even believe in a coming Messiah (Christ), they 
just don't believe Jesus is Him.
```

- **References:** `<dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <GYXnf.256524$ir4.74044@edtnps90> <J7Znf.8421$kP5.5458@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <uoo0q1hnnok7hbutqrh3o58nv4sk5uqbhp@4ax.com> <JNZnf.8430$kP5.4376@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <1134590230.286602.182240@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <Oi0of.8457$kP5.7222@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:Oi0of.8457$kP5.7222@bignews5.bellsouth.net...

> You are unfamiliar with the Old testament, I see. From the beginning, many 
> of the names and words for God in the Old Testament are plural.

In form, yes, in particular the third word of the Hebrew Bible:  "Elohim". 
Not clear that it had plural *meaning*, however.

> The "central creed" of Judaism is "Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the 
> Lord is one!" (Deuteronomy 6:4). The actual Hebrew here says "Our God 
> (plural) is One (singular)."

Not exactly.  Better:  "Hear, O Israel:  YHWH our-Elohim [is] one [YHWH]", 
where our-Elohim is actually "Eloheynu", plural in form simply because 
Elohim is plural in form.

> The plurality that we now know as the Trinity is deeply embedded in these 
> Scriptures kept by Jews since Moses.

Funny that the *term* Trinity doesn't appear until about 200AD.

> The Jews have known all along that there was a mysterious plurality about 
> God, but only after the appearance of Jesus was the nature of this 
> plurality made clear (to Christians).

I think that's rather an overstatement of the significance of the plural 
form of Elohim.

> The Jews won't accept that, of course, before accepting Jesus. The Jews 
> even believe in a coming Messiah (Christ), they just don't believe Jesus 
> is Him.

Conflation of concepts.  The Jew has to accept *two* concepts contrary to 
their beliefs:  first, Jesus is the Anointed One (= Christos in Greek, 
Meshiach in Hebrew) promised to them in scripture -- they don't believe he 
matches the prophecies -- and *second* that Jesus is the Second Person of 
the Holy Trinity, which goes directly against the clear meaning of the Sh'ma 
to them.

Kind of like asking an Evangelical Christian to accept *as fact* that Jesus 
and Mary Magdalen had a bunch of kids and that those kids were the 
foundation of (and survive as) the major royal houses of Europe.   Or to 
defend the position thatJesus' mom was not only a virgin in the strictest 
sense when Jesus was born but was by nature spiritually flawless (the 
Immaculate Conception) throughout her life and was assumed bodily into 
heaven (the Assumption) while still alive, because nothing less would be 
appropriate for the Mother of God.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:J7Znf.8421$kP5.5458@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
[snip]
>>    Not nescessarily. You can compare this to a child playing with toy 
>> soldiers. From the point of view of those soldiers, that child is 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> hand, frequently do all of the above. :-)

    The soldiers make the decisions that the child wanted them to make, and 
take the actions that the child wanted them to make. There is no evidence 
that there isn't some being forcing us to make the decisions we make and 
forcing us to take the actions we take.

>
>>> The real God permits people a choice, calls upon them to do right, 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Doesn't seem likely to me.

    I argue that the God who gives us no free will cares more about our 
wants and desires than the God who does. The God who gives free will might 
do so because he thinks each of our wants and desires is a unique a 
beautiful thing, or he may do so because he simply doesn't care. But the God 
who gives us no free will must specifically assign us with wants and desires 
if we are to have them at all. A God who specifically assigns us with a 
desire does so because he wants us to have that specific desire, as opposed 
to any other desire. Between a laid back God who lets things happen however 
they happen versus a control-freak God who wants everything to happen in an 
exact, specific way, the latter probably cares more.

    Instead of a boy playing with soldiers, imagine now a girl playing with 
dolls. While the boy might have sought entertainment from violence, the girl 
is more interested in emotional interaction and social conflict between her 
toys. She forces her male doll to fall in love with her female doll, and she 
forces her female doll to not love him back, and entertains herself with the 
drama that unfolds.

    - Oliver
```

- **References:** `<Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <GYXnf.256524$ir4.74044@edtnps90> <J7Znf.8421$kP5.5458@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <7z%nf.256590$ir4.31133@edtnps90> <4e11q1lqiaauk3ccm5koman2h6r3ojum1b@4ax.com> <s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com>`

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> proper.

God has given us the evidence of the entire creation.

He has given us the Bible as evidence, in which God clearly warns us about 
the coming judgment. For every word of mercy and grace in the Bible, there 
are three words describing the judgment. The Bible declares that only a fool 
says there is no God.

He has given His only Son to die for our sins, to save us from that terrible 
judgment.

He has given us the evidence of the testimony of millions of Christians who 
know Him.

We accept or reject these evidences as we choose. A holy and just God who 
has done all these things to save a sinful and rebellious people, then sees 
those He created, who depend on Him for their every breath, reject His 
evidence and His sacrifice, is going to do what? Give those who reject Him a 
gentle reprimand? Listen to those who reject Him for advice? He offers us 
the choice of following Him or rejecting Him. He clearly spells out the 
consequences of each choice in His Word, the Bible. Each of us gets what we 
choose. Those who reject a merciful God Who has sacrificed His Son to save 
them, get no say in the matter of consequences, nor should they. Do 
criminals get to decide the punishment for their crimes? Should they? Do 
they typically think the punishment is fair, or even admit their guilt? Such 
is the human heart.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84>`
- **References:** `<Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <GYXnf.256524$ir4.74044@edtnps90> <J7Znf.8421$kP5.5458@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <7z%nf.256590$ir4.31133@edtnps90> <4e11q1lqiaauk3ccm5koman2h6r3ojum1b@4ax.com> <s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com> <Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> guilt? Such is the human heart.

    There are a few important differences between 
criminals-versus-democratic-government and sinners-versus-God. I'll list a 
few, but surely people can come up with others:

    * In most democratic governments, you aren't considered a criminal the 
moment you are born, and are forced to actively rid yourself of your 
criminality. E.g. you aren't a criminal because someone, a very long time 
ago, commited "the original Crime" for which all citizens must now pay for.
    * In most democratic governments, you DO have the option of changing the 
laws. You can vote for representatives who support the laws you agree with, 
and not vote for those that support the laws you disagree with.
    * There are checks and balances in place specifically to ensure that the 
government does not have "too much" power, because it is widely believed 
that absolute power corrupts absolutely.
    * The members of the government exists in the natural world. That is, 
there is scientific evidence for the existence of the government. There are 
experiments that anyone can conduct to convince themselves of the existence 
of the government.
    * In the same vein, the enforcers of the law (i.e. police officers) 
exists in the natural world, so you can witness them too. You can ask such 
an enforcer if you will be punished for such a crime, and not only you will 
hear the answer, but everyone around you will again. It is not a case of "I 
*know* in my heart of the enforcer's existence", but rather, multiple people 
have all witnessed the same event, and thus can vouch for the same 
interpretation of "what actually happened".
    * Most punishments that the government issues are not "for all 
eternity". Most of them are not even "for your entire life". If you don't 
believe that you will get punished for breaking a law, you can try it, and 
when you get punished (assuming the existence of the government here), 
you'll learn your lesson, and then are re-released, and now you know better.
    * If you don't like the government, you are free to leave for a country 
where another government is in power. You have choices.

    - Oliver
```

- **References:** `<Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <GYXnf.256524$ir4.74044@edtnps90> <J7Znf.8421$kP5.5458@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <7z%nf.256590$ir4.31133@edtnps90> <4e11q1lqiaauk3ccm5koman2h6r3ojum1b@4ax.com> <s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com> <Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84>`

```
"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
> for.

Your description is incorrect. We are only responsible for our own sins, and 
(as explained in another post) babies and young children are not held 
accountable for sin. The importance of Adam is that, once Adam decided to 
sin, Adam's spirit became corrupted, breaking his intimate fellowship with 
God, and he passed that trait along to all of us. The Bible says the human 
heart is "deceitful and wicked." It is true that the result of Adam's 
original sin is that all of us are born with a sin nature, therefore we all 
sin. God's intervention throughout history is to the purpose of bringing 
manking back into fellowship with Him, culminating with the sacrifice of His 
sinless Son Jesus to pay for those sins. After Adam and Eve's Fall, God said 
to Satan: "And I will put enmity Between you and the woman, And between your 
seed and her Seed; He shall bruise your head, And you shall bruise His 
heel." (Genesis 3:15) "Her Seed" here is generally understood to be a 
reference to the coming Messiah.

>    * In most democratic governments, you DO have the option of changing 
> the laws. You can vote for representatives who support the laws you agree 
> with, and not vote for those that support the laws you disagree with.

True. But when God creates an entire universe from nothing all by Himself, 
then God gets to choose the laws. What's wrong with that? If criminals could 
make the law, do you think those laws would be good? I don't think so. In 
this situation, we are the criminals, every one of us.

>    * There are checks and balances in place specifically to ensure that 
> the government does not have "too much" power, because it is widely 
> believed that absolute power corrupts absolutely.

Yes, but when the issue of corruption is removed, as it is with a holy and 
just God, there is no point. The best form of government there is would be a 
" wise and benevolent dictatorship". Unfortunately, with human government 
guaranteed wisdom and benevolency is impossible, therefore human 
dictatorships are always a disaster. Which serves to illustrate my point 
above.

>    * The members of the government exists in the natural world. That is, 
> there is scientific evidence for the existence of the government. There 
> are experiments that anyone can conduct to convince themselves of the 
> existence of the government.

God Himself came into this world as a man. It is without question the best 
documented event of comparable antiquity. People who don't believe it aren't 
disbelieving because of lack of evidence, they simply choose not to believe. 
See the book of John, chapter 1.

>    * In the same vein, the enforcers of the law (i.e. police officers) 
> exists in the natural world, so you can witness them too. You can ask such 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the same interpretation of "what actually happened".

Hah! If you have 20 people see an accident, you will get 20 differing 
versions, many of them mutually contradictory. :-)  However, if you like 
witnesses, why do you ignore the witness of the Bible, and millions of 
Christians?

>    * Most punishments that the government issues are not "for all 
> eternity". Most of them are not even "for your entire life". If you don't 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> better.

Those governments do not exist "for all eternity", those governments did not 
*create* the beings being judged, nor do they by the constant exercise of 
their power and authority provide the very life and existence of those being 
judged. The Bible says, in reference to Jesus:

"He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn over all creation. For 
by Him all things were created that are in heaven and that are on earth, 
visible and invisible, whether thrones or dominions or principalities or 
powers. All things were created through Him and for Him. And He is before 
all things, and in Him all things consist." (Colossians 1:15-17)

The original Greek language translated as "in Him all things consist" 
expresses that everything continues to exist (present and ongoing) through 
the constant exercise of His power.

>    * If you don't like the government, you are free to leave for a country 
> where another government is in power. You have choices.

We have choices too, and so do criminals. I'm just not sure why you would 
hand over the government to the criminals, unless you are a criminal, which 
we all are before God, of course. However, God has prepared a pardon for us; 
all we have to do is receive it.

What human government has ever offered a complete, blanket pardon for *every 
single criminal* on the simple terms that they only have to do one thing: 
receive this pardon by choosing to believe in it?

And people still accuse God of being unjust. Just as the Bible says, the 
human heart is truly deceitful and wicked above all things. :-)
```

ever was but the word translated as 'seed' (root zayin-resh-ayin 
pronounced 'ZEH-rah') is used for all manner of descendants/offspring.

other verses this word is used to refer to plants, crops, miscellaneous 
progeny... how is this now taken to be 'generally understood to be a 
reference to the coming Messiah'?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 49)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-12-17T23:47:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
>
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>
I think so too, and I don't think Howard is wrong to express concern about 
it. I am surprised that you think it is OK...

>>>> If we had such torture here on earth, would anything else be as
>>>> important as stopping it?
>>>>
>>>> Of course, that is a big issue to people who dislike torture.

Well, even if there's just a few of us (and I believe there are many), it is 
an issue for the world at large to re-examine.

I'm not persuaded that punishment actually serves any useful purpose. 
Correction can be done without punishment.
Given that I'm not sure about punishment, it is hardly surprising that I 
share Howard's concern about torture.

I don't actually worry about threats from God because I don't accept that He 
is real (and if He was, He certainly wouldn't be making such threats...in 
fact, He'd handle His whole relationship with Man a lot better than has been 
the case.)

God behaves like Man (and small men at that) because He was invented by Man, 
in response to Man's own needs. He reflects the mores and values that were 
correct at the time the Bible was written. If God were real, that would be 
unlikely. In an age where punishment was severe, people expected severe 
punishment and had little respect for anyone who didn't dish it out. The 
world has moved on. The same behaviour viewed by today's society, simply 
appalls and repels us.

The Bible today has a much more thinking audience; many don't like what they 
read.



>>>>
>>>> It doesn't make sense to me that so many people accept it as right and
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> Bible, there are three words describing the judgment.

That says a lot about it's failure to capture the imagination in the way 
that, say, the Da Vinci Code did... Thinking people are not so easily 
intimidated as peasants who can't even read and need a Priest to tell them 
what to think.


>>>The Bible declares  that only a fool says there is no God.

I completely agree with that. It is sound common sense. Denying the 
existence of God is foolish in the extreme. It changes no minds and creates 
tension and friction with True Believers. I have done so here because I'd 
rather be considered a fool than be considered dishonest.

>>>
>>> He has given His only Son to die for our sins, to save us from that 
>>> terrible judgment.
>>>

It was pointless and unnecessary, if it were true. Jesus was undoubtedly 
crucified (Roman records attest to it) but not for the sins of the world. It 
was because he was a danger to the status quo (particularly the established 
Jewish administration under the Roman Governor. He said and did things that 
were blasphemous to the establishment, and he refuted Old Testament values 
that had been held sacred for thousands of years. Such a man is dangerous. 
He had to be removed and discredited. He was. Even the Roman governor was 
pretty disgusted by it, but had little choice other than to go along. (There 
was enough restlessness in Judaea already; Pilate could not afford more...)

It was only the apostles who claimed he died for Man. It was only the 
apostles who claimed he rose again. They had a vested interest (especially 
Paul...).

I contest your statement above. Mainly because, despite repeated requests to 
do so, you have made no plausible explanation as to why such a blood 
sacrifice was necessary. An all powerful god would have used a different and 
less obtuse way to get the message to Mankind.

>>> He has given us the evidence of the testimony of millions of Christians 
>>> who know Him.
>>>

And conflicting testimony from  even more millions of equally devout 
non-Christians who will all burn in Hell because they don't wish to join the 
elite Jehovah club.

>>> We accept or reject these evidences as we choose.

Only if he isn't infinite and has granted us this ability. (If He's infinite 
he cannot grant it...By definition, He must be part of every choice we make, 
and He must have had prior knowledge of what that choice would be, for every 
possible choice of every possible individual on the Planet...)

>>> A holy and just God who has done all these things to save a sinful and 
>>> rebellious people, then sees those He created, who depend on Him for 
>>> their every breath, reject His evidence and His sacrifice, is going to 
>>> do what?

If He's Holy, He should be above such things as revenge and punishment. If 
He's just, He should respect the choice freely made (why give Man the power 
to make choices, if You are going to punish the choices he then makes?).

Draw your own conclusion.



>>>Give those who reject Him a gentle reprimand? Listen to those who reject 
>>>Him for advice? He offers us the choice of following Him or rejecting 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
Judson, you live in a terrible world. I'm really glad I'm not part of it.

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> accountable for sin.

I'm glad. It seems to me the relationship with God wasn't working anyway.

Please show where Jesus ever said that or anything approximating it. The Old 
Testament prophets, specifically Jeremaiah, (and if ever someone needed 
help, it was him), said that.

I refute it as both a generalisation and an undistributed middle.

Some people are deceitful and wicked, sometimes. Some such people do see the 
error of their ways and reform, others remain bad until the end of their 
days. Either way, there is no justification for tarring all of us with the 
same brush. The only reason I can think of to do so would be to induce 
guilt. Once you have induced guilt, you can make a nice little earner in 
helping people get over it...Good Business. Start a church.

> It is true that the result of Adam's original sin is that all of us are 
> born with a sin nature, therefore we all sin.
So new-born babies are NOT innocent. Above, you said they were.

> God's intervention throughout history is to the purpose of bringing 
> manking back into fellowship with Him, culminating with the sacrifice of 
> His sinless Son Jesus to pay for those sins.

Where does Jesus claim to be sinless? And why does it matter?

> After Adam and Eve's Fall, God said to Satan: "And I will put enmity 
> Between you and the woman, And between your seed and her Seed; He shall 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No it isn't; it is generally understood to be Mankind.

(In particular, Australians...)

(...well, that's no more ridiculous than what you said....:-))

>>    * In most democratic governments, you DO have the option of changing 
>> the laws. You can vote for representatives who support the laws you agree 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> so. In this situation, we are the criminals, every one of us.

Actually, I have no problem with God making the laws, so long as both He and 
they, are consistent. That's where it all goes pear shaped...

As for being a criminal in the eyes of God, speak for yourself...

>
>>    * There are checks and balances in place specifically to ensure that 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> point above.

It may illustrate the point above but it doesn't address the point that was

Holy? But that Holiness doesn't stop Him demanding retribution and behaving

>
>>    * The members of the government exists in the natural world. That is, 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> believe. See the book of John, chapter 1.

Yeah, right. Written at least 65 years after the events (may have been as

Gospel is quite different to the others.


>

I had to scan and store a text document for a friend of my father about
one year back. The guy had retired but was heavily involved in studying
criminality and had quite a good reputation in those academic circles.

laws creates criminals and that creating more laws would not reduce the
number of criminals but would unnecessarily criminalize more people. He
seemed to advocate the abolish of law.

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Not quite the same thing, Alastair.

Punishing people who break the law can be argued to be axiomatically 
ineffective. We have lessened the severity of punishments over the centuries 
and crime has not decreased. We increase the severity of punishments and 
crime does not decrease.

Many criminals claim that punishment is not a deterrent to them.

I'm not saying we shouldn't lock people up and remove them from society for 
certain crimes; I'm simply saying that doing so in order to punish them, 
serves no useful purpose.

We should either work to change their mind about crime (rehabilitation), or 
simply remove people who have shown they cannot live in society without 
creating mayhem, and are unlikely to be rehabilitated. (Incarceration or 
execution). The imposition of penalties purely as "punishment", to my mind, 
serves no useful purpose. It comes from the primitive desire to "get even". 
"An eye for an eye..." etc. until the whole world is blind and toothless...

The point is I am not advocating the removal of penalties for breaking the 
law; I am advocating the removal of penalties which are imposed purely from 
a desire to punish.

Pete.

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net...
>
> Punishing people who break the law can be argued to be axiomatically 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Many criminals claim that punishment is not a deterrent to them.

    Instead of looking at punishment (i.e. the intent of the authorities in 
power), we might want to look at pain (i.e. the effect). The reason people 
don't like the be punished is because it is "painful" (perhaps 
psychologically or financially, if not physically).

    If there were no penalty for not paying the parking meter, for example, 
I suspect a lot less people would pay it. It is primarily out of fear of 
receiving a parking ticket that one pays the meter.

>
> I'm not saying we shouldn't lock people up and remove them from society 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> from a desire to punish.

    Agreed. This is the conclusion that comes out of my "no free will" 
philosophy too.

    - Oliver
```

<SNIP>
> Punishing people who break the law can be argued to be axiomatically 
> ineffective. We have lessened the severity of punishments over the 
> centuries and crime has not decreased. We increase the severity of 
> punishments and crime does not decrease.

You should read the following:
http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/uk/4544238.stm

It's along the same lines but if I recall correctly a counter argument for 
the most part.

> Many criminals claim that punishment is not a deterrent to them.

> I'm not saying we shouldn't lock people up and remove them from society 
> for certain crimes; I'm simply saying that doing so in order to punish 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

The Guv'nor Ahnuld Schwarzenegger had an opportunity to make this kind of 
statement with Tookie but decided to focus on denial of the original 
murders.  It was a missed opportunity to change the system from punishment 
to rehabilitation (or at least redemption) but it never happened.  It was a 
clemency appeal, not a "free me" appeal.

Whether he deserved to die or not is not for me to say; however, I hope that 
the opportunity to discuss "redemption" as a whole isn't thrown away.

> The point is I am not advocating the removal of penalties for breaking the 
> law; I am advocating the removal of penalties which are imposed purely 
> from a desire to punish.

I'd like to see a penalty that didn't punish

> Pete.

- **References:** `<Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <GYXnf.256524$ir4.74044@edtnps90> <J7Znf.8421$kP5.5458@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <7z%nf.256590$ir4.31133@edtnps90> <4e11q1lqiaauk3ccm5koman2h6r3ojum1b@4ax.com> <s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com> <Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <UU6qf.45288$6e.8827@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```

"Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:UU6qf.45288$6e.8827@tornado.tampabay.rr.com...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
> news:40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/uk/4544238.stm

OK, I did. It is not what I'm arguing about. It is about reclassifying the 
crime of murder, and providing different levels of penalty for different 
degrees of abhorrence. It makes sense to me, but it is not really what I was 
talking about.

>
> It's along the same lines but if I recall correctly a counter argument for 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
And you know this... how?

>> I'm not saying we shouldn't lock people up and remove them from society 
>> for certain crimes; I'm simply saying that doing so in order to punish 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> a clemency appeal, not a "free me" appeal.

I have no idea what that was about, but I wouldn't think the idea I am 
presenting is known or understood by AS.


>
> Whether he deserved to die or not is not for me to say; however, I hope 
> that the opportunity to discuss "redemption" as a whole isn't thrown away.
>

Again,  I cannot comment.

>> The point is I am not advocating the removal of penalties for breaking 
>> the law; I am advocating the removal of penalties which are imposed 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

OK, here's just a few...

Community service...
Working with "bad" kids....
Penalties that involve the perpetrator doing something positive, maybe 
directly with the victim as an "amends project"...
Corrective establishments that work to increase the self esteem of the 
inmates, and make them worthwhile members of society. This could involve 
trade training, adult teaching, or even arts and the humanities.

All of the above are technically "penalties" (inasmuch as some of the 
perpetrator's time and freedom is taken off them); but rather than them just 
sitting in a cell or breaking rocks, they can acquire skills that help them 
grow. The net result is that the penalty was paid, but the perpetrator was 
not "punished" (there were no floggings or other degradation).

Paying the penalty for misbehaviour is not necessarily punishment. Rather, 
it can be a growth experience that lessens the likelihood of recidivism 
(when payment is made in a positive way).

In my opinion, everybody (the perpetrator, the victim, and society as a 
whole) can win if someone's mind can be changed about needing to commit 
crime.

For confirmed criminals and certain crimes that are "beyond the pale" 
(anythng involving children, or cowardly acts of terrorism, where there is 
no chance of rehabilitation, no doubt whatsoever of guilt, and no sign of 
remorse, I would advocate a quick clean, painless, needle in the back of the 
hand. This saves society as a whole (and the taxpayer in particular) wasting 
any more time and money on a hopeless cause.

The point is, it isn't about "punishment" (at least, it shouldn't be...) it 
is about changing attitudes and salvaging people where possible, and not 
wasting time where that isn't possible...

Pete.
```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:40sq24F1c7lssU1@individual.net...
>
> "Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> was talking about.

But the positions taken in argument were:
"shorter sentences and it will be seen by a number of criminals as a licence 
to murder"
and
"I'd much rather have that than have many, many more manslaughter verdicts"

Which both address the level of crime in a a roudabout kind of way.  On the 
one head lessening punishment could increase crime and diametrically that 
lessening punishments could decrease to more convictions (which I assume 
means less crime due to incarceration at least and that repeat offenders are 
common).

On second reading though, it is not aligned to your argument.  My apologies.

>> It's along the same lines but if I recall correctly a counter argument 
>> for the most part.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> And you know this... how?

Because I extended from all other areas of life.  People are surprised being 
caught "downloading porn", "having an affair", "taking a 'mental health' 
day", taking "political contributions", "eavesdropping".   To me, the act of 
wearing a mask  or a hat, using a stolen vehicle or hiding behind fake ids 
says that effort is made to to _not_ get caught.  Those that commit crime in 
the broad of day appear [in general based on hearsay] be desperate or 
extremely cocky.  The desperate don't care if they get caught, and the cocky 
still don't think they will.

I'd lie if I said I have been to a state pen and taken a poll on the number 
of people who thought that they'd actuall be there.  So perhaps the correct 
statement was "In my opinion.......most criminals don't expect to get caught 
AND not get away with some minor punishment".

>>> I'm not saying we shouldn't lock people up and remove them from society 
>>> for certain crimes; I'm simply saying that doing so in order to punish 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> presenting is known or understood by AS.

Am I inferring that you think Austrians are stupid? Or you think US 
governors are stupid? Hmmm....for me to know.

Tookie was the founder of the Crips but later in life - to some at least - 
condemned the gang way of life and had turned his life around.  He was 
alleged to have killed 4 people but the main evidence was hearsay of other 
convicted felons.  He denied that he had pulled the trigger and for that 
reason clemency was denied as he had failed to show remorse.  That's my 
summary.

>> Whether he deserved to die or not is not for me to say; however, I hope 
>> that the opportunity to discuss "redemption" as a whole isn't thrown 
>> away.
> Again,  I cannot comment.

But his appeal cuts right down the center of your argument.  Tookie - was 
there value in his execution ?  Did he fulfil the criteria for "beyond the 
pale" ?


>>> The point is I am not advocating the removal of penalties for breaking 
>>> the law; I am advocating the removal of penalties which are imposed 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Working with "bad" kids....
Criminals are perhaps the wrong people to be working with bad kids

> Penalties that involve the perpetrator doing something positive, maybe 
> directly with the victim as an "amends project"...
When forced by judgment this aids who?

> Corrective establishments that work to increase the self esteem of the 
> inmates, and make them worthwhile members of society. This could involve 
> trade training, adult teaching, or even arts and the humanities.
This happens inside a number of systems.  Sometimes it works. They need to 
do more to determine why it doesn't.

> All of the above are technically "penalties" (inasmuch as some of the 
> perpetrator's time and freedom is taken off them); but rather than them 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> degradation).

Ok.  So you're liberal with what constitutes no punishment - but I'm with 
you.  The issue is not so much what they do when they are "IN" jail, it's 
what they are do when they are "OUT" of jail.   No point giving someone work 
to do and feeding them and patting them on the back if you release them back 
to the environment from whence they came.....they've "cured" drug addicts 
and then sent them back to crack homes...the results are obvious to say the 
least.

> Paying the penalty for misbehaviour is not necessarily punishment. Rather, 
> it can be a growth experience that lessens the likelihood of recidivism 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> wasting any more time and money on a hopeless cause.

Ah. The caveat.  Every good opinion needs one :-)

> The point is, it isn't about "punishment" (at least, it shouldn't be...) 
> it is about changing attitudes and salvaging people where possible, and 
> not wasting time where that isn't possible...

Agree in principle -   How's about that!  I think I struggle more to apply 
the fantasy to reality.  It's kind of a "I wish the world were.........." 
argument.

> Pete.

DU

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Cjfqf.76$km.45@edtnps89>`
- **References:** `<Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <GYXnf.256524$ir4.74044@edtnps90> <J7Znf.8421$kP5.5458@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <7z%nf.256590$ir4.31133@edtnps90> <4e11q1lqiaauk3ccm5koman2h6r3ojum1b@4ax.com> <s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com> <Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <UU6qf.45288$6e.8827@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40sq24F1c7lssU1@individual.net> <ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
"Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
>> I have no idea what that was about, but I wouldn't think the idea I am 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> governors are stupid? Hmmm....for me to know.

    I think all one can reasonably infer from this is that Pete thinks AS is 
ignorant/stupid, not that he thinks all those who shares a particular 
attribute with AS are ignorant/stupid.

[...]
>> Working with "bad" kids....
> Criminals are perhaps the wrong people to be working with bad kids

    "Criminal" does not nescessarily imply "morally bad". I think every 
government has its share of weird, obscure law. According to 
http://www.lawguru.com/weird/part01.html, in Texas, it is illegal to milk 
another person's cow for example.

    Now, let's ignore for the moment whether it is true or not that this 
actually really is a law in Texas; it's certainly plausible, so for the sake 
of this example, let's just assume it's true (if it turns out to be false, a 
similar example could be constructed with another obscure, weird law that 
happened to be real).

    Say someone, person A, is considered to be THE leading expert on working 
with bad kids. This person is a child psychologist, pedagogist, loving 
mother, won several Nobel prizes for their advancement in the area of 
rehabilitating "bad" kids, etc. And as a favour to her neighbor and at that 
neighbor's request, she milked her neighbor's cow for her (perhaps the 
neighbor was sick that day and could not do it herself).

    Now this person A is a criminal, but obviously is the best person to be 
working with bad kids. This is of course an extreme example, but it 
demonstrate that being a criminal has no direct relation to being a "bad 
person". Ideally, the laws of a government and the "laws" of morality are in 
alignment, but the definition of the word "criminal" does not require this 
assumption to be true before labeling someone a "criminal".

    If the criminal in question were a child molester, then yes, I'd be wary 
about letting that criminal near my child (assuming I happen to have a "bad" 
child). On the other hand, if the crime was shoplifting, joy riding, or 
something else, then I'd be a lot less wary -- especially if I got a chance 
to meet the criminal and judge their character myself.


>> Penalties that involve the perpetrator doing something positive, maybe 
>> directly with the victim as an "amends project"...
>
> When forced by judgment this aids who?

    This is a good point, and one of the reasons I don't like the idea of 
affirmative action. However, this "punishment" need not be forced by the 
judge. It could be presented as one choice among many that the criminal may 
choose from (e.g. the amends project, community service or time in prison, 
perhaps).

    - Oliver
```

>

Ooops! There you go being logical again, Oliver :-).  While your logic is 
impeccable and I commend it, I actually don't think AS is either ignorant or 
stupid. All I think is that he PROBABLY hasn't thought about "punishment" in 
the terms I described...

(I certainly wouldn't want to offend The Terminator... :-))
> [...]

>>> Working with "bad" kids....
>> Criminals are perhaps the wrong people to be working with bad kids
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Good point, I'm not quite sure whether people here have options or not. (If 
not, I agree it would be a very good idea...)We certainly try to avoid 
"punishment" because in, and of itself, it is of no value.

Note that the justice system of many countries includes "punishment" because 
religions show God meting it out. Therefore it must be right. We don't need 
to think about it. Any right thinking society will follow God's word, right? 
And that was what my original post was about.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <GYXnf.256524$ir4.74044@edtnps90> <J7Znf.8421$kP5.5458@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <7z%nf.256590$ir4.31133@edtnps90> <4e11q1lqiaauk3ccm5koman2h6r3ojum1b@4ax.com> <s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com> <Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <UU6qf.45288$6e.8827@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40sq24F1c7lssU1@individual.net> <ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

"Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
> news:40sq24F1c7lssU1@individual.net...
>>
<snip>>>
>>> Most criminals don't expect to get caught.
>>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Thank you. That is a very good response.

I think you're probably right (but I can't prove it any more than you can... 
:-))

>>>> I'm not saying we shouldn't lock people up and remove them from society 
>>>> for certain crimes; I'm simply saying that doing so in order to punish 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No, there was no implication. I can state my opinion in a more forthright 
manner as being the following:

1. I have no personal acquaintance with AS, and I don't live in California 
(or even the USA) so I am not subject to his thoughts in the media.

2. On the few interviews I have seen he seems to support a fairly right wing 
position and this would be reinforced in the public's mind by the macho 
image he presents in his movies. (I do not imply that this is bad; I 
actually have no opinion about it.)

Based on the limited set of information above, (and accepting that I could 
be wrong and he might actually be a very gentle man who loves animals and 
children and collects butterflies so he can release them), it seems unlikely 
that he would not ascribe to the principle of punishment. It is deeply 
ingrained into many societies and comes from the (in my opinion, mistaken) 
idea that punishing people makes them better people or dissuades them from 
offending in the first place.

There was no suggestion that  either Austrians or Californian Governors are 
stupid; I have lived in Austria and visited California many times.

> Tookie was the founder of the Crips but later in life - to some at least - 
> condemned the gang way of life and had turned his life around.  He was 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Thanks. It seems unfair to expect a man who claims he never did something, 
to show remorse for having done it. Perhaps his general attitude to what 
happened might have been more indicative...

>>> Whether he deserved to die or not is not for me to say; however, I hope 
>>> that the opportunity to discuss "redemption" as a whole isn't thrown 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> pale" ?

If he murdered 4 people he certainly did. If he didn't, but was involved, 
there is reason for some clemency. If his attitude was assessed as rendering 
him unlikely to offend again and he was sincere about having changed his 
life, then there was certainly a case for assisting him. The punishment 
achieved little (although the benefit to California taxpayers is 
undeniable...)
>
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Criminals are perhaps the wrong people to be working with bad kids

Funny you should say that... it works here. (Of course they are not 
unsupervised and they are very carefully psychologically screened before 
being allowed to do so.)
>
>> Penalties that involve the perpetrator doing something positive, maybe 
>> directly with the victim as an "amends project"...
> When forced by judgment this aids who?
>
Sometimes people start out being compelled to do something, then, when they 
do it, they find their attitude changes. I think most of us have had that 
experience. Think of times you have been compelled by social pressure to do 
things you really didn't want to do, then found you actually enjoyed them, 
in spite of yourself.

>> Corrective establishments that work to increase the self esteem of the 
>> inmates, and make them worthwhile members of society. This could involve 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It certainly works here. So does community service. But not infallibly... 
occasionally you get the "bad egg" who really doesn't belong in a civilized 
society. I reckon these ones should be exiled (Australia comes to mind... 
:-)) to a place where they are more likely to "fit in"...

>> All of the above are technically "penalties" (inasmuch as some of the 
>> perpetrator's time and freedom is taken off them); but rather than them 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
For a very long time I believed that the majority of drug addicts (and 
alcoholics, for that matter) were "incurable". This was based on friends I 
had lost through these terrible addictions. (Some of them were brilliant 
minds who had the potential to achieve much in their lives; dead at 27, or 
younger...).

However, I have now lived longer and seen more.

I now realize that there IS hope for such people. A very close young (34) 
friend of mine became attached to a cocaine and heroin addict and decided 
she was going to salvage him. I thought she had no chance; today they are a 
family, the man involved adores his wife and baby, has been straight for 
three years (and from quiet one-on-one chats we have had, I believe he will 
NEVER go back), lives an active outdoor lifestyle (surfing, fishing, 
workouts, etc.), and is becoming a model husband and father. It took five 
years with many falls, but finally the guy is so motivated and enjoying his 
life so much there is little chance of him reverting. He was an addict for 
nearly 10 years but managed to turn his life around in five. He had the 
support of a loving wife (who went through Hell), a  set of supportive 
extended family and friends, and the deep desire to do it. (And he didn't 
find Jesus along the way, either... :-)). I could mention two other cases I 
know of personally, but this one is the most dramatic.

Your point about the environment is very well taken. The case described 
above broke all connection with the environment where he was likely to be 
offered drugs. Part fo the responsibility for helping such people has to be 
ensuring they have the "best shot" possible of staying clean when they come 
out. Sometimes, where it is feasible, a move to a different town might be a 
requisite for continued support.

>> Paying the penalty for misbehaviour is not necessarily punishment. 
>> Rather, it can be a growth experience that lessens the likelihood of 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I may be "liberal", but I'm not an idiot... :-)

>> The point is, it isn't about "punishment" (at least, it shouldn't be...) 
>> it is about changing attitudes and salvaging people where possible, and 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Every advance ever consciously made by humans, started with a desire for 
change...

Pete.
```

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

Village of A will rebuild it.  I guess that if there is any semblance of a 
guilt then that would be horrible :-)

I enjoy very little that I am forced to do where I see no benefit.  I keep 
hoping someone forces me to do charity work at some point.  I'm so cynical I

issue.   I'd like to think that everyone can be helped.  I do think that it 
should be done in a secular manner...I don't like replacing a drug addiction

> However, I have now lived longer and seen more.

- **References:** `<Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <UU6qf.45288$6e.8827@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40sq24F1c7lssU1@individual.net> <ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40ubb7F1bt3lnU1@individual.net> <qrAqf.159$bd.139@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
On Thu, 22 Dec 2005 16:24:54 GMT, "Defaultuser"
<defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Oh, I can prove it...it just would require me finding a sufficient sample of 
>those who have been caught to get them to answer a few questions.  As they 
>might be biased...I would have to find another sampling of people who have 
>not been caught....ah, ok, I see the problem...

While I don't have supporting evidence - I believe while most crimes
are not solved - most criminals get caught.    This is because people
repeat what appears to work.   When crime pays, criminals continue to
commit crimes.

But s**t happens.    We've all had screw-ups in our work, whether we
are at fault, others are at fault, or just bad luck.   If your job is
crime, the results of screw-ups can be getting caught - or worse.

We end up with a population of people who are sometimes willing to
discuss their careers of crime, including that part of their careers
that occurred before the screw-up.
```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1135371462.109753.320120@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com...
>
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Less tahn 10 % of criminals get caught. Crime does pay.

And 87% of all statistics are made up on the spot :-)  The "success rate" in 
crime solution here is around 34%. Serious crimes would have a much higher 
solution rate.  I believe a lot more than 10% of criminals get caught here, 
but it is impossible to know how many without knowing the size of the pool 
of criminals... In the area where I live crime is low. This may be due to 
effective neighbourhood watch, or just that life is fairly easy and people 
are not therefore generally driven to crime. There was a burglary about 6 
months ago in a house a couple of streets away, and the offender was caught. 
The crime was drug (guy was on P) related.

I sleep at night with windows open and I leave my car unlocked all the time. 
(I was advised to do this by the cops... it is a convertible and the cost of 
replacing the hood would be far more than the cost of replacing the radio 
(which is pin coded anyway, so "professional" thieves won't touch it). In 
the 10 years I have owned this car (I can't bring myself to part with it 
:-)) I have never had anything removed from it, and the hood has never been 
slashed in order to gain entry.
>
>  This is because people
>> repeat what appears to work.   When crime pays, criminals continue to
>> commit crimes.
>>
Very likely. But punishment makes little difference.

Pete.
```

If I've mentioned it previously, sorry ! I think it was our Hyundai; we 
had a nose-bra to protect it against stones shooting up from the road.
Parked outside as we don't have a garage. Anyway some local yobbos or 
mischievous teenagers took it, (the bra, not the car) - never to be seen 
again. Don't know why we bothered but we phoned the police.

Dutifully two 14-year olds wearing police uniforms came to follow up our 
query. (Well they might have been 18, but who knows ?). Took down the 
facts then to summon it up I asked, "What are the chances of getting our 
bra back ?". One replied with a straight face, "Absolutely zero. And 
that's being optimistic".

Jimmy

>And 87% of all statistics are made up on the spot :-)  The "success rate" in 
>crime solution here is around 34%. Serious crimes would have a much higher 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>The crime was drug (guy was on P) related.

Chances are, that burglar denied that other burglary 8 months ago.
That crime is unsolved, so some people assume the miscreant hasn't
been caught.     But maybe he has been caught for some other crime.

How many people do you know who have had a full career without a screw
up happening?

When someone screws up a program that I'm responsible for, I fix it at
midnight and have to explain what happened to my boss.    When a
screw-up happens for a career criminal, the consequences are more
severe.
```

Unfortunately, it doesn't support your statement that <sic> "Less tahn 10 % 
of criminals get caught."

What it is talking about is crimes being solved, not criminals getting 
caught. If the detection rate is 2.2% then all you can say is that "less 
than 10% of crimes (in the U.K.) are solved".

For your statement to be true, you would need to know how many criminals 
there are in the U.K. This number is unknown. Some (most?) criminals commit 
more than one crime, some get caught, some don't, and some are of a criminal 
persuasion but do not directly participate in crime.

Without wishing to "split hairs" or be pedantic, there is a big difference 
between what your statement says, and what the statistics you supplied say.

BTW, I don't think it is really important... :-)

Pete.

```
On Fri, 30 Dec 2005 14:12:42 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>For your statement to be true, you would need to know how many criminals 
>there are in the U.K. This number is unknown. Some (most?) criminals commit 
>more than one crime, some get caught, some don't, and some are of a criminal 
>persuasion but do not directly participate in crime.

If every criminal commits exactly one crime apiece, then these figures
would be the same.    If you solve 10% of the crimes, you will catch
10% of the criminals.

If every criminal commits exactly two crimes apiece, then if you solve
10% of the crimes, you will catch somewhere between 10% and 20% of the
criminals.

If someone's profession is crime, he continues committing crimes.
Rolling the dice, his number will come up eventually.   Even for the
perfect professional (in programming or crime) - screwups happen.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Politics

_(reply depth: 64)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-01-04T15:59:50+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<420s1bF1gnvo3U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<40sq24F1c7lssU1@individual.net> <ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40ubb7F1bt3lnU1@individual.net> <qrAqf.159$bd.139@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <vullq1lbadpghrp4dbdhd12oslapqn01cs@4ax.com> <1135371462.109753.320120@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2cp2r1pqhetjra4taldb9jop1akg7kis21@4ax.com> <1135898516.371026.250460@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <41jfscF1e0qm9U1@individual.net> <t4iar1h0972vdgvndsqlaqak7l4hd5utng@4ax.com> <41llmvF1f32bdU1@individual.net> <k32lr11er3ql9vcrte8p30d6d3vsqorpnj@4ax.com>`

```
 
That is a very narrow and pedantic definition, Howard.

I agree it is technically accurate, but only in the exact sense of the 
words.

People who support crime, who endorse criminal values, who see a life of 
crime as romantic and bohemian, who sell goods they never stole themselves 
(but know full well to have been stolen) in pub bars and car boot sales, who 
think there is "street cred" in fraternising with and taking favours from, 
criminals, while they may never have committed a crime technically, are 
still, to my wayof thinking, much more culpable (and "criminal") than the 
guy who parks too long on a meter, jaywalks a crossing, or is found to be 
drunk and disorderly.

In all of my posts, I have used the term "criminal" in the wider, rather 
than narrower sense.

Even in the narrower sense, we still can't know how many "criminals" are out 
there because many crimes are not reported, for various reasons.

Pete.

TOP POST nothing new below.

Pete Dashwood wrote:

> I agree it is technically accurate, but only in the exact sense of the
> words.

So, your claim is that using a word accurately is 'narrow and
pedantic'.

> People who support crime, who endorse criminal values, who see a life of
> crime as romantic and bohemian, who sell goods they never stole themselves
> (but know full well to have been stolen) in pub bars and car boot sales,

If receiving stolen goods is a crime (and it is) then they _are_
criminals even when the word is used 'narrowly and pedantically'.

> who
> think there is "street cred" in fraternising with and taking favours from,
> criminals, while they may never have committed a crime technically, are
> still, to my wayof thinking, much more culpable (and "criminal") than the

You seem to want to condem people for _thinking_ about crime, bring on
the thought police.

> guy who parks too long on a meter, jaywalks a crossing, or is found to be
> drunk and disorderly.

Many of which are _not_ crimes (depending od country), thus the
infringers are _not_ criminals.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Politics

_(reply depth: 66)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-01-05T02:56:45+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4222h1F1gl0kqU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<40sq24F1c7lssU1@individual.net> <ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40ubb7F1bt3lnU1@individual.net> <qrAqf.159$bd.139@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <vullq1lbadpghrp4dbdhd12oslapqn01cs@4ax.com> <1135371462.109753.320120@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2cp2r1pqhetjra4taldb9jop1akg7kis21@4ax.com> <1135898516.371026.250460@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <41jfscF1e0qm9U1@individual.net> <t4iar1h0972vdgvndsqlaqak7l4hd5utng@4ax.com> <41llmvF1f32bdU1@individual.net> <k32lr11er3ql9vcrte8p30d6d3vsqorpnj@4ax.com> <420s1bF1gnvo3U1@individual.net> <1136349782.125618.89930@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1136349782.125618.89930@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com...
>
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Sometimes, if the word has a broader meaning, yes. Doesn't make it wrong, 
but it changes the argument...

>> People who support crime, who endorse criminal values, who see a life of
>> crime as romantic and bohemian, who sell goods they never stole 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> criminals even when the word is used 'narrowly and pedantically'.

Sure, I never argued that... But there are people who sell goods without 
technically "receiving" them in the legal sense of the word. Are they 
"criminals"? Not technically, in the strict meaning of the word, but I 
believe they are... My point (broadly) is that, while commiting a crime 
certainly makes one a criminal (in any sense of the word), there are people 
who don't necessarily commit crimes, but are part of a criminal community. I 
believe "punishment" should be designed to get people to realise that the 
negative outcomes from crime are bad for all of us and, if they are part of 
the criminal community but not yet technically criminal, encourage them to 
not go the next step. I further believe that  a softer, sensible approach is 
more likely to have this effect than a hard line God of Vengeance approach 
with "punishments" that demean the person who has to administer them, the 
perpetrator who is alienated by them, and all of the rest of us, who, as a 
community, condone these pointless penalties.

>> who
>> think there is "street cred" in fraternising with and taking favours 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You should know me better than that, Richard. It isn't a crime (and 
hopefully never will be) to THINK about ANYTHING.

Alternatives must be weighed and subjective decisions reached.  I'm not 
talking about people who think about crime; I'm talking about people who 
condone the criminal "way of life" and see it as a winning thing. People who 
think the general public are "mugs" and "fair game". I believe these people 
are just as much a problem for society as the criminals who have already 
broken the law. The difference is that these people are "innocent" under the 
law, even though they may not be in actuality. I'm not suggesting we arrest 
people for holding a point of view :-); I am suggesting we target such 
people with a view to changing their minds...

Social engineering in this day and age is achievable. Look at the drink and 
drive campaigns here in NZ that have caused a dramatic drop in the road 
toll... Blanket TV campaigns with slogans like "They drink; we die", "If you 
drink and drive, you're a bloody idiot" etc. After living in Germany for a 
number of years (where the idea of drinking and driving has been considered 
socially unacceptable for at least 25 years to my knowledge, and nobody  (i 
know) does it...) and then returning to NZ where it was considered a bit of 
a game to beat the cops and "everyone knew" that it was really OK to drink 
and drive as long as you only had a few... I have been agreeably surprised 
to find more and more people of all ages simply not doing it. Their minds 
have been changed. The result is a diminishing road toll, despite traffice 
increasing dramatically (around 10% per year).  Interestingly (and very 
sadly) this holiday period bucked the trend:
2000 = 20,
2001 = 21
2002 = 17
2003 = 13
2004 = 11
2005 = 20

Despite the increased holiday toll, the overall road deaths this year (404) 
was the lowest for 42 years. The campaigns are working. For statistical 
breakdowns over 5 years, check this link: 
http://www.ltsa.govt.nz/research/toll.html

It can be done. So far it hasn't been tried with crime, but I see no reason 
why it couldn't be.

>> guy who parks too long on a meter, jaywalks a crossing, or is found to be
>> drunk and disorderly.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, if you relate it to countries where this IS a crime, my argument 
stands.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<40sq24F1c7lssU1@individual.net> <ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40ubb7F1bt3lnU1@individual.net> <qrAqf.159$bd.139@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <vullq1lbadpghrp4dbdhd12oslapqn01cs@4ax.com> <1135371462.109753.320120@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2cp2r1pqhetjra4taldb9jop1akg7kis21@4ax.com> <1135898516.371026.250460@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <41jfscF1e0qm9U1@individual.net> <t4iar1h0972vdgvndsqlaqak7l4hd5utng@4ax.com> <41llmvF1f32bdU1@individual.net> <k32lr11er3ql9vcrte8p30d6d3vsqorpnj@4ax.com> <420s1bF1gnvo3U1@individual.net> <1136349782.125618.89930@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <4222h1F1gl0kqU1@individual.net>`

```

Pete Dashwood wrote:

> > Many of which are _not_ crimes (depending od country), thus the
> > infringers are _not_ criminals.
> >
> Well, if you relate it to countries where this IS a crime, my argument
> stands.

You miss the point, the law is divided up into Civil Law, Criminal Law
and Torts. Jaywalking and parking infringements are against the law,
but are only Torts and are not part of _Criminal_ Law, thus are not
'crimes'.

Or is that too narrow, pedantic and accurate for you ?
```
