```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:4222h1F1gl0kqU1@individual.net...

> I believe "punishment" should be designed to get people to realise that 
> the negative outcomes from crime are bad for all of us and, if they are 
> part of the criminal community but not yet technically criminal, encourage 
> them to not go the next step.

Getting people to realize that it is important not to do anything that's bad 
*for others* -- criminal or not -- is not so easy.  There are a *great* many 
people out there who are so egocentric that they are fundamentally unable to 
notice or to care that an action might deleteriously affect someone else. 
*Informing* someone that such an act is fundamentally harmful to all of us 
is a far cry from ensuring that they *learn* that that is the case, 
particularly when the actor is convinced that their actions are justifiable 
or reasonable under the circumstances.

     -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Politics

_(reply depth: 68)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-01-05T11:35:41+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4230u4F1gmo0eU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<40sq24F1c7lssU1@individual.net> <ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40ubb7F1bt3lnU1@individual.net> <qrAqf.159$bd.139@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <vullq1lbadpghrp4dbdhd12oslapqn01cs@4ax.com> <1135371462.109753.320120@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2cp2r1pqhetjra4taldb9jop1akg7kis21@4ax.com> <1135898516.371026.250460@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <41jfscF1e0qm9U1@individual.net> <t4iar1h0972vdgvndsqlaqak7l4hd5utng@4ax.com> <41llmvF1f32bdU1@individual.net> <k32lr11er3ql9vcrte8p30d6d3vsqorpnj@4ax.com> <420s1bF1gnvo3U1@individual.net> <1136349782.125618.89930@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <4222h1F1gl0kqU1@individual.net> <dph62s$29nb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
news:dph62s$29nb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> that their actions are justifiable or reasonable under the circumstances.

Yes, I completely agree. I used the word "persuade"; simply telling people 
is not enough. We have an industry designed to "persuade" people to buy 
stuff they neither need nor want. It is very effective. So this leads me to 
believe that people can be persuaded :-)

It needs to be a many-pronged attack starting in early school. And I see 
penalties for lawbreaking as being part of it. We have abolished the cat of 
nine tails and thumbscrews and hot irons.... let's simply continue this 
growth path and make penalties meaningful instead of pointless.

If there were penalties that fit the crime and had a positive outcome, I 
believe some minds could be changed. It seems to work here in places where 
we have done it.  Letting perpetrators see the full effect of their actions 
and the pain caused, not just to the victim, can have an effect. (It won't 
always, but neither does locking them up...) Responsibility and genuine 
remorse with compensation to the victim by the perpetrator, and constructive 
work to "pay off the debt" to society has to be a better form of 
"punishment" than simple deprivation of freedom or breaking rocks. 
Obviously, there are some crimes where this can't be done and I covered that 
in an earlier post.

That is the nub of my argument here.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Politics

_(reply depth: 66)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-01-04T07:33:52-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<bsmnr1dtvmmvpusnt6heb71tkm6f5rcfli@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<vullq1lbadpghrp4dbdhd12oslapqn01cs@4ax.com> <1135371462.109753.320120@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2cp2r1pqhetjra4taldb9jop1akg7kis21@4ax.com> <1135898516.371026.250460@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <41jfscF1e0qm9U1@individual.net> <t4iar1h0972vdgvndsqlaqak7l4hd5utng@4ax.com> <41llmvF1f32bdU1@individual.net> <k32lr11er3ql9vcrte8p30d6d3vsqorpnj@4ax.com> <420s1bF1gnvo3U1@individual.net> <1136349782.125618.89930@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 3 Jan 2006 20:43:02 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> guy who parks too long on a meter, jaywalks a crossing, or is found to be
>> drunk and disorderly.
>
>Many of which are _not_ crimes (depending od country), thus the
>infringers are _not_ criminals.

I don't like the "broader" definition.   It is too much like the way
the Religious Right defines sins that are not well defined in the
Scriptures.   Stuff that they don't like are just "wrong". 

Some conservative values are like this.   My music is right, my kids
music is wrong.   That person speaking Spanish is a criminal wetback.
Smoking is criminal.   Homosexuals will go to Hell.

Some liberal values are like this as well, wanting to punish thought
crimes.

If we step beyond what the law (or the scriptures) actually say and
allow us to interpret crime and sin to our tastes, we are in dangerous
territory.
```

exactly what I mean by the "broader" sense of "criminal". However, I realise 
that in a forum such as this the literal interpretation will be preferred. I 
don't believe there is any real effort to understand the very complex idea I 
am setting forth; it is easier to have an argument about the exact meaning 
of the words. That's CLC... :-)

Pete.

Pete.

- **Message-ID:** `<Znvwf.46733$km.36678@edtnps89>`
- **References:** `<UU6qf.45288$6e.8827@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40sq24F1c7lssU1@individual.net> <ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40ubb7F1bt3lnU1@individual.net> <qrAqf.159$bd.139@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <vullq1lbadpghrp4dbdhd12oslapqn01cs@4ax.com> <1135371462.109753.320120@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2cp2r1pqhetjra4taldb9jop1akg7kis21@4ax.com> <1135898516.371026.250460@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <41jfscF1e0qm9U1@individual.net> <t4iar1h0972vdgvndsqlaqak7l4hd5utng@4ax.com>`

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:t4iar1h0972vdgvndsqlaqak7l4hd5utng@4ax.com...
>
> If someone's profession is crime, he continues committing crimes.
> Rolling the dice, his number will come up eventually.   Even for the
> perfect professional (in programming or crime) - screwups happen.

    Assuming that a "criminal" is "someone who commits at least one crime", 
then there might exist a person who originally had no intent in ever 
becoming a criminal (i.e. has a regular day job), but then finds himself in 
a situation where he can commit a crime and be virtually guaranteed not to 
get caught, with a tremendous payoff. He mulls over the probabilities for a 
few days, and weighs the benefits versus the drawbacks, and eventually 
decides to do it. And never gets caught. And never commits a crime again.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Politics

_(reply depth: 63)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-01-09T08:52:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b215s1l88s1ld71dja4qapa8bt9sff7s1i@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<40sq24F1c7lssU1@individual.net> <ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40ubb7F1bt3lnU1@individual.net> <qrAqf.159$bd.139@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <vullq1lbadpghrp4dbdhd12oslapqn01cs@4ax.com> <1135371462.109753.320120@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2cp2r1pqhetjra4taldb9jop1akg7kis21@4ax.com> <1135898516.371026.250460@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <41jfscF1e0qm9U1@individual.net> <t4iar1h0972vdgvndsqlaqak7l4hd5utng@4ax.com> <Znvwf.46733$km.36678@edtnps89>`

```
On Mon, 09 Jan 2006 15:34:17 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
wrote:

>> If someone's profession is crime, he continues committing crimes.
>> Rolling the dice, his number will come up eventually.   Even for the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>decides to do it. And never gets caught. And never commits a crime again.

Sure.   I suspect a fair percentage of murders are like this.   But
there's no way that most criminals stop with one.   

I used the term "professional criminal", but we can expand this.   How
many speeders speed only once?   How many politicians accept exactly
one bribe?    How many abusers or rapists stop with one atrocity?

When you get away with something, the tendency is to keep going.    It
is my contention that most criminals keep committing crimes.

And it is my contention that when those who keep committing crimes
find that screw-ups happen.

I read a John Dortmunder story where this fictional thief was hired to
advice a rich man on how to steal a sculpture that that man's wife had
as part of her divorce settlement.   Dortmunder vetoed one of the rich
man's ideas because it involved breaking the law (flying a helicopter
to her house in Manhattan).    His rule was - only break the one law
you intend to break - people get arrested for double parking when they
meant to rob a bank.     Westlake is funny, but his point is valid -
stuff happens.
```

_(reply depth: 63)_

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41oh0aF1feei7U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <UU6qf.45288$6e.8827@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40sq24F1c7lssU1@individual.net> <ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40ubb7F1bt3lnU1@individual.net> <qrAqf.159$bd.139@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <vullq1lbadpghrp4dbdhd12oslapqn01cs@4ax.com> <1135371462.109753.320120@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2cp2r1pqhetjra4taldb9jop1akg7kis21@4ax.com> <1135898516.371026.250460@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <51iar1laco0g97jpubb7uos61f6ujccla8@4ax.com> <41llq7F1es2q3U1@individual.net> <VSitf.6804$l87.826552@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
 

"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message 
news:VSitf.6804$l87.826552@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It certainly seems that way sometimes. Generations of criminals (I became 
acquainted once with a professional pickpocket who was very proud of the 
fact that he was continuing a fine family tradition, now in its sixth 
generation, with skills and training being passed to each child) certainly 
exist, and many of them have never been caught. I don't know what the answer 
is in cases like that. I don't know how you persuade someone who has been 
exposed to that as a way of life, all their life, to go and get a job... :-)

You may have noticed that in several of my posts recently I have advocated 
that we stop relying/waiting/depending on God to sort the world out (which 
generally means salvation for the faithful and torture for the rest of us; 
not really much of a "sort out" in my opinion), and, instead, start taking 
some personal responsibility for ourselves and our communities. This is 
something that each of us can do, even if in a small way. We need to help 
the needy, not the greedy, and we need to support and encourage each other 
to grow. Parents need to show their kids (ideally by example) that they are 
part of a bigger picture and have a responsibility not just to their family 
and their community, but to all men (actually to all life forms...), so 
don't drop litter, don't vandalize, don't step on bugs or snakes, don't 
bully or abuse other kids, etc.

Pete.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<420se2F1g3dsnU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40ubb7F1bt3lnU1@individual.net> <qrAqf.159$bd.139@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <vullq1lbadpghrp4dbdhd12oslapqn01cs@4ax.com> <1135371462.109753.320120@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2cp2r1pqhetjra4taldb9jop1akg7kis21@4ax.com> <1135898516.371026.250460@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <51iar1laco0g97jpubb7uos61f6ujccla8@4ax.com> <41llq7F1es2q3U1@individual.net> <VSitf.6804$l87.826552@news20.bellglobal.com> <u82lr1huao3jbp6piu3ga8fulr0jikevt5@4ax.com>`

```
 

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:u82lr1huao3jbp6piu3ga8fulr0jikevt5@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 30 Dec 2005 17:52:37 -0500, Donald Tees
> <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I don't want to answer for Donald (who is more than capable of answering for 
himself :-)), but my take on this is that a "non-barbaric" system, by 
seeking to actually persuade people against crime and change their minds 
about the desirability of committing it (not just by threatening fearful 
punishments, but by actually addressing the root causes and helping 
individuals to deal with them), has at least a fighting chance of breaking 
the inherited cycle of bad behaviour. Sometimes people can be persuaded that 
what their fathers did may no longer be optimal. It can be done without 
denigrating or diminishing their fathers (different strokes for different 
times) but the fact is that at THIS place, in THIS time, it probably isn't a 
good thing to rape and pillage unsuspecting villages... :-) I'm sure you 
catch my drift... :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Politics

_(reply depth: 66)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-01-05T11:54:07+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<42320lF1fvmtuU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<40ubb7F1bt3lnU1@individual.net> <qrAqf.159$bd.139@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <vullq1lbadpghrp4dbdhd12oslapqn01cs@4ax.com> <1135371462.109753.320120@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2cp2r1pqhetjra4taldb9jop1akg7kis21@4ax.com> <1135898516.371026.250460@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <51iar1laco0g97jpubb7uos61f6ujccla8@4ax.com> <41llq7F1es2q3U1@individual.net> <VSitf.6804$l87.826552@news20.bellglobal.com> <u82lr1huao3jbp6piu3ga8fulr0jikevt5@4ax.com> <420se2F1g3dsnU1@individual.net> <apmnr1pnj0k9dbmgs6mel4kd7an2vv71h0@4ax.com>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:apmnr1pnj0k9dbmgs6mel4kd7an2vv71h0@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 4 Jan 2006 16:06:37 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Whatever we come up with (including my own ideas on this) is unlikely to be 
a 100% solution. It requires a fundamental approach at an early age and it 
has to be "many-pronged". Family, schools, the community as a whole, needs 
to foster a desire for positive outcomes that improve things for everybody. 
People need to feel part of their community and not isolated by or from it. 
It's a big ask. It can't happen overnight but the starfish approach is 
pertinent here.

(Two men are walking along a beach after a recent storm. There are thousands 
of starfish washed up on the beach. One man starts picking them up and 
throwing them back to the water. The other man says: "There's no point. 
There are thousands of them. We couldn't possibly save them all. Whatever we 
do can't make a difference."
The first man replies: "It made a difference to the ones I threw back.")

Every kid who accepts responsibility for their crime, does what they can to 
make it right, does community service or benefits by helping old folk or 
working with kids, and has his/her mind changed about a life of crime, is a 
success that eventually will filter through to society as a whole.

There will always be criminals. The reasons that cause people to embark on 
such a life are complex and manifold. As a society at large, we can show 
more about our own maturity and understanding by the penalties we impose on 
lawbreakers, and this, in turn, has an effect on the community. It's a bit 
like parenting. Kids follow the role models. If you live in a narrow minded, 
vengeful society, you will tend to be narrow minded and vengeful. 
Fortunately, the converse is also true.

Pete.
```

back is the fact that it would stop all of the appeals against

identified him and that his uncles' DNA shows sufficient similarity to
the DNA of the perpetrator to be proof enough.

Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
> > Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why hanging?

That is what we had before. It would be easier to re-introduce it than
to bring in a novel form of capital punishment.

> Originally considered to a be a quick and merciful form of
> excution, we now know it isn't...

Why is it not humane? Personally I would prefer the guillotine but just
so long as the head was restrained and prevented from banging into the
basket. The Spanish used to use a garotte. You would be restrained in a
chair with a metal collar around your neck. A screw would be tightened
and would eventually snap the neck. Not a pleasant way to go.

> Even the guillotine was supposed to be
> merciful but is considered too gory for modern taste. Favour of capital
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Obviously, it was NOT "proof enough" for a jury of his peers...

The jury of his peers convicted him (1960s?). He maintained his
innocence up to the point of death. His family carried out the appeal
AFTER the similarity between the uncles' DNA and that of the
perpetrator had been established (proving either that he or the uncle
committed the crime). The appeal court got the right verdict.

>
> Pete.


Technically, Pete, as a former crown colony with the right of appeal to
the highest court in the UK, you also have the death penalty (for
treason against Her Majesty).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

I have a friend (the one who thinks that I am a nutter) who has been a
habitual criminal since his childhood. From the extensive discussions
that I have had with him it is clear that deterrence does not enter
into his calculations. Rather, he sees an opportunity and carries out a
criminal act. If he gets caught, then he considers the punishment. He
does not see the punishment as a problem, rather more as consequence of
being caught rather than a punishment. He does enjoy being in jail! He
sees it as a holiday.

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1135282770.910032.144860@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> Defaultuser wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

    For what it's worth, detention was not a deterrent for handing in my 
essays late, in the last few years of highschool.

    For whatever reasons, the policy there was that if I did not hand in my 
essay on the due date, then I would have to go to detetention that day until 
some time in the evening (they said when, but I've since forgotten the exact 
hour), and every subsequent school day (i.e. not weekends) until I *did* 
hand it in. There was no, for example, marking penalties for being late.

    So in other words, in exchange for handing in my essays late, I was 
offered a quiet, well lit environment in which I could get my homework done, 
free of distractions.

    This ended up with me handing in several of my essays several months 
late. It's not that it took me an inordinate amount of time to write the 
essay; rather, I would take advantage of that detention period to do the 
non-essay homework for the following day (which DID have a penalty for being 
late), and then, if I had time left over, only then would I actually start 
to work on the essay proper.

    I'm not sure that they approved of my "strategy", but they never 
commented on it, and they did not change the policy while I was a student 
there.

    - Oliver
```

```
 

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1135282770.910032.144860@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> Defaultuser wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
So, you agree that punishment is no deterrence?  I see it as of no value 
because it does not address the fundamental problem that leads to crime. If 
the attitude of people was changed by punishment, or it helped them grow in 
some way, I'd support it. It doesn't. Quite the opposite. All it does it 
make the rest of society feel that "justice" was served. (Actually, 
punishment has nothing to do with justice; it has to do with vengeance and 
"getting even". Very much an Old Testament concept where God set a fine 
example to us all, with obscene punishments based on His petulance. It's 
time we grew up and let it go...)

We should have penalties for crime, but they should be useful, positive 
penalties that grow the perpetrator to the point where he/she no longer sees 
crime as a winning way to get by in society. The way to solve crime is to 
change people's minds about wanting to commit it. Punishment doesn't do 
that; rehabilitation (where feasible), has a chance of doing it. Instilling 
the right attitudes in kids (as many parents try to do) is the best hope of 
defeating crime. I grew up in poor neighbourhood. We didn't go round robbing 
each other. Our parents pointed out that antisocial behaviour is just that; 
antisocial. Comunities need to work together, not against each other. Poor 
communities need to work even harder.

Punishment is not the answer.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 56)_

- **From:** "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2005-12-26T11:01:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1135623689.836527.158210@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<418h2bF1e1ugoU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <GYXnf.256524$ir4.74044@edtnps90> <J7Znf.8421$kP5.5458@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <7z%nf.256590$ir4.31133@edtnps90> <4e11q1lqiaauk3ccm5koman2h6r3ojum1b@4ax.com> <s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com> <Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <UU6qf.45288$6e.8827@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40sq24F1c7lssU1@individual.net> <ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <1135282770.910032.144860@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <418h2bF1e1ugoU1@individual.net>`

```

Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
> > Defaultuser wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> So, you agree that punishment is no deterrence?

Punishment is no deterrence unless the fear of being caught is
sufficiently high. With modern detection failure rates, punishment is
no deterrence. Increasing the punishment would have the effect of
causing criminals to make more and more extreme effort not to get
caught (A London, UK, rapist resorted to killing his victims before he
finally got caught). There are circumstances where severe punishment
may result in a deliberately greater crime (who accused of treason
would willing surrender in a siege with hostages knowing that they were
for the chop?).

> I see it as of no value
> because it does not address the fundamental problem that leads to crime. If
> the attitude of people was changed by punishment, or it helped them grow in
> some way, I'd support it. It doesn't. Quite the opposite.

My criminal friend was put on a community service order. He was
employed to build an adventure playground for children. He enjoyed the
task so much that he went back at night and vandalised the playground
so that he would be brought back in to repair the facility. He enjoyed
that too. His punishment had been oriented towards a useful and
personally fulfilling activity but that did nothing to mend his ways.

> All it does it
> make the rest of society feel that "justice" was served.

Agreed.

> (Actually,
> punishment has nothing to do with justice; it has to do with vengeance and
> "getting even". Very much an Old Testament concept where God set a fine
> example to us all, with obscene punishments based on His petulance. It's
> time we grew up and let it go...)

Agreed.

>
> We should have penalties for crime, but they should be useful, positive
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> that; rehabilitation (where feasible), has a chance of doing it.

There are so many criminals and so little effort made to rehabilitate
so this aspect is not working. It would require a massive effort on the
part of the authorities to address this issue. Some crimes can not be
rehabilitated, especially wrt society (see rape and child molestation).
Even where a criminal has served a full prison sentence, undergone
psychotherapy and rehabilitation, society will not forgive some crimes.

> Instilling
> the right attitudes in kids (as many parents try to do) is the best hope of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> communities need to work even harder.

We did not commit crime nor did we commit vandalism (despite having
fewer means of usefully occupying ourselves). I was never aware of my
parents instilling any sense of social responsibility but they must
have done. Certainly, I know many parents who seem to blame anybody
else other than themselves for their childrens' bad behaviour. I am
sure that many newspapers and social commentators would blame the
abolition of the lash at school for the (apparent) drop in social
responsibility. People have to accept that they are responsible for
their own actions (and parents are responsible for the behaviour of
their children) and a socially deprived childhood does not excuse
crime. Was it not in America where a kleptomaniac tried to blame
his/her parents for their crime, saying that his/her mother was beaten
by the father during preganancy and the phrase "Take that!" was
repeated? Surely a perfect excuse for perpetrating a certain boy band
on the world but not for theft?

>
> Punishment is not the answer.

In the UK, some frequent offenders have been severely punished by being
sent on all-expenses-paid luxury holidays; much to the disgust of the
rest of society. If you reward criminals then you will alienate other
deprived law-abiding echelons of society and demonstrate to them that
crime is a viable option.

Finally, in the UK, it is recognised that burglars tend to steal from
their own, that is to say, if you live in Walthamstow (mythical place)
then you will tend to commit your crime in or near to Walthamstow. This
observation is true to other crimes (child molestation/abuse, rape,
muggings). 

> 
> Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: politics

_(reply depth: 57)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-12-27T07:49:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<unk2r1podk30qnbkkguu8id8aegb2knert@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <UU6qf.45288$6e.8827@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <40sq24F1c7lssU1@individual.net> <ijdqf.17764$md.14147@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <1135282770.910032.144860@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <418h2bF1e1ugoU1@individual.net> <1135623689.836527.158210@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 26 Dec 2005 11:01:29 -0800, "Alistair"
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>Punishment is no deterrence unless the fear of being caught is
>sufficiently high. With modern detection failure rates, punishment is
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>for the chop?).

Here's an interesting trade off.   With the cost of deterrence being
high, increasing the duration of the terms fills up our prisons -
meaning that lessor criminals get out sooner, or never go in the first
place.    If instead of spending money on longer prison terms, we
spent more money in getting more people to prison sooner, the
deterrence would be more effective.

My father once took a test to be allowed to sell Mutual Funds.   One
of the questions was in the order of "if you misrepresent yourself, is
the prison sentence 4 years, 6 years, or 8 years?".     For most
people it doesn't matter.    What matters is how likely you believe
you will get punished at all (for those contemplating a life of
crime).
```

On 29 Dec 2005 14:44:37 -0800, "Alistair"
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>Perhaps we should shorten the longer terms on the principal that this
>would empty the prisons sooner and we really are mostly only concerned
>with the petty crimes (muggings, burglary) and less concerned with the
>greater crimes which fewer are subject to (rape, murder). I would agree
>to getting people in to court soonest.

That would be a hard sell, but it is an interesting thought.

If a guy cold-bloodedly kills his mother for her insurance money, most
people want to put him away for life (or death).

If a guy goes ballistic and starts bashing passer-by's, lots of people
are more willing to cut him slack.

But which of these two people is the bigger danger to you and yours?
```

On Wed, 21 Dec 2005 09:21:45 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Many criminals claim that punishment is not a deterrent to them.

It's obvious that some deterrence works.   For instance, I drive no
more than 6 miles over the speed limit because I don't want a ticket
and increased insurance rates.    I don't do so because *that's the
law", I do it because I don't want to be punished.

There are other laws that I obey because I agree with them.

But it is also obvious that the curve between the effectiveness of the
detergence and the toughness of the punishment is no where near
straight.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 54)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-12-22T07:26:27-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m8dlq1pb6iptaaclhiq4ojh5shresmt3jt@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com> <Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <tmqiq19ipj2h0dqiok4656p52usuhbet06@4ax.com> <8Thqf.16214$kP5.713@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <40uc79F1bgsk1U1@individual.net>`

```
On Thu, 22 Dec 2005 14:01:23 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>However, I'd like the population as a whole to actually consider what 
>"punishment" actually achieves, rather than just accept it because God does 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>important as the answers... :-))

Punishment does the following:

1.   Some penalties reduce the recurrence of the crime or danger
directly.   (In some states, the danger might not be criminal - it
could be political or possibly legal but determined to be giving
support to perceived enemies such as terrorists).

2.  The fear of punishment deters some amount of future crime from
happening.

3.  Some population feel good about revenge.    Note:   Martha Stewart
was convicted for insider trading.   The public doesn't care about
insider trading, but was glad to punish her for being too "little Miss
Perfect".      When a CEO legally steals from the pension plan, and
than takes a huge golden parachute - the public wants to find
something else to hang him on - for revenge.   It doesn't matter what.
4.   Some punishment is really an excuse to steal.   The money the
state got from the tobacco was about power.   The fact that one
tobacco company used a cartoon camel in its ads was an excuse (protect
the kids).   Since nobody likes the tobacco companies, the state could
use this as a power grab.
```

Unfortunately Howard, while your post, as usual, makes very good sense, it 
is not possible to corroborate points 1, 2, and 4 with any evidence that 
would stand up in court.

I still contend that "Punishment" serves no useful purpose, whatsoever.  It 
doesn't help the victim, it doesn't help the perpetrator, and it desn't help 
society as a whole. However, all of these statements are simply my own 
personal opinion. Sadly, like you, I cannot prove my position, for the same 
reasons that you cannot. :-)

Pete.

[post re-ordered to be bottom-posting]

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:411pn0F1ci0c4U2@individual.net...
>
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
> news:m8dlq1pb6iptaaclhiq4ojh5shresmt3jt@4ax.com...
>>
[snip]
>>
>> 2.  The fear of punishment deters some amount of future crime from
>> happening.
>>
[snip]
>>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

    There's a lot of noise going on in North America about violent 
videogames, and whether or not they should be restricted. Here's an 
interesting statement that a Judge made during a ruling:

<quote>
When the state defends a regulation of speech as a means to "prevent 
anticipated harms," however, "it must do more than simply 'posit the 
existence of the disease sought to be cured.'" Rather, "[i]t must 
demonstrate that the recited harms are real, not merely conjectural, and 
that the regulation will in fact alleviate these harms in a direct and 
material way."
</quote>

    Source: http://arstechnica.com/news.ars/post/20051203-5680.html

    Of course, the situation of banning violent videogames and banning 
punishment differ, but this statement may give insight into the sort of 
things that need to be demonstrated in courts of law.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 57)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-12-23T20:30:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k7Zqf.165276$ki.90898@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<IUUqf.12540$m05.804@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com> <Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <tmqiq19ipj2h0dqiok4656p52usuhbet06@4ax.com> <8Thqf.16214$kP5.713@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <40uc79F1bgsk1U1@individual.net> <m8dlq1pb6iptaaclhiq4ojh5shresmt3jt@4ax.com> <411pn0F1ci0c4U2@individual.net> <IUUqf.12540$m05.804@clgrps12>`

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
> [post re-ordered to be bottom-posting]
> 
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Providing those Courts of Law are basing their judgments on the written 
  law. Including UK, the old concept of Common Law has gone out of the 
window, with an attempt, largely like the American system, which is to 
attempt to codify everything. It just doesn't work.

The net result is Supreme Courts, (some dozen people), have taken it 
upon themselves to become our 'social conscience' or 'socially activist 
judges'. None better than up here with Beverley McLachlan who with a 
majority assent, and two or three *strong* dissenters has just 
judicially decided that 'Swinger parties' are OK, regardless of what 
sexual acts they want to get up to.

I don't suppose Beverley's name means a damn thing to our friends down 
'South' - but the Herald Editorial Board recently issued a condemnation 
of her approach, and as she was visiting Kiwi-land at the time, advised 
our Kiwi friends to ignore her.

It is 'parliament' or the 'two houses' in the case of the States, that 
must be the only ones to make law. If on appeal the judiciary find fault 
with a particular law, they must NOT interpret beyond what the written 
law states. There has to be a *written* mechanism where the judges throw 
it back at the politicians to clarify the particular act/law - either by 
recording a unanimous 'No' to what the judges are querying, or in the 
light of current public thought, amending the law in a specified given 
time period.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: plurals

_(reply depth: 61)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-01-03T09:24:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dpec0d$hdn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <tmqiq19ipj2h0dqiok4656p52usuhbet06@4ax.com> <8Thqf.16214$kP5.713@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <40uc79F1bgsk1U1@individual.net> <m8dlq1pb6iptaaclhiq4ojh5shresmt3jt@4ax.com> <411pn0F1ci0c4U2@individual.net> <IUUqf.12540$m05.804@clgrps12> <k7Zqf.165276$ki.90898@pd7tw2no> <1135373208.864717.315920@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <vd_qf.55038$2k.39648@pd7tw1no> <72m2r15tql1e0unqk6n67d0ojjobaud3v4@4ax.com>`

```
Top post, no more below.

On the model of auris (ear), ignis (fire), orbis (circle), and tussis 
(cough), it appears (based on the public domain "A Latin Grammar" by Charles 
E. Bennett, which tome is available for browsing and for download from 
http://www.textkit,com ) that the regular nominative plural of the Latin 
word "penis" (tail) is, according to the rules for the Third Declension, 
"penes".

    -Chuck Stevens

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:72m2r15tql1e0unqk6n67d0ojjobaud3v4@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 23 Dec 2005 21:44:59 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
> <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> I do like alternative plurals - such as "subschemata".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: plurals

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-01-03T17:36:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dpecmq$jvu$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <vd_qf.55038$2k.39648@pd7tw1no> <72m2r15tql1e0unqk6n67d0ojjobaud3v4@4ax.com> <dpec0d$hdn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
In article <dpec0d$hdn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>,
Chuck Stevens <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>Top post, no more below.

... or so some might soon well wish, perhaps.

>
>On the model of auris (ear), ignis (fire), orbis (circle), and tussis 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>"penes".

And so... if the defendants in the case were to assert that, in a period 
of genial intoxication, they were broken-in on by a local maniac, Peter, 
who coerced them, at halberd-point, to allow him to affix their anatomies 
in the described fashion... then might it be said that...

... Peter Piker pegged a pack of pickled penes?

DD
```

Teller kicked each one in the seat of the pants, we would be forced to 
assume:

If Peter Piker pegged a pack of pickled penes, perhaps Penn's 
prestidigitation pulverised the pack of pickled penes that Peter Piker 
pegged...

Teller ,of course, was simply kicking against the pricks...

Pete.

- **In-Reply-To:** `<hul2r1di9b8p4uursboh07r55eks2kkoah@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <tmqiq19ipj2h0dqiok4656p52usuhbet06@4ax.com> <8Thqf.16214$kP5.713@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <40uc79F1bgsk1U1@individual.net> <m8dlq1pb6iptaaclhiq4ojh5shresmt3jt@4ax.com> <411pn0F1ci0c4U2@individual.net> <IUUqf.12540$m05.804@clgrps12> <k7Zqf.165276$ki.90898@pd7tw2no> <hul2r1di9b8p4uursboh07r55eks2kkoah@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Fri, 23 Dec 2005 20:30:08 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
> <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Limits to power are good.

Certainly for the USA the rules are pretty clear. If an intended new law 
  abrogates the tenets of your Constitution then it is automatically 
unconstitutional. But of course, then you have the neat loophole - amend 
your Constitution. Does anybody know of an instance where an Amendment 
to the Constitution was rejected by the US Supreme Court ?

English History makes a big deal of Magna Carta (1215 ?) - but in fact 
it was only the appetizer/hors d'oeuvre; real democracy only starts to 
occur from about 1840 onwards and that was after regicide (beheading of 
Charles I).

While there have been numerous acts of parliament, primarily aimed at 
protecting the populace, health, education, defence, working conditions 
etc., much was done with Common Law (Common sense law). That pattern was 
followed by much of the Empire, particularly the English speaking 
'dominions'.

Don't know how it's working out in Australia or New Zealand, but for 
historic reasons we brought 'home' the British North America Act and 
substituted the Charter of Rights. It is so bloody ambiguous that you 
could have somebody challenging you on spitting on the sidewalk ! Our 
Supreme Court has taken it upon themselves to *interpret* and *extend* 
meanings contained in that Charter which isn't even 30-years old yet !

Ironically, led by a series of Catholic prime ministers, there has been 
much politically correct 'social engineering'. The one that has raised 
its head in the upcoming election (Jan 23 I think) - same sex marriage. 
Having rammed through legislation, Martin (Liberal) is now trying to use 
Harper (Conservative) as the bogey man, which I suspect, most of Eastern 
Canada (the major voting bloc), will swallow. Harper's position - he 
want to raise the issue again, should he form a government; if a revised 
parliament is in agreement then he will pursue the topic - otherwise he 
will just let it drop. Give their druthers our Supreme Court would 
follow the Liberal position - seeing that the Liberals have been in 
power for so long, hardly surprising that the *appointed* judges  are 
all Liberals.

The other aspect, which your Constitution covers, is we have had, both 
in UK and Canada too much of one governing party - Thatcher, Tony Blair, 
and in Canada Liberals under Chretien/Martin. In both countries the PMO 
(Prime Minister's Office), tends to reign supreme - rather like your 
Administration being responsible for implementation of law, as opposed 
to the need to seek approval from Congress. The actual MPs don't have a 
say in what goes on and for the most part are a subservient group to the 
PMO's wishes.

I would rather a legislature of some 300 have the power than twelve 
appointed judges.

Nothing to do with above - Election booths were open for upcoming 
election on Christmas Day ! Eh ? When a writ is dropped for a new 
election, the voting booths have to be opened for advance voters, (those 
who will be out of the country), 'x' days before election - Christmas 
Day happened to be day 'x'.

Jimmy
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3833r1tq96kar4tvssjk7rqndli2tdvl86@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <tmqiq19ipj2h0dqiok4656p52usuhbet06@4ax.com> <8Thqf.16214$kP5.713@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <40uc79F1bgsk1U1@individual.net> <m8dlq1pb6iptaaclhiq4ojh5shresmt3jt@4ax.com> <411pn0F1ci0c4U2@individual.net> <IUUqf.12540$m05.804@clgrps12> <k7Zqf.165276$ki.90898@pd7tw2no> <hul2r1di9b8p4uursboh07r55eks2kkoah@4ax.com> <mdfsf.88734$2k.1521@pd7tw1no>`

```
On Tue, 27 Dec 2005 17:54:26 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

>Certainly for the USA the rules are pretty clear. If an intended new law 
>  abrogates the tenets of your Constitution then it is automatically 
>unconstitutional. But of course, then you have the neat loophole - amend 
>your Constitution. Does anybody know of an instance where an Amendment 
>to the Constitution was rejected by the US Supreme Court ?

That doesn't make sense - amendments to the Constution are by
definition, constructional.

>English History makes a big deal of Magna Carta (1215 ?) - but in fact 
>it was only the appetizer/hors d'oeuvre; real democracy only starts to 
>occur from about 1840 onwards and that was after regicide (beheading of 
>Charles I).

No English monarch has been named after John - and the royals have
looked favorably on literature that made John out to be a bad guy. His
crime was to allow Nobels some more power.   The gentry, commoners,
and serfs got nothing from the Magna Carta.

>Don't know how it's working out in Australia or New Zealand, but for 
>historic reasons we brought 'home' the British North America Act and 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>meanings contained in that Charter which isn't even 30-years old yet !

They even do that with explicit written Constitutions - but it is much
easier with unwritten (or poorly defined) Constitutions.   Where does
your Constitution start and where does it end?    On the other hand -
the people in the U.S. who complain most about the U.S. Supreme Court
making law are conservatives.   What the British Constitution *is* is
semi-codified conservatism.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 60)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2005-12-27T16:43:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<11r3df74jvth36a@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <tmqiq19ipj2h0dqiok4656p52usuhbet06@4ax.com> <8Thqf.16214$kP5.713@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <40uc79F1bgsk1U1@individual.net> <m8dlq1pb6iptaaclhiq4ojh5shresmt3jt@4ax.com> <411pn0F1ci0c4U2@individual.net> <IUUqf.12540$m05.804@clgrps12> <k7Zqf.165276$ki.90898@pd7tw2no> <hul2r1di9b8p4uursboh07r55eks2kkoah@4ax.com> <mdfsf.88734$2k.1521@pd7tw1no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:mdfsf.88734$2k.1521@pd7tw1no...
> Howard Brazee wrote:
> > On Fri, 23 Dec 2005 20:30:08 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> to the Constitution was rejected by the US Supreme Court ?

While I am not aware of any such case, this does not
mean that the Supreme Court of the United States
(SCOTUS) can not find an amendment to the
US Constitution to be unconstitutional. The Fifth
Amendment requires "due process of law", the
US Constitution is law, Article V of the US Constitution
prescribes the process for proposing and ratifying
amendments, therefore, if the process in Article V is
not followed perfectly, due process of law will not
have been followed, and SCOTUS could find the
amendment to be unconstitutional. [Whether the
Fifth Amendment's "due process of law" even applies
to the process of amending the US Constitution is an
unanswered question. I am assuming it does.]

There was one instance (the Fourteenth Amendment)
where the ratification was in doubt (rejections followed
by approvals). [Congress even went so far as to order
the Secretary of State to promulgate the amendment;
but the Secretary of State delayed until ratification was
without doubt.] Article V does not address whether
multiple votes may be taken on amendments, thus the
problem presented by rejection followed by approval,
or vice versa, has never been answered. [The Equal
Rights Amendment, I believe, never reached the magic
38 approvals in seven years or it might have been
taken before SCOTUS to decide whether it had been
ratified.]
```

Oliver Wong wrote:
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
>     There's a lot of noise going on in North America about violent
> videogames, and whether or not they should be restricted. Here's an
> interesting statement that a Judge made during a ruling:

Read "Popcorn" by Ben Elton.

The book is well written and makes a case against violent films.

As a young man I watched "Warriors" in the 1970s. Afterwards, I had no
fear about walking home along a coastpath in the dark. I have no doubt
that watching a film can influence your mood and may lead to one
commiting a crime.

However, I do not believe that watching a film will, on its' own, lead
to commiting a crime.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: politics

_(reply depth: 58)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-12-27T08:18:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v8m2r1l7gr97s9nmgm89hlfj5eo7nbpf4s@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <tmqiq19ipj2h0dqiok4656p52usuhbet06@4ax.com> <8Thqf.16214$kP5.713@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <40uc79F1bgsk1U1@individual.net> <m8dlq1pb6iptaaclhiq4ojh5shresmt3jt@4ax.com> <411pn0F1ci0c4U2@individual.net> <IUUqf.12540$m05.804@clgrps12> <1135372476.333977.59390@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 23 Dec 2005 13:14:36 -0800, "Alistair"
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>However, I do not believe that watching a film will, on its' own, lead
>to commiting a crime.

No.   But there is ample evidence that people change what they believe
is normal and acceptable from what they see others do (look at war
crimes).    Evidence to show that media define what is normal is more
limited - but it does exist.   

I think the media have made more people accept homosexuals, for
instance.   But if this were true, it doesn't indicated that media has
made people more likely to commit violence, except in rare cases (a
kid pulling a WWW or Karate move on another kid).
```

Pete Dashwood wrote:
> I still contend that "Punishment" serves no useful purpose, whatsoever.  It
> doesn't help the victim, it doesn't help the perpetrator, and it desn't help
> society as a whole.

Simply put, whilst crime exists let the criminals get away with it.

My view - if we do not punish then there is no deterrent. Without a
deterrent then there will be uncontrolled crime. Laws make for
criminals. If you have the possibility of ciminals then you must have a
punishment as a deterrent. Otherwise there will always be criminals.
```

- **References:** `<Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <tmqiq19ipj2h0dqiok4656p52usuhbet06@4ax.com> <8Thqf.16214$kP5.713@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <40uc79F1bgsk1U1@individual.net> <m8dlq1pb6iptaaclhiq4ojh5shresmt3jt@4ax.com> <411pn0F1ci0c4U2@individual.net>`

```
On Fri, 23 Dec 2005 21:10:03 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Unfortunately Howard, while your post, as usual, makes very good sense, it 
>is not possible to corroborate points 1, 2, and 4 with any evidence that 
>would stand up in court.

Certainly an executed or incarcerated criminal will not be a direct
danger to the people on the streets.    I think this can be tested
with evidence that will stand up in court. (case 1)

Fear of punishment requires psychologists to testify.  (case 2).
Psychologists have had too much success in court (IMHO), but certainly
they have had success that has stood up in court.    And if I were a
juror, I would believe that since I am deterred by punishment from
speeding, others might be deterred as well.

Case 4 is obviously a bad reason for punishment (as is case 3). Courts
have allowed other courts to steal from the tobacco companies "in the
name of the children".    They do deny the motive - but the results
stand.

I suppose it doesn't matter whether it stands up on court - if it
happens anyway.     But it might matter when we evaluate our
motivations and support for reform to understand the nature of those
with various opinions on the subject.


P.S.   I got Penn & Teller's BullShit DvDs for Christmas.   My son and
I had a ball watching the first disk yesterday - while his children
and our womenfolk were at a birthday tea party for my granddaughter.
I'd like to watch more of it with him, but don't know when we will get
such an opportunity.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 55)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-01-03T08:36:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dpe972$fk8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com> <Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <tmqiq19ipj2h0dqiok4656p52usuhbet06@4ax.com> <8Thqf.16214$kP5.713@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <40uc79F1bgsk1U1@individual.net> <m8dlq1pb6iptaaclhiq4ojh5shresmt3jt@4ax.com>`

```
Top post, no more below:

I'm neither a particular fan nor a particular enemy of capital punishment.

I am however of the opinion that, from the standpoint of actions, anybody 
can do whatever they wish to do *so long as* they are willing to pay the 
price.

If a given society has determined that the price for a given action is to be 
drawn and quartered, it is inappropriate for the perpetrator of that action 
to expect any consequence other than being drawn and quartered.

If a given society has determined that the price of a given action is to be 
slapped on the hand and told "Naughty!  Naughy!", it is appropriate for the 
perpetrator of that action to expect nothing more unpleasant than being 
slapped on the hand and told "Naughty!  Naughty!".

I don't think of criminal sentences as being punishments so much as 
consequences that [a given] society has determined are appropriate to a 
given action.   In an ideal world there would be a one-to-one correspondence 
between the action and its predetermined consequence.

And as far as I'm concerned that's entirely appropriate regardless of the 
perpetrator's subsequent rehabilitation.  You do the crime, you do the time. 
If you don't want the consequences, don't take the action in the first 
place.

    -Chuck Stevens


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:m8dlq1pb6iptaaclhiq4ojh5shresmt3jt@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 22 Dec 2005 14:01:23 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **References:** `<Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <tmqiq19ipj2h0dqiok4656p52usuhbet06@4ax.com> <8Thqf.16214$kP5.713@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <40uc79F1bgsk1U1@individual.net> <m8dlq1pb6iptaaclhiq4ojh5shresmt3jt@4ax.com> <dpe972$fk8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Tue, 3 Jan 2006 08:36:51 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>I am however of the opinion that, from the standpoint of actions, anybody 
>can do whatever they wish to do *so long as* they are willing to pay the 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>to expect any consequence other than being drawn and quartered.

IMO, the worst aspect of capital punishment is when it is used as a
political tool.   Often times there are two types of people being
punished by the state - those who have been found guilty of committing
crimes, and those that have been found to be enemies of the state.   

The enemies of the state fall into two categories - those who are
guilty of crimes (maybe civil disobedience), and those who haven't.
Certainly the latter category don't fit your description.

The other area of concern is when innocent people get convicted of
crimes.   I'm not one of those who believe life in prison is more
humane than execution.    But there have been times when minor crimes
have had major punishments.     While it might be inappropriate for a
perpetrator to expect to avoid that punishment - it is extremely
appropriate for an innocent bystander to expect to avoid that
punishment.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<420uahF1g3qi9U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com> <Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <tmqiq19ipj2h0dqiok4656p52usuhbet06@4ax.com> <8Thqf.16214$kP5.713@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <40uc79F1bgsk1U1@individual.net> <m8dlq1pb6iptaaclhiq4ojh5shresmt3jt@4ax.com> <dpe972$fk8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
 

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
news:dpe972$fk8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
> Top post, no more below:
>
> I'm neither a particular fan nor a particular enemy of capital punishment.
>

Me neither. I do think death is pretty drastic as "punishment", but there 
are some crimes that demand elimination of the perpetrator from civilised 
society. Exile doesn't really work because you are just unloading your scum 
onto other countries, so death is probably the most expedient option. I 
s'pose you could see if any other country would take them on bleeding heart 
or religious grounds, but, if they drew a blank it has to be the little 
needle...

> I am however of the opinion that, from the standpoint of actions, anybody 
> can do whatever they wish to do *so long as* they are willing to pay the 
> price.

Hmmm... not sure about "whatever they wish"... some imaginative people might 
dream up crimes that it would be impossible to set a suitable punishment 
for... Things so wicked that death (even a slow lingering tortured one) 
could not begin to balance the ledger (actually, this is one of the reasons 
I don't believe that "punishment" balances the crime, although I agree that 
it should, where this can be done feasibly and with a positive outcome)... 
"whatever they wish" is fine for sane people, but perhaps not for the 
criminally deranged. Anyway, leaving this reservation aside for the moment, 
I see what you are saying, and do not disagree.

>
> If a given society has determined that the price for a given action is to 
> be drawn and quartered, it is inappropriate for the perpetrator of that 
> action to expect any consequence other than being drawn and quartered.
>
Quite so. However, I would personally argue for something more positive than 
drawing and quartering...

> If a given society has determined that the price of a given action is to 
> be slapped on the hand and told "Naughty!  Naughy!", it is appropriate for 
> the perpetrator of that action to expect nothing more unpleasant than 
> being slapped on the hand and told "Naughty!  Naughty!".
>
Quite so. However, I would personally argue for something more positive than 
being slapped on the hand and told "Naughty!  Naughty!".


> I don't think of criminal sentences as being punishments so much as 
> consequences that [a given] society has determined are appropriate to a 
> given action.
Yes. The problem is that very often the "consequences" are required by the 
society to be much more negative than is really necessary.

> In an ideal world there would be a one-to-one correspondence between the 
> action and its predetermined consequence.
>
In MY ideal world there would be a one-to-one correspondence between the 
action and its predetermined consequence, with the emphasis of the 
consequence being positive, and intended to persuade that a repeat of the 
offence (or any other offence, for that matter...) was definitely a bad 
idea....

> And as far as I'm concerned that's entirely appropriate regardless of the 
> perpetrator's subsequent rehabilitation.  You do the crime, you do the 
> time. If you don't want the consequences, don't take the action in the 
> first place.
>
I agree with your principle, it is only the means of consequence that we 
would need to discuss.
<snip>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-01-04T08:59:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dpgute$25fq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com> <Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <40l2j0F19ka9uU1@individual.net> <1135019731.599662.166360@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <40r7evF1avmuqU1@individual.net> <tmqiq19ipj2h0dqiok4656p52usuhbet06@4ax.com> <8Thqf.16214$kP5.713@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <40uc79F1bgsk1U1@individual.net> <m8dlq1pb6iptaaclhiq4ojh5shresmt3jt@4ax.com> <dpe972$fk8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <420uahF1g3qi9U1@individual.net>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:420uahF1g3qi9U1@individual.net...

> I agree with your principle, it is only the means of consequence that we 
> would need to discuss.

One of the issues that remains open is the meaning of "I would personally 
argue for something more positive ...".  What is "positive" and what is 
"negative" is in the eye of the beholder, whereas what is *effective* may 
depend more on the mindset of the particular offender than anything else.  A 
great many offenders are unwilling or unable to accept responsibility for 
their actions, and part of the issue here is society's insistence that yes, 
indeed, we *are* responsible for their actions.

Examples of this can be drawn from cases as extreme and diverse as the "evil 
government" approach from Timothy McVeigh, the Twinkie Defense (an urban 
legend, as it turns out) of Dan White, and "the devil made me do it" made 
famous by Geraldine Jones (AKA Flip Wilson).  Point being, offenders 
frequently feel their actions can be justified, or that they are powerless 
to avoid the actions in the first place, in which case no amount of 
persuasion will convince them otherwise.

I've thought a good deal about the McVeigh case.  And the conclusion I came 
to is that, presuming he was legally both *sane* and *not sociopathic* at 
the time of the crime the *appropriate sane* response to his actions would 
be to commit suicide.   The fact that he could *not* bring himself to accept 
responsibility for the horrors he wrought and instead argued for the bombing 
of the Murrah building as an *appropriate and reasonable* response to the 
government's actions against the Branch Davidians in Waco, argues 
persuasively for his *sanity*.   I don't believe a sane, nonsociopathic 
person could truly face having murdered 160 people without either blaming 
someone or something else for the act, or committing suicide.

Stated rather more briefly, the person who uses "Yeah, but ..." a great deal 
is not likely to be convinced by "nevertheless, ...".

    -Chuck Stevens
```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:v80or1hrfqqmui5f3dp8l1i0rrgja92o7a@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 4 Jan 2006 08:59:25 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> consequences of their actions.

True enough.  Is a "suicide bomber" who somehow survives likely to be 
convinced subsequently that his or her actions were, at either the temporal 
or eternal level, inappropriate, and that appropriate amends need to be made 
to those that were, or would have been, harmed?

Put a slightly different way, if a perpetrator of a heinous crime is 
unwilling to agree that maybe, just maybe, what he or she did was an 
"oopsie", what action should society take to prevent that person from 
committing similar (or even unrelated) crimes subsequently?  If the best 
available method short of extermination for preventing the person from 
harming somebody else in future is a straitjacket for the rest of his/her 
life, is that somehow more humane than lethal injection?

    -Chuck Stevens

On Thu, 5 Jan 2006 09:14:41 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>Which is exactly my point.  To try to convince such a person as a point of 
>law that the actions they take in the name of their religion are illegal is 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>his religion!

Certainly the novel _1984_ gives an example of reeducation that has
been shown to be seen as the ideal for many totalitarian states.

One of the arguments for negative income tax is as follows:

Everybody gets this "welfare".   Most of us don't bother to pick up
our check, preferring to simply use it as our standard personal
deduction from our income tax.

But since everybody gets it - nobody is a "welfare mom".    Why is
this important?   It is important because "my mom is smart and good",
therefore what she does is smart and good, and her identity is mine.
Figuring out how to get money from the state is smart and good.

If everybody gets that paycheck - then my Mom works during the busy
hours on Friday and Saturday nights at the local restaurant (why not,
it doesn't disqualify her from any welfare, and every little bit
helps?).   Now instead of being a welfare mom, she's a waitress. Now,
instead of having an identity of someone who knows how to use the
system, our identity is of being workers within the system.
```

It is dilemmas such as this which led originally to the need for separation 
of Church and State. If your religion requires you to strangle chickens 
publicly in the local Mall at noon every second Thursday, or to eviscerate 
your neighbour's eldest child when the Planet Mars is in Perigee, there is 
likely to be some resistance from the community at large.

Preventing you from doing unacceptable acts, but still allowing you to 
practice your religion freely, is the responsibility of the State. For 
example, the State may require you to do your chicken strangling in your own 
back yard or ensure that it is done somewhere where other people cannot see 
it and be offended by it. It might be suggested that the practice of child 
evisceration be reconsidered and if not altogether abolished, perhaps a goat 
or lamb could be substituted...(The Indian government have exactly this 
problem in trying to stamp out the practise of suttee, and the caste 
system).

For all of these reasons, it makes sense for the secular authority to be 
superior to (or have authority over) the religious one. Religious fanatics 
who argue for religious government will find it is only achievable in places 
where the majority of the population ascribe to the particular religion. In 
diverse populations it can never work, and even in populations where 
everybody agrees with the religion, it hinders the growth of the society and 
limits it to a status quo that can last for centuries.

The sooner the world grows out of the need for religion, the better it will 
be for all of us. Having said that, I don't expect it to happen anytime in 
the next couple of thousand years... :-)

I am thankful that I live in a free (secularly governed) society where 
holding such a view does not make me subject to burning at the stake, or 
having hot instruments applied to my body. (Some progress has been made in 
the last couple of thousand years... :-))

Pete.

>    -Chuck Stevens
>
>
```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:425hk3F1helikU1@individual.net...

> If your religion requires you to strangle chickens publicly in the local 
> Mall at noon every second Thursday, or to eviscerate your neighbour's 
> eldest child when the Planet Mars is in Perigee, there is likely to be 
> some resistance from the community at large.

Nigh onto twenty years ago, a co-worker informed me to my face that it was 
his God-given religious obligation to organize and participate in my 
execution on the front steps of our facility by stoning;  that he deeply 
resented the fact that neither the US government nor the State of California 
would permit him to carry out that requirement of his religious belief; and 
that the only recourse he had was to ensure that I had absolutely no 
credibility within my company.  For a time he was marginally successful in 
the latter effort, but that success was deflected somewhat by my ability to 
report and document concrete acts of harrassment *within* the workplace.

Would that I were joking.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

So, would Iraq be better off if control were turned over to those who
are controlling the suicide bombers?  Are the insurgents (suicide
bombers, etc.) the true representatives of the Iraqi people?  While I
can make a very good case that the US should never have deposed
Hussein and never have invaded, I do not believe that turning over
control to those who are targeting non-government employees will
improve the situation.  In short, given that at least some fair sized
percentage of the Iraqi population wants to see the current efforts
succeed and have placed their lives on the line by doing such things
as voting and working in various essential services such as rail
transportation, who do you want to see take over?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Compare and contrast: Criminals & Government vs Sinners and God (WAS: Making money from Java)

_(reply depth: 48)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-19T19:20:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_JDpf.9246$ic1.9010@edtnps90>`
- **References:** `<Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <GYXnf.256524$ir4.74044@edtnps90> <J7Znf.8421$kP5.5458@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <7z%nf.256590$ir4.31133@edtnps90> <4e11q1lqiaauk3ccm5koman2h6r3ojum1b@4ax.com> <s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com> <Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> is the human heart.

    I left this in just to remind people that the main intent of my previous 
post was to point out that saying "Is this the right way for the government 
to behave towards criminals? No? So why should God because this way towards 
sinners?" is not a good argument because the situation between criminals and 
the government is very different than the situation between sinners and God. 
Therefore how the government deals with criminals should not nescessarily be 
the same as how God deals with sinners.

>>
>>    There are a few important differences between 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> understood to be a reference to the coming Messiah.

    Yes, hence why I think the government is different from God and that the 
two should not nescessarily be compared the way the have.

>
>>    * In most democratic governments, you DO have the option of changing 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> so. In this situation, we are the criminals, every one of us.

    There's something like a logical fallacy above, though I don't have a 
name for it. There's a hint of circular reasoning with the words "criminal" 
and "law". Anyway, let me try to show via an example:

    If the government arranged the laws so that EVERYONE was a criminal, 
then the obviously the government is "wrong", assuming a democratic process 
(and assume there are more people who are not the government than there are 
who are the government). Let's say the government made it illegal to breath 
oxygen. All of a sudden, everyone is not a criminal. The "criminals" would 
overthrow the government and create a new one, where breathing oxygen is now 
considered legal. This is a GOOD thing.

    I think there is some sort of implicate that being a criminal implies 
that one is morally corrupt. This is not the case. Being a criminal means 
you broke the law, and has nothing to do with morality. If the government is 
a "good" one, then it will tend to create "good" laws, and so the concepts 
of being a criminal and being a bad person would overlap. But if the 
government is "bad", then it will make "bad" laws, and the concept of being 
a criminal and being a "good" person would overlap (see Robin Hood, for 
example).

>
>>    * There are checks and balances in place specifically to ensure that 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> point above.

    Yes, which is another reason why governments and God differ, and why we 
should say just because the government deals with criminals a certain way 
doesn't mean God should deal with sinners the same way.

>
>>    * The members of the government exists in the natural world. That is, 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> believe. See the book of John, chapter 1.

    There is no evidence, AFAIK, of God coming into this world as a man. 
There may be evidence of a man claiming that he is God, but he may have been 
lying (perhaps a shocking concept, but we can't rule out this possibility).

>
>>    * In the same vein, the enforcers of the law (i.e. police officers) 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Christians?

    What exactly did these millions of Christians witness? Certainly it was 
not a child asking an angel for clarifications on the laws of God (which 
would be the most straightforward analogy to the situation of a child asking 
the police officer for clarifications on the laws of the government). If you 
like, I can borrow my brother's digital camera, record a video of me asking 
the police officer whether it is illegal or not to murder someone, and then 
post the video online for anyone to see.

    I would very much like for you to borrow someone's camera (or use your 
own if you have one), and record a video of you speaking to an angel and 
asking an angel whether or not it is a sin to kill murder someone, and post 
a video of it online for anyone to see.

    That's another major difference in obeying the government's law versus 
obeying God's law.

>
>>    * Most punishments that the government issues are not "for all 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> the constant exercise of His power.

    Regardless of how long governments exists, or whether the government 
creates its own citizens or not, there is still a very big ethical concern 
over punishments that last for all eternity. The main intent behind a 
punishment is to deter someone from performing an undesirable action. I 
believe it's known as "negative reinforcement" in psychology: If doing 
action A hurts, then you'll learn to stop doing action A. When you commit a 
crime against the government, you're punished, and then you learn not to 
commit that crime again (or if you do commit it again, you're given a 
harsher and harsher punishment, until you DO learn to stop doing it).

    With God, there is no learning, because you're never released from your 
punishment. It is just endless sadistic torture.

>
>>    * If you don't like the government, you are free to leave for a 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> human heart is truly deceitful and wicked above all things. :-)

    I think Canada's a great country. I love it. But let's say, for whatever 
reasons, I wish to move out of the country. Maybe my girlfriend, who has 
family in Japan, wishes to return to Japan and be with her family. I want to 
be with her, so I'm going to move to Japan too. The Canadian government is 
not going to torture me for all eternity because I'm a heretic scum.

    That's what I mean by "You have choices". In the sense that you can 
choose to live under the rule of a DIFFERENT government if you don't like 
your current one. The Christian portrayal of God implies that you do NOT 
have the choice to leave the Chirstian God for the Buddhist one, for 
example.

    - Oliver
```

_(reply depth: 49)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<6j2eq19farq2g9lkfr4h9a908pe3dpojuh@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <GYXnf.256524$ir4.74044@edtnps90> <J7Znf.8421$kP5.5458@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <7z%nf.256590$ir4.31133@edtnps90> <4e11q1lqiaauk3ccm5koman2h6r3ojum1b@4ax.com> <s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com> <Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <_JDpf.9246$ic1.9010@edtnps90>`

```
On Mon, 19 Dec 2005 19:20:26 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
wrote:

>    Regardless of how long governments exists, or whether the government 
>creates its own citizens or not, there is still a very big ethical concern 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>punishment. It is just endless sadistic torture.

The reasons for punishment include:

1.  Deterrent.   Torturing someone for a billion years would be as
much deterrent as torturing someone for infinitely longer.    But the
question is - how much torture is necessary to make someone believe in
God?    What kind of punishment would someone who didn't believe in
god believe in - to make him change his mind?

2.  Behavior modification.   People learn to grow and be better via
punishment.   If Hell offered a parole, this would be an option.

3.  Vengence.   You hate the criminal sufficiently to be glad to see
him suffer.    This option has some Biblical support.

The other option is - 
4.  I don't care what happens to people who aren't my servants.   The
punishment has nothing to do with me.    This option has some Biblical
support.


The one that doesn't fit is:

5.   I love my children so much that I'll let them be tortured if they
are fooled into not believing in me.
```

```
> There is no evidence, AFAIK, of God coming into this world as a man.
> There may be evidence of a man claiming that he is God, but he may have been
> lying (perhaps a shocking concept, but we can't rule out this possibility).

There is also evidence of people being a god, such as the Pharoahs,
Chin 1 and Hirohito. In fact it is likely that the word was used by the
writers knowing that their gods were as this, as powerful supreme
leaders - [war]lords. It is quite likely that the scribes were aware
that their gods (El, Jehohvah, Ball) did actually live just as they
knew the Pharoahs were men. Jesus did claim that he was descended from
family of the god of the jews and therefore should be the leader of the
jews (ie their god).
```
