- **References:** `<Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <GYXnf.256524$ir4.74044@edtnps90> <J7Znf.8421$kP5.5458@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <7z%nf.256590$ir4.31133@edtnps90> <4e11q1lqiaauk3ccm5koman2h6r3ojum1b@4ax.com> <s31of.8473$kP5.3238@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <6j43q1p5sqibtg34v4c618oatnq0ir3ig2@4ax.com> <Nyyof.30471$Y72.1143@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <6SAof.7901$wg4.1821@edtnps84> <wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:wJUof.189$RZ6.130@bignews7.bellsouth.net...

>  We are only responsible for our own sins, and (as explained in another 
> post) babies and young children are not held accountable for sin.

That has historically differed from the Roman Catholic position of Original 
Sin, which holds that each of us (with the single exception of Mary), 
carries the stain of the sin of Adam and Eve on our soul at conception. 
Baptism washes this away; that's one reason the Church teaches infant 
baptism.  The fate of infants who die before being baptized, *as well as 
adults who die without having committed sin*, has been Limbo, apparently for 
quite a long time.  See the articles on "Limbo" and "Original Sin" in the 
Catholic Encyclopedia.

Note that I don't agree with the Roman Catholic position on this.



> It is true that the result of Adam's original sin is that all of us are 
> born with a sin nature, therefore we all sin.

No, RC doctrine is that "original sin" is more than merely a propensity to 
sin; we are barred from heaven at birth, and it is only by baptism that we 
stand a chance (and it is by baptism that infants who die after baptism and 
before they can be considered accountable for their actions are allowed into 
Heaven).  Again, that's not my opinion, but that's the RC teaching as I 
understand it.

Since it's the RC Church that seems to have invented the term, I'd have to 
say reference material on what it means from that source is to be preferred 
over individual's understanding of what it might mean based on one's own 
understanding of scripture, in exactly the same way that citations from the 
Catholic Encyclopedia can be used to state without fear of meaningful 
contradiction that the doctrine of "immaculate conception" refers to Mary, 
*not* to Jesus.  One may disagree, but at one's peril; the subject is not a 
matter of opinion or speculation, it's a matter of historical fact.

     -Chuck Stevens
```

On Wed, 14 Dec 2005 16:34:20 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>> We don't get saved from torture by being good.   We get saved from
>> torture for not being fooled.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>This is free will.

We get saved for not being fooled.

Evidence presented to different people in different cultures varies
considerably.   Some Hindu child sees loving and good parents and
accepts their view of the world and of the supernatural - and is
eternally tortured for it?   Someone gets raped by a priest and
rejects the priest's teachings and is eternally tortured for it?

The vast majority are not believers.   Lots of believers are like you
who believe those will be tortured because Satan was able to fool us
into not getting it.   No matter that we're good people.   We just
believed the wrong preachers, or maybe our own eyes.
```

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
> 
>     Teaches me to talk about something I don't know much about. =P

That's OK by me - I think you do pretty good :-)

>     I guess I had fallen prey to the Jewish view of Roman Catholicism as the 
> face of Christianity. It was not until this post that I had realized that 
> Catholicism and Christianity were two seperate concepts.

Still haven't got the last bit quite right. Catholicism is not a 
separate concept but a branch of Christianity, just like Puritans, 
Methodists, Baptists, Pentecostal, Salvation Army, Church of 
England/N.American Anglicans, etc.

There was a time - which I state elsewhere - when it was just 
Christianity consisting of Eastern based on the historic Hellenic empire 
and the Western branch which focused on Rome the seat of the new 
Imperial Rome. The East doesn't accept the Pope as the head of the 
church; doctrinally I don't think there is a major difference between 
the two, but without knowing specifics, I don't think Orthodox is 'too 
wild' about Catholic Marian doctrine - that is the place of the mother 
of Jesus.

Orthodoxy - Greek and Russian, primarily, have continued in their sphere 
of influence without any breakaways that I'm aware of.

'Western' Christians (Rome) - this is where the 'split' from the others 
above occurs. Around 1400s-1500s there was both a theological resentment 
together with the rigid conservatism of the established church. You 
don't go around building magnificent edifices and decorate them like St. 
Peter's in Rome or the glorious Gothic cathedrals throughout Europe with 
ease. Back then and as now, you need big bucks. The Vatican 
administration was in charge, their marketing people dreaming up 
different ways as to how they could get their hands on more money for 
their real estate ventures.

Coupled with this, if you were Simon the Simple, a villein toiling in 
the fields on some feudal lord's land, how could we possibly let you 
read/discuss/interpret Holy Scriptures in Latin - let alone in the 
vernacular !

Given the Renaissance, the world was-a-changing; how dare Galileo 
suggest you couldn't fall off the edge of the known World. In the 
theological world, fresh thought on the Church's doctrines and why can't 
we read the Good Book in our own language. Consistently Christendom, 
even after the Reformation has been aligned with the Establishment, 
whether France, Spain or England - hence being part of the Establishment 
a leaning towards the accepted order of government - Conservatism.

While there were certainly agnostics and atheists during the 19th 
century it is disillusionment after WWI that really brought into focus 
this alignment of politics/church - so now we have three groups, 
agnostics, atheists, and 'the don't care'. As a counter to this - you 
get some resurgence in Christianity, Billy Graham, expansion of Mormons, 
Jehovah Witnesses, and other non-catholic groups. Decline in Europe, 
with a major expansion occurring in Africa for both Catholicism and 
Anglicanism.

Meanwhile, be assured Papa Benedict ain't in charge - just his own crowd.

Jimmy
```

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1134588070.599409.158760@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...

> You are mistaken. While the first Popes were assigned (by the Roman
> Emperor) to have control over the roman church of the time

At the time of Constantine's establishment of Christianity as the official 
religion of the Roman Empire, there was but one organized church (with the 
occasional heretic group).

> there has
> been considerable diversity since then,

Two big splits:  the schism of 1054, in which Pope Leo IX and Patriarch 
Michael 1 excommunicated each other, and the Protestant Reformation begun 
with Luther.  The question is whether you regard the doctrinal differences 
between, say, Mennonites and Amish, or "high-church" Lutherans and 
Anglicans, as being significant.

A side note:  I was taught before Vatican II that the Roman Catholic Church 
accepts (begrudgingly) that the Church of England (and by extension some 
Episcopalian groups in the US), some Lutheran groups, and so far as I know 
all of Orthodoxy has *retained* the apostolic succession and thus the 
sacraments performed by those clergy that are within that "bloodline" are 
entirely valid.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
> At the time of Constantine's establishment of Christianity as the official
> religion of the Roman Empire, there was but one organized church (with the
> occasional heretic group).

That is certainly not true. It was the Council of Nicaea that brought
most of the diverse churches together to argue a way of having a single
theology and organization. "Immediately afterward, Constantine issued a
decree that everyone must obey the rulings of this council, on pain of
imprisonment or death."

It may be said that Constantine _imposed_ a single organisation, not
that there _was_ one.

> Two big splits:

Previous 'splits' had been suppressed by the decree or by inquistions.

>The question is whether you regard the doctrinal differences

It matters not what _I_ regard, but how the many diverse sects regard
the differences. There are thousands of different churches which give
no authority to the Pope, or the ArchBishop of Canterbury, or to
whoever is the equivalent in the other thousands of churches.
```

```
Can we do Bill a favour, and at his request switch this to an "OT" ? 
Having personally started "Making Money from Java" - all I got was (1) 
from Donald - Sun Microsystems sue the rest of the world and (2) some 
useful URLs from Stephen Gennard :-)

Now a bottom posting :-

Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
> news:1134588070.599409.158760@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>     -Chuck Stevens 

Yep I recognize Chuck's word 'begrudgingly' - depends who taught him.

So the priesthood in Catholicism, "Once a priest always a priest". A 
seminarian studies and as a deacon in due course will prostrate himself 
in front of an altar before a bishop. The ritual involves the 'laying of 
hands' or passing on of the Holy Spirit - the deacon is now a 
consecrated priest.

The Seven Sacraments, (of Catholicism) are Baptism, Penance/Confession, 
Holy Eucharist/Communion, Confirmation, Matrimony, Holy Orders and 
Extreme Unction; the latter being a ritual of anointing a dying person 
with oils - which you sometimes see in war movies where a chaplain is 
praying with a dying soldier. Priesthood comes in as #6, Holy Orders.

So back to Chuck's comments - and let's just stick with Merrie Old Tudor 
England. The hierarchy, bishops, priests etc., were all ordained 
Catholic priests. So when there was the split with Rome and Thomas 
Cranmer was made Archbishop of Canterbury he automatically qualified as 
being an ordained Catholic priest - "Once a priest, always a priest". 
Therefore he was able to logically pass on the priesthood to fellow 
Protestants.

The idea didn't sit well with a lot of catholic clergy, nor for that 
matter some of the Anglican clergy. However today any open-minded 
catholic theologian would admit to the principle. Similarly, many 
knowledgeable Anglican clerics are aware of the same loophole.

Jimmy
```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> of christain philosophy.


Humans do not get into Heaven by being good, they get into Heaven in one and 
only one way, by receiving salvation by the grace of God, through faith in 
Jesus Christ, who is the Son of God, and Who died a substitutionary death to 
pay the penalty for our sins.

By definition, all Christians believe this, or they are not Christians. 
Fortunately, as much as I disagree with some Catholic doctrines, I believe 
the doctrine of the Roman Catholic Church is entirely in agreement with that 
statement. Being right or wrong on lesser matters of doctrine may beneficial 
or detrimental, but that one doctrine is an absolute essential.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 41)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-14T15:15:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dnq921$lk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cvkuo154sjfungkb2jgjmq2q28nl4tu9aj@4ax.com> <AZPjf.7025$Eq5.5412@pd7tw1no> <J5Xjf.5157$Et5.353943@news20.bellglobal.com> <TFXjf.47085$6y4.8198@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <y6Yjf.7656$wf2.836017@news20.bellglobal.com> <FoZjf.47094$6y4.38133@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <1133550230.780919.303800@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <i21kf.47144$6y4.15970@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqd1b$259m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net...

> Humans do not get into Heaven by being good, they get into Heaven in one 
> and only one way, by receiving salvation by the grace of God, through 
> faith in Jesus Christ, who is the Son of God, and Who died a 
> substitutionary death to pay the penalty for our sins.

If their works don't *demonstrate* their faith, however great the faith 
might be, that's open to discussion.

> By definition, all Christians believe this, or they are not Christians.

All Christians believe that, if your behavior doesn't match your 
proclamations, you are nonetheless "saved"?

> Fortunately, as much as I disagree with some Catholic doctrines, I believe 
> the doctrine of the Roman Catholic Church is entirely in agreement with 
> that statement. Being right or wrong on lesser matters of doctrine may 
> beneficial or detrimental, but that one doctrine is an absolute essential.

The terms "sola fide", "sola gratia" and "sola scriptura" (faith alone, 
grace alone, scripture alone) are specifically characteristic of the 
Reformation, in particular identified with Luther, in direct opposition to 
the doctrines of the Roman Catholic Church.  I don't believe this changed 
with the Counter-Reformation.

Since you insist that *all* Christians believe at least the first of these 
(and probably the first two), you directly *exclude* Roman Catholics from 
your definition of "all Christians" (along with anyone else who doesn't 
accept Luther's principles) despite your protestations to the contrary.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> might be, that's open to discussion.

Actually, discussion doesn't enter into it. God knows who are the true 
believers, and doesn't ask our opinion on the matter. :-)

>> By definition, all Christians believe this, or they are not Christians.
>
> All Christians believe that, if your behavior doesn't match your 
> proclamations, you are nonetheless "saved"?

I have a very good friend who is a devout Christian. In 1975 when he was 
saved, and for a short time after, he was doing drugs and cursing and such 
because he didn't know any better. It wasn't his conduct that made him a 
Christian, but his faith in Jesus. We might look at someone and decide he is 
or isn't a Christian because of his actions, but we really can't be sure. 
Every Christian sins from time to time, and occasionally someone gets into a 
Big Sin. But it is still their faith in Jesus, not their actions, that make 
them a Christian. That's what it means to be saved "by grace". We don't 
deserve it.

>> Humans do not get into Heaven by being good, they get into Heaven in one 
>> and only one way, by receiving salvation by the grace of God, through 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> accept Luther's principles) despite your protestations to the contrary.

You certainly know more about Catholic doctrine than I do, I've never been a 
Catholic. But I have seen written declarations from the Catholic Church 
expressing precisely the definition I give above (in almost the exact same 
words), or I wouldn't have said so. Anyway, it doesn't matter what I say, 
those principles are clearly and repeatedly defined in the Bible and beyond 
dispute. I would be very shocked and disappointed to learn that the Catholic 
Church deviated so far from God's Word as to make themselves a cult.

Do Catholics believe that we are saved by the grace of God and not by works?

Do Catholics believe that we are saved by faith in Jesus Christ?


Do Catholics believe that Jesus is the only way to be reconciled to God?

Do Catholics believe that Jesus is the Son of God?


Do Catholics believe that Jesus died for our sins?

These are what I said all Christians believe. Surely you are not telling me 
that the answer to any of those is 'no'?
```

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> determined?


By God, of course. Only God and the individual can know with absolute 
certainty if that individual believes in Jesus for their salvation. But 
humans deal with issues all the time that have not been (perhaps cannot be) 
absolutely determined. I've never seen Mt. Everest, but I can be reasonably 
certain about it being there. Even seeing something may not be *absolute 
proof*. Most "knowledge" that we deal with as humans falls into this 
category, so why object to the same situation in determining who is a "real 
Christian?"
```

```
In article <HRyof.30608$Y72.10019@bignews1.bellsouth.net>,
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
>> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>certainty if that individual believes in Jesus for their salvation.

Hmmmmm... a 'God only knows' condition, it seems.

>But 
>humans deal with issues all the time that have not been (perhaps cannot be) 
>absolutely determined. I've never seen Mt. Everest, but I can be reasonably 
>certain about it being there.

... and you can, given the inclination, go to the place where others have 
reported seeing it or being on it, attempt to duplicate their actions and 
come back to report on the results.

>Even seeing something may not be *absolute 
>proof*. Most "knowledge" that we deal with as humans falls into this 
>category, so why object to the same situation in determining who is a "real 
>Christian?"

... because you cannot go to the place where others have reported the 
results exist, attempt to duplicate their actions and come back to report 
on the results.  As I understand it the final determination of 'who is a 
"real Christian"' (" original) is made post-mortem; near-death experiences 
(some say it is the result of oxygen deprivation) might be as valid a 
description of what happens post-mortem as near-New York experiences (in 
the words of that Profound Philosopher, Bugs Bunny: 'Hoboken?  Ooooooh, 
I'm *dyin'*, again!') might be of Times Square.

DD
```

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

So? As I stated, some facts can be proven, some cannot. For example, could a 
person *prove* to their spouse or child that their love for them was real? 
Not in the way you seem to expect here. Yet many people are completely 
content and confident in such love. The situation of 'knowing' whether 
another person is a 'real Christian' is precisely similar. We simply have no 
way to know with absolute certainty what is in the heart of another person. 
As I said, we live with these realities all the time, why should you find it 
strange in this particular area?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

In article <vMUof.193$RZ6.175@bignews7.bellsouth.net>,
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
>> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

[snip]

>>>Yet many people are completely
>>>content and confident in such love. The situation of 'knowing' whether
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>contradictory nonsense. I have no idea how to make a logical response to it.

Mr McClendon, what appears to you to be contradictory?  Consider the 
example you used regarding the determining of the existence of Mt Everest; 
I responded that you can duplicate the experiences of others - go so many 
miles in so (x) direction, etc. - and then report to others what you have 
experienced.

Since the results of salvation are determined only post-mortem this avenue 
- going where someone else went and then coming back to report on this - 
is unavailable, there is no way with which I am familiar for anyone to 
say, or have heard said, 'I did (x) and it resulted in my post-mortem 
salvation.'

Those are among my reasons for considering the two instances differently, 
Mr McClendon; what do you see as contradictory?

DD

> <docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:

    Doc Dwarf asked a question (Who determines who gets saved?), Judson

existence of Mt. Everest). There is no "So?" to resolve here.

> We simply have no way to know with absolute certainty what is in the heart 
> of another person. As I said, we live with these realities all the time, 
> why should you find it strange in this particular area?

    We simply have no way to know anything. Like you wrote earlier, you may 
have evidence for the existence of Mt. Everest. For example, perhaps you've 
saw it with your own eyes, touched it, and even climbed to its peak. But you 
can't be sure your senses weren't lying to you. You might be very confident 
in the existence of Mt. Everest, but you can never be sure of its existence.

    HOWEVER! you can then tell other people why you are confident (I saw it, 
touched it, etc.) and you can give them instructions on how they can attempt 
to replicate the experiment that you did. If they come back and they say 
they too saw it, and touched it, etc., then as a group, you and those other 
people can become yet even more confident of the mountain's existence. If 
they come back and say they didn't see any mountain, or did not feel 
anything, then you might become LESS confident in its existence.

    This is the scientific process: performing reproduceable experiments, 
sharing results, and coming up with theories based on those results ("I 
theorize that Mt. Everest exists, and here is the evidence supporting my 
theory.")

    Some theories come from evidence gathered via the scientific process, 
and some theories do not. It is no surprise, then, that people might 
approach these two different classes of theories with two different levels 
of skepticism.

    - Oliver
```

…[66 more quoted lines elided]…
> of skepticism.


Thank you, Oliver. As usual, your explanation is very lucid. I think I 
understand, and pretty much agree with, everything you say there.

However, I still do not understand why DD, who apparently has no problem 
living in a world where a vast amount of what we know cannot be 
experimentally verified, has a problem with issues like "who is a 'real 
Christian'", or if the Bible is true, or if a Christian's testimony is true, 
when the situation is *precisely similar* to that vast number of other 
situations we live with every day, such as knowing if a person tells you the 
truth when they say they love you? This clear dichotomy of perspective on 
precisely similar situations was the point of my objection, and I see 
nothing in what you or DD said that answers that objection. I never intended 
to suggest that there is not a qualitative difference between evidence that 
is testable by direct personal observation, and evidence that is not. If my 
comments appeared to imply that, it was unintentional. :-)
```

> Mr McClendon, this is confusing... if you agree with Mr Wong that 'tere is
> no way to know who is a real Christian' then how can one - yourself

A Christian is someone who has received the Lord Jesus as Saviour; trusts 
Him alone for the forgiveness of sins, and puts no trust in his own efforts 
to please God; and repented from his/her sins.

Therefore one can assume that Judson, or in fact anyone else, cannot assert 
what a real Christian does, for knowing that would make them God; and that 
would be - I imagine - somewhat blasphemous.

Now -  Judson did say I believe it was him in answer to the question who 
determines if one is saved:

"By God, of course. Only God and the individual can know with absolute 
certainty if that individual believes in Jesus for their salvation."

Now, if one believes in Jesus *for* their salvation that wouldn't even pass 
my test for receiving the Lord Jesus....and I'm probably an easier grader 
than Him.

I'd like to know how anyone believes that they are able to comprehend what 
criteria He would use in determining whether one is a real Christian.  It 
seems to me that to consider oneself capable of being able to understand His 
criteria would be somewhat, well, ambitious if not presumptuous to the point 
where He might be inclined to make a nay decision if he were only leaning 
one way or the other.

But anyway.

To answer your question...EVEN a real Christian doesn't know if he/she is a 
real Christian until that time that they are or they aren't (or of course 
just worm fodder, or worse - just missing out on 42 virgins).

But then again, the chances of my house flooding are minimal at best but I 
still have to carry flood insurance.
Not that I'm comparing a Preacher to my mortgage lender or anything.

DU

```
"Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> if you're willing to share.


As I said, the "positive proof" is to me personally, and I have shared this 
already in earlier posts. Essentially, it is an intimate "communion" with 
God, in which His presence is clearly felt, and His communications are 
'heard' and understood. Most of the time God speaks with a "quiet voice" 
(see Elijah's experience at Mt. Horeb, 1 Kings 19:11ff). But there have been 
occasions when the effect was comparable to a shouted imperative or as 
urgent as a fire alarm going off from two feet. We're dealing with things 
for which I have no adequate language, but they're definitely real. In 
countless discussions with other Christians, these realities are common, and 
they are frequently discussed in Christian writing.

I will relate one personal experience that you may find interesting. About 
20 years ago, I was eating in a large restaurant with a friend. A man that I 
had not noticed stood up from his table across the room. As he stood, it was 
as if a fire alarm went off right behind my head, and I instantly *knew* 
that he was a con man. If this man had been covered with flashing neon signs 
saying "Warning! Con Man!" it would have been no more obvious to me. I lost 
all sense of my conversation with my friend, my attention was riveted on 
this man, and on the overpowering alarm that was ringing through my whole 
nervous system. Someone shaking my shoulder would have been less 
distracting. The man began to walk out of the restaurant, and as he came 
closer, he noticed my friend. Turns out, he was a high school classmate of 
my friend that I had never met, and my friend had never mentioned or even 
seen since high school. They talked for a couple of minutes, and all the 
while this urgent alarm was distracting me. By the time the man left, I was 
actually shaken by the power of the experience. On the way back to work, I 
quickly shared what had happened to me, and as urgently as possible, I 
warned my friend against trusting this guy. I repeated the warning several 
times over the next few days. Several weeks later, my friend came to me and 
sheepishly admitted that, after the meeting in the restaurant, the man had 
become friendly with him for a few weeks and eventually talked him into 
cosigning on a $2,000 loan, and then skipped town, leaving my friend to pay.
```

news:HVkqf.16260$kP5.14252@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:

    This is not as unreasonable as it might first appear. I mentioned in 
another branch of this thread the anecdote of this physics professor asking 
for a way God could undeniably prove that he was, in fact, actually God.

    The trick was for God to tell the skeptic "Okay, try as hard as you can 
to remain skeptical and not believe I am God... Are you ready?" And then 
poof; he would use mind-control powers to change the skeptic into a 
believer.

    I think this is undeniable proof (literally! The former-skeptic is 
unable to deny it!), but it is proof that cannot be shared with others.

    - Oliver

Fair enough.  I'm not possibly in a position to question you on this (not 
that I would).  I'm finding that the amount of patience you have defending 
your position incredible.....This is similar to what Daniel went through 
with Terri Schiavo.  I don't find anyone's views more viable than anyone 
else's so I'm easy to please :-)  I just find that I get curiouser as to 
what it's all about as time goes on....


> I will relate one personal experience that you may find interesting. About 
> 20 years ago, I was eating in a large restaurant with a friend. A man that 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> skipped town, leaving my friend to pay.

I have a number of similar events but I don't attribute them to God.   I 
once picked up the phone (pre caller id days) and went "Yo, <name withheld>, 
what's up".

Well the end party was shocked - I hadn't spoken to him in 6 months.  I have 
no idea how I knew it was him, or why I even answered the phone that way. 
I didn't think it significant enough to be spiritual.

I've also played soccer with a guy when I was 18.  I told him, I know who 
you are....and he looked at me like I was insane....I said you're <name 
withheld> - he just flipped his wig.    He was someone I know in passing 
when I was about 5 that had moved abroad.  I don't know how I 
knew....figured it was a mannerism trigger.

I've driven down a road at 55-70 about 1500 times....one day I approached 
the hill and thought....I should do the speed limit of 45 really......then 
there were a train of police cars and a man with a radar gun......Why that 
day ? I haven't since!

I have a lot of questions I would ask, but that would be too personal :-)
Along the lines of :
Ever wrong and judged someone ..how would you know you're wrong?...
Why in this instance would He have cared ? .....
Any other reasons you went through ?...

But I don't need answers.  You're happy with who and what you are, so that's 
all good as far as I am concerned.

I think that will be my last post on anything religious - everyone and no 
one has the right answer.

It's not Schrodingers Cat, it's God's People


DU
```

It is usually not noticed, but there is a good deal of background noise
on a 'phone call, even in quiet places.  I quite frequently know who has
picked up the 'phone at the other end, even before they speak, simply
because of the sounds I hear.  I am utterly unable to elucidate the
various components of this; I thought at one time it might be their
breathing, but since I know just as soon as the 'phone is picked up,
before it is applied to the face of the recipient, I don't think that
that can be the case.  Does everybody give off a characteristic sound
signature, I wonder?

PL

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 58)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-12-22T10:26:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1135275998.863379.90540@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<qrAqf.158$bd.142@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<cvkuo154sjfungkb2jgjmq2q28nl4tu9aj@4ax.com> <do19aq$aab$1@reader1.panix.com> <kODpf.9247$ic1.1418@edtnps90> <3uZpf.16260$k76.15780@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <doacr5$o5q$1@reader1.panix.com> <UE6qf.39872$8d.35720@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <fjgqf.16190$kP5.9261@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <eJgqf.40007$8d.25806@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <HVkqf.16260$kP5.14252@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <qrAqf.158$bd.142@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
> I have a number of similar events but I don't attribute them to God.   I
> once picked up the phone (pre caller id days) and went "Yo, <name withheld>,
> what's up".

The critical number is not the number of times that you do that but the
number of times you remember.  How many times have you said 'yo <name>'
and it wasn't ? You probably just forgot because it wasn't a
significant event that the memory stored away.

One test that I have used on others is to ask how many times when
reading a book with the radio on that they read a word at the same time
it is said on the radio (or TV). They claim never, and then a week
later they are annoyed because they notice it two or three times and
hour and it is distracting. Asking about it made it significant so that
they did remember.

The same with 'premonitions'. How many times has there been an accident
and your mother said 'I dreamt that was going to happen' ? The point is
that 99% of the time they dream or think that something will happen but
then forget they did, except those times when something did happen
which brought it from the background.

Predictions are so much easier when done in retrospect.
```

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1135275998.863379.90540@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> The same with 'premonitions'. How many times has there been an accident
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Predictions are so much easier when done in retrospect.

    There is also a reproduceable[*] scientific experiment which shows that 
the mind is often confused about the order in which events occur. The 
experiment was explained in Roger Penrose's book "The Emperor's New Mind" 
which I have at home, but I don't recall the details of the experiment right 
here off the top of my head.

    Given that the human mind is confused about the order in which events 
occur, it is entirely possible, for these sequence of events to occur 
(listed in chronological order).

Event A occurs.
You witness event A.
Your brain stores in its memory that event A occured.
Your brain immediately retrieves this memory of event A having occured.
Your brain believes that this memory was stored before event A actually 
occured.

    And so, from your brain's perspective, this is the sequence of events:

You "remember" event A occuring.
Event A occurs.

    And then it would seem to you that you had some sense of premonition.

    - Oliver

[*] reproduceable if you are comfortable performing open skull surgery while 
the patient is still conscious and implanting electrical impulse detection 
nodes in their head.
```

>

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

that
> the mind is often confused about the order in which events occur. The
> experiment was explained in Roger Penrose's book "The Emperor's New Mind"
> which I have at home, but I don't recall the details of the experiment
right

…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> [*] reproduceable if you are comfortable performing open skull surgery
while
> the patient is still conscious and implanting electrical impulse detection
> nodes in their head.

As I understand it, what you describe is called
d�j� vu (the illusion of having previously
experienced something actually being encountered
for the first time). This is different than premonition
(an intuitive anticipation of a future event) in that the
event anticipated by the premonition need not occur,
but that encountered leading to the illusion of d�j� vu
must occur.

Precognition (knowledge of a future event or
situation, especially through extra sensory means)
seems a better term for 'prediction' by dreams and
better than the alternative, prophesy, which is more
toward 'prediction' by divine inspiration.

I experience d�j� vu on occasion. It seems to
occur most often when I encounter words or
phases, knowing that I have written or seen these
words or phases before. The times I have checked,
I could not locate them.
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:CU4of.8526$kP5.2446@bignews5.bellsouth.net...

>>> Humans do not get into Heaven by being good, they get into Heaven in one
>>> and only one way, by receiving salvation by the grace of God, through
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> believers, and doesn't ask our opinion on the matter. :-)

So you don't accept the Epistle of James as canonic?  Or is it just the

second chapter you don't like?  ;-)

>>> By definition, all Christians believe this, or they are not Christians.

Just a reminder:  as I read it, the presumption is salvation by faith
(alone) through grace (alone).

I asked:

>> All Christians believe that, if your behavior doesn't match your
>> proclamations, you are nonetheless "saved"?

You replied:

> I have a very good friend who is a devout Christian. In 1975 when he was
> saved, and for a short time after, he was doing drugs and cursing and such
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> grace". We don't deserve it.

Entirely irrelevant to my question.

> You certainly know more about Catholic doctrine than I do, I've never been
> a Catholic. But I have seen written declarations from the Catholic Church
> expressing precisely the definition I give above (in almost the exact same
> words), or I wouldn't have said so.

Look up "sola fide" in Wikipedia.  It says "Sola fide (by faith alone),
also
historically known as the justification of faith, is a doctrine that
distinguishes Protestant denominations from Catholicism and Eastern
Christianity in Christianity."   Martin Luther invented the slogan.
Martin
Luther is also reported in the Wikipedia article as having referred to
the
Epistle of James as the "epistle of straw".  Martin Luther also
inserted the
word "allein" (alone) in his translation into German of Romans 3:28, "
...
is justified by faith *alone* ...", an insertion for which there seems
to be
no justification based on any early text for this passage in any
language.

> Anyway, it doesn't matter what I say, those principles are clearly and
> repeatedly defined in the Bible and beyond dispute.

"Don't bother me with the facts, my mind is made up!"?   It may be
possible
to defend these principles from the Bible, but that does not prohibit
someone else from coming to a different, and equally defensible,
conclusion
from the same text.   The Roman Catholic Church seems to have done so.

> I would be very shocked and disappointed to learn that the Catholic Church
> deviated so far from God's Word as to make themselves a cult.

No, Jordan.  They deviated that far from *your interpreatation* of
God's
word.    The Roman Catholic Church (and Eastern Orthodoxy) has, whether
you
like it or not, a direct and unbroken link in its hierarchy going all
the
way back to Peter and the bishops he ordained.  Only bishops can ordain

bishops and priests.  Whether you accept a "mystical" component to the
Apostolic Succession, it has a practical component in that what the
Early
Church Fathers *said*, and what they *understood*, beyond what they
*wrote*,
stands more of a chance of being preserved there than in an
organization
whose traditions began on October 31, 1517.

> Do Catholics believe that we are saved by the grace of God and not by
> works?

According to Wikipedia, "[sola fide] is rejected by Catholics, who say
faith
and works acting through God's Grace are necessary parts of salvation."

> Do Catholics believe that we are saved by faith in Jesus Christ?

In part ...

> Do Catholics believe that Jesus is the only way to be reconciled to God?

Yes, I would say that's true; however, I suspect I understand "nobody
comes
to the Father save through me" differently from you.

> Do Catholics believe that Jesus is the Son of God?

Yes, and is also one substance with the Father, the second person of
the
Holy Trinity (the third being the Holy Spirit, which proceeds from the
Father and the Son).

> Do Catholics believe that Jesus died for our sins?

Yes.

> These are what I said all Christians believe.

You said all Christians believe in salvation by faith, and in that is
the
implicit premise that works don't matter.  The Roman Catholic church
does
not agree.

> Surely you are not telling me that the answer to any of those is 'no'?

Sure am, Judson, from material that's as readily available to you as to
me.

There are *long* discussions on this subject in the online Catholic
encyclopedia at http://www.newadvent.org/cathen/ under the subjects
"justification" and "grace".

Maybe it would be a good idea for you to digest not only Roman Catholic

doctrine, but also the reasons for each part of that doctrine, before
expounding on what that doctrine is and why it's right (or wrong) in
detail?

And that brings up the subject:  Which uncontrivertable and
universally-accepted authority gave *you* the right to decide and
decree
*for others* who is, and who is not, a real Christian?   It is one
thing to
*opine*, based on your understanding of Scripture and your
understanding of
a particular point; it is quite another to presume that your personal
understanding (and what you have been taught) is the Only Possible Way
to
read Scripture.

Can you not understand that others might perceive such a decree as
evidence
of both pride and arrogance on your part, neither of which is likely to
be
regarded as consistent with Christian behavior?

Can you not also understand that there is no difference between
accepting
the teachings of your *denomination* about what is and is not a real
Christian and the very attitudes in the Roman Catholic Church that
Luther
railed against in 1517?

If you're spouting your denomination's line, then your denomination's
taken
over Sole Ownership of The Only Correct Understanding of Scripture.
That's
arrogant *of them*, just as arrogant as the Reformation-era Western
Church.

If you've come to these conclusions *yourself*, and concluded that they

represent The Only Correct Understanding of Scripture, and it's your
job to
convince everyone how lousy *their* understanding is and how great
*yours* 
is, well ...

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **Message-ID:** `<mkAof.31296$Y72.29808@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<y6Yjf.7656$wf2.836017@news20.bellglobal.com> <FoZjf.47094$6y4.38133@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <1133550230.780919.303800@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <i21kf.47144$6y4.15970@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqd1b$259m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <dnq921$lk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <CU4of.8526$kP5.2446@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <1134677095.550825.225980@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> second chapter you don't like?  ;-)

I just do not interpret James as you apparently do. I understand James to be 
saying (in paraphrase) "If you do not have faith that is demonstrated by 
works, then your faith is not real, it is 'dead'. If you have real faith, 
you will have works." Faith is not simple belief, it also implies trust. 
Even the demons believe in God, and tremble (James 2:19), but their 
intellectual belief does not save them. James is clearly talking about this 
distinction. To interpret James as meaning that works are required as 
requisite for salvation, you must take portions of what he wrote out of 
major and minor context.

>>>> By definition, all Christians believe this, or they are not Christians.
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Entirely irrelevant to my question.

Not irrelevant, central to your question! A person does not become perfect, 
then is saved. People are saved *from a state of sin*. To declare that a 
person's actions must be in perfect alighment with Holiness before being 
saved would completely destroy the whole concept of salvation. When we truly 
accept Christ in our hearts, we are instantly "justified", legally accounted 
to be perfect, in Christ, as a soverign act of God, whereby He attributes to 
us the perfection of Jesus, based on our faith in Him. But our habits and 
thoughts are not entirely and instantly renovated. It is only through a 
process that lasts the rest of our lives are we "sanctified", that is, we 
learn about God, we get to know Him personally, and learn to be obedient to 
Him, and (hopefully) become more and more like Jesus in our character and 
actions. This is a process that is never entirely completed in this life, 
though some Christians get much farther along than others. The speed and 
degree to which this happens is very much dependent on how freely we submit 
to God, and how dilligently we pursue God through Bible study and prayer. 
There *is no* Christian who walks an absolutely sinless life, in practice. 
Our obedience to God brings reward, but it is our faith in Jesus as Savior 
that permits God to account us as without sin. The Scriptural backing for 
the above is extensive and comprehensive. Paul, for example, goes to great 
length to explain these principles, and they are implicit throughout the New 
Testament in particular, but also in the Old Testament (e.g. Psalm 51).

>> Anyway, it doesn't matter what I say, those principles are clearly and
>> repeatedly defined in the Bible and beyond dispute.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> from the same text.   The Roman Catholic Church seems to have done so.

Nothing could prevent people from coming up with different interpretations 
of anything, no matter how clearly it is written. :-)

> You said all Christians believe in salvation by faith, and in that is the
> implicit premise that works don't matter.  The Roman Catholic church
> does not agree.

I didn't say (nor does the Bible say) that "works don't matter." Works are 
important, and God expects us to produce works. They just don't help save 
us.

> Maybe it would be a good idea for you to digest not only Roman Catholic
> doctrine, but also the reasons for each part of that doctrine, before
> expounding on what that doctrine is and why it's right (or wrong) in
> detail?

Well I didn't just speak on whim, I have read and heard quite a bit about 
Roman Catholic doctrine over the years. But I am no expert, I readily 
concede this.

> And that brings up the subject:  Which uncontrivertable and
> universally-accepted authority gave *you* the right to decide and
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> read Scripture.

And I might ask, what authority gave you the right to post any advice to 
anyone on anything? Duh! We all speak from what we know. We share our 
knowledge and what we think with others on a wide variety of subjects. It 
happens that I have been studying the Bible in a very serious way for over 
30 years. Whether you agree with me or not, I believe that qualifies me to 
express more than a neophyte's opinion on these issues. :-)

> Can you not understand that others might perceive such a decree as 
> evidence
> of both pride and arrogance on your part, neither of which is likely to be
> regarded as consistent with Christian behavior?

When you speak with confidence from your (admitedly extensive) programming 
experience, do you expect yourself to be accused of pride and arrogance?

I understand that Christians throughout the last 2000 years have been 
persecuted and killed for simply sharing with others what Jesus said. Jesus 
Himself was crucified, and He was perfect. Jesus made it very clear that if 
the Master is persecuted, so will His followers. You think I should wait 
until no one is going to disagree with me before stating things that I know 
from long and dilligent study are the truth? Is that your philosophy 
regarding your own areas of knowledge? It doesn't appear so to me. :-)  In 
fact, your vociferous response makes me understand the motivations behind 
the Roman Inquisition a bit more clearly. What was that you were saying 
about how Christians should act? ;-)

> Can you not also understand that there is no difference between accepting
> the teachings of your *denomination* about what is and is not a real
> Christian and the very attitudes in the Roman Catholic Church that Luther
> railed against in 1517?

It depends on the validity of those teachings, does it not? Luther opposed 
particular Roman Catholic doctrines because those doctrines were in conflict 
with the Bible. I do not belong to a 'denomination'. I don't have a problem 
with denominations, but what human denominations say about God are not the 
issue. It is what God has declared in His Word that matters. Not every issue 
in the Bible is so clear that there can be no disagreement. But there is a 
lot of disagrement over issues that are perfectly clear, where there should 
be no disagreement. I can't help that. I had no intention of trying to 
criticize Roman Catholic doctrine. If I had, I certainly would have picked 
another topic in which to do so. If you recall, I was actually stating 
something on which I thought (actually, I still do think) that the Catholic 
Church agrees. :-)
```

- **References:** `<y6Yjf.7656$wf2.836017@news20.bellglobal.com> <FoZjf.47094$6y4.38133@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <1133550230.780919.303800@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <i21kf.47144$6y4.15970@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqd1b$259m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <dnq921$lk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <CU4of.8526$kP5.2446@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <1134677095.550825.225980@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <mkAof.31296$Y72.29808@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:mkAof.31296$Y72.29808@bignews1.bellsouth.net...
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>> And that brings up the subject:  Which uncontrivertable and
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> express more than a neophyte's opinion on these issues. :-)

    From what I've seen on this newsgroup, when Chuck says something about 
COBOL, he usually qualifies it with "According to such and such standards 
document, this is the semantics of that statement, but note that 
implementors occasionally deviate from the standard." This is in contrast to 
"the Only Possible Way". I don't think Chuck is accusuing you of being a 
neophyte with respect to the bible. Rather, I believe he is saying that 
there is no reason to believe your interpretation is more "correct" or 
"valid" than all other interpretations that have been put forward by others.

[snip]
>
> I understand that Christians throughout the last 2000 years have been 
> persecuted and killed for simply sharing with others what Jesus said. 
> Jesus Himself was crucified, and He was perfect. Jesus made it very clear 
> that if the Master is persecuted, so will His followers.

    I'm not sure what these sentences have to do with what follows ...

> You think I should wait until no one is going to disagree with me before 
> stating things that I know from long and dilligent study are the truth?

    ... but just to provide a balanced view, non-Christians have also been 
persecuted and killed for simply sharing with others that they don't believe 
the same things as others. There was an CNN article on this, posted December 
8th, 2005:

http://www.cnn.com/2005/EDUCATION/12/08/creationism.professor.ap/index.html

    Professor Paul Mirecki was planning on teaching a course entitled 
"Special Topics in Religion: Intelligent Design, Creationism and other 
Religious Mythologies".

<quote>
On Monday, Mirecki was treated at a Lawrence hospital for head injuries 
after he said he was beaten by two men on a country road. He said the men 
referred to the creationism course. Law enforcement officials were 
investigating.
</quote>

    To be fair, Mirecki did send out an e-mail in which he did some 
name-calling:

<quote>
A recent e-mail from Mirecki to members of a student organization referred 
to religious conservatives as "fundies" and said a course describing 
intelligent design as mythology would be a "nice slap in their big fat 
face." Mirecki apologized for those comments.
</quote>

    Obviously, I do not approve of Mirecki's e-mail, but I do not feel it 
warranted a physical beating. Mirecki does not like religious-conservative 
people. Fine. While perhaps prejudicial, he is entitled to his opinion.

<quote, from Slashdot 
ref="http://science.slashdot.org/comments.pl?sid=170687&cid=14225378">
If you look at Mirecki's areas of expertise his irritation with 
fundamentalists becomes all the more understandable. His areas areas are 
Ancient Mediterranean Religion, Early Christianity, and Coptic Papyrology. 
That means that he knows a lot about about religion in the area in which 
Christianity developed about the origins of Christianity, and about branches 
of Christianity that either died out (e.g. gnosticism) or have followed a 
rather different course from the one that led to fundamentalism (e.g. Coptic 
Christianity). For someone with this background, the belief of 
fundamentalists that their interpretation of the particular compilation of 
texts that they consider holy is God's Truth must seem particularly crazy. I 
can't speak for him, but I bet that to him fundamentalists seem ignorant, 
naive, and arrogant even if one looks just at the religious texts and their 
interpretation, without concerning oneself with the conflict between 
fundamentalist beliefs and science.
</quote>

    There's also this account, from someone claiming to be a student of the 
professor:

<quote 
ref="http://science.slashdot.org/comments.pl?sid=170687&cid=14225669">
As someone who is both interested in dismissing the conspiracy theories and 
was enrolled in the cancelled class, I think it is time to post some real 
information.

First, Paul Mirecki is a well-respected scholar in the field of 
Christianity. He is regularly chosen as the lecturer for the week that 
covers the Old Testament of the Bible in an Honors Western Civilization I 
course. Mirecki's personal beliefs regarding religion never came up in that 
lecture - he stuck to the facts. My experiences echo those of nearly every 
student who has taken a course taught by him. In his 20+ years as head of 
the Religious Studies department, Mirecki's scholarship and teaching have 
been praised by scholars and students alike.

Second, the email in question was sent via a Yahoo listserv to members of 
the KU Society of Open-Minded Atheists and Agnostics (SOMAA). While the 
group may be a part of KU, it is about as disconnected from the everyday 
processes of the University as can be. Student groups are funded through the 
KU Student Senate, which means the University's own democratic processes 
(which include plenty of Christians as voters) elected a body of individuals 
who sanctioned funding of SOMAA. The Christian individual who released the 
one paragraph of the email had no business doing so. The released text was 
taken out of any context (the vast majority of the message had nothing to do 
with the class in question). In addition, complaining about SOMAA being 
anti-Christian is akin to me joining the listserv for any of the multiple 
Christian student groups and then whining when they mention God or Jesus in 
their emails.

Third, the Kansas legislature has a history of trying to destroy the 
University of Kansas. Several years ago, a state legislator claimed a 
student came to her and accused the professor of a popular Human Sexuality 
course of promoting pedofilia among other things. After much hubub and 
threats from the state, it came out that the 'student' was actually an aide 
of the legislator in question, had been encouraged to falsify her claims, 
and an investigation by the University found zero evidence to back the 
claims. Keep in mind here folks that we have more than one legislator 
without so much as a high school diploma. A prominent representative from 
wealthy Johnson County has vocally voiced her opposition to the 19th 
Amendment and women's suffrage. The conservative majority in our state 
legislature is uneducated, inept, and scary - only our governer is keeping 
things from getting too out of control.

And finally, the beating is real. I notice one of the sources often cited 
for inconsitencies in stories is www.kansan.com . That is the online version 
of our student newspaper, and I would shudder to think that The Kansan would 
be used as a serious resource. The journalists on our newspaper staff have 
difficulties differentiating between their/there/they're, much less getting 
their facts straight on a criminal investigation. Please, if you're going to 
cite a Lawrence paper, at least go with something more reputable like the 
Journal World. After visiting with several faculty members of the Religious 
Studies department, they all gave similar accounts of Mirecki's injuries. 
Sorry to say, but I trust the accounts of professors with whom I have 
developed personal friendships over CNN journalists who probably did not 
even know where Lawrence was before this whole incident occurred.
</quote>

    Anyway, I'm not suggesting that all fundamentalists (nor all Christians) 
are violent, or that the action of these two reflects on religion as a 
whole. I'm just saying don't think Christians are alone in being persecuted 
for their beliefs.

And then there are these statistics, which don't have a citation, so they 
may be questionable, but the "Percentage of American presidents who 
self-identify as Christian" sounds accurate to me.

<quote 
href="http://science.slashdot.org/comments.pl?sid=170687&cid=14225666">
Some facts

Percentage of the U.S. population who self-identify as Christian: %82

Percentage of Senators = 89%

Percentage of Representatives = 90%

Percentage of Supreme Court Justices = %78

Percentage of Presidents = %100

Percentage of Current Governors = 94%

Christmas = Federal Holiday

and I can go on and on.

Poor little Christians. So very, very oppressed.
</quote>

    - Oliver
```

"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> implementors occasionally deviate from the standard."

When Chuck speaks about Unisys A Series COBOL compilers, he speaks with 
confidence, usually not leaving any wiggle room. As I would expect, because 
Chuck is very knowledgable in this area. I have used these compilers for 
years myself, but I would be reluctant to take issue with something Chuck 
said about them, because his knowledge is much broader than mine.

> This is in contrast to "the Only Possible Way". I don't think Chuck is 
> accusuing you of being a neophyte with respect to the bible. Rather, I 
> believe he is saying that there is no reason to believe your 
> interpretation is more "correct" or "valid" than all other interpretations 
> that have been put forward by others.

We should want to verify anything that anyone says about the Bible, and 
should consult the Bible directly, check various translations, consult the 
original language using reference helps as necessary. I highly recommend it. 
The issue is too vital to simply take someone elses word about what the 
Bible says. :-)  All that any of us say here simply reflects (hopefully) the 
best that we know. None of us have perfect knowledge, and none of us are 
without flaw. From direct and personal experience, I can assure you, this is 
quite true with respect to myself. :-)  However, that being said, when 
someone has devoted a lot of effort and time into studying an issue, they 
have a right to speak with confidence about the accuracy of what they say. 
Don't all of use do that on a fairly regular basis? I know Chuck does. :-)

> <quote 
> href="http://science.slashdot.org/comments.pl?sid=170687&cid=14225666">
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> /quote>

Percentage of media (news) people = 2%  (survey from the mid 90's)

In article <QLVof.203$RZ6.53@bignews7.bellsouth.net>,
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:

>> <quote 
>> href="http://science.slashdot.org/comments.pl?sid=170687&cid=14225666">
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

... and yet because of that small percentage, Mr McClendon, the 
overwhelming percentage of publicly-elected officials seem to be as Mr 
Wong stated above.  Amazing, isn't it?

DD
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> tell you now. I would call you an IDIOT :-)


Anyone who did that, I would agree. :-) You're supposed to do some 
verification on your own. The more important the issue is, the more 
verification you should want. These are things a person has to decide for 
themselves.

But the issue I was addressing above was whether or not a person is 
justified in making definite statements about things. I believe a person 
should state things with a definiteness that corresponds with the degree of 
confidence they have in the statements. If someone states something as very 
definite, it simply means that they are confident in it. This is only being 
honest. Whether a person believes those statements or not is up to them. The 
credibility attributed to the person making the statements, and the 
confidence (if any) placed in their judgment, and all the other known 
factors should enter into any assessment. And, they should attempt to verify 
for themselves the validity of the statements, if the issue is important. 
This seems to me a very reasonable position to take. :-)

If you can show my comments to be false, then have at it. If you don't agree 
with them, then say so. But don't complain because I have confidence in any 
particular statement I make. Give me a break, don't I have the right to be 
more or less confident in the things I believe and say? The rest of you 
folks sure are. ;-)
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:QLVof.203$RZ6.53@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Percentage of media (news) people = 2%  (survey from the mid 90's)

http://yahoo.businessweek.com/bwdaily/dnflash/dec2005/nf20051214_8338_db016.htm

<quote>
The Christian Right would seem to have a lot to celebrate as Christmas 
approaches. In recent days, it has scored a number of significant victories 
over its perceived opponents in Corporate America. Many of the nation's 
biggest cable operators have just announced plans to offer a "family tier" 
of networks in response to the Christian Right's long-running campaign 
against "indecent" TV programming.

Disney (DIS ), which has come under fire from some Christian groups, is now 
cultivating Christians to promote its big new hit, The Chronicles of Narnia. 
On Dec. 9, the American Family Assn. (AFA) called off its boycott of Target 
(TGT ) after the retailer said it would include Christmas in its advertising 
and in-store promotions.

[...]

CHRISTMAS SPIRIT.  The Catholic League has had better luck with its campaign 
to fend off corporate efforts to malign Christmas. On Nov. 11, the League 
called off its boycott of Wal-Mart after the world's largest retailer 
apologized for a statement it gave a customer who had complained that it was 
replacing "Merry Christmas" with "Happy Holidays."

The statement argued that Christmas was "an ancient tradition" with roots in 
pagan cultures. And this month, the Catholic League got Land's End to 
repudiate its earlier explanation of why it no longer mails out catalogs 
wishing customers a "Merry Christmas." The reason: It didn't have any way to 
determine which customers celebrated Christmas.

But are these companies bowing to the Christian right -- or to economic 
realities? Britt Beemer, chairman of America's Research Group, a market 
research firm based in Charleston, S.C., thinks it's the latter. "Some of 
these retailers are awakening to a sleeping giant," he argues.
</quote>

It would seem that Christians are not "powerless" in the media circles 
either.

    - Oliver
```

```
In article <1135022895.332611.171370@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>,
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>> The statement argued that Christmas was "an ancient tradition" with roots in
>> pagan cultures.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bach, Hamburg?  Well... he wasn't a musician of equal caliber but a few 
years ago I recall hearing Tom Lehrer sing:

Christmas time is here, by golly,
Disapproval would be folly.
Deck the halls with hunks of holly,
Fill the cups and don't say when...
Kill the turkeys, ducks and chickens,
Mix the punc, draaaag out the Dickens -
Even though the prospect sickens:
'Brother, here we go again!'

On Christmas day you can't get sore,
Your fellowman you must adore,
There's time to rob him all the more
The other three-hundred and sixty-four...

Relations, sparing no expense'll
Send some useless, old utensil
Like a matching pen-and-pencil:
'Just the thing I need.  How nice.'

It doesn't matter how sincere it is
Or how heartfelt the spirit;
Sentiment will not endear it - 
What's important is The Price!

Hark!  The Herald Tribune sings,
Advertising won'drous things!

God rest ye merry, merchants,
May ye make the Yuletide pay!

Angels we have heard on high,
Tell us to go out and Buy!

Soooooo...

Let the raucous sleigh-bells jingle,
Hail our dear, old friend, Kris Kringle,
Driving his reindeer across the skyyyyyyy...
Don't stand underneath when they... fly... by!

(curious things one keeps in RAM, aye)

DD
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:AxZpf.16314$k76.12687@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
>> <quote>
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> This is the marketplace, not the media. -)

    "Disney" and "cable networks" are in the business of media, I think. As 
for the walmart thing, what "The Catholic League" was complaining about here 
had nothing to do with prices or products offered (i.e. things to do with a 
market), but rather with what was written on catalogs (which has to do with 
media -- text). I.e. they weren't unhappy with the products and services of 
Walmart, they were unhappy with the message that their advertisement sent 
out. This is a media issue too.

    - Oliver
```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:cnhjq1lj6emfqktk3t26tkfr1eb762vsgt@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 21 Dec 2005 15:06:38 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
> <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> discussions are pretty interesting though.

    Picking up the ball where Howard dropped it, I found this site:

http://www.usatoday.com/life/books/news/2005-12-07-narnia-gresham_x.htm

<quote>
Gresham, who is a stepson of C.S. Lewis, the celebrated writer of the Narnia 
series and religious scholar, is the co-producer of the film The Chronicles 
of Narnia: The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe. The movie arrives in 
theaters Friday.

[...]

Gresham met Lewis, the man known as Jack to family and friends, in 1953, 
three years after the publication of The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe, 
one of seven books in the Narnia series.

[...]

Gresham is insistent that the Chronicles are "certainly not a Christian 
allegory. Jack himself denied that.
</quote>

    So USA Today claims that Gresham claims that Jack, who is allegedly the 
same person as C.S. Lewis, claims that Narnia is not a Chirstian allegory.

    - Oliver
```

"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
news:84kqf.1196$km.1136@edtnps89...
>
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I didn't realise you guys haven't seen it yet. It premiered here last week 
and I went with a friend who is currently visiting from the UK. This lady is 
Jewish so the alleged Christian allegory may have been lost on her, but she 
certainly enjoyed it.

It is really very well done and faithful to the book as I remember it. 
There are a myriad of strange and wonderful creatures in the film, and they 
are all totally believable.

Sometimes it is a bit incongruous... (I can't imagine why a beaver would 
talk with an East London accent :-) - some of you Canadians may be amused by 
this), but it is a right good yarn and the lion, Aslan is incredible.

While we're talking movies...anyone seen King Kong yet? Long, but absolutely 
breath taking. Best King Kong so far made, I reckon. Jackson is a genius.

> [...]
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Beautifully expressed, Oliver :-)

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 55)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-22T11:28:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ckBqf.26483$k76.2648@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<CU4of.8526$kP5.2446@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <1134677095.550825.225980@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <mkAof.31296$Y72.29808@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <qKCof.2064$ic1.905@edtnps90> <QLVof.203$RZ6.53@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <J0Epf.9251$ic1.4561@edtnps90> <1135121880.926288.249960@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <fpgqf.16192$kP5.15005@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <2c6jq1t6hmul72fgja8ocfbvk85msbq18h@4ax.com> <9rjqf.16236$kP5.14258@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <cnhjq1lj6emfqktk3t26tkfr1eb762vsgt@4ax.com> <84kqf.1196$km.1136@edtnps89> <40ud00F1c6ob8U1@individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> While we're talking movies...anyone seen King Kong yet? Long, but 
> absolutely breath taking. Best King Kong so far made, I reckon. Jackson is 
> a genius.


King Kong is great! I thought the special effects and cinematography were 
extraordinary, as good as it gets. Whoever did Kong's facial expressions 
alone should get an Oscar. Much better movie than I expected. Gobs of 
thrilling action scenes, and the biplane sequence was wonderful. If I were 
to criticize, it would be that the attachment between Kong and the girl was 
way over the top. I realize it is important to the plot, but there was no 
reasonable justification in the depicted events for such an unbelievably 
strong bond.

In the original cut of the 1933 Kong, after Kong shook the men off the log, 
the survivors in the valley floor were attacked by huge insects. Test 
audiences were horrified by the scene, so someone (don't remember who) cut 
the scene that night and it was (AFAIK) never seen again. Jackson put a 
similar scene back in. I also really liked the way Jackson interweaved 
events and dialog from the original movie. This movie is unquestionably a 
labor of love.
```

>King Kong is great! I thought the special effects and cinematography were

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>strong bond.

The plot never made sense.   The concept that western civilization's
physical beauty would win over an ape is pretty perverse when you

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> strength's lie in another direction.

One thing I liked about this movie is that they seemed to avoid implied 
sexuality between Kong and the girl. In this movie she (being a vaudeville 
entertainer) got the idea of entertaining Kong, and it worked. The 
attraction for the ape was that he was extremely lonely, being the last of 
his kind, and was attracted to the girl for amusement and company. She was 
affectionate toward Kong because he protected her at some danger and harm to 
himself.
```

> Judson McClendon wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> so I guess tha actor in the ape suit won't get one either.

The same actor (Peter Cirkis?) who did Gollum also did the motion capture 
for Kong. But there was no "ape suit"; Kong was, as Peter Jackson put it, 
"fully realized in CGI". If you see the movie (and this is in some 
previews), note Kong's expression, particularly the eyes, upon seeing the 
biplanes for the first time.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 57)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2005-12-22T18:31:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<doerdj$iog$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<qKCof.2064$ic1.905@edtnps90> <40ud00F1c6ob8U1@individual.net> <ckBqf.26483$k76.2648@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <84plq1prbt7r1cerdgfuinse4arr83fqpv@4ax.com>`

```
In article <84plq1prbt7r1cerdgfuinse4arr83fqpv@4ax.com>,
Howard Brazee  <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

[snip]

>Is there any part of the movie which *does* make sense?   (I have only
>seen the original)

Sure there is... the part where the movie-guy finds the gal as she's 
caught stealing fruit from a vendor; in order to calm the peddler's ire at 
the theft he passes over a dollar and there's an interchange along the 
lines of:

Movie-guy: 'Here's a buck.'
Peddler: 'A buck?  Scram!'

But really... in 1933 this film had it *all*... a giant ape wreaking havoc 
on the Technological Capital, breaking chains of 'chrome steel', knocking 
down an elevated subway train, climbing the tallest building in the world 
and then getting shot down... by biplanes... with machine guns!  You get 
all that and you want 'sense', too?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-12-22T11:38:56-07:00

```
On Thu, 22 Dec 2005 18:31:16 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>But really... in 1933 this film had it *all*... a giant ape wreaking havoc 
>on the Technological Capital, breaking chains of 'chrome steel', knocking 
>down an elevated subway train, climbing the tallest building in the world 
>and then getting shot down... by biplanes... with machine guns!  You get 
>all that and you want 'sense', too?

As I said, its strengths were in other directions.

I liked it when the explorers wanted to appear nonchalant in the
jungle, pushing their hats at the stylish "cool" angle down on their
foreheads.   I suspect the director was purposefully being funny there
- but having seen it a half century after the fact, I didn't know for
sure.
```

I will admit to only having seen excerpts of the remake and I have seen
the original film. I love the original and acknowledge that the cgi on
the new film is brilliant but I personally would prefer the original. I
hate the idea that all great films have to be remade to be worthy of
viewing in a modern age. I also hate the idea that I will live to see
the remake of the remake of King Kong.

Talking of remakes; sometimes it does work. A week or so back saw 'The 
Preacher's Wife', the good-looking Denzel Washington as the angel, the 
dishy Whitney Houston, presumably before she screwed up her life, and 
Courtney B. Vance, plus a complete black cast; not a single 'White' in
sight.

Then only night before last picked up on the CBC showing of "The 
Bishop's Wife", starring handsome Cary Grant as the angel, David Niven 
and Loretta Young.

OK so they are both Christian syrupy Christmas tales. But the remake 
held up well against the original.

Jimmy

is entertaining.

>     - Oliver

I can not contemplate the notion that Casblanca would be any better on
the remake. Maybe they'll have the Mummy returning for the third time?
```
