###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Remakes of movies

_(reply depth: 61)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-01-09T15:54:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hHvwf.46735$km.40115@edtnps89>`
- **References:** `<qKCof.2064$ic1.905@edtnps90> <QLVof.203$RZ6.53@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <J0Epf.9251$ic1.4561@edtnps90> <1135121880.926288.249960@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <fpgqf.16192$kP5.15005@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <2c6jq1t6hmul72fgja8ocfbvk85msbq18h@4ax.com> <9rjqf.16236$kP5.14258@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <cnhjq1lj6emfqktk3t26tkfr1eb762vsgt@4ax.com> <84kqf.1196$km.1136@edtnps89> <40ud00F1c6ob8U1@individual.net> <ckBqf.26483$k76.2648@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <84plq1prbt7r1cerdgfuinse4arr83fqpv@4ax.com> <boCqf.169704$Gd6.47105@pd7tw3no> <1135285228.000852.147670@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <CLFqf.333$OU5.206@clgrps13> <1135375616.234551.254840@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1135375616.234551.254840@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> Oliver Wong wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> the remake. Maybe they'll have the Mummy returning for the third time?

    One issue with remakes is that people (including movie makers) generally 
only remember the classics, or "good" movies, and so when they want to do a 
remake, it'll probably be one of those movies that will get remade. If they 
took a really lousy movie, and did a remake of it, it'd be a lot easier for 
the remake to be better than the original, but there'd be less incentive to 
advertise what movie this new one is a remake of.

    This isn't a movie, but "Chrono Trigger" was one of my favorite video 
games. It was released in 1995 for the Super Nintendo console. The plot of 
the game, I thought was excellent, and the graphics were decent for that 
age. Screenshot at:
http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/d/d5/ChronoTriggerInside.PNG

    Some fans of CT got together and decided to make a remake of the game. 
As far as I know, it would have the exact same plot, but the graphics, music 
and some of the gameplay would be revised. Screenshot at: 
http://www.opcoder.com/projects/chrono/Imps.jpg

    There's a trailer also available for the remake, and it was quite an 
emotional experience for me watching it. The music and scenes invoked a lot 
of memories. What I was basically thinking is that I could re-enjoy Chrono 
Trigger again, and in all aspects, it would be equal or better. 
Unfortunately, the project got cancelled for legal reasons (namely they 
didn't acquire the rights to the license).

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Remakes of movies

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-01-09T09:56:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cn35s1134iv732rmeh3tqtt4q6piht9ngl@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9rjqf.16236$kP5.14258@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <cnhjq1lj6emfqktk3t26tkfr1eb762vsgt@4ax.com> <84kqf.1196$km.1136@edtnps89> <40ud00F1c6ob8U1@individual.net> <ckBqf.26483$k76.2648@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <84plq1prbt7r1cerdgfuinse4arr83fqpv@4ax.com> <boCqf.169704$Gd6.47105@pd7tw3no> <1135285228.000852.147670@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <CLFqf.333$OU5.206@clgrps13> <1135375616.234551.254840@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <hHvwf.46735$km.40115@edtnps89>`

```
> I can not contemplate the notion that Casblanca would be any better on
> the remake. Maybe they'll have the Mummy returning for the third time?

Occasionally the remake is better than the original.   How many people
have seen the 1931 version of _The Maltese Falcon_?    I haven't. But
the thing is the 1931 version is the original *movie*, not the
Dashiell Hammett novel.

Popular culture being what it is, when a work is remade a lot, origins
get confused.   The makers of _The Adams Family_ movies used a
character from the TV show, and ended up paying royalties to those who
owned the TV series (they claimed they were basing the movie directly
off the Charles Adams comics in The New Yorker magazine).   

The movie _Little Shop of Horrors_ was based off the play which was
based off the movie _The Little Shop of Horrors_.

We have other non-musical -> musical examples.   The movie _My Fair
Lady_ wasn't really a remake of the movie _Pygmalian_, but instead was
a filming of the play which was the remake.    Cole Porter turned
_Philadelphia Story_ into _High Society_ with his songs.   These
examples are good movies remade into good movies.

We are CoBOL programmers.   We remake programs.   We copy code.   We
plagiarize code.    Because we have the same story to tell again and
again - with our environments changed just enough so that the old way
doesn't quite work.

There's a SF story (by Spider Robinson?) where in the future computers
check every song that ever created for plagiarism.   A rich widow of a
songwriter lobbies to make this less inclusive after her husband,
trying to write her another love song kept failing to come up with
something that wasn't derivative committed suicide.   They needed to
recycle creativity.

We don't have patents on steering wheels or foot throttles.   We don't
have patents on shooting up monsters in the arcade.

Patents have been very good for society for some time now, but are
possibly coming to the end of their useful life cycle.    We don't
seem to get innovation via big companies' patents anymore - and I
don't think we are getting the products from the small guys via
patents so much either.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Remakes of movies

_(reply depth: 63)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-01-09T18:02:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lzxwf.80029$6K2.49736@edtnps90>`
- **References:** `<9rjqf.16236$kP5.14258@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <cnhjq1lj6emfqktk3t26tkfr1eb762vsgt@4ax.com> <84kqf.1196$km.1136@edtnps89> <40ud00F1c6ob8U1@individual.net> <ckBqf.26483$k76.2648@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <84plq1prbt7r1cerdgfuinse4arr83fqpv@4ax.com> <boCqf.169704$Gd6.47105@pd7tw3no> <1135285228.000852.147670@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <CLFqf.333$OU5.206@clgrps13> <1135375616.234551.254840@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <hHvwf.46735$km.40115@edtnps89> <cn35s1134iv732rmeh3tqtt4q6piht9ngl@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:cn35s1134iv732rmeh3tqtt4q6piht9ngl@4ax.com...
>
> We don't have patents on steering wheels or foot throttles.   We don't
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> patents so much either.

    I believe that the original intent of patents was to promote innovation. 
That is, previously, people didn't bother to think up ideas, because it 
wasn't profitable. People could just take the ideas and build their own 
products based on the idea. So patents were created so that the person who 
came up with the idea was allowed a monopoly on the idea for a few years, 
giving him/her a chance to make some profit off of the idea.

    Nowadays, patents are stiffling innovation. People are too afraid to 
create new products 'cause it might infringe on some big company's patent. 
Big companies are not patenting everything because it's too dangerous not 
to. Even altruistic companies need to do this: If I were head of a "good" 
software company, and had the opportunity to patent the concept of using the 
mouse and keyboard to interact with a computer, I definitely would; not 
because I plan then on suing everyone else who infringed on my patent, but 
because I don't want to risk allowing my competitor to get this patent and 
then sue me!

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Remakes of movies

_(reply depth: 64)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2006-01-10T18:51:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dq0vnq01ua7@news4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<9rjqf.16236$kP5.14258@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <84kqf.1196$km.1136@edtnps89> <40ud00F1c6ob8U1@individual.net> <ckBqf.26483$k76.2648@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <84plq1prbt7r1cerdgfuinse4arr83fqpv@4ax.com> <boCqf.169704$Gd6.47105@pd7tw3no> <1135285228.000852.147670@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <CLFqf.333$OU5.206@clgrps13> <1135375616.234551.254840@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <hHvwf.46735$km.40115@edtnps89> <cn35s1134iv732rmeh3tqtt4q6piht9ngl@4ax.com> <lzxwf.80029$6K2.49736@edtnps90>`

```

In article <lzxwf.80029$6K2.49736@edtnps90>, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> writes:
> 
>     I believe that the original intent of patents was to promote innovation. 

The original intent of patents - from "letters patent", ie letters
published to the general public - in this sense (as opposed to
land-grant patents, letters patent conveying a title, and so forth)
was to reward people for service to the crown by giving them an
artificial monopoly.  No innovation was required.  It wasn't long
before governments started issuing letters patent granting monopolies
in order to make risky enterprises, notably overseas trade, economi-
cally viable, and so created the Dutch East India Company and the
like.  Again, the people receiving those patents weren't being
rewarded for being clever - ruthless is more like it.

> That is, previously, people didn't bother to think up ideas, because it 
> wasn't profitable.

This was a joke, right?

> People could just take the ideas and build their own 
> products based on the idea.

Not when artificial-monopoly patents were introduced they couldn't.
This was feudal Europe at the beginning of the Early Modern period;
mercantilism and the entrepreneurial class were just starting to
appear.  Sure, there were coffeeshops (a harbinger of the rise of the
middle class in Europe; see Stallybrass and White's _The Politics and
Poetics of Transgression_) starting to appear in the most cosmopoli-
tan cities, but the idea of commercial competition was still mostly
just a gleam in a clerk's eye.

Very few people were in any position to take an idea and profit from
it, at least by providing goods or services.

> So patents were created so that the person who 
> came up with the idea was allowed a monopoly on the idea for a few years, 
> giving him/her a chance to make some profit off of the idea.

Intellectual-property patents apparently first came on the scene (in
Europe at least) in Venice, but letters patent granting monopolies had
already been around for some time.  In English law, the intellectual-
property patent wasn't the dominant type until James I, and there was
plenty of innovation before then.

> Nowadays, patents are stiffling innovation.

That's a popular opinion, and like most popular opinions, it's a
gross generalization.  There is no clear evidence or compelling
argument to show that innovation would be significantly greater were
patents suddenly overturned or significantly restricted.  While it's
difficult to argue that patent-granting authorities such as the USPTO
don't grant numerous foolish patents (and they're certainly over-
whelmed by volume, political pressure, and lobbying), it's not
immediately clear how to fix that without making the patent-granting
process even more difficult and expensive - which raises the barrier
to entry even higher for individuals and small businesses.

Intellectual property law is a mess.  But it's always been a mess
(when it's existed at all, and only the very naive think things would
be better in a state of nature).  It has of late undergone significant
and useful change, and it remains in flux, so at least there's hope
for progress.  Which is more than you can say of many problems.
```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:42hht9F1ihrl5U1@individual.net...
>
> Interesting, Oliver. So what's next? Space Invaders :-)?
>

    Videogame remakes are popular for hobbyist programmers, because they can 
just concentrate on the programming. The graphics, music, story, etc. can 
all be lifted from the original game.

    A Google search for "space invader remake" (without the quotes) yields 
about 50'000 hits, the first 10 at least are all to sites that talk about a 
space invader remake, possibly offering one for download.

    Going to sourceforge.net and searching for "remake", of the first 25 
results, 24 are remakes of games. One of them is some sort of command like 
interpreter which happens to have the word "remake" show up in its 
description.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-23T10:04:08-06:00

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> James J. Gavan wrote:
>> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> the remake of the remake of King Kong.


I have seen two TV specials on the new KK, in which Peter Jackson comments 
on his motivations. Jackson states that he truly loves the original KK. 
Jackson was so moved by the original movie (which he saw "at the age of 9 or 
10", and says was *the* movie that motivated him to get into films), that I 
believe he sees his remake as a sincere homage using the latest technology. 
This is *The Movie* that Jackson has always wanted to make, and it shows in 
the production.
```

have had the theatrical 'Zummerzet' accent, denoting somebody from the 
countryside.

beaver.

Jimmy

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 60)_

- **From:** "Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-27T06:55:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nz5sf.58419$6e.21688@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<CU4of.8526$kP5.2446@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <1135284512.923026.241450@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <RNEqf.169926$Gd6.142127@pd7tw3no> <1135375441.228103.109920@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <doi41f$l97$1@reader1.panix.com> <1135624508.189226.209600@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1135624508.189226.209600@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com...
>
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Then I would presume that you are not proud to be an American?

I believe the term "I'm proud to be an American" is less about being proud 
of being an american and more proud of the fact that there are laws and 
freedoms that are built to provide shelter for all beliefs and ideas. 
Whilst they are also "born" into this system, the argument is that it takes 
each and every individual to stand up and ensure that this freedom 
continues; therefore it is one's actions that perpetuate freedom.  In the 
same way, I doubt you (and not your dad) have pride in being part of a 
generalized socio-economic group represented by low-skilled, minimum-waged 
labour; however, I'm sure you're proud of the hard work your dad put forth 
to bring you up and take care of you.

I know some people who have parents that are middle class with working class 
children.  So the complaint was silly I suppose. The single biggest crisis 
facing America - IMHO- today is in fact the fact that people do NOT take 
pride in being working class.  There appears to be this belief that no 
matter how little effort is put forth, children of the future can only 
remain or move up the class chain.   Modern children are spoiled into the 
notion that being middle class is a privelege, and not something earned.

So I think it's tremendously important that we continue to spread the idea 
that you should be proud of any work that you do, and anything that you earn 
as a result of that work.

I'm not sure there is a working class anymore anyway - there is rich, poor, 
and doing ok, and the migrant workers that we don't count.

DU
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 60)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2005-12-27T13:57:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dorh7e$pfh$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<CU4of.8526$kP5.2446@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <1135375441.228103.109920@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <doi41f$l97$1@reader1.panix.com> <1135624508.189226.209600@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1135624508.189226.209600@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>,
Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Then I would presume that you are not proud to be an American?

I do my best, Mr Maclean, not to take pride, period.  I am satisfied with 
the services I have been allowed to render to my country and I do my best 
to aspire to fulfilling the princples which - while changing and subject 
to re-evaluation - compose it.  Being a citizen of the United States of 
America is, to me and in my case, an accident of birth... but since such 
an accident happened I'll do what I cn to make the best of it.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-12-27T08:33:22-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<04n2r1570sknuhhlumghvc6bppbja3qme6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<CU4of.8526$kP5.2446@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <1135284512.923026.241450@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <RNEqf.169926$Gd6.142127@pd7tw3no> <1135375441.228103.109920@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <doi41f$l97$1@reader1.panix.com>`

On Sat, 24 Dec 2005 00:16:47 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>>I am working class and proud of it (dad was a private/corporal in 'er
>>Maj's army).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>in accidents of birth.

I agree with you much more than most people, but...

Not many people are taught not to be proud of their family members. We
are condemned if we fail to be proud of our country.

And what I am and what I do is largely from who I was born, and how I
was raised.    

It's odd that we aren't allowed to be proud of being tall, but we are
allowed to be proud of being smart.    I guess smart people make the
rules here.
```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1135284512.923026.241450@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com...
>
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Bollocks!

The working Class extends a long way from East London. It includes the 
Midlands and the North. There are MANY regional dialects which can be 
considered "Working Class".

I was raised by a man born in Katherine Road, Forest Gate. I have also been 
there and lived in London 8 years. I said "East London" (Not "Cockney" or 
"East End") because the accent is an East London accent (it extends out to 
Essex). Even after 20 years in New Zealand, the man I am referring to would 
lapse into the vernacular when annoyed. (As a child, I would sometimes wind 
him up just to hear him... :-))

Pete.
```

- **From:** "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

>

I know Stevenage well, having managed a major project for ICL and caught the 
9:04 to Royston out of Kings Cross every morning. I met my (now) ex-wife on 
that train, and she had "Royston 9:04" inscribed inside her wedding ring. 
(Well, that's her story... I reckon she had a secret boyfriend named 
"Royston" and the numbers were a code for his performance... :-))

 I also have some good friends in Hitching. At the time I was there, the 
Hertfordshire form of North London accent was not overlaid with East London. 
I guess, as you say, the expansion is bringing the dialects with it.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <dnq921$lk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <CU4of.8526$kP5.2446@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <1134677095.550825.225980@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <mkAof.31296$Y72.29808@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <qKCof.2064$ic1.905@edtnps90> <QLVof.203$RZ6.53@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <J0Epf.9251$ic1.4561@edtnps90> <1135121880.926288.249960@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <fpgqf.16192$kP5.15005@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <2c6jq1t6hmul72fgja8ocfbvk85msbq18h@4ax.com> <9rjqf.16236$kP5.14258@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`

```
> I would like to see a reference to that quote.

You probably _won't_ like it.

http://rds.yahoo.com/_ylt=A0LaSuYD0qlD3i0BERhXNyoA;_ylu=X3oDMTBwdXJwbHBnBGNvbG8DdwRsA1dTMQRwb3MDMQRzZWMDc3I-/SIG=12usu0e4a/EXP=1135289219/**http%3a//www.keepersofthefaith.com/BookReviews/BookReviewDisplay.asp%3fkey=4

""" -------------------
Let me begin with the great "Christian" lie about The Chronicles of
Narnia. Over the years we have often received correspondence asking how
we can write a review of C. S. Lewis and be so ignorant as to not know
that "scholars" have said that The Chronicles of Narnia is a
Christian allegory. Well, we do know that "scholars" have said as
much. However, even though we stated it in our original review, we will
state it again here. C. S. Lewis, himself, denied that this was so. Our
question to any such "scholars" is, Why are they promoting this
falsehood? We do notice that this idea emanates generally from
ecumenical sources, and therefore assume that they feel that it helps
their cause of bringing Christians back under the papacy. The following
quotes sum up the feelings of C. S. Lewis about the allegorical status
of the Chronicles of Narnia.

Lewis was a member of a literary group called The Inklings. The
following quote is from The Inklings Handbook, a
historical/biographical work by a couple of fellows enchanted by the
Inklings. "CSL [Lewis], however, argued strongly that the Chronicles
of Narnia should not be viewed as an allegory like Bunyan's
Pilgrim's Progress, though he acknowledged the intentional parallels
between the main features of Christian teaching and the Chronicles, not
least in the unambigously supernatural Christ-symbol, Aslan the
Lion."

Again from The Inklings Handbook-"The earliest hints of Narnia come
very early in CSL's life, long before he became a Christian; the
common assumption that he wrote the whole series as an extended
allegory of the Christian faith, with a strong evangelistic motive, is
one that CSL always denied and which is not borne out by the facts."

So, who are we to believe-scholars who wish to justify their
untenable positions favoring exploration into occultism, or the author
himself? By the way, there is very good reason for Lewis' denial.
That is why our original review speaks to this argument on the basis of
the content in question. We have already examined the passage regarding
the servant of Tash (Satan) being in paradise and its explanation-its
idea being that just because God turns the works of His enemies to His
own advantage, He will also warmly receive those enemies into heaven.
It is quite easy to expose this as neither scriptural nor Christian to
anyone who has ever spent any time in the Bible.
-------------- """
```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>> I would like to see a reference to that quote.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pilgrim's Progress,
================
> though he acknowledged the intentional parallels
> between the main features of Christian teaching and the Chronicles, not
> least in the unambigously supernatural Christ-symbol, Aslan the
> Lion."

================

I think we're talking semantics here. In Pilgrim's Progress there is a very 
close and direct parallel between the experiences of Pilgrim and Christian 
life. I agree with Lews that Narnia is not like that. But the part I 
highlighted above, attributed to Lewis, seems sufficient to establish the 
story as an allegory, as I understand the term. From Google:

Allegory: typically a narrative in prose, verse, or drama that 
self-consciously presents its meaning through concrete symbols.
```

- **References:** `<i21kf.47144$6y4.15970@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <dnq921$lk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <CU4of.8526$kP5.2446@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <1134677095.550825.225980@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <mkAof.31296$Y72.29808@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <qKCof.2064$ic1.905@edtnps90> <QLVof.203$RZ6.53@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <J0Epf.9251$ic1.4561@edtnps90> <1135121880.926288.249960@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <fpgqf.16192$kP5.15005@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <PJpqf.167322$Gd6.88860@pd7tw3no>`

```
 

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:PJpqf.167322$Gd6.88860@pd7tw3no...
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Can you post some references to support this, please, Jimmy?

My understanding is that the Hobbit and LOTR was strongly influenced by 
Tolkien's love of Wagnerian opera (Der Ring des Niebelungen and Das 
Rhinegold in particular), and had nothing to do with Christianity. He and 
Lewis both studied Norse mythology and, although Tolkien disliked Wagner at 
a personal level, he enjoyed the music. (Lewis, before his conversion to 
Roman Catholicism was an even more ardent fan of Norse Mythology than 
Tolkien.) Although Tolkien claimed he had not borrowed from Wagner, it is 
probably truer and fairer to say they both used the same sources.

I know the stories pretty well (Norse myth and Tolkien) and, apart from the 
struggle between good and evil I see few parallels with the Gospels. 
Certainly, Narnia is much closer with Aslan laying down his life for one of 
the children (Edmund, who least deserved to have the sacrifice), but posts 
here have shown that C. S. Lewis did not want his stories viewed as 
Christian allegories.

Here is the reference for the Wagnerian theory, but I knew about it long 
before there was an internet:

http://www.isi.org/lectures/text/pdf/birzer.pdf

In the above it does suggest that Tolkien may have seen "The Return of the 
King" as the establishment of the Church of Rome in the world, but it is 
tenuous and not the same as suggesting the whole trilogy is a Christian 
allegory.

Pete.
```

```

Pete Dashwood wrote:
> My understanding is that the Hobbit and LOTR was strongly influenced by
> Tolkien's love of Wagnerian opera (Der Ring des Niebelungen and Das
> Rhinegold in particular), and had nothing to do with Christianity. He and
> Lewis both studied Norse mythology and, although Tolkien disliked Wagner at
> a personal level, he enjoyed the music.

Beyond the fact that LOTR and Der Ring des Nibelungen share Nordic
mythology, having sat through all 9 hours of DRDN (more than once!) I
see little in common between them.
Rather, I got the impression that LOTR borrowed heavily from
Anglo-Saxon and Nordic mythologies (e.g. Beowulf and the sagas). I did
wonder if Tolkein was pointing an accusing finger at mass production
and business with his descriptions of Morder and hankering for a rustic
middle ages golden age.


> I know the stories pretty well (Norse myth and Tolkien) and, apart from the
> struggle between good and evil I see few parallels with the Gospels.

The only thing that I can say is that the depiction of Gollum has been
said to be an anti-semitic depiction of the archetypal Jew.

> Certainly, Narnia is much closer with Aslan laying down his life for one of
> the children (Edmund, who least deserved to have the sacrifice),

Which is probably why Aslan laid down his life. However, it is easier
to die if you know that you are to be born again.
```

```

"Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:me6qf.17725$md.14456@tornado.tampabay.rr.com...
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
> news:J0Epf.9251$ic1.4561@edtnps90...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> worthwhile, forms of entertainment....

    Not sure I understood what you meant, but it sounds like you're saying 
this campaign against "indecent TV programming" might be a good thing.

    I'll be honest here. I like watching pornography. I know lots of people 
who also like looking at pornography. Not that it should matter, but some of 
those people happen to be female. Da Vinci... well, some of his work is 
interesting, but sometimes I'm not quite in the mood to look at diagrams of 
early helicopters, etc. I've read some George Orwell and Kurt Vonnegut (I 
don't like the works of the latter much) too, and they're fine pieces of 
text, but sometimes I am feeling horny, and I would like to satiate myself 
by reading erotic (or pornographic) texts as well. I don't think this makes 
me a "bad" person, at least from a moral standpoint. Sex between two (or 
more) consenting adults is an enjoyable experience for everyone involved, 
and causes harm to no one. So what's the big deal?

    Certain people don't like pornography. Fine. They don't have to read it. 
Maybe I don't enjoy reading the Bible. Rather than going on a campaign 
trying to ban the sales of bibles to minors (or banning bibles outright), I 
simply don't read them. Isn't that a much more reasonable solution? Maybe I 
didn't like Kurt Vonnegut's books. I'm not petitioning to ban those either.

    I have no problem with "decent" TV programming. If the Christians (or 
anyone else) wants to write letters to cable networks saying "Please include 
more shows which don't contain smut, violence, etc.", that's fine by me. 
What I don't like is people writing letters saying "Please *REMOVE* the 
shows which DO contain smut, violence, etc." Why can't mature adults just 
watch whatever they want to watch?

    Why are parents so concerned with hiding the secret of sex from their 
children? I'm pretty sure most parents have had sex themselves at one point 
or another. I can understand that you don't want your children to become 
sexist, for example. So you can teach your child to respect both men and 
women, and not to treat members of either gender as objects. That does not 
nescessarily mean pretending that there is no such thing as sex, or that 
pretending sex is a shameful act. I'm not saying you should show pornograph 
to your children, I'm just asking why is there this conspiracy to hide sex 
from them?

    - Oliver
```

```
"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
>
>    Why are parents so concerned with hiding the secret of sex from their 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> to hide sex from them?


None of the Christians I know are trying to 'hide' sex from their children. 
I not only discussed these issues with my children, I gave them educational 
materials on the subject, all as soon as I thought they were mature enough 
to process it. They were also encouraged to ask questions, which were 
honestly answered. No 'storks' at our house. :-)

The Christian objections to pornography come from a different direction. The 
Bible indicates that God judges cultures as well as individuals. Sodom and 
Gomorra in particular were destroyed because sexual sins pervaded their 
culture. Therefore, the issue to Christians isn't simply one of individual 
judgment on the ones who do such things, but on the culture that permits, 
even encourages such things. The Bible does not sanction any kind of sexual 
activity outside heterosexual marriage.  Even "looking on a woman to lust 
after her" is said to be a sin. Pornography would seem to fit this 
description. However, the Bible does seem to give carte blanc to "the 
marriage bed".
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:tPgqf.16199$kP5.10376@bignews5.bellsouth.net...

> The Christian objections to pornography come from a different direction.
> The Bible indicates that God judges cultures as well as individuals. Sodom
> and Gomorra in particular were destroyed because sexual sins pervaded
> their culture.

Some would also say that such wholesale cultural destruction and
condemnation was characteristic of the Old Testament, but the need for
(and the possibility of) such things as Noah's Floode and the
fire-and-brimstone associated with Sodom and Gomorrha (as well as Lot's
wife being turned into a pillar of salt) was obviated by the Work of
the Cross.  Why is that *not* the case, either in the particular
context of sexual sins or otherwise?  Seems to me judgment is
*individual* these days, not *cultural*.

> Therefore, the issue to Christians isn't simply one of individual judgment
> on the ones who do such things, but on the culture that permits, even
> encourages such things.

So a good, observant Christian can be condemned to hell for eternity
for the horrendous abomination before God of being an American (or,
perhaps, all Americans deserve Special Berating just for being
Americans, just as Paul seems to have felt about residents of Crete --
see Titus 1:12ff)?

Seems to me 1 John 2:15-16 speaks to the issue of not revering the
world (however one defines it!) is, but doesn't require the individual
to be responsible for stamping out the transgressions of others!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **References:** `<WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <dnq921$lk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <CU4of.8526$kP5.2446@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <1134677095.550825.225980@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <mkAof.31296$Y72.29808@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <qKCof.2064$ic1.905@edtnps90> <QLVof.203$RZ6.53@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <J0Epf.9251$ic1.4561@edtnps90> <me6qf.17725$md.14456@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <9Dfqf.167$km.64@edtnps89> <1135287127.025384.321040@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1135287127.025384.321040@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> I have no problem with pornography but I don't want to see it on TV.

    I believe nowadays, before presenting content as indecent as "PG-13", 
networks will show a screen indicating the rating that the content received, 
and a voice will say the rating as well.

    If you are using digital TV, the content contains, in addition to video 
and audio, meta data, so if you accidentally stumble onto a channel, you can 
press a button to see its rating, and a brief summary of what the show/movie 
is about (and other info like title, year of production, actors/actresses 
involved, etc.).

> To
> give an example, there is a scene in Basic Instinct where Michael
> Douglas has sex with Sharon Stone. It goes on too long and is too
> detailed to just be something that adds to the film. Most of it could
> have been cut without detriment to the film or the story line.

    Yes, but it was the movie producer's right to put whatever he/she/they 
wanted into the film. If they wanted, they could have suddenly switched the 
film into an animation (instead of live actors), or played video clips 
backwards. Maybe some people wouldn't enjoy a film like that. Well, nobody 
is forcing them to watch it.

    I do not find pleasure in being scared (I know some people do), so I 
don't ride on roller coasters and I don't watch horror movies. Recently 
(last year or so), I've noticed a few movie trailers which pretend to be 
non-scary, and then startle the audience with some scary bit. That upset me. 
Previously, movie trailers for horror films were obviously trailers for 
horror films (e.g. you'd see a dark mansion, and hear footsteps or 
something). The new stuff might play happy, romantic music, involve a couple 
hugging and kissing in a well lit room, and then suddenly, in the next 
frame, the girl's face is replace with the a disfigured one, and a scream is 
heard.

    I can sympathize with people who don't want to see sex, and are startle 
by a sudden sex scene. People should have a right to know what they are 
watching. For people who do not like "indecent" content, that's what the 
ratings are for.

    But again, just because *I* don't like horror movies, doesn't mean I 
write letters to the network demanding that the remove all horror movies 
from their programming, or write letters to congress demanding that horror 
movies be banned. I understand that some people find pleasure in being 
scared. I would not deny them that pleasure.

    - Oliver
```

- **References:** `<y6Yjf.7656$wf2.836017@news20.bellglobal.com> <1133550230.780919.303800@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <i21kf.47144$6y4.15970@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqd1b$259m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <dnq921$lk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <CU4of.8526$kP5.2446@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <1134677095.550825.225980@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <mkAof.31296$Y72.29808@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <qKCof.2064$ic1.905@edtnps90> <QLVof.203$RZ6.53@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:QLVof.203$RZ6.53@bignews7.bellsouth.net...

> The issue is too vital to simply take someone elses word about what the 
> Bible says. :-)

Saying "The Bible clearly and definitely states ..." is a FAR CRY from "What 
I read in the Bible leads me to believe ... ".  It is precisely the 
absoluteness of the first that speaks of arrogance about one's opinion, 
because it elevates it to the level beyond confidence to *incontrovertible* 
certainty.

Behavior reflecting humility I find more convincing of a Christian life than 
behavior reflecting hubris.

> All that any of us say here simply reflects (hopefully) the best that we 
> know. None of us have perfect knowledge, and none of us are without flaw.

I agree.  If you believe that, then what you write ought to reflect it 
consistently.  It hasn't, at least to me.

> From direct and personal experience, I can assure you, this is quite true 
> with respect to myself. :-)

Show me, don't tell me!

> However, that being said, when someone has devoted a lot of effort and 
> time into studying an issue, they have a right to speak with confidence 
> about the accuracy of what they say. Don't all of use do that on a fairly 
> regular basis? I know Chuck does. :-)

I speak with confidence on issues with respect to the COBOL standard, at 
least in part because I have played an active role in its development over 
the last five years, and have been actively involved in ensuring that three 
different COBOL implementations were in conformance with their respective 
standards for over twenty years.  I also have access to the standards 
themselves and am reasonably facile in ferretting out answers to questions 
of what the standard states and what it does not.  But when I speak clearly 
on behalf of the standard it is because the standard, having been written in 
English in the first place, is incontrovertibly explicit on the particular 
subject.  E.g., it's not a leap to describe the behavior of MOVE statements 
in which the operands overlap as undefined in ANSI-85 implementations given 
ANSI X3.23-1985 page VI-69, 6.4.5, Overlapping Operands:  "When a sending 
and a receiving item in any statement share a part or all of their storage 
areas, yet are not defined by the same data description entry, the result of 
the execution of such a statement is undefined."   I don't think there's 
much wiggle room on that one.  If an implementor defines the behavior, well, 
that's up to the implementor, but there's no guarantee that any other 
implementation will be the same.   In a few cases in '02 COBOL and more in 
the upcoming draft, I've actually written some of the features specified in 
the standard.

I speak with authority on the Unisys MCP COBOL implementations because I'm 
looking at the Original Text and can tell (in most instances) *exactly* what 
they do.  I also have full access to the change history over the last twenty 
years for these products and am therefore able to track exactly what each 
*used* to do.   And in the particular case of the COBOL74 compiler in that 
environment, I've personally revised around a third of the compiler either 
to improve its maintainability and eliminate ambiguities at the source 
level, or (in the most obvious example of MOVE statements involving group 
items and the PICTURE processor) rewritten all of the logic associated with 
the feature.  I have confidence in my understanding of the rules for PICTURE 
because I've written *three different* parsers for the PICTURE 
character-string in my time, each time with careful study of the standard, 
and each time painfully aware of the pitfalls therein.

Put a different way, I speak from authority on the subject of Unisys MCP 
COBOL precisely because I am an architect for the products, and it's my job 
to define as well as state what they do.

I speak from a position of authority on the subject of *standard* COBOL 
based on two decades' experience and involvement in defending and/or 
correcting the behavior of the Unisys MCP compilers relative to their 
respective standards for over two decades, which means that I have 
considerable experience finding my way around the documents.

I also speak from a position of authority as that I have been serving as the 
Unisys representative on INCITS/J4, the committee that writes, modifies and 
amends the COBOL standard for the last five years, and as part of those 
duties, served as a part of the US delegation to ISO/IEC JTC1/SC22/WG4, the 
international committee chartered with the overall direction and technical 
content of the COBOL standard.   I've also been appointed convener (chair) 
of the latter committee for a three-year term, which gives my opinions 
*some* credibility as to what conforms to the standard and what does not!

Despite all that, if you notice, my opinions usually begin with the likes of 
"My reading of <relevant standard>, in particular <page-chapter-and-verse 
citation>, leads me to believe ... that " and similar.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> "What I read in the Bible leads me to believe ... ".

Yes it is. In order to say it honestly you have to be that convinced. Does 
it offend you that I am that convinced? You may be completely happy 
believing that everything is relative, but there are absolutes.

> Behavior reflecting humility I find more convincing of a Christian life 
> than behavior reflecting hubris.

Ha! You find *nothing* convincing of a Christian life, as you have 
repeatedly pointed out in great detail. You simply want Christians to be as 
uncertain as yourself. Sorry. :-)  The fact is, when a person is willing to 
submit their will, their very life, to God, that is a very clear indication 
of humility, at least in that respect. Being certain about God has no 
relationship whatever to hubris. But taking upon yourself the decision to 
reject your Creator from your life, *that* is hubris. :-)

>> All that any of us say here simply reflects (hopefully) the best that we 
>> know. None of us have perfect knowledge, and none of us are without flaw.
>
> I agree.  If you believe that, then what you write ought to reflect it 
> consistently.  It hasn't, at least to me.

Sorry, I can't help that.

>> From direct and personal experience, I can assure you, this is quite true 
>> with respect to myself. :-)
>
> Show me, don't tell me!

Sorry. If you don't believe the evidence Creation, if you don't believe the 
evidence of the Bible, and you don't believe the witness of millions who 
have met Jesus, then there's nothing I can do for you, except pray for you. 
I've been doing that. :-)

>> However, that being said, when someone has devoted a lot of effort and 
>> time into studying an issue, they have a right to speak with confidence 
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
> similar.

I suggest that the question of where your soul will spend eternity is one in 
which you should have more certainty than you express by that language. 
Would you think it preferable if I were to be a bit more uncertain about my 
own destiny? If I expressed lack of certainty in the things in which I place 
my faith, you would simply criticize that as lack of faith in what I 
believe. You have constructed, by your choice of attitude, a situation in 
which you would feel justified in attacking my position either way. Sorry. 
:-)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<do9v3s$a50$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<y6Yjf.7656$wf2.836017@news20.bellglobal.com> <dmqd1b$259m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <dnq921$lk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <CU4of.8526$kP5.2446@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <1134677095.550825.225980@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <mkAof.31296$Y72.29808@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <qKCof.2064$ic1.905@edtnps90> <QLVof.203$RZ6.53@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <do75i9$1m52$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <IWZpf.16589$k76.7165@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:IWZpf.16589$k76.7165@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
> Yes it is. In order to say it honestly you have to be that convinced. Does 
> it offend you that I am that convinced? You may be completely happy 
> believing that everything is relative, but there are absolutes.

It offends me that your conviction about your opinions leads you to 
categorize the views of those who disagree with you, not as differences of 
opinion, but as fundamental errors in fact.  What emphasis to place on which 
meaning of what words in which passages is a matter of *opinion* and of 
*choice*, not of *fact*.

>> Behavior reflecting humility I find more convincing of a Christian life 
>> than behavior reflecting hubris.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> as uncertain as yourself. Sorry. :-)

No, I find a *great deal* convincing of a Christian life.

I don't happen to believe that if someone publically states that anyone who 
doesn't agree with your conclusions about "faith alone" (or "grace alone" or 
"scripture alone", for that matter), as you deem them clearly and explicitly 
declared in scripture in so many words is therefore deprived of the right to 
consider himself a "true Christian" and is therefore condemned forever, such 
a person is clearly demonstrating a "Christian life" in those actions!

What gives you the right to describe me as "uncertain"?  Have I *ever* 
indicated agnosticism, or anything other than a firm acceptance of Jesus as 
Messiah/Christos, as Son of God, as part of the traditional Trinity, as 
Savior?   Why is it that my disagreements with you over what the Bible 
actually *says* directly lead you to the presumption that I must therefore 
be "uncertain"?

> The fact is, when a person is willing to submit their will, their very 
> life, to God, that is a very clear indication of humility, at least in 
> that respect.

It may demonstrate humility to *oneself*, and it might even be taken as 
humility *to God*.  It is not clear that "You're wrong and I'm RIGHT, you're 
condemned and I'm SAVED!" is a demonstration *to anyone else* of that 
humility.

Take your own inventory before taking mine.   *Verify* that there are no 
rafters sticking out of *your* eyes before worrying abut the splinters in my 
eyes!

> Being certain about God has no relationship whatever to hubris.

Who said I was uncertain about God?  Have you decided that despite my 
protestations to the contrary I'm one of the self-proclaimed atheists or 
agnostics (or deists or theists) who have posted to this group?  I don't 
think *I've* included myself among them!

> But taking upon yourself the decision to reject your Creator from your 
> life, *that* is hubris. :-)

OK, Mr. McClendon.  You've made this assertion about me and my life.  Please 
point out to me, and to everyone else, the message in which I have written 
such a thing.  Please point out to me, and to everyone else, where I have 
*done* such a thing.

I deny that I have made such a decision, I deny that I make such a decision 
today, I deny that I have described such a decision anywhere in this forum.

For you to state or intimate in a public forum that I have rejected my 
Creator (to say nothing of Christianity as *I* understand it) from my life 
qualifies as libel, slander, and calumny.

And I contend libel, slander and calumny do not represent behavior patterns 
one would reasonably accept as admirable Christian behavior.   I believe a 
Christian would take the time to verify any such characterization before 
making it, and therefore a person who claimed to be Christian and 
demonstrated such behavior would be behaving in an *unChristian* manner.

I have studied the same Scriptures you have studied.  I have come to 
different conclusions, based on that careful study (and specifically 
including the languages in which those Scriptures were originally written) 
over decades, from those to which you seem to have come.  That I disagree 
with your opinions on certain matters of what the text actually says does 
not mean that I have rejected my Creator, or my Savior.   *Or yours*, if you 
insist that they differ (which I don't!).

>> All that any of us say here simply reflects (hopefully) the best that we
>>> know. None of us have perfect knowledge, and none of us are without 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sorry, I can't help that.

Why not?   If your behavior reflects badly, why is it inappropriate for you 
to change it?  You're the one that declared Catholicism a Christian belief 
*solely* on the basis of "sola fide", which tenet they explicitly and 
vehemently reject!

> Sorry. If you don't believe the evidence Creation, if you don't believe 
> the evidence of the Bible, and you don't believe the witness of millions 
> who have met Jesus, then there's nothing I can do for you, except pray for 
> you. I've been doing that. :-)

Why would you suggest that belief in Jesus as Messiah and as Savior 
*requires* belief, for example, that the universe was created -- more or 
less -- on the nightfall before Sunday, October 23, 4004BC?   I *personally* 
don't share the opinion that that's a particularly vital part of what being 
a Christian is about, particularly since I don't think the original *Hebrew* 
text requires that interpretation!

I don't mind people praying for me; in fact, I encourage that.  But I would 
seek to discourage you from praying that I be raised to the high level of 
understanding that you, praise God! have managed to reach.

On a more-than-daily basis, I pray to turn both my will and my life over to 
the care of God (and not take it back).  On a more-than-daily basis, I ask 
God to remove anything that stands in the way of my ability to serve God and 
my fellow man.  On a more-than-daily basis, I ask God to help me improve my 
conscious contact with Him, to improve my knowledge of His will for me and 
to give me the power and the willingness to carry that out.  I ask these 
things for myself, many times a day, and I have no objection to your praying 
for these things for me.    I do object to any hint of a prayer that I would 
be elevated to your lofty level of spiritual enlightenment.

> I suggest that the question of where your soul will spend eternity is one 
> in which you should have more certainty than you express by that language.

I flatly refuse to place myself in a position of stating anything along the 
lines of "I'm going to Heaven -- and YOU'RE NOT!!!! Nyah!  Nyah! Nyah!". 
All I can do is the best that I can do.

If I am *certain* about where I will spend eternity, what I do here, how I 
behave toward my fellow man, is immaterial, and I therefore have *license* 
to commit any sin.  I do *not agree* with that premise.  I also *do not* 
agree with the premise that, by virtue of a person's proclamation of having 
been SAVED (or BORN AGAIN) their behavior is forevermore EXEMPLARY of 
Christian behavior.   That ain't so.  And I contend that such behavior 
matters *in this life and in the next*.  I've already cited C&V on the 
subject.

My life will be an open book at the Judgment Bar.  I have strengths and I 
have weaknesses, I have accomplishments and I have failures.  I believe in 
Jesus' salvation, and in Jesus' intercession.  What happens at the Judgment 
Bar with respect to that record is up to God.   I'm not willing to say that 
I'm *guaranteed* a place in Heaven, nor do I agree that *anyone* can, or 
should, say that about himself.

> Would you think it preferable if I were to be a bit more uncertain about 
> my own destiny?

So, we're back to "I'm going to Heaven --- and YOU'RE NOT!  Nyah!  Nyah! 
Nyah!"?

> If I expressed lack of certainty in the things in which I place my faith, 
> you would simply criticize that as lack of faith in what I believe.

Would I, now?

> You have constructed, by your choice of attitude, a situation in which you 
> would feel justified in attacking my position either way. Sorry.

Nope.  I don't describe as *wrong* the position that e.g. the universe was 
created on the evening before October 23, 4004BC.  I state that my reading 
of the Hebrew text of the Creation story does not convince me that that's 
the only possible interpretation of the text.  That's but one example among 
many (including "born again" for which I prefer "begotten from above").

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 51)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-12-21T02:07:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

charles hottel wrote:
> Chuck,
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> very well.

I didn't comment earlier Charles, but I liked your response also, even 
if it did take you a week to type it. We are both very much on the same 
wavelength about animals. My beloved Emma, a border collie - seems such 
a short while ago since I could hold the little black and white ball in 
the palms of my hands. Now she's thirteen, losing hearing and sight - it 
is only a matter of time and I will be heartbroken.

Sadly, I think Catholic doctrine is that animals don't have 
souls/spirits - so there's no way presumably they are going up 'There'. 
Funny though that we have a Saint Francis of Assissi, patron saint of 
animals. Honestly, if I can't meet my beloved friends again, I'd rather 
not go to that place.

Jimmy
```

"charles hottel" <jghottel@yahoo.com> wrote:
> What about the uncounted numbers of people that have lived and died 
> without ever hearing of Jesus Christ or of even any idea of a messiah that 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> of their own?


Good question. The short answer is, they're probably lost. It is one of the 
terrible consequences of man's decision to sin against God. Here are some 
Scriptures:

Jesus said to him, "I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to 
the Father except through Me." (John 14:6)

And He said to them, "Go into all the world and preach the gospel to every 
creature. He who believes and is baptized will be saved; but he who does not 
believe will be condemned." (Mark 16:15,16)

However, there other Scriptures (I'm tired and won't look up all the 
references now, but I will later if you want) that indicate that God judges 
people based on what they know of His law, and on their heart. The Bible 
also declares that God is good and just and full of mercy. Exactly how all 
this will play out remains to be seen.

But this is why Christians want to share the Gospel (Good News) of Jesus, to 
save as many as possible.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-16T09:35:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<cvkuo154sjfungkb2jgjmq2q28nl4tu9aj@4ax.com> <TFXjf.47085$6y4.8198@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <y6Yjf.7656$wf2.836017@news20.bellglobal.com> <FoZjf.47094$6y4.38133@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <1133550230.780919.303800@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <i21kf.47144$6y4.15970@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqd1b$259m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12>`

```
"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "charles hottel" <jghottel@yahoo.com> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> loving omniscient God to be fair.

I've dealt with this before, in other posts. God is kind and loving. But He 
is also holy and just. God has given the evidence of His creation, the 
evidence of the Bible, the evidence of the testimony of Christians, and has 
given His Only Son to die in payment for the sins of a rebellious and sinful 
people. Those who choose to ignore all this are free to do so. But the Bible 
says "It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God." 
(Hebrews 10:31) Jesus Himself said "And do not fear those who kill the body 
but cannot kill the soul. But rather fear Him who is able to destroy both 
soul and body in hell." (Matthew 10:28) God loves us and Gave His Son so 
that we might be saved. We reject that Supreme Sacrifice to our extreme 
peril.

>> However, there other Scriptures (I'm tired and won't look up all the 
>> references now, but I will later if you want) that indicate that God 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> religious advocates over another.

There is a certain validity to your point. When someone is exposed to the 
Gospel (Good News) of Jesus *and rejects it*, that rejected knowledge 
becomes a condemnation. Also, imagine spending an eternity of suffering with 
the memory of being told how to avoid it, and not believing. God has not 
told us exactly what the judgment will be of those who have never heard the 
Gospel. Apparently the severity of punishment will not be the same for 
everyone, for Jesus said "And that servant who knew his master's will, and 
did not prepare himself or do according to his will, shall be beaten with 
many stripes. But he who did not know, yet committed things deserving of 
stripes, shall be beaten with few." (Luke 12:47,48)  But the Bible declares 
"for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23). No 
one can stand before God sinless, unless their sins have been forgiven. So 
everyone who does not accept the forgiveness provided by God's grace in 
Jesus, will receive punishment for their sins. We do not know when we will 
die and face God, and many people die every minute. So there really is an 
urgency about all this, spreading the Gospel, and receiving it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2005-12-16T15:43:14+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<dnunai$9je$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<cvkuo154sjfungkb2jgjmq2q28nl4tu9aj@4ax.com> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net>,
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

[snip]

>But the Bible 
>says "It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God." 
>(Hebrews 10:31)

How interesting... in common use 'to place in God's hands' is often a term 
of resignation ('There's nothing more the doctors can do') or finality 
('She got hit by a bus and died, her soul's in God's hands now').  While 
it can be argued that such are 'fearful' situations I don't see the term 
being applied as one of fear, in the fashion of '... and then hung by the 
neck until you are dead, may God have mercy on your soul'.

DD
```

If you are an innocent victim, you seek the law for protection. If you are a 
criminal, you should fear the law, because it will hold you accountable. 
Same principle here. In this instance, Jesus is speaking of God in the 
latter sense.

is to be feared.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 48)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-17T09:49:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EpWof.206$RZ6.205@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<cvkuo154sjfungkb2jgjmq2q28nl4tu9aj@4ax.com> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnunai$9je$1@reader1.panix.com> <gsBof.31781$Y72.25759@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnuq41$833$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> is to be feared.


Vernacular aside, consider this: A policeman is running after a serial 
killer of young children, whom the policeman has been tracking, and just 
witnessed killing his latest victim. Without losing sight of the fleeing 
killer, the policeman sees the killer snatch a young child from the street 
and draw back with a knife in preparation to stab the child, as is the 
killer's modus operandi. Unable to reach the killer in time to save the 
girl, the policeman, a superb marksman, aims his drawn gun from 40 feet and 
shoots the killer in the head, instantly killing him before he can stab the 
child. I contend that this policeman has done an act of kindness, and 
charity, by protecting the innocent child from a known killer. If justice is 
done, this policeman will likely be awarded a medal and receive public 
acclaim. The kindest and most charitable policeman in the world would 
probably do exactly the same thing, in the exact same situation. This isn't 
an analogy, it is an illustration of this principle: bringing deserved 
judgment and destruction to the guilty does not imply a lack of kindness and 
charity.

There is nothing inconsistent about a kind and charitable God bringing 
judgment against guilty sinners who reject His offer of a free pardon, 
purchased with the death of His only Son. The sacrifice and offer of that 
free pardon is more than sufficient evidence of the kindness, charity, mercy 
and grace of God.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2005-12-18T01:49:11+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<do2f6n$579$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
In article <EpWof.206$RZ6.205@bignews7.bellsouth.net>,
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
>> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>witnessed killing his latest victim.

Consider this, Mr McClendon... a policeman is a human being, by definition 
irrational to an omniscient, omnipresent, omnipotent and omnibeneficent 
deity.  That might be remembered when one attempts to create an analogy in 
which such a deity is involved.

In an earlier posting I mentioned a hymn I was shown based on Maimonides' 
thirteen principles of faith... I ran across another quote of his in which 
he was addressing the concept of a paradise after death but it seems to 
apply to anything pertaining to an omni-stuff deity as mentioned above: 
'To believe so is to be a schoolboy who expects nuts and sweetmeats as 
compensation for his studies.  Celestial pleasures can neither be measured 
nor comprehended by a mortal being, any more than a blind man can 
distinguish colors or the deaf appreciate music.'

(from http://www.jewishencyclopedia.com/view.jsp?artid=65&letter=P)

DD
```

- **References:** `<cvkuo154sjfungkb2jgjmq2q28nl4tu9aj@4ax.com> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnunai$9je$1@reader1.panix.com> <gsBof.31781$Y72.25759@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnuq41$833$1@reader1.panix.com> <EpWof.206$RZ6.205@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Sat, 17 Dec 2005 09:49:10 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>Vernacular aside, consider this: A policeman is running after a serial 
>killer of young children, whom the policeman has been tracking, and just 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>charity.

But that policeman doesn't take that killer and turn him over to a
sadistic torturer and let him at him for ever and ever.   What good
would that do?    Eternal torture doesn't have a good side.  It
doesn't have a neutral side.  It only has an incredibly evil side.

But God doesn't stop the girl from being killed.   He doesn't even
appear to be concerned with it.   His concern appears to be whether we
believe in him.   If the killer said "yes, believe you're a real cop",
before killing the girl, he could have saved himself from being
punished.   Even if the cop was wearing a clown outfit.

>There is nothing inconsistent about a kind and charitable God bringing 
>judgment against guilty sinners who reject His offer of a free pardon, 
>purchased with the death of His only Son. The sacrifice and offer of that 
>free pardon is more than sufficient evidence of the kindness, charity, mercy 
>and grace of God.

What would you think if you believed your daughter was an unbeliever.
That it was OK for her to be tortured forever and ever because of
this?    That her torture was because God is just in His great love
for her?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 50)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-19T14:19:05-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zyEpf.6629$k76.2723@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<cvkuo154sjfungkb2jgjmq2q28nl4tu9aj@4ax.com> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnunai$9je$1@reader1.panix.com> <gsBof.31781$Y72.25759@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnuq41$833$1@reader1.panix.com> <EpWof.206$RZ6.205@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <m4jdq15j704dr0d5nv5vjqo3hqei5r9cu3@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> doesn't have a neutral side.  It only has an incredibly evil side.

Howard, I said that was not "an analogy" but "an illustration", that being 
kind and merciful does not necessarily imply refusing to bring just 
judgment, even harsh judgment.

>>There is nothing inconsistent about a kind and charitable God bringing
>>judgment against guilty sinners who reject His offer of a free pardon,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> for her?

We can't be absolutely certain whether another person is saved or not, as we 
have discussed. But I do have loved ones, both living and dead, that I am 
very concerned about. I'm concerned about you, believe it or not. But I do 
not blame God. I believe that, since God created us, and we rebelled against 
His authority, He could have done anything He wanted with us and been 
justified. Instead, he has gone to great effort and personal sacrifice to 
help us avoid His judgment. If you were in God's situation, your viewpoint 
would be different. Your problem with this Howard, is that you simply do not 
want to acknowledge any debt to your Creator, and you resent the suggestion 
of such debt. Like the majority of humanity, you want to have your own way 
and not submit your will to God. You have that freedom, because God gave it 
to you, as He gave you life, and moment by moment gives you every breath you 
breathe. God could snap you out of here and throw you into the Lake of Fire 
before you could blink. If He simply wanted to torture us, what would stand 
in His way? He could do it right now, or done it the instant Adam and Eve 
(or we) disobeyed Him. God did not have to give us a way to escape His 
terrible judgment, but He did. He has given His Son to pay for our sins, and 
He patiently waits for all non-believers to follow Him, as we were created 
by God to do. Don't blame God if you sin against Him (as we all have), then 
reject His unbelievably merciful and gracious offer, bought with the 
sacrifice of His sinless Son, and thereby, through your very own choosing, 
come to face that grim and terrible judgment. At his very moment, in 
obedience to Jesus' command, I am trying to keep you from that very 
judgment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 51)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-20T18:05:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YJXpf.13489$wg4.7496@edtnps84>`
- **References:** `<cvkuo154sjfungkb2jgjmq2q28nl4tu9aj@4ax.com> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnunai$9je$1@reader1.panix.com> <gsBof.31781$Y72.25759@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnuq41$833$1@reader1.panix.com> <EpWof.206$RZ6.205@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <m4jdq15j704dr0d5nv5vjqo3hqei5r9cu3@4ax.com> <zyEpf.6629$k76.2723@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:zyEpf.6629$k76.2723@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
>
> I believe that, since God created us, and we rebelled against His 
> authority, He could have done anything He wanted with us and been 
> justified.

    Even if we assume that the events described in the Bible actually did 
literally occur (e.g. that there really was an Adam and Eve and that they 
really ate the apple, etc.), it still does not justify God punishing ALL 
humans for a "sin" performed by two specific humans. To me, it is the same 
error as dimissing all christians as illogical lunatics based an encounter 
with two specific christians who happened to be illogical lunatics.

> Instead, he has gone to great effort and personal sacrifice to help us 
> avoid His judgment.

    The way you phrased it, you make it sound like he has gone to great 
effort and personal sacrifice. Of course, he hasn't. He's God. Everything he 
does is effortless.

    Imagine I am in a room with two buttons; a red one and a green one. If I 
press the red button, 99% of the human populace will suffer unimaginable 
torture for all humanity, and the remaining 1% will be unaffected. If I 
press the green button, 99% of the human populace will lead extremely 
satisfying and joy-filled lives, and the remaining 1% will be unaffected. 
Furthermore, I am guaranteed to be unaffected; i.e. that I will be part of 
the 1% of the population, and I will never encounter anyone who is a member 
of the 99% of the affected population, and I am forced to press exactly one 
button. Just for the heck of it, I press the red button. Why red? I don't 
know. I just chose it arbitrarily; it would have been just as easy to press 
the green button instead.

    Most people would consider that action to be cruel; I had the 
opportunity to help out a lot of people at no sacrifice or effort on my 
behalf, but instead I chose to deliver pain to them.

    What God is doing, according to your belief, is no different. He could 
have just as easily made life easy for all humans, but instead he chose to 
make it painful for them.

> If you were in God's situation, your viewpoint would be different. Your 
> problem with this Howard, is that you simply do not want to acknowledge 
> any debt to your Creator, and you resent the suggestion of such debt.

    This is a "forced" debt. If the creator had asked Howard if he was 
willing to live under God's rule in exchange for coming into existence, 
perhaps Howard would not have accepted the terms of this agreement. But He 
didn't ask. He just created Howard, and now Howard is under the Creator's 
debt. It's like the Mafia claiming that you are under their debt, because 
they are offering "protection" to you. You have no choice in the matter; you 
did not agree to anything, but now you are indebted to someone.

> Like the majority of humanity, you want to have your own way and not 
> submit your will to God. You have that freedom, because God gave it to 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> His terrible judgment, but He did.

    (1) Person A could have done terrible act number 1 (e.g. murder 100 
newborn babies).
    (2) Instead, Person A did slightly less terrible act number 2 (e.g. 
murder 20 newborn babies).
    (3) Therefore, Person A is a good person.

    This is, of course, fallacious reasoning.

> He has given His Son to pay for our sins, and He patiently waits for all 
> non-believers to follow Him, as we were created by God to do. Don't blame 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> am trying to keep you from that very judgment.

    When you say "Don't blame God if you sin against Him (as we all have)", 
I'm not sure what you are referring to. If you are saying that we've all 
sinned against him because we are born with that link to the "First Sin", 
then yes, I do blame God. God, if he exists, has control over the entire 
universe. For whatever reasons, he chose to arrange destiny in such a way so 
that I was born with this link to the first sin. Therefore, the link to that 
sin is God's doing, not mine. I could have been born on another planet 
somewhere else in the universe, where there was no Adam and Eve and there 
was no original sin. But instead, he chose to tie me to that sin, and force 
this debt upon me. Not fair.

    If instead you meant "We're all human, so there is a very high 
probability that at some point in your life, you've commited at least one 
sin", then is it not conceivable that there could exists someone who 
coincidentally happened not to commit any Christian sin, and yet chose not 
believe in the Christian God? I'm assuming here that not believing in God is 
not considered a sin, otherwise the posibility is zero by definition.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 52)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-12-21T01:04:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CS1qf.160080$Gd6.96643@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<YJXpf.13489$wg4.7496@edtnps84>`
- **References:** `<cvkuo154sjfungkb2jgjmq2q28nl4tu9aj@4ax.com> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnunai$9je$1@reader1.panix.com> <gsBof.31781$Y72.25759@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnuq41$833$1@reader1.panix.com> <EpWof.206$RZ6.205@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <m4jdq15j704dr0d5nv5vjqo3hqei5r9cu3@4ax.com> <zyEpf.6629$k76.2723@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <YJXpf.13489$wg4.7496@edtnps84>`

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
> 
>     If instead you meant "We're all human, so there is a very high 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> not considered a sin, otherwise the posibility is zero by definition.

Well I don't know whether or not it is part of Judson's dogma, but we 
Catholics as part of our Seven Sacraments have Confession. Go checking 
that CE if you want specifics. Even given you have any form of 'sin' a 
simple, "Oh my God I'm sorry for ....", suffices - providing you mean 
it. Obviously if we believe in an Omnipotent God then he already *knows* 
whether or not we mean it.

Two extreme examples :

Josef Stalin - with his horrendous record of killings, died a natural 
death. If just for the merest point in time before his final breath he 
thought or said "I'm sorry", then he is forgiven.

Adolf Hitler - committed suicide - which is a real No-No. "Faith, Hope 
and Charity and the greatest of these is Charity". Never studied it but 
I think that word 'Hope' is in relation to having a 'positive'; by 
comparison suicide is giving up all Hope - the 'negative'.

Nevertheless even Adolf, even microseconds before he pulled the trigger, 
had he thought or said "I'm sorry....", then he is forgiven also.

Not unnaturally there are many Ukrainian and Jewish dead and their 
respective living descendants who would claim, "That's not fair". My 
heart goes out to them, but that is our belief. We could of course have 
it completely wrong but that's what Catholics believe their particular 
God to be about. And although they may not have Confession as a 
sacrament, I believe many other Christian groups feel the same.

Jimmy
```
