```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net...
> When someone is exposed to the Gospel (Good News) of Jesus *and rejects 
> it*, that rejected knowledge becomes a condemnation.

That rejection *we* as humans cannot judge.   I think it takes more than 
"exposure", it takes a deeper understanding.   We are responsible for what 
we *know*, not for what *somebody's told us*.  A child who's been molested 
will likely not react all that well to an insistence that he or she think of 
climbing into Jesus' (or God the Father's, if you prefer) lap, and they are 
not condemned for refusing to embrace the image.

And there's the previously-cited example of the man I knew who was molested 
at the age of ten by his Baptist minister uncle, publically humiliated from 
the pulpit, condemned personally by the minister as a spawn of Satan on the 
following Sunday and physically thrown out the front door of the church and 
down the steps as his parents watched.  I do not believe he is accountable 
for brushing the dirt off his clothes, never setting foot in a church again 
and walking out of the room whenever the conversation turned to religious 
topics, for the rest of his life.   What he experienced was a large part of 
his *understanding* of Christianity and Christian behavior, and I think it 
*reasonable* of him to want no part of it.

> the memory of being told how to avoid it, and not believing.

Many people in my childhood kept raising the image of the bleeding Christ on 
the cross, about how horrible the Crucifixion was, and kept reminding me 
that every single misstep I made was another thorn that I had personally 
hammered into His head.

I did not have an easy childhood.  My father was an active alcoholic, and my 
mother was an active uninstitutionalized paranoid-schizophrenic, and our 
Yankee family was not well-liked in a small German Texas town.  To this day 
I do not relate to the Crucifixion.  I'd have traded nine hours on the cross 
for what I went through *in a heartbeat*.

> God has not told us exactly what the judgment will be of those who have 
> never heard the Gospel.

*Judgment* is not necessarily *condemnation*.   I don't believe God is so 
petty that salvation boils down to a cosmic game of "Simon says ...".

> So everyone who does not accept the forgiveness provided by God's grace in 
> Jesus, will receive punishment for their sins.

"Christians aren't perfect, just forgiven" (unlike the rest of you poor 
ignorant schlubs!) ???

> We do not know when we will die and face God, and many people die every 
> minute. So there really is an urgency about all this, spreading the 
> Gospel, and receiving it.

The hubris comes in presuming that we're uniquely qualified to spread this 
"good news" to any particular group, or any particular individual.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 46)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-17T14:12:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4g_of.434$RZ6.288@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<cvkuo154sjfungkb2jgjmq2q28nl4tu9aj@4ax.com> <FoZjf.47094$6y4.38133@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <1133550230.780919.303800@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <i21kf.47144$6y4.15970@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqd1b$259m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnv4on$2uqh$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> When someone is exposed to the Gospel (Good News) of Jesus *and rejects 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> are not condemned for refusing to embrace the image.

I completely agree.

> And there's the previously-cited example of the man I knew who was 
> molested at the age of ten by his Baptist minister uncle, publically 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> it.

I believe God will take all this into consideration when judging the nephew 
and the uncle.

> Many people in my childhood kept raising the image of the bleeding Christ 
> on the cross, about how horrible the Crucifixion was, and kept reminding 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> on the cross for what I went through *in a heartbeat*.

My childhood, my entire life, has been very difficult, but for different 
reasons. I have spent weeks, months, in pain comparable to torture. In fact, 
my life has been an almost unbroken series of disappointments and 
heartaches, emotional and physical pain and abuse, and failures. When I was 
2 to 6 we lived in a small city, moving back to our farm during my 1st grade 
year. As a newcomer I started out behind the 8-ball, socially. One 
experience made me an almost permanent social outcast. Water fountains at 
school were water faucets with a vertical copper nipple 1/4" across, over a 
sink. The first time I went to drink, I was fascinated by the meniscus of 
water on the nipple, and reached out to touch it. The teacher had told the 
class before I came not to touch the nipple, but I hadn't been told. Someone 
saw me and told the teacher. In outrage, she placed me in the center of the 
room, arranged everybody in a circle around me, and had them to point and 
jeer and laugh at me. This event helped to entrench me as a literal outcast 
for the next eight years. All through school, I blew the curve on 
achievement tests, and my mom made the serious mistake of not only telling 
me, but explaining the other kids hostility as jealousy. In my child's mind 
it evolved into a war between me and the rest of the world. I was a scrawny 
kid, and my only weapon was my intellect. I felt a constant sense of threat 
and desperation. They would trip me or slam me against the wall between 
classes, and I would retaliate by verbally humiliating them in class. Once 
about 10 boys ganged up on me with baseball bats, fully intending to beat my 
brains out, and I spent the next ten minutes walking backward over the 
playground as they advanced on me, talking them out of it. For me, school 
was like going to hell every day. This continuous and (to me) very desperate 
struggle made me fanatically competitive. To this day, when confronted, I 
have an almost instinctive tendency to want to "go for the throat". I had to 
learn intellectually how to get along with people, like one learns history 
or algebra. As a social outcast during my formative years, I hadn't learned 
normally. It didn't help that, around the age of 5-7, I was sexually abused 
by an older cousin for a time, I don't remember how long.

In 12th grade I fell in love with a classmate. She had played the piano and 
organ at her church for ten years. To all appearances she was a quality 
person. We dated for 2 1/2 years. Three days after we were married, I 
discovered by accident, and she admitted, that she had gone parking with 
another guy only 2 weeks before our wedding. I spent the next 20 years 
wondering about her apparent infidelities, hearing her deprecate me before 
our children, and enduring her public humiliations. After ten years of doing 
my best to please her, I finally (yes, I'm slow sometimes) realized the 
problem was inside her and I could never, ever please her. I lost hope of 
having a happy marriage. For the next ten years, not a single person on the 
planet believed me about this. My best friend, a pastor who worked with me 
every day, when I shared some of the things I was enduring, called my wife 
behind my back, told her things she later hit me with that only he knew, and 
believed her over me. This same guy would, to this day, swear in court that 
he has never known or heard of me being less than 100% honest, and would 
probably stake his life on the honesty of anything I said. My own parents, 
who would say the same thing about me since I was a young boy, did not 
believe me either, until after almost 20 years, she decided to divorce me 
and began to let her facade slip. She took everything I had, except for 
custody of our kids. I wanted the kids, she didn't. Either of our children 
would describe her in similar terms.

From a teenager, I had pretty bad headaches maybe 3 or 4 times a year. 
During 1977, I was taking night classes at UAB, I rewrote the county vehicle 
license system (5 months of 16+ hour, 7 day weeks), we moved into a new 
house, and I was rear-ended going to work, which caused whiplash. I started 
having migraines so severe that I had to go home two or three times a week, 
sometimes so sick my wife had to come get me. I remember times I barely made 
it to the driveway at home, rolling out of the car onto the ground, throwing 
up. I would lie in bed in a fetal position, the room as dark and cool as I 
could get it, for two or three days at a time, with pain so severe I would 
have memory blackouts. Light and sound actually hurt. This continued for 
many years, and working barely enough to survive was the best I could 
manage. I once calculated that I have spent something over 25% of my entire 
adult life with a headache. I still have migraines, though less severe and 
frequent. I have had several kidney stones, one for six weeks. People have 
committed suicide from less pain in less time. If you have never had a 
kidney stone, imagine someone thrusting a dagger into your back and 
twisting, and you get a pretty good idea. I have had to take so many (legal) 
pain killers that I have to be careful taking them, or I will get headaches 
from them. These are only a few samples from my life, and there is much, 
much more.

Earlier this year I carefully added it up and, since I was 20 (37 years), I 
have enjoyed a *total* of about 18 months that I have not had to deal every 
day with gut-wrenching personal problems with severity comparable to an 
unfaithful spouse, that I did not cause and could not fix, no matter how 
hard I tried. As a result of all the stress and pain, my effective IQ has 
dropped probably 30 points, and my once near-photographic memory now seems 
more like a sieve. I have spent almost 10 years under treatment for clinical 
depression. The last six year stretch I was so depressed I could not work, 
in spite of treatment. If I had not had ongoing contracts and subcontractors 
to do the work, I would have been destitute. There have been many times when 
I was in so much physical and emotional pain, and became so discouraged, 
that I would have welcomed, have actually begged for, death or even complete 
annihilation. I would never have been able to survive the life I have had if 
it had not been for the encouragement of God's Spirit, and His physical 
provision. I've never gone hungry, without shelter or clothes, or missed a 
bill payment. If I had not known a God who loves me in spite of my faults, I 
would have committed suicide many years ago, simply to escape the pain. I do 
not know why I have had to suffer so much. I am certainly not the only one. 
This world, as beautiful and wonderful as it can be, is all too often a 
terrible, lonely, bitter place. But I know that God loves me, even so. He 
has proven His love to me through His presence, encouragement and provision, 
even through times when I was bitter and resentful toward Him because of my 
pain. And He loves each one of us.

>> God has not told us exactly what the judgment will be of those who have 
>> never heard the Gospel.
>
> *Judgment* is not necessarily *condemnation*.   I don't believe God is so 
> petty that salvation boils down to a cosmic game of "Simon says ...".

Neither do I. It is a bit frustrating to me when people insist on 
interpreting the Bible (or my comments) in this way.

>> So everyone who does not accept the forgiveness provided by God's grace 
>> in Jesus, will receive punishment for their sins.
>
> "Christians aren't perfect, just forgiven" (unlike the rest of you poor 
> ignorant schlubs!) ???

Not only are Christians forgiven, they are *extremely blessed* (the world 
would say "extremely lucky") for being in position to hear and believe. 
Knowing this, many Christians are eager to get the Gospel to everyone they 
possibly can, as Jesus commanded.

>> We do not know when we will die and face God, and many people die every 
>> minute. So there really is an urgency about all this, spreading the 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> "good news" to any particular group, or any particular individual.

I have no idea of where the 'uniquely' came from. Jesus commanded that those 
who believe in Him should tell everybody else about Him. There is no hubris 
in being obedient to Jesus' command. I believe in Jesus, so do millions of 
others. Is it hubris for me to believe that I believe? :-)
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:


>> <from Chuck > ....I did not have an easy childhood.  My father was an active alcoholic, and 
>>my mother was an active uninstitutionalized paranoid-schizophrenic, and 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 2 to 6 we lived in a small city, .....,snip.

Well above are both two sad stories, yours Judson more so than Chucks. 
My heartfelt thoughts go out to you both. And at the back of it all I am 
well aware over the years that you are honest and sincere in what you 
believe.

But oh, you leave me so uncomfortable. I can appreciate how you would 
latch onto something which would offer you such a 'positive'. I've been 
blessed by the God you write about, without realizing it - I never had 
to face such awful challenges.

Now this is what our atheist friend Richard really wanted me to say, 
when he wrote, "Did I think we could get by without the Pope ?". I'm not 
even sure he cares; rather he was trying to use me to take a dig at Judson.

YES - we can get by without a Pope. It just so happens that like the 
local soccer club, square-dancing club, if you belong to an organized 
religious group you can receive guidance and help - without question.
That makes it much easier for you to take the escalator 'upstairs', when 
you number comes up.

What concerns me is your total immersion in the WHOLE of the Bible, 
liberally quoting from the Jewish Bible or the New Testament as 
appropriate. My own very personal view - I consider the God of the 
Jewish Bible to be somewhat harsh - perhaps that was how he had to get 
his message across to those Semitic nomads all those years ago.

Exactly the same God, but the New Testament consistently talks of 
LOVE/CHARITY, while at the same time stressing you can receive damnation 
   if you are content with your own intellectual arrogance, having been 
tuned into the fact that there is an alternative to your current 
thinking. In fact, if it had so chosen, Christianity could have got by 
without any reference to the Jewish Bible. (The Koran started from 
square one with passing references to Abraham, Jesus and Mary).

Regrettably you have already indicated that some who missed the boat are 
SOL. I don't buy that at all. Please do yourself a favour. You don't 
have to believe it anymore than Bhuddism. Go googling for this :-

http://www.newadvent.org/cathen/02258b.htm

Don't have to read the whole shebang. Go to Section X which stipulates 
'Types of Baptism' - then you *should* at least understand why I 
disagree with your stance on salvation.

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
> What concerns me is your total immersion in the WHOLE of the Bible, 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> message across to those Semitic nomads all those years ago.

The Bible declares that God does not change, that "there is neither 
variableness nor shadow of turning" about God. However, that doesn't mean 
that God does not do different things in different situations. God's dealing 
with mankind has been a progressive revelation, a progression of growing 
intimacy, ever since the fall. I see this as somewhat similar to how one 
deals with children differently as they mature. Paul says that the Mosaic 
Law was "a schoolmaster". Originally, God walked literally with Adam in the 
Garden of Eden. After the fall, because of sin, no human being could endure 
in the presence of God's unveiled Holy Glory. At Sinai, God was a distant 
fire and thundering voice from a mountaintop. At the birth of Jesus, God 
became a baby, who could be seen and touched, who was vulnerable and could 
be eventually struck, spit upon, nailed to a cross, stabbed with a spear, 
and killed. After Jesus' resurrection and ascension, at Pentecost, God sent 
His Spirit to live within us, the most intimate of possible contacts, even 
closer than Adam enjoyed. We don't have to go to a mountain, we don't have 
to go to a temple. Jesus told the Samaritan woman at the well that the hour 
is coming when men would not have to go here or go there to worship, but 
would worship God in spirit and truth. This is the case. We can speak, even 
think, and God is right here in our hearts to hear and respond. The Church 
(true believers) are the Body of Christ. God literally feels what we feel. 
God felt the suffering I have felt in my life, even as I felt it. Even the 
prophets in the Old Testament only received the presence of the Holy Spirit 
on occasion. Those who will believe in Jesus now can have that privilege and 
blessing 24/7.

> Exactly the same God, but the New Testament consistently talks of 
> LOVE/CHARITY, while at the same time stressing you can receive damnation 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> passing references to Abraham, Jesus and Mary).

Jesus quoted the Old Testament often, and so did the authors of the New 
testament. When viewed correctly, there is no conflict between the Old 
testament and the New Testament. (If The Bible is indeed God's Word, and you 
see contradiction, then you aren't looking at it correctly. Many wonderful 
truths, that would have otherwise have been overlooked, have been unearthed 
by following this principle, and looking diligently for Scriptural 
explanations to apparent contradictions. I believe this is one reason why 
God permits apparent contradictions. Unbelievers see an apparent 
contradiction then stop, seeing it as a flaw in the Bible, and receiving no 
blessing.) Jesus said that not one tiny part of a letter of the (Mosaic) law 
would pass away until heaven and earth pass away (Matthew 5:18). Jesus did 
not do away with the law, He fulfilled the law, for everyone who believes in 
Him. We don't have to fulfill the law ourselves, because Jesus fulfilled it 
for us, not because the law has "gone away". We are under grace, not law. I 
could give you a multitude of verses supporting all this. God's Word is 
God's Word, we don't just "throw out" what we may think is archaic. God was 
not so myopic as to say things, then decide "Oh! Oh! I have to change that." 
He's God, after all. :-)

> Regrettably you have already indicated that some who missed the boat are 
> SOL. I don't buy that at all. Please do yourself a favour. You don't have 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> with your stance on salvation.

Jimmy, you are making what I view to be a common mistake made by people who 
have been exposed to Catholicism or another liturgical denomination. There 
is (as you pointed out) a truly vast amount of "stuff" proposed as doctrine 
by such denominations. However, consider this. If God loves us, and wants to 
have a relationship with us, then He would have had to make what He requires 
of us simple enough for us to know and comprehend. And I mean, for everybody 
to comprehend, not just priests and scholars. Examine my SIG, that is John 
3:16. This single sentence encompasses the entire necessary essence of being 
a Christian. This is so simple, so profound, that even little children can 
understand. *THIS* is how a loving and merciful God communicates to us the 
essence of salvation. Sure, there are complex topics in the Bible, but they 
*are not necessary* to understand how to receive God's salvation. It is my 
opinion that, if anyone is so young, or so mentally impaired, that they are 
unable to understand and receive John 3:16, that they are so simple that God 
will hold them innocent of sin. Again, this is how a merciful and loving God 
acts toward us. This is the God I know.
```

```
> Jesus quoted the Old Testament often, and so did the authors of the New 
> testament. When viewed correctly, there is no conflict between the Old 
> testament and the New Testament. (If The Bible is indeed God's Word, and you
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 50)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-12-17T20:17:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> is not the message I get from the New Testament.

You should examine the New testament more thoroughly. :-)

"If your right eye causes you to sin, pluck it out and cast it from you; for 
it is more profitable for you that one of your members perish, than for your 
whole body to be cast into hell. And if your right hand causes you to sin, 
cut it off and cast it from you; for it is more profitable for you that one 
of your members perish, than for your whole body to be cast into hell." 
(Matthew 5:29, 30)

"And do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. But 
rather fear Him who is able to destroy both soul and body in hell." (Matthew 
10:28)

"Serpents, brood of vipers! How can you escape the condemnation of hell?" 
(Matthew 23:33)

"For if God did not spare the angels who sinned, but cast them down to hell 
and delivered them into chains of darkness, to be reserved for judgment; and 
did not spare the ancient world, but saved Noah, one of eight people, a 
preacher of righteousness, bringing in the flood on the world of the 
ungodly; and turning the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah into ashes, condemned 
them to destruction, making them an example to those who afterward would 
live ungodly; and delivered righteous Lot, who was oppressed by the filthy 
conduct of the wicked (for that righteous man, dwelling among them, 
tormented his righteous soul from day to day by seeing and hearing their 
lawless deeds) then the Lord knows how to deliver the godly out of 
temptations and to reserve the unjust under punishment for the day of 
judgment, and especially those who walk according to the flesh in the lust 
of uncleanness and despise authority. They are presumptuous, self-willed. 
They are not afraid to speak evil of dignitaries, whereas angels, who are 
greater in power and might, do not bring a reviling accusation against them 
before the Lord." (2 Peter 2:4-11)

"For as the Father has life in Himself, so He has granted the Son to have 
life in Himself, and has given Him authority to execute judgment also, 
because He is the Son of Man" (John 5:26)

"Therefore you are inexcusable, O man, whoever you are who judge, for in 
whatever you judge another you condemn yourself; for you who judge practice 
the same things. But we know that the judgment of God is according to truth 
against those who practice such things. And do you think this, O man, you 
who judge those practicing such things, and doing the same, that you will 
escape the judgment of God? Or do you despise the riches of His goodness, 
forbearance, and longsuffering, not knowing that the goodness of God leads 
you to repentance? But in accordance with your hardness and your impenitent 
heart you are treasuring up for yourself wrath in the day of wrath and 
revelation of the righteous judgment of God, who "will render to each one 
according to his deeds": eternal life to those who by patient continuance in 
doing good seek for glory, honor, and immortality; but to those who are 
self-seeking and do not obey the truth, but obey unrighteousness-indignation 
and wrath, tribulation and anguish, on every soul of man who does evil ..." 
(Romans 2:1-9)

"Some men's sins are clearly evident, preceding them to judgment, but those 
of some men follow later." (1 Timothy 5:24)

"And as it is appointed for men to die once, but after this the judgment, so 
Christ was offered once to bear the sins of many." (Hebrews 9:27,28)

"... the Lord knows how to deliver the godly out of temptations and to 
reserve the unjust under punishment for the day of judgment" (2 Peter 2:9)

I didn't even scratch the surface. Revelation largely consists of the 
desrcriptions of the judgment God will bring onto this world. Jimmy, the 
*whole point* of salvation is that we are saved *from judgment*. If there is 
no judgment, then salvation from judgment is meaningless. :-)

>> Jimmy, you are making what I view to be a common mistake made by people 
>> who have been exposed to Catholicism or another liturgical denomination. 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> and atheists respond to this particular gem.

I understand that you disagree with what I said. But "cop out"? I really 
don't know what you mean. One of the Big Things that Jesus tasks the 
Pharisees over was that they put so much emphasis on the body of human 
writings *about the Scriptures* that they neglected *the Scriptures*. Why is 
it a "cop out" for me to point out the same pitfall Jesus did, with regard 
to modern religion? This is one of Jesus' comments:

"Hypocrites! Well did Isaiah prophesy about you, saying: 'These people draw 
near to Me with their mouth, And honor Me with their lips, But their heart 
is far from Me. And in vain they worship Me, Teaching as doctrines the 
commandments of men.' "  (Matthew 15:7-9)

Jimmy, it is not what men write about the Bible that is so important, it is 
what the Bible says that is important. Think about it. Read what the Bible 
says for yourself. If I have misrepresented what the Bible says, then please 
point it out to me and I will swiftly apologize. :-)
```

```
"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mts.net> wrote in message
news:43A5A6FE.8DB93585@mts.net...

> Lot: he was the guy who offered his two virgin daughters to the mob just
> as long as they would leave him and his two guests alone, wasn't he?
> (KJV, Genesis 19: 1-8).

Yeah, he's also the guy who had sex with those same two virgin
daughters, on two successive nights.  He got 'em both pregnant; with
Moab in the first case and with Ammon in the second.  The Bible records
that he was so drunk that he never even noticed that his daughters were
in bed with him, much less that he had sex with them, either time
(ibid., 19:31ff).

Nobody seems to have gotten too much of a burr under their respective
saddles about this at the time, as the story switches back to Abraham
at this point.    

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> charles.stevens@unisys.com wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

The first part of my post has been lopped: Judson quoted the Bible where
it states that Lot was righteous.  If it's righteous to treat your
daughters as he did - sorry, I don't buy it.  It's disgusting at the
very best.  I was aware of the bit about his daughters getting knocked
up; but as I read it, they decided on their own to do; so while Lot is
guilty of incest, it wasn't something he did voluntarily.

Cain got a wife from somewhere, all right; but it doesn't follow that
she must have been an un-named descendant of Adam & Eve.  The Bible
doesn't say that; and I really don't think you can use something that
the Bible DOESN'T say as an answer to anything.  I can do that, too;
it's easy.  But it doesn't make it so.

PL
```

```
"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>
> The first part of my post has been lopped: Judson quoted the Bible where
> it states that Lot was righteous.  If it's righteous to treat your
> daughters as he did - sorry, I don't buy it.  It's disgusting at the
> very best.

I agree, it is disgusting. But you're missing the point. Lot was not 
righteous because of his actions, Lot was righteous because of his faith in 
God. Every single person (other than Jesus Christ) is a sinner, and must be 
saved by God's grace, through faith in God. This was as true for Lot as it 
is for any Christian today.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 56)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2005-12-20T15:40:34-06:00

- **References:** `<jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnv4on$2uqh$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4g_of.434$RZ6.288@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <c02pf.127731$ki.103138@pd7tw2no> <Lo3pf.674$RZ6.558@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <ko4pf.129708$ki.125936@pd7tw2no> <zYgpf.1040$k76.75@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <43A5A6FE.8DB93585@mts.net> <1135031522.125478.278880@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <iNIpf.149846$Gd6.144963@pd7tw3no> <43A7716B.72A86BCB@mts.net> <8b_pf.16687$k76.12561@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> "Peter Lacey" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> --


Take it a bit further, then.  Would you use Lot as a role model?  Does
being "righteous because of one's faith" excuse one from acting in a
righteous way?  It's a real question, not a rhetorical one; I have any
number of instances close to home where being a Christian (of any stripe
- in the cases I'm thinking of, they claim to be born again) does not
prevent one from committing grievous sins.  (There is no point in
supplying names or details; suffice it to say this sort of thing has now
extended over three generations that I know of).  Are we to ignore all
actions because of the person's faith?  

I have a hypothetical tale to probe this question but it's a bit too
direct for this discussion.  Anyone who's interested can contact me
off-group.

PL
```

- **Message-ID:** `<yshqf.16209$kP5.11420@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnv4on$2uqh$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4g_of.434$RZ6.288@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <c02pf.127731$ki.103138@pd7tw2no> <Lo3pf.674$RZ6.558@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <ko4pf.129708$ki.125936@pd7tw2no> <zYgpf.1040$k76.75@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <43A5A6FE.8DB93585@mts.net> <1135031522.125478.278880@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <iNIpf.149846$Gd6.144963@pd7tw3no> <43A7716B.72A86BCB@mts.net> <8b_pf.16687$k76.12561@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <43A87A52.8225F604@mts.net>`

```
"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>> "Peter Lacey" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> actions because of the person's faith?

Your viewpoint, as expressed above, appears to be misleading. When a person 
receives Jesus Christ as Savior, God stamps "PAID" across their sins. As 
long as that person continues to have faith in Jesus for their salvation, 
that is the end of the matter, as far as facing God in judgment for their 
sins. However, sin has consequences. When Christians sin, it damages their 
fellowship (not relationship: your dad is still your dad, even if you do 
have a tiff) with God, it makes them poor witnesses to others, just the kind 
of the thing you describe above. This is an unfortunate thing, and the Bible 
says that Christians will be 'chastised' for it, both in this world, and in 
eternity by losing 'reward'. Though Christians will not be judged for sin, 
their works will be judged at the "Judgment Seat of Christ", where they will 
receive reward commensurate with their works.

Also, in this life, Christians still have to put up with the same sin nature 
as everybody else does. The Bible says that the 'old sin nature' is not 
removed until Christians are 'glorified' with new bodies when meeting Jesus 
at death, or at the Rapture. So Christians have the same temptations as 
everyone else. The Bible also says that Christians are no longer "in 
bondage" to sin, in that God has provided a way for Christians to avoid sin. 
But they are still tempted by sin, and still have free will, therefore 
Christians do sin sometimes, and some Christians sin big time. They do pay a 
price for it, both here and forever. Salvation is not a "free ticket" to 
sin.
```

```
> set on keeping that belief?

The Bible says that we appropriate God's grace by our faith in Jesus. No 
faith, no appropriation. As for losing faith after once being saved:

For it is impossible for those who were once enlightened, and have tasted 
the heavenly gift, and have become partakers of the Holy Spirit, and have 
tasted the good word of God and the powers of the age to come, if they fall 
away, to renew them again to repentance, since they crucify again for 
themselves the Son of God, and put Him to an open shame. (Hebrews 6:4-6)
```

- **References:** `<GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnv4on$2uqh$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4g_of.434$RZ6.288@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <c02pf.127731$ki.103138@pd7tw2no> <Lo3pf.674$RZ6.558@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <ko4pf.129708$ki.125936@pd7tw2no> <zYgpf.1040$k76.75@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <43A5A6FE.8DB93585@mts.net> <1135031522.125478.278880@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <iNIpf.149846$Gd6.144963@pd7tw3no> <43A7716B.72A86BCB@mts.net> <8b_pf.16687$k76.12561@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <43A87A52.8225F604@mts.net> <yshqf.16209$kP5.11420@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:yshqf.16209$kP5.11420@bignews5.bellsouth.net...

> Your viewpoint, as expressed above, appears to be misleading. When a
> person receives Jesus Christ as Savior, God stamps "PAID" across their
> sins. As long as that person continues to have faith in Jesus for their
> salvation, that is the end of the matter, as far as facing God in judgment
> for their sins.

So that means a person who has "received Jesus Christ as Savior" who
"misbehaves" in some way, through ignorance or through deliberate
action, causing direct harm to another human being, has no real need to
worry about changing his behavior to avoid that misbehavior in the
future, because God has forgiven him for his failings, and ultimately,
nothing else matters?

I don't think that's the way it works.

It is *not* a given that somebody who has taken this step will
immediately and for the rest of his life be incapable of wrongdoing,
and I think it's *particularly* incumbent upon Christians *consciously*
to strive that they do no harm.

Matthew 25:31ff is about actions, not attitudes, and those actions
require *positive* effort.

A friend of mine says "You can do anything you choose to do, so long as
you are willing to pay *the* price."   Neither I nor you gets to
decide, for any action, what *the* price is.

I don't believe devout Christians who behave like self-righteous prigs
are any less accountable -- in life or in the hereafter -- than the
person who's never heard of Jesus.   I don't think Jesus was limiting
his diatribe in Luke 11:17ff *only* to scribes and pharisees; I think
the diatribe was about form over function, about show over action.

A person who insists "Oh, I'm NOT behaving like a self-righteous prig,
it's clearly *your* perception that's erroneous, because I've accepted
Jesus, and you OBVIOUSLY haven't, or you wouldn't think I was behaving
like a self-righteous prig in the first place!  Must be the DEVIL
working overtime in YOUR life!" is not demonstrating behavior
appropriate for a Christian *to me*.

I don't believe devout Christians are *fundamentally incapable* of such
behavior, and I don't believe a devout Christian who insists that his
bad behavior doesn't matter to God is behaving in a Christ-like manner.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

>>>"After he begot Seth, the days of Adam were eight hundred years; and he 
>>>had sons and daughters." (Genesis 5:4,5)
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> from marrying close relatives, that came much later.

Well that was refreshing "(as far as we know)" rather than the "Bible 
says". You're a quick learner aren't you  :-).

As for Adam and his 800 years - I wouldn't even want to live 100 years. 
Just think of his memory bank of thoughts for 800 years. He would 
certainly blow it sticking to your COBOL 85 dictum. However, by only 
utilising classes currently necessary, he could throw way the ones not 
currently instantiated.

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>>>>"After he begot Seth, the days of Adam were eight hundred years; and he 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> says". You're a quick learner aren't you  :-).

Yes, I am trying to be more careful how I phrase these posts. :-) But in 
this particular case, I would have stated it similarly all along. The Bible 
gives strong indication that there was ancient knowledge of which we no 
longer have record (not necessarily the knowledge, but the fact of their 
posessing it). The fact that the Bible records no command to abstain from 
marrying siblings earlier is not proof that God did not give such a command 
earlier. Though I believe He would have told us this if He had, because of 
such questions as we are discussing. :-)

> As for Adam and his 800 years - I wouldn't even want to live 100 years. 
> Just think of his memory bank of thoughts for 800 years. He would 
> certainly blow it sticking to your COBOL 85 dictum. However, by only 
> utilising classes currently necessary, he could throw way the ones not 
> currently instantiated.

Adam lived 930 years, 800 of them after Seth was born. I believe that after 
the Fall, God gave mortality here on earth as a blessing. Who would want to 
live forever in this trouble, grief and pain filled world?
```

```
Hmmmmm... I wonder.  Granted that n(cc) of beer = n(cc) of beer (given 
constant temperature and pressure)... but if you put it into a 2n(cc) mug 
is it 'half a beer'?  If you put it into an n-1(cc) glass then truly, your 
cup overfloweth... perhaps it is time to ponder the wisdom of 'No doubt 
about it, I gotta get another hat!'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 64)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-12-24T10:45:26+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4139frF1cs61pU3@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <kwhqf.16210$kP5.13969@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <06qqf.49355$2k.10868@pd7tw1no> <NCBqf.26487$k76.2001@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <doeri0$5df$1@reader1.panix.com> <411r2pF1ckkccU3@individual.net> <4fUqf.459$bd.324@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
 

"Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:4fUqf.459$bd.324@tornado.tampabay.rr.com...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> DU
Savour what remains to the last drop. It tastes even better as it 
disappears... :-)

There is a lovely story in Zen about a man being chased by a tiger. He jumps 
over a cliff and grabs a small plant to save himself. The tiger is waiting 
above, the rocks are below. As he waits, a small animal starts to gnaw 
through the plant he is holding onto. At this moment he notices a wild 
strawberry growing within reach. He plucks the strawberry and eats it. How 
sweet it tasted!

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-23T11:10:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Y9Wqf.28048$k76.23796@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnv4on$2uqh$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4g_of.434$RZ6.288@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <c02pf.127731$ki.103138@pd7tw2no> <Lo3pf.674$RZ6.558@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <ko4pf.129708$ki.125936@pd7tw2no> <zYgpf.1040$k76.75@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <43A5A6FE.8DB93585@mts.net> <1135031522.125478.278880@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <iNIpf.149846$Gd6.144963@pd7tw3no> <11qg8cpfd1rgs87@corp.supernews.com> <Q3_pf.39879$2k.26424@pd7tw1no> <rp_pf.16773$k76.7112@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <4F%pf.150860$ki.66502@pd7tw2no> <kwhqf.16210$kP5.13969@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <06qqf.49355$2k.10868@pd7tw1no> <NCBqf.26487$k76.2001@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <411qveF1c6qeuU3@individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> If you feel that way about it, Judson, why bother to live in it at all?


In my case, three words: duty, responsibility and obedience. I have family 
who are directly dependent on me, other family who would be hurt by my 
death, and God does not give me the right to take my life.

Don't get me wrong, I have been blessed with many things for which I am very 
thankful. I grew up in a stable, happy home. I have children and 
grandchildren whom I love very much. I have been blessed in my professional 
career and have not often had financial difficulty. But from the time I was 
very young, my life has also been filled with severe and almost continuous 
emotional and physical pain. This has continued to this day, with no end 
that I can see, for this life. A number of months ago, while I was still 
taking anti-depressants, I shared with my doctor (a very good pshrink, whom 
I really like) a lot of what I was dealing with. His stunned comment was 
"Wow! It's like your life has become a real-life 'horror story'!" This 
surprise is from a man who has professionally counseled depressed people 
every day for 25 years. :-) I continue to endure it simply from duty, 
responsibility and obedience. God can and does give me peace about these 
things, but I still feel the pain, I still get weary from the constant 
struggle. I am most thankful to God that I have an expectation of a much 
better place, without all the pain, once I'm outta' here. :-)
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Tue, 27 Dec 2005 18:00:08 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
> <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> is OK and should be ignored?

There is no such nomenclature as 'official hatred'. As a convenience, 
using one example, you are attempting to tar all with the same brush. To 
my knowledge he made no statements about approving a quick ticket to 
Hell, merely stated in his *sincere* belief, "Regrettably, that's how I 
see it - but here's a let-out - join me !"

I've pointed out the Catholic position, (forms of Baptism and 
Confession),  which I learned at school, and it has been the same set of 
thoughts since way before the Reformation.

Naturally we would like you to 'join us'. A bit of chump change in the 
collection plate wouldn't hurt. Hell ! It takes a lotta money to run 
those beautiful cathedral/church buildings.

Jimmy
```

```
"Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:OfZrf.43582$8d.31047@tornado.tampabay.rr.com...
>
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
My comment above was not originally intended to suggest that Judson kill 
himself; (although I agree, that meaning can be fairly ascribed to it.) It 
was more of a general question. For example, perhaps the whole idea of 
bringing children into a world you see as dismal and painful, could bear 
re-examination...


> I believe it is unless it's done by someone related to a rather religious 
> group.  Then it's just meeting with God and waiting until everyone has a 
> chance to reunite.  If the family is not religious then obviously you're 
> going to hell.  It's quite a simple concept really.
>

Yes, I'm inclined to agree that if you believe the next world will be much 
better than this one, there is every incentive to move on...

Those of us who hold no such belief may find our time better employed by 
making the most of our time here, rather than the promise of a happy future.

Works for me :-)

Pete.
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1135625224.441312.324170@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com...
>
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I don't believe in original (or any other kind of) "sin". It is 
self-evidently obvious that people behave badly sometimes, but the moral 
implications of this are entirely man made; I don't believe the universe 
knows or cares... all it sees are events.

This position does not make me amoral or unethical. I believe bad behaviour 
should be corrected, but because it is inefficient, unnecessary and has 
negative effects on all of us, not because it is "sinful".

Pete.
```

```
Kinda reminds me. Oooh about two, three years back. There's this black 
lab technician (Jamaica I think), taking my monthly blood sample. Says 
she did her nursing studies in London.

"Where did you study ?".

"Don't remember the name of the hospital but it was downtown London".

"Downtown London ? Lady. Gimmie a break !"

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 53)_

- **From:** "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2005-12-19T05:31:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1134999105.869898.293770@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<do4fui$alc$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ko4pf.129708$ki.125936@pd7tw2no> <zYgpf.1040$k76.75@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134933806.113943.12460@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <do4fui$alc$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <1134933806.113943.12460@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>,
> Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

When you can play marbles with ones' shiny new appendage, it is no
laughing matter.

A story from years past:

When at University it was my wont to imbibe at a hostelry five miles
from my hall of residence. One evening, in the company of a number of
other budding Brains of Britain, I was walking back along the coastpath
in a pitch black night. A hasty shake of the head had my most recent
pride and joy bounce off down the path. "Wow, guys. My eye has dropped
out," quoth I. Four undergrads on their knees in search of the orb and
back came a question: "What does it look like?"
```

```
On Mon, 19 Dec 2005 21:27:12 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

>> The big difference is that Christians get to pick and choose which
>> parts of the Old Testament they wish to accept as scriptures, and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Spell that last bit out with examples.

The Old Testament has God telling us how to live, with many statements
of do this and do that.   Some of these are dietary, some of these are
about clothing, some are about procreation, some are about tithing and
duty.

Most Christian churches say that since Jesus came, we no longer have
to what God commanded in the Old Testament - those weren't meant for
modern men like us anyway.   Except for the commandments about stuff
that is yucky, such which we still follow.

(Yucky stuff gets a lion's share of our attention.   I don't mean
manly stuff like war - I mean yucky stuff like other people's sexual
practices).
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Mon, 19 Dec 2005 21:27:12 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
> <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
OK following makes sense.

> The Old Testament has God telling us how to live, with many statements
> of do this and do that.   Some of these are dietary, some of these are
> about clothing, some are about procreation, some are about tithing and
> duty.

Dietary - you are an idiot if you eat pork in the Middle East. Even in 
the West - have you ever seen a nice Sunday joint of pork when a 
bluebottle had climbed all over it.

> Most Christian churches say that since Jesus came, we no longer have
> to what God commanded in the Old Testament - those weren't meant for
> modern men like us anyway.   Except for the commandments about stuff
> that is yucky, such which we still follow.

Again see what you are saying. But my personal belief is that the Jewish 
Testament (Bible) is an historical document of their lives, customs and 
their religion.

Although we hold a HUGE debt to Judaism I think we (Christians), start 
anew with the New Testament but acknowledges the same God, and of course 
there are cross-references to the Jewish Bible.

> (Yucky stuff gets a lion's share of our attention.   I don't mean
> manly stuff like war - I mean yucky stuff like other people's sexual
> practices).

Yucky stuff. Presumably you are not referring to down Deep South where 
something like soixante-neuf was a criminal offence ? The game is only 
allowed to be played in the missionary position.

Presumably you are referring to lesbianism or sodomy ?
Again "Forgive the sinner not the sin". Further allow for both 
'religious/cultural marriages' and same-sex 'civil unions'. A few people 
latched on to the Reuters (?) thing on the Vatican giving 'Limbo' a 
look-over to see whether the idea should be dropped.

Even though we know, from Boccaccio for one, there were lots of nuns and 
monks bed-jumping the RC Church has been consistent. Who picked up on 
the other news item that the Vatican, triggered on by the abominable 
nonsense in N. America, is wanting to exclude from the priesthood any 
seminarian who even has the slightest inclination towards homosexuality.
Vatican II allowed for a degree of liberalism - and look where it got us.

Jimmy
```

- **References:** `<1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnv4on$2uqh$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4g_of.434$RZ6.288@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <c02pf.127731$ki.103138@pd7tw2no> <Lo3pf.674$RZ6.558@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <ko4pf.129708$ki.125936@pd7tw2no> <ftjdq1pd7ecaahogk3b1chi9dp8iftvntq@4ax.com> <QAFpf.147801$Gd6.16292@pd7tw3no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:QAFpf.147801$Gd6.16292@pd7tw3no...

>> The big difference is that Christians get to pick and choose which
>> parts of the Old Testament they wish to accept as scriptures, and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Spell that last bit out with examples.

The most obvious:  circumcision, and the Jewish dietary laws against 
foodstuffs considered "abominable":  pork, catfish, shellfish, or insects, 
for example.

I believe the whole discussion came up because some demanded that Gentile 
Christians undergo circumcision as part of the Abrahamic covenant before 
being accepted as Christians, and Paul and Barnabas disagreed with that 
practice and took off for Jerusalem to talk to the apostles about it.  Peter 
sided with Paul and Barnabas, and ultimately came up with a "short list": 
stay away from food that had previously been offered to idols; don't drink 
blood; don't eat the meat of animals that had been strangled; and stay away 
from profligate sex.   For gentile converts to Christianity, the rest of the 
640 Commandments went out the window.  See Acts 15:1-29.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Much of the Torah is about "The Lord said to Moses, 'Tell the Israelites 
...' ", and Judaism takes these instructions very seriously.  According to 
www.aish.com, which cites Maimonedes, there are 613 of them (not 640, as I 
had remembered it), and they specifically include the Decalog, though not 
necessarily giving these ten instructions more weight than the other 603.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:4g_of.434$RZ6.288@bignews7.bellsouth.net...

>> The hubris comes in presuming that we're uniquely qualified to spread 
>> this "good news" to any particular group, or any particular individual.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> millions of others. Is it hubris for me to believe that I believe? :-)

If you're referring to the Great Commission in Matthew 28:19ff, I'm of the 
opinion that one is obligated to look at the audience Jesus was addressing, 
and as I read it, that was exactly eleven people, namely, the Apostles with 
the exception of Judas who had by this time committed suicide.

Jesus commanded *those eleven people* to "go ye therefore", and not one of 
them has yet come to me and given me the authority to take over for him. 
Until one of them does, or one whose authority I find myself unable to 
dispute does, I'm disinclined to assume that it's either my right or my duty 
to carry out this particular direction.   Why is it yours?   Is it so 
unreasonable that I would view that attitude as a little on the arrogant and 
self-aggrandizing side?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **References:** `<i21kf.47144$6y4.15970@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqd1b$259m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <NBHof.6648$ic1.5375@edtnps90> <1134778053.918561.152960@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>> Again, assuming your beliefs are true, then you should ONLY share the
>> gospel with people who you expect will believe in it.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> tharts.


With the exception of  "He expects _everyone_ to believe", you express it 
pretty well. The Bible says that most people will not believe (Matthew 
7:13,14). I do understand that many people are going to be skeptical and not 
believe. I can even understand some of their reasons.

Richard, a good analogy of why I hold tenaciously to my beliefs would be: If 
you stuck your hand into fire and was burned, and then got burned by fire 
again, from time to time, even if everyone in the world tried to argue you 
out of believing that fire will burn you, you would continue to believe what 
your senses did and continue to make so abundantly clear to you. Just ask 
yourself, if everyone on earth began to tell you that fire would not burn 
you, would you accept their unbelief?

Well, I have believed in Jesus, and I do have a personal relationship with 
God, and His Spirit does live within me (as in every believer). I would be a 
fool to let someone talk me out what my own experience has shown to be true. 
I did not have such conviction the first day I believed in Jesus, of course. 
But years of fellowship with God, and seeing Him work patiently in my life, 
have made His presence as real to me as any other thing. I speak to God and 
He hears me. God speaks to me and I hear Him. Have I ever misunderstood God? 
Sure, occasionally. But I sometimes misunderstand what people say to me 
also. 'Proof' and 'evidence' are not always things you can measure with a 
ruler or thermometer. I have seen miracles on several occasions, but they 
are not nearly as persuasive to my belief as God's presence.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 48)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-12-17T14:32:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1134858776.068408.235610@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<sk%of.442$RZ6.66@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <NBHof.6648$ic1.5375@edtnps90> <1134778053.918561.152960@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <sk%of.442$RZ6.66@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
> if everyone on earth began to tell you that fire would not burn
> you, would you accept their unbelief?

You do seem to have reasons for your obsession, but it is no different
from others who hold many other beliefs in completely different things.
I am interested in how this belief system mechanism evolved and became
part of many people but the actual beliefs themselves are not
particularly interesting being so wide and varying.  If obsessive and
fanatical adherence to a belief were taken that it was 'real' in any
way then there are thousands of realities. You have chosen, or only
know about, just one of them.

Given your other posts I can see how you have shut off the real world
(the one that _I_ see) and retreated into a fantasy world where there
is at least some goodness and some hope, where you see life as a
testing ground that must be endured for rewards later, and this is how
you survive it. I suspect that you felt much the same about Santa, you
could survive the agonies of 12 months for the rewards that you had
earnt. When you discovered the truth you were shattered because there
was no need for the suffering, the year was not a test for what rewards
the presents would be.

So you transferred to christianity where your life of suffering was for
the later rewards. It gave meaning to your pain, and the strength to
suffer for the rewards promised.

Very interesting.
```

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message

Who wrote this bit above - I don't think Judson. Why should the writer 
expect a loving omniscient God to be fair, when I assume the writer 
doesn't even believe in that omniscient God. It appears you want to 
craft something you don't believe in to be the particular type of God 
that YOU want.

>>
>>I've dealt with this before, in other posts. God is kind and loving. But 
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> so that they may be beaten with few stripes rather than with many.

What are you and Charles *actually* challenging - (1) Judson's 
particular set of beliefs or (2) Christianity or (3) both ?

If you and Charles are truly interested in enquiring into 'truth' rather 
than an academic word-game - plenty of sites out there that spell it out 
for you to satisfy your curiosity, whether or not you agree with the 
answers you find. Take the initial aspect of Christianity - Baptism - 
now go googling and see what answers you get - be forewarned you will 
get different answers. For disparity check out the Pentecostals, Mormons 
and Catholicism - doubt you could possibly get a wider spectrum than that.

I could give you an easy answer Catholic-wise that should satisfy both 
you and Charles, as regards native Indians, or native anything else, 
including native Agnostics and native Atheists - so if you are *truly* 
curious go googling.

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:ZiLof.128277$Gd6.98837@pd7tw3no...
> Oliver Wong wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
>> news:37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net...
>>
>>>"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
[snipped, for clarity]
>>>>   This sounds very unfair (how is it their fault?), and while I don't 
>>>> expect "real life" to be nescessarily be fair, I do expect a kind and 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> want.

    I (Oliver) wrote it. I'll explain the reasoning for my beliefs: Humans 
tend to like "fairness". I believe the religion(s) under consideration claim 
that humans are modeled after God; and even if that is not so, for whatever 
reasons, God (if He exists) built humans in such a way so that they like 
"fairness". Since God is loving, presumably, God would give the humans what 
they like, if they behave well. So if God is loving, he should also be 
"fair". Either give blessings to *everybody* (which would be more loving 
than fair), or, if he must punish people, at least punish those who deserve 
it.

[snip]
> What are you and Charles *actually* challenging - (1) Judson's particular 
> set of beliefs or (2) Christianity or (3) both ?

    Probably (1), because I don't know enough about (2) to make specific 
challenges to it, though I have the feeling I would probably not "agree" 
with it based on my past experience with religion in general.

> If you and Charles are truly interested in enquiring into 'truth' rather 
> than an academic word-game - plenty of sites out there that spell it out 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Catholicism - doubt you could possibly get a wider spectrum than that.

    I *have* gotten a wide variety of answers (haven't tried googling, but 
I've heard different view points from different friends, and I've read a 
Wikipedia article or two on western religions in general). And I don't know 
which one is "the right one". Like Judson mentioned earlier, a few 
mis-representatives can make the whole religion look bad. And I've seen a 
lot of things which simply don't make sense to me. I don't know if that 
means religion and I are generally not compatible, or if I just got unlucky 
and hit a lot of bad representatives of those religions.

    - Oliver
```

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
> 
>     I *have* gotten a wide variety of answers (haven't tried googling, but 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> and hit a lot of bad representatives of those religions.

Fine in the above, and I can understand your reaction. How I view the 
topic; there are essentially three groups Believers (in ANY religion), 
Agnostics and Atheists. As Judson has commented with which I agree, if 
there is NO God then we are all eventually just dust - no harm done, 
except us suckers that did believe were spending a lot of time believing 
in something that didn't exist. However if a particular God DOES exist 
......?

To an extent I see agnosticism as a half-way house where you hedge your 
bets, so that hopefully nobody is able to apply the label to you, 
"damned if you do, and damned if you don't". Agnosticism is a religious 
insurance policy to protect your arse ! I think you (Oliver), owe it to 
yourself to be honest to yourself. You can sit on the fence for your 
remaining days on earth, quite a few more decades than I will be around, 
and still check-out as an agnostic. No harm done and you didn't have to 
make any decisions.

Quite likely you have met a lot of mis-representatives in various 
religions. Introduce me to a group of say Catholics that you know - I 
could recommend which ones to stay away from once I know their 
characters. Whether it is Sikhism, Bhuddism, Christianity etc..., there 
will always be the bad eggs or the people that don't practice what they 
preach; actually most of the baddies probably don't even 'preach'. Any 
religion starts out with theological thought, but being mankind we 
gravitate to organized structures. It is *not* the original concept that 
is necessarily wrong but human ego that insists on taking that thought 
and using it in a particular fashion, finishing up with an 
organizational hierarchy and a stiff-necked attitude to any dissenters.

Judson likes the historical bits, so let's throw one in. You should be 
vaguely familiar with her as in you are in Quebec. Jeanne d'Arc, (1412 - 
1431). The English (not British) were doing very nicely in their 
territorial wars in France, with devastating results on the French at 
Agincourt and Crecy. Out of the blue comes this peasant girl with 
visions and musters the Dauphin's army, with good results for the 
French. Obviously with connivance from some in the French ranks the 
English captured her and put her on trial.

 From the Catholic Encyclopedia :-

"There can be no doubt that the English, partly because they feared 
their prisoner with a superstitious terror, partly because they were 
ashamed of the dread which she inspired, were determined at all costs to 
take her life. They could not put her to death for having beaten them, 
but they could get her sentenced as a witch and a heretic......"

Suffice to say the trial was a rigged court by her English and 
Burgundian detractors. Eventually she was found guilty, burned and her 
ashes thrown in the Seine.

CE : "Twenty-four years later a revision of her trial, the procï¿½s de 
rï¿½habilitation, was opened at Paris with the consent of the Holy See. 
The popular feeling was then very different, and, with but the rarest 
exceptions, all the witnesses were eager to render their tribute to the 
virtues and supernatural gifts of the Maid. The first trial had been 
conducted without reference to the pope,  indeed it was carried out in 
defiance of St. Joan's appeal to the head of the Church. Now an 
appellate court constituted by the pope, after long inquiry and 
examination of witnesses, reversed and annulled the sentence pronounced 
by a local tribunal under Cauchon's presidency. The illegality of the 
former proceedings was made clear, and it speaks well for the sincerity 
of this new inquiry that it could not be made without inflicting some 
degree of reproach upon both the King of France and the Church at large, 
seeing that so great an injustice had been done and had so long been 
suffered to continue unredressed. Even before the rehabilitation trial, 
keen observers, like Eneas Sylvius Piccolomini (afterwards Pope Pius 
II), though still in doubt as to her mission, had discerned something of 
the heavenly character of the Maid. In Shakespeare's day she was still 
regarded in England as a witch in league with the fiends of hell, but a 
juster estimate had begun to prevail even in the pages of Speed's 
"History of Great Britaine" (1611). By the beginning of the nineteenth 
century the sympathy for her even in England was general. Such writers 
as Southey, Hallam, Sharon Turner, Carlyle, Landor, and, above all, De 
Quincey greeted the Maid with a tribute of respect which was not 
surpassed even in her own native land. Among her Catholic 
fellow-countrymen she had been regarded, even in her lifetime, as 
Divinely inspired".

She didn't become St Joan until 1920.

How the stiff-necks can get it so wrong !

So Oliver, be true to thyself. Don't sit on the fence, research ALL 
religious groups. You may come to the decision that it makes sense you 
should be an atheist. However, the alternative is you may find something 
in ANY religion which gives your soul the comfort you may yearn for. Go 
for it.

The God I believe in and my own particular brand of religion has been 
consistent in this thought; if you accept ANY religion my God will 
accept you; if you stay an agnostic, I can't know His mind, but he will 
be somewhat disapproving although you will still be acceptable; if you 
are an atheist, and providing you stance is not based on intellectual 
arrogance, again He will accept you. Now go look at the following 
Section X dealing with types of Baptism - you have the intellect to 
understand what is being stated :-

http://www.newadvent.org/cathen/02258b.htm

You will see that this is completely opposed to what Judson is offering. 
Not in the same words but when asked, "What about those in the past who 
didn't believe in the *right* God ?", his reply could be phrased, "Then, 
sorry, I guess they are shit out of luck".

I said he coppd-out. He completely missed the point. I was referring him 
to the same URL as above. But with the white heat intensity of his 
belief, and having got it right, he saw no necessity to even just take a 
curious peek.

That's his second cop-out. Did the same when I suggested his 
journalistic daughter should research Ann Coulter. Gave us some BS about 
her being a single Mom with two kids etc.... Well I'll make it real easy 
for him - see my next under McCarthyism.

Dubya just has to LOVE supporters like Judson.

Jimmy
```

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>> he saw no necessity to even just take a curious peek.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> follow the link.


If you're looking for a flashlight, you will continue looking into room 
after room, drawer after drawer, until you find one. Once you have the 
flashlight in your hand, turn it on to see that it works, you stop looking. 
Why would the search for God be any different? I found God, I know He is 
God, and He is as real to me as any other person. I would be nuts to keep 
looking, but you try to make it sound irrational. Try to remember, I do not 
have your misgivings. I have clear and convincing evidence that God is real, 
and Who the Bible says He is. I just can't "take it out and show it to you." 
Anyway, why would you object to my not looking any further, considering you 
aren't even looking? :-)
```

```
Richard wrote:
>>he saw no necessity to even just take a curious peek.
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Ok it looks interesting. What you don't know is that I have just 
received another paper on exactly the same topic - so I'll check that 
out first for comparison.

I'll certainly read it, but for starters take the following :-

"Many of the stories in that epic were eventually incorporated into the 
book of Genesis. Borrowed from the Epic of Gilgamesh are stories of the 
creation of man in a wondrous garden, the introduction of evil into a 
naive world, and the story of a great flood brought on by the wickedness 
of man, that flooded the whole world".

I think I'd probably buy that, but as you say this is a scholarly paper. 
I never did the university bit, but as I understand it a 'prof' wants a 
thesis backed up by references. Now if this guy doesn't provide them at 
the end of the article.....? Why should I accept what he says any more 
than you would accept what Judson writes.

Anyway - will get back to you. This CEO, chief cook and bottle washer 
has to digress. That 'Seasonal' event is coming up and I must break to 
make sausage rolls and mince pies. The puds are already done. Oooh yes - 
gotta make the brandy butter too ! She, Who Must be Obeyed, says the 
turkey is to be removed from the freezer to the fridge tomorrow so that 
we get sufficient 'meltdown' before stuffin' sausage meat up its arse !

Jimmy
```

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:rsZpf.39476$2k.25236@pd7tw1no...
> Oliver Wong wrote:
>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> ......?

    There's also the possibility (perhaps minute), that there exists a God, 
but that God only likes agnostic people, and anyone who is either a believer 
or an atheist will be sent to hell for all eternity. Or maybe there exists a 
God who only likes people who post on comp.lang.cobol. Or maybe He only 
likes people who are born on even days, and anyone born on an odd-numbered 
day will burn in hell for all eternity. Or maybe he just chooses randomly, 
and who you are or what you do really has no effect on whether He'll send 
you to heaven or hell.

    That's the "problem" with a lot of religions. They seem to 
(arbitrarily?) assume that He might be loving or kind or good. As far as I 
can tell, there really is no evidence to lead us to believe that might be 
true.

>
> To an extent I see agnosticism as a half-way house where you hedge your 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> as an agnostic. No harm done and you didn't have to make any decisions.

    (Let's ignore for this discussion the question of free will)

    I am not agnostic because I am unwilling to make a decision; rather, I 
have actively "decided" that I am agnostic. When someone says they are sure 
that God exists, I may be interested in hearing why, 'cause if God really 
does exists, I'd like to know that too. But more often than not (i.e. every 
time), it'll turn out that choosing to believe in God was really arbitrary, 
and there was no rational, logical evidence for making that choice (in my 
opinion, anyway). Similarly, if someone says that they are sure that God 
doesn't exist, I will want to know why the yare so sure. But I find that 
their explanations too are not always logically sound.

    The only thing we can say about God, I think, is that we know pretty 
much nothing about Him. And that is a very agnostic viewpoint.

[story of Joan of Arc snipped]

>
> So Oliver, be true to thyself. Don't sit on the fence, research ALL 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> for it.

    There are so many religious groups though! Researching ALL of them is 
pretty much infeasible. I figure if an omnipotent being wants me to believe 
in a certain religion, he'll find a way to make it happen. Until then, I've 
got plenty of other things to keep me occupied.

>
> The God I believe in and my own particular brand of religion has been 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.newadvent.org/cathen/02258b.htm

    I took a quick glance at it when you posted it earlier, elsewhere in 
this thread, and I was a bit confused about how it says there are 3 types of 
baptism, and then describes only two of them. I'll try to take a closer look 
at it later tonight.

    - Oliver
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1135123935.116102.310550@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com...
>
> Oliver Wong wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> I see no compelling evidence for God. Is that good enough?

    No. =)

    I'm not saying you are right or wrong, I'm just saying that to me, that 
isn't enough to be "sure". I'm using the relaxed definition of "sure" here 
(i.e. I'm avoiding the philosophical question of "How can we be sure of 
anything?") "There is no evidence" is a strong argument against the belief 
that God exists, but it is not a strong argument for the belief that God 
does not exist (in my opinion anyway).

    - Oliver
```

_(reply depth: 53)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-21T19:26:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G%hqf.906$km.877@edtnps89>`
- **References:** `<hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <NBHof.6648$ic1.5375@edtnps90> <ZiLof.128277$Gd6.98837@pd7tw3no> <tTGpf.9281$ic1.8745@edtnps90> <rsZpf.39476$2k.25236@pd7tw1no> <360qf.13552$wg4.10167@edtnps84> <1135123935.116102.310550@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2Rfqf.232$km.101@edtnps89> <vi4jq1pqpcqpr0n86q1h53fq2r2r31d1hb@4ax.com>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:vi4jq1pqpcqpr0n86q1h53fq2r2r31d1hb@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 21 Dec 2005 16:58:38 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> of True Belief)?

    I'm not sure I followed everything you said here, but as an attempt to 
answer your question...

    I think probabilities are a question of perspective. That is, the 
probability of something occuring changes as new information is gathered. Or 
the probabilities may be evaluated to two different values as seen by two 
different people simultaneously, as they may have different information.

    For example, given a dice, person A might assume the chance of rolling a 
3 if 1/6, but person B might know that the dice is rigged, that the 
probability of rolling a 3 is actually 2/3. Both persons are looking at the 
same dice at the same time, but come to different conclusions because they 
have different information.

    So given a *wrong* religion, it's unlikeliness is 100%, and in that 
sense, yes, all wrong religions are of equal unlikeliness (they are all 
100%).

    But if you don't know beforehand that a religion is wrong, then 
different religions might have different probabilities of turning out to be 
true, depending on what claims they make and what information you have.

    - Oliver
```

```
wrote:

>    But if you don't know beforehand that a religion is wrong, then 
>different religions might have different probabilities of turning out to be

So how does someone narrow down his search to the more probable
religions and other beliefs in the supernatural?    

Evidence in the natural world?    (This doesn't look promising)

The society that he happened to be born into?   (Most True Believers
use this route)

Which religion is popular with the rich and powerful? (That has
historical success).

Those which don't have a lot of hatred directed towards them?
(astrology, ghosts)

Those which are not morally repugnant? (those that believe in Heaven &
death vs those who believe in Heaven & Hell)

What kinds of statistical analysis can be done to find the more likely
roads to Truth?
```
