_(reply depth: 55)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-21T21:57:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ockqf.1197$km.553@edtnps89>`
- **References:** `<NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <NBHof.6648$ic1.5375@edtnps90> <ZiLof.128277$Gd6.98837@pd7tw3no> <tTGpf.9281$ic1.8745@edtnps90> <rsZpf.39476$2k.25236@pd7tw1no> <360qf.13552$wg4.10167@edtnps84> <1135123935.116102.310550@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2Rfqf.232$km.101@edtnps89> <vi4jq1pqpcqpr0n86q1h53fq2r2r31d1hb@4ax.com> <G%hqf.906$km.877@edtnps89> <gnbjq1laa8q76q93dki1718jsafp67aglq@4ax.com>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:gnbjq1laa8q76q93dki1718jsafp67aglq@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 21 Dec 2005 19:26:30 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
> wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> roads to Truth?

    In my case, I assume that logic holds, so any belief set (whether it be 
religious or otherwise) which is logically inconsistent, I can dimiss as 
having zero probability of being correct.

    Other than that, it's tough. The probability that God is as the 
Spaghetti Monster religion describes him is lower than some other religions, 
because the SMR is very specific in its description of God (e.g. that he is 
composed of spaghetti). This is based on the same principle that the 
probability that God has exactly 87 fingers is lower than the probabilty 
that God has between 50 and 200 fingers, which is lower than the probability 
that God has some integer number of fingers, which is lower than the 
probability that God may or may not have fingers, etc. The more specific you 
get, the lower your chances of being correct.

    - Oliver
```

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:JGnqf.48760$2k.34729@pd7tw1no...
> Oliver Wong wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Monte Carlo simulation to confirm there is/isn't a God :-)

    Rather than saying I've "moved", I think it's just two different views 
of the same belief system.

    It's not so much that I'm actually running a Monte Carlo simulation to 
determine the answer; rather, I'm acknowledging that different religions 
have different probabilities of correctness and therefore (by definition), 
some are more likely to be "the True Religion" than others.

    I'm just noting the probabilities; I haven't nescessarily just picked 
the most probable one and said "I chose to believe in this one!"

    - Oliver
```

```
That particular novel is reminiscent of Harrison.

Laumer's pre-stroke writings were quite enjoyable.   That said, my
favorite Retief story was his first - before they were comedic.

When I was young, I loved _Earthblood_.   Later on, I recognized that
it is part of the Van Vogt style of science fiction based upon the "we
are so superior" centricism that I now find uncomfortable.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kjjlq154oe3qijpv8n5iv69cjv1abmvjh8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <NBHof.6648$ic1.5375@edtnps90> <ZiLof.128277$Gd6.98837@pd7tw3no> <tTGpf.9281$ic1.8745@edtnps90> <rsZpf.39476$2k.25236@pd7tw1no> <360qf.13552$wg4.10167@edtnps84> <1135123935.116102.310550@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2Rfqf.232$km.101@edtnps89> <vi4jq1pqpcqpr0n86q1h53fq2r2r31d1hb@4ax.com> <G%hqf.906$km.877@edtnps89> <JGnqf.48760$2k.34729@pd7tw1no> <tGzqf.1510$m05.364@clgrps12>`

```
On Thu, 22 Dec 2005 15:32:41 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
wrote:

>    It's not so much that I'm actually running a Monte Carlo simulation to 
>determine the answer; rather, I'm acknowledging that different religions 
>have different probabilities of correctness and therefore (by definition), 
>some are more likely to be "the True Religion" than others.

Those with different amount of empirical evidence supporting them will
have different probabilities of being true.

After sorting those out, I suppose all of those with logical
inconsistencies could be separated into two camps:
1.  Those where the supernatural has the power to overcome logic.
2.  Those where the supernatural requires logical consistency.

And there isn't really a requirement that a deity be supernatural. If
God wanted to be measurable, testable, part of Nature - He could be
natural.

But deciding between Christianity and Islam is deciding between two
religions that have very similar philosophies about how we are
convinced about their veracity.   The major difference is the claim
that the Koran is written in a way to make man-made changes in it very
difficult.    Ask a Christian why Islam is wrong - and the answer is
invariably because it conflicts with Christianity.   (Or that
particular sects - like particular sects of Christianity - do bad
things).
```

- **Date:** 2005-12-22T20:54:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WnEqf.51541$2k.5252@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<tGzqf.1510$m05.364@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <NBHof.6648$ic1.5375@edtnps90> <ZiLof.128277$Gd6.98837@pd7tw3no> <tTGpf.9281$ic1.8745@edtnps90> <rsZpf.39476$2k.25236@pd7tw1no> <360qf.13552$wg4.10167@edtnps84> <1135123935.116102.310550@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2Rfqf.232$km.101@edtnps89> <vi4jq1pqpcqpr0n86q1h53fq2r2r31d1hb@4ax.com> <G%hqf.906$km.877@edtnps89> <JGnqf.48760$2k.34729@pd7tw1no> <tGzqf.1510$m05.364@clgrps12>`

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
> news:JGnqf.48760$2k.34729@pd7tw1no...
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Can't help feeling the words "correctness" and "probabilities" are 
inappropriate when referring to *any* religious belief. Religion is the 
'inner soul' or human spirit if you prefer, whether your "religion" is a 
deity, agnosticism or atheism.

The guiding thought has to be, "Be true to thyself". Unless you become a 
true monster, I think that's something we are all capable of. Regardless 
of how the outside world sees us, we are devoid of anything if we can't 
truly talk to ourselves, modestly accepting any good features we may 
have, or think we have, but at the same time acknowledging our 
weaknesses. Then of course comes the tricky part - correcting our 
weaknesses. The spiritual path you choose must be based on your 
innermost person.

Should one choose to follow a deity - I don't think that should be based 
on, 'Am I going to getting a bonus or frequent flyer points, somewhere 
down the road'. Rather, does a particular deity have meaningfulness to 
your own life values.

There's only one concrete way of proving the existence of any deity - 
your own death.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Agnoticism (WAS: Making money from Java)

_(reply depth: 57)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-12-22T14:07:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f55mq1ts20uhdd8ris2itvks9mck0ok2qm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <NBHof.6648$ic1.5375@edtnps90> <ZiLof.128277$Gd6.98837@pd7tw3no> <tTGpf.9281$ic1.8745@edtnps90> <rsZpf.39476$2k.25236@pd7tw1no> <360qf.13552$wg4.10167@edtnps84> <1135123935.116102.310550@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2Rfqf.232$km.101@edtnps89> <vi4jq1pqpcqpr0n86q1h53fq2r2r31d1hb@4ax.com> <G%hqf.906$km.877@edtnps89> <JGnqf.48760$2k.34729@pd7tw1no> <tGzqf.1510$m05.364@clgrps12> <WnEqf.51541$2k.5252@pd7tw1no>`

```
On Thu, 22 Dec 2005 20:54:14 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

>Can't help feeling the words "correctness" and "probabilities" are 
>inappropriate when referring to *any* religious belief. Religion is the 
>'inner soul' or human spirit if you prefer, whether your "religion" is a 
>deity, agnosticism or atheism.

Religious people have come around to this belief as a response to
queries about evidence.  There is nothing about religion in general
requiring an inner belief, nor is there anything in agnosticism nor
atheism requiring this.

It *is* necessary to say external evidence is not part of beliefs if
you are a true believer and external evidence is not available.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Agnoticism (WAS: Making money from Java)

_(reply depth: 57)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-12-23T22:11:34+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<411tacF1d3s0vU3@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <NBHof.6648$ic1.5375@edtnps90> <ZiLof.128277$Gd6.98837@pd7tw3no> <tTGpf.9281$ic1.8745@edtnps90> <rsZpf.39476$2k.25236@pd7tw1no> <360qf.13552$wg4.10167@edtnps84> <1135123935.116102.310550@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2Rfqf.232$km.101@edtnps89> <vi4jq1pqpcqpr0n86q1h53fq2r2r31d1hb@4ax.com> <G%hqf.906$km.877@edtnps89> <JGnqf.48760$2k.34729@pd7tw1no> <tGzqf.1510$m05.364@clgrps12> <WnEqf.51541$2k.5252@pd7tw1no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:WnEqf.51541$2k.5252@pd7tw1no...
> Oliver Wong wrote:
>> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
>
This strikes me as being a bit drastic, Jimmy. :-)

If your deity exists and requires worship, it's not going to get much after 
you're dead, is it? Unless, of course, you believe in an eternity of worship 
with said deity after popping your clogs... But then, if that were true, 
there is no requirement for religion in this life, because the next one is 
eternal and you can do all the worshipping the deity requires then. All you 
have to do is say "sorry" on your death bed, and there you are, strumming a 
harp and whispering vespers for all eternity. (Put like that, the party down 
at the Lake of Fire sounds almost appealing... :-))

Of course, if the deity doesn't exist, what happens when you're dead is 
irrelevant...(this is pretty close to my own position, oddly enough...) so 
you might as well do the best you can and get the most out of THIS life 
because there won't be another one.

I think your position on this spoils the whole game :-)

If you can't know until you're dead, it's all a bit academic to be pursuing 
a deity in this life, isn't it? Surely any sporting minded deity would give 
us a clue and play the game while we're alive?

Consider an archery contest where the targets are out of sight behind a 
hill. You have to shoot your arrows where you THINK the targets are. If you 
actually hit your designated target you are allowed to continue the game; if 
you don't, you will be severely punished and your bow and arrows will be 
confiscated. You cannot know whether you succeeded or not, until you walk 
over the hill. However the rules are that once you walk over the hill, you 
can never return. You have around 70 years within which to fire your arrows. 
You may opt not to play, but after 70 years you will be told that not 
playing is the same as missing, and carries the same penalty, as does 
deliberately firing your arrows in the wrong direction or simply not 
shooting them at all.

Would you enter such a pointless contest? I wouldn't. Even if it was 
revealed that not entering carried the same penalties as missing.

It can be argued that there is nothing to lose by entering, as the result 
can be no worse than if you didn't enter, and you MIGHT hit the target.

But for me, just playing the game gives it validity and makes it look 
reasonable, or fun or however games are supposed to look.

I'll wander over the hill when I get fed up with the beer tent on my side of 
it. I won't shoot my arrows because the game is stupid. (Besides, after a 
few beers, I might hurt somebody...)

If the Judge decides I am to be severely punished for not  playing, I'll 
make my case and accept the consequences.

At least I enjoyed the beer... :-)

Pete.
```

```
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
> news:WnEqf.51541$2k.5252@pd7tw1no...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

<snipped> All your stuff - but I had a good laugh :-)

Now what's your comment going to be if I replace that last sentence with :-

"I am totally happy with my firm belief in my God and don't need to 
await death to confirm his existence".  ???

1. Narnia/Hobbits - spent some fifteen minutes diving through the paper. 
Found a comment on Narnia in the Religious Section but that wasn't what 
I was after. The Section Editor had done a separate article with another 
author. That's what I was trying to find. I've left a message with the 
Entertainment Editor as it might have been in that Section.

2. I've indicated my prowess with reference to the sciences. I've 
concluded I must be Jewish ! Can't type the whole damn thing, but I 
found this hilarious :-

Gene Weingarten, Washington Post -
---------------------------------
"As a professional cynic, I'm not the type of person who has warm 
holiday stories for Christmas season. But this year, Providence 
intervened. Gather 'round kiddies, and hear the true tale of the beer 
that saved Thanksgiving.

Every year my wife and I give a Thanksgiving feast for family and 
friends. This year two days before the event, we noticed there was a 
small pool of water in the basement, beneath the furnace. We called our 
friend Mark the Contractor, whom we call every time something gores 
wrong in the house. This is because I was born into the Jewish culture, 
and for all our many fine qualities, Jewish men are not known for their 
ability to fix things, (with the exception of Arnold Rothstein, who 
fixed the 1919 World Series............"
--------------------------------------
It gets funnier from thereon in. Mark doesn't like what he finds and 
suggests they contact a plumber.
----------------------------------------
Enter Steve the Plumber who diagnosed the problem as a blockage in the 
main sewage pipe line from our house. He tried to snake it out without 
success, encountering mud. This, he said, was bad news. The main line 
out of the house seemed to be broken.

OK I said cheerfully. Fix it ! Steve, who, like Mark, is not Jewish 
found this hilarious. He said that, in all probability, our front yard 
would need to be dug up by a backhoe in a procedure that could take many 
days and cost many thousands of dollars. In the meantime, he explained, 
we could not run any water or flush any toilets.

"B-ut I have to cook for 15 people !", my wife said. "We can't poop !", 
I said. etc..........."

"Steve", I said resignedly, "would you like a beer ?".
---------------------------------------------------------

Seems the beer set Steve up to the challenge. He told them to run every 
tap in the house, continuously flushing toilets, while like a madman in 
the basement, with an ordinary plumber's helper, began to plunge for all 
he was worth. It worked.
----------------------------------------------------------
"And so it was kiddies, that Thanksgiving was saved".


3. OK so I'm no scientist, but following you and Oliver on  time and 
space. (I don't care if 'Ollie' never codes a line of COBOL - he's just 
enjoyable to read). My simple mind says, 'this ain't kosher'. Ignore the 
'religious' bit - I just can't buy the concept of Nothingness existed 
before Somethingness. Many moons ago I recall Carl Sagan demonstrating, 
I think, the Big Bang concept in a large flask. He created 'life' if you 
like - but he still needed an *existing* source to energize that life.


Jimmy
```

```
 

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:d6Yqf.173179$Gd6.77470@pd7tw3no...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
…[85 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Thanks for the above, it was amusing and I enjoyed it.

It's really OK if you don't buy the idea of "something" coming from 
"nothing", Jimmy. Many people don't. They are not stupid or ignorant. It is 
a very difficult concept and it is controversial. It took me around two 
years and a great deal of readng to get my ahead around it. As a result, I 
DO buy it, but I certainly wouldn't expect or require everyone else to...

The secret is that there is something else besides the observable reality 
(it is called virtuality) and at a certain threshold (called the Planck 
length and represented by a small "h" with a bar over it - "h bar"), things 
cross from reality to virtuality and vice versa, depending on random 
fluctuations at a quantum level. In effect, particles and energy (the same 
thing, really) pop in and out of "existence" all the time without us being 
aware of it. I certainly believe that everything is connected, because at 
one time the entire known universe was contained in an unimaginably tiny 
space (and some people might call this the palm of "God"s hand...).

But I also believe the universe is primarily a "star factory" and we are 
here simply by accident (in an almost infinite universe the most unlikely 
events will happen) with about as much relevance as a tiny patch of mould on 
the wallpaper near the floor, in the farthest corner of the Albert Hall. It 
is only our own arrogance that leads us to believe the whole Cosmos was 
"created" for our benefit. The universe daily goes about its business with 
no concept of "good" or "evil" or "fair" or "unfair", or any other 
Man-created concept; just random fluctuations in fields which are obeying 
the laws of Physics (which we don't quite fully understand yet, but are 
working on and making progress...)

I understand and respect your faith, even though I don't share it. My own 
faith is of a different nature. Whatever works for people is good.

Tolerance and goodwill are the two things that can bridge the gaps.

Pete.
```

```
Well I have heard of the this concept but I do not pretend to understand it. 
At one time it was thought that space was filled with and "ether" but I 
thought that was disproved. Is this the new ether? When I hear the word 
"virtual" I tend to think 'simulation' i.e. using one resource to simulate 
another. Maybe the universe is a humongous holodeck after all.

I was reading Scientific American at the doctors office today and they said 
these virtual particles appear in pairs. They think it is possible for them 
to pop into existence near the event horizon of a black hole such that one 
is sucked into the black whole and the other escapes. They called it the 
Hawking effect and said if it happened often enough that the black hole 
could evaporate. A lot of this is very hard to visualize and understand just 
from words.  I am sure if a person can understand the mathematics that it is 
more precise but very hard to put into language. I do not understand how the 
universe has no center and no edges even in the past when it was much 
smaller.  Also it is expanding but it is not into anything else. I hope when 
I retire I can read more about this stuff.
```

```
Well I did not get to read the entire article as my time in the waiting room 
was short. IIRC there are some incompatibilities between general relativity 
and quantum mechanics.  They want to develop a theory of quantum gravity but 
they are having trouble. (As an aside I saw a program on string theory where 
some versions of string theory solve some of these inconsistencies at least 
at the mathematical level. But the strings are so small that there is little 
hope of ever confirming or denying string theory based on experimental 
observation.  Some physicists don't like string theory because they claim it 
cannot be verified. But who knows what the future may hold.) Anyway back to 
quantum gravity. At scales down near the Planck length space is supposed to 
be highly curved but they have not had much success observing this in their 
attempts at a theory of quantum gravity, so some scientists have taken this 
"space as a fluid" approach to try and make some advances.  Sorry but I 
cannot remember the exact name of the sound/fluid theory that already exists 
that they are making analogies from.
```

```
news:1135626020.892351.45310@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com...
>
> Pete Dashwood wrote:

Yes, it might. Or then again it might not :-) I believe that within the 
dimensions we have postulated (11 of them), space does not entirely behave 
as a liquid. There are similarities, but the differences are more important. 
Sometimes the WRONG analogy is worse than no analogy at all.

Einstein sat in on one of his garduate students discussing Riemann geometry 
and the curvature of space. The student used the analogy of a teacup (I have 
heard others use a trumpet). After the lecture Einstein took him aside and 
remarked gently that the analogy was better served by a cup and saucer...

Pete
```

```
"charles hottel" <jghottel@yahoo.com> wrote in message 
news:64908$43acbfc1$4f9c609$19616@DIALUPUSA.NET...
>
> Well I have heard of the this concept but I do not pretend to understand 
> it. At one time it was thought that space was filled with and "ether" but 
> I thought that was disproved.

It was found to be unneccesary in order to explain the wave nature of light.

The question was: "If light is a wave, what is it that is waving?" We now 
know that light is a both a wave and a particle ("wavicle" has been used...) 
and behaves like whatever one you want it to.

No, really... :-).

If you do an experiment to prove it is a wave (Young's famous "two slit" 
experiment - Richard Feynmann claimed that if you could understand this 
experiement fully, you could grasp all of Physics), it will be found to be a 
wave; if you do an experiment to prove it is a particle (Einstein received 
his Nobel prize not for Relativity, but for showing that "particles" of 
light could knock electrons out of certain metals and the emission of the 
electrons depended on the wavelength and intensity of the light), it will be 
found to be a particle. What is it REALLY? A continuous, self propagating, 
interwoven, oscillation of an electric and a magnetic field, that has 
certain probabilities of occurring, and can be fully described by 8 
equations formulated by James Clerk Maxwell in the 19th century. No need for 
"ether".

>Is this the new ether?

No.

>When I hear the word "virtual" I tend to think 'simulation' i.e. using one 
>resource to simulate another. Maybe the universe is a humongous holodeck 
>after all.
>
Maybe...:-). Maybe it is many things, depending on the observer. No one 
really knows what matter and energy are doing when they are not observed...

> I was reading Scientific American at the doctors office today and they 
> said these virtual particles appear in pairs. They think it is possible 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> hole could evaporate.

Stephen Hawking and Roger Penrose did the seminal work on this. Hawking was 
the first to question the idea that nothing could escape from a black hole. 
He sums it up in his chapter "Black holes ain't so black". (See - "A Brief 
History of Time") The separation of the particle pairs across the event 
horizon of a black hole, as you describe, predicts that black holes should 
"radiate". It is not yet established whether they do or not (as far as I am 
aware), but my money would be on Hawking. His expostulation makes sense.

The black hole would not evaporate, it would explode in a burst of energy. 
It is the negative energy of these virtual particles flowing into the black 
hole that causes a loss in mass of the BH. As the mass of the BH decreases, 
the event horizon gets smaller, but this decrease in the entropy of the BH 
is compensated for by the entropy of the "emitted" radiation , so the second 
law is never violated.

The lower the mass of the BH, the higher its temperature so, as it's mass 
continues to decrease it gets hotter, faster. This causes more mass to leak 
away (evaporation). What happens when the mass approaches zero is not 
entirely clear but the best guess is a tremendous emission of the remaining 
mass, in the form of a spectacular explosion.


> A lot of this is very hard to visualize and understand just from words.  I 
> am sure if a person can understand the mathematics that it is more precise 
> but very hard to put into language. I do not understand how the universe 
> has no center and no edges even in the past when it was much smaller.

It has an origin, which is kind of like a centre. As for no edges: Consider 
a balloon inflating. It is not infinite, yet it is boundless. (There is no 
boundary or edge, and you could wander all over it without constraint, just 
like the planet Earth.)

>  Also it is expanding but it is not into anything else.

That's right. It is the SPACE (and, along with it the time...) which is 
actually expanding, not the universe expanding into space. It is very hard 
for our three dimensional brains to imagine "no space" or even "curved 
space", yet that is the reality of our universe. The Big Bang was not an 
explosion that occurred in space; it was the creation of space and time 
(which have been expanding ever since).

After Einstein gave a famous lecture about the curvature of space 
(Relativity had predicted the warping of space by gravitational fields 
asociated with matter - Matter tells space how to bend; space tells matter 
how to move. The orbits of the planets are actually "straight" lines through 
curved space...), a member of the audience is supposed to have said: "That's 
all very well Professor, but if I go to the "edge" of this expanding space, 
and stick out my umbrella, where is it?".

Einstein replied: "Sticking in the other side".

It's a bit like the infinite field of Space Invaders... as things disappear 
off the bottom of the screen, they reappear at the top. (Similarly with the 
sides.)

> I hope when I retire I can read more about this stuff.
>
Me too :-)

Pete.
```

```
I can understand that a two dimensional creature on the surface of an 
expanding balloon could find distances increasing due to expansion of the 
balloon. I can also understand how they could walk all around the balloon 
and not find any edges and not know about the center of the balloon because 
they have no experience of a third dimension. But I think the main reason 
that I can understand this is because my mind is imagining it from a three 
dimensional perspective. From that perspective there are edges and a center. 
My problem is visualizing this in our three or more multi-dimensional 
universe. One article I read claimed that there are no edges and no center 
even from a four dimensional perspective. I have always had problem with 
this type of thing. I once took an advanced calculus class where the book 
talked about a four dimensional hypercube and the author suggested that even 
though we cannot visualized it we could visualize its three dimensional 
shadow, but I found myself unable to visualize it. I do understand the 
mathematics as you just add another variable for each dimension and 
manipulate according to the mathematical rules.
```

```
charles hottel wrote:
> I can understand that a two dimensional creature on the surface of an
> expanding balloon could find distances increasing due to expansion of the
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

Perhaps I can help (my apologies if I muddy the water a little).

Consider the surface of the earth. At first this appears to be a flat
surface but observations will lead to the realization that we live on a
sphere. The angles of a triangle on a sheet of paper add up to 180
degrees. If you inscribe a similar triangle on a globe then the angles
do not add up to 180 degrees (each angle is slightly smaller than the
paper angle equivalent). This is also true of space. On the human scale
it is flat but on the universal scale it is curved. Consequently (he
says, grossly oversimplifying), on earth you can start at any point and
go away from it and find yourself coming back to the point of
departure. So too on the universal scale.

The fun then starts when you consider the extra dimensions and realise
that they might be a lot closer than we realise.
```

```
I believe that the sum of the angles on a spherical triangle is always
*greater* than 180, not less than. In fact, a 1/8 sphere, with corners
at the pole, and 90 degrees apart on the equator, is a triangle with
three 90 degree angles.

Donald
```

```
> <jghottel@yahoo.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It isn't so much that "space curves back on it itself"; rather that space 
itself is (roughly) spherical (and expanding...).  Anything emanating from a 
point on the "edge" of it will eventually describe a great circle and return 
to the point where it originated (given all the things required to allow 
this to happen..)

Pete.
```

```
On Thu, 29 Dec 2005 01:58:21 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>> Please describe what you mean when you say "space curves back on
>> itself".
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>this to happen..)

It is expanding - and there are galaxies which are far enough away
from us that their current light will never reach us (we see them from
when the universe was smaller).    People have played with the idea
that light from an object will eventually make it back to that object
after going around the universe, but have found no evidence to support
this idea.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41fpfaF1do3leU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<tGzqf.1510$m05.364@clgrps12> <WnEqf.51541$2k.5252@pd7tw1no> <411tacF1d3s0vU3@individual.net> <d6Yqf.173179$Gd6.77470@pd7tw3no> <413attF1cpb5dU3@individual.net> <64908$43acbfc1$4f9c609$19616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <414o7nF1d2adfU3@individual.net> <bc342$43ad63f3$4f9c637$23393@DIALUPUSA.NET> <1135627181.624068.111770@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <233e5$43b15477$4f9c61f$29540@DIALUPUSA.NET> <a2u2r1pejlcsth4tj7ppvbt3jjsvcct5ar@4ax.com> <41fgfjF1drp9tU1@individual.net> <0b85r157lipsmptcr4vmih3oik3n8hu7qb@4ax.com>`

```
 

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:0b85r157lipsmptcr4vmih3oik3n8hu7qb@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 29 Dec 2005 01:58:21 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> this idea.

There may be no real evidence (it is pretty difficult to set up an 
experiment that will take around 15 billion years to confirm or deny :-)), 
but there is a considerable amount of theory to suggest that such may be the 
case.

Given that a photon has infinite time (being massless, it can travel at the 
speed of light and time therefore stops for it), it will, (according to 
Feynmann), cover every possible path in the universe. This includes the one 
which takes it back to where it originated. Of course, many things can 
happen to a photon in infinite time; one of them could be popping out of 
existence due to quantum field fluctuations... or it could be absorbed into 
another particle and never emitted... there are many reasons why it might 
not arrive back where it started from, but it certainly cannot be proven 
that it won't, if nothing untoward happens to it.

Pete.
```

```
In article <a2u2r1pejlcsth4tj7ppvbt3jjsvcct5ar@4ax.com>,
 Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> On Tue, 27 Dec 2005 09:49:29 -0500, "charles hottel"
> <jghottel@yahoo.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> itself".

I'm a late arrival to this thread, but I will perhaps make an attempt.

There are two possible universe styles the "open" and the "closed".

The idea of the "closed" universe is such that the gravity of the 
universe pulls the expanding edges back into itself.  This configuration 
will eventually collapse upon itself.

The idea of the "open" universe is that the gravity generated by the 
mass of the universe is not enough to pull the entire thing back in upon 
itself.  Imagine picking up a pancake by placing a finger in the center.  
The sides of the pancake would drape down and form a saddle shape -- 
this is the "open" universe.  It will continue to expand, but its own 
gravity will distort its shape.

In both configurations, it is "possible" that using the "closed" ball 
shape or the "open" saddle shape it might be possible to travel from 
point A to point B faster than the presumptive linear distance.

In either configuration there is no way to ever reach the edge, you 
would either be unable to keep pace (open) or collapse just before you 
got there (closed).
```

```
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>will eventually collapse upon itself.

Black holes are an example of a third possibility - closed universes
within a greater universe.   

While it doesn't fit current theory, one could have clusters of
galaxies forming a closed universe if the gravity was right - with
other clusters forming another closed universe.     We would need lots
more mass in those clusters for it to work, but it's a fun idea.
```

```

"charles hottel" <jghottel@yahoo.com> wrote in message 
news:bc342$43ad63f3$4f9c637$23393@DIALUPUSA.NET...
>I can understand that a two dimensional creature on the surface of an 
>expanding balloon could find distances increasing due to expansion of the 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>dimension and manipulate according to the mathematical rules.

    There's this joke about a mathematician and a physicists attending a 
lecture. The lecturer goes on about terribly abstract concepts, mostly 
centered around a 37 dimensional figure. The physicist is frantically taking 
notes, scratching his head, and furrowing his brow, while the mathematician 
just sits calmly, arms crossed, occasionally nodding in agreement.

    At the end of the lecture, the physicists ask the mathematician a few 
questions, and soon realizes that the mathematician had no trouble 
understanding the concepts involved at all. He asks the mathematician how he 
was able to visualize a 37 dimensional space. The mathematician responded 
that he simply visualized an n-dimensional space, and lets n = 37.

    The trick that I've heard and use, is don't bother trying to visualize 
spaces of dimensions 5 or higher. You might be able to manage 4 dimensions, 
by thinking 3 spaces plus time (i.e. an "animation"), or you might be 
satisfied to limiting your visualizations to 3 dimensions or lower. Just try 
to understand the concepts in 0, 1, 2 and 3 dimensions, use induction to 
convince yourself that whatever concept you believe in will also be true in 
arbitrarily higher dimensions, and just leave it at that.

    The "shadow" trick you mention, I assume has to do with the idea that a 
3 dimensional object casts a 2 dimensional shadow onto a 2 dimensional 
surface, and so I suppose you could extend that to a n dimensional object 
casting an n-1 dimensional shadow onto an n-1 dimensional surface. I prefer 
to think in terms of "slices" than "shadows". Take a 3 dimensional object, 
and imagine taking a (3D) knife and cutting an infinitely thin slice out of 
it. You get a 2D object. If parts of the original object were "hollow", then 
you might not end up with a single continuous objects, put rather what 
appears to be several small 2D objects. For example, a slice through a donut 
might look like 2 circles that aren't attached to each other. When I try to 
visualize hypercubes, I usually visualize 3D slices of it. I usually don't 
get very far though.

    A 1D creature moving about a line might ask how it's possible for his 
universe to be without edges or centres? A 2D creature would say obviously 
that the "line" that the 1D creature is moving about must form a circle. But 
then the 2D creature moving about a surface will turn around and ask how 
it's possible for his universe to be without edges or centres. A 3D creature 
would say that this surface must be a sphere. Then the 3D creature moving 
about a volume will turn around and ask how it's possible for his universe 
to be without edges of centres. And this is where I stop visualizing, and 
just assume that this process can continue indefinitely onto to arbitrarily 
high dimensions.

    - Oliver
```

```
at the centre and go outwards in a straight line. With curved space
then you would invitably find yourself travelling a 'great circle
route' and heading back to the centre without having reached a
boundary.
```

```
I suppose that one could explain the radiative effect of a bar fire by
a similar theory. I do not like the idea of virtual particles
spontaneously genereating with one falling in (in a straight line
through curved space :-)  ) and one radiating away. I thought that the
information radiates at the poles (much as with quasars) and not at the
equator. If it is escaping from the poles then I would expect that is
an artefact of a spinning black hole expelling its' contents. But then
I'm not a rocket/nuclear/particle physicist.
```

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41bbmrF1dhq4uU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <NBHof.6648$ic1.5375@edtnps90> <ZiLof.128277$Gd6.98837@pd7tw3no> <tTGpf.9281$ic1.8745@edtnps90> <rsZpf.39476$2k.25236@pd7tw1no> <360qf.13552$wg4.10167@edtnps84> <1135123935.116102.310550@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2Rfqf.232$km.101@edtnps89> <vi4jq1pqpcqpr0n86q1h53fq2r2r31d1hb@4ax.com> <G%hqf.906$km.877@edtnps89> <JGnqf.48760$2k.34729@pd7tw1no> <tGzqf.1510$m05.364@clgrps12> <WnEqf.51541$2k.5252@pd7tw1no> <411tacF1d3s0vU3@individual.net> <d6Yqf.173179$Gd6.77470@pd7tw3no> <413attF1cpb5dU3@individual.net> <64908$43acbfc1$4f9c609$19616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <1135625916.744635.151910@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
 

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1135625916.744635.151910@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> charles hottel wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> through curved space :-)  ) and one radiating away.

The actual pathway of the particle doesn't matter (it could be curved or 
straight or a random squiggle); what is important is that is captured and 
added to the negative energy sea...

> I thought that the
> information radiates at the poles (much as with quasars) and not at the
> equator.

The "information" (good choice of word) does not "radiate" at all. The term 
"Hawking radiation" is a misnomer. The particles only APPEAR to radiate. As 
they were never inside the BH in the first place, they cannot radiate from 
it. A more "correct" picture would be like the event horizon "clipping" 
particle pairs like some giant lawnmower, and placing one particle in the 
grass catcher and the other on the ground. A lawnmower often appears to be 
emitting  the grass that misses the catcher, but we know it isn't really.

> If it is escaping from the poles then I would expect that is
> an artefact of a spinning black hole expelling its' contents. But then
> I'm not a rocket/nuclear/particle physicist.
>
Spinning BHs have a somewhat different behaviour. It was first described by 
a Kiwi Physicist named Roy Kerr in 1963. Until then it was believed that 
black holes could only be formed by the collapse of a perfectly spherical 
body, and the "hole" itself must be perfectly spherical. Kerr showed that 
only a rotation of zero produces a perfectly spherical body. Spin changes 
the shape and the consequent "emission" characteristics. As already 
desribed, a BH (spinning or otherwise) does not expel its contents.


Pete.
```

- **References:** `<rsZpf.39476$2k.25236@pd7tw1no> <360qf.13552$wg4.10167@edtnps84> <1135123935.116102.310550@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <2Rfqf.232$km.101@edtnps89> <vi4jq1pqpcqpr0n86q1h53fq2r2r31d1hb@4ax.com> <G%hqf.906$km.877@edtnps89> <JGnqf.48760$2k.34729@pd7tw1no> <tGzqf.1510$m05.364@clgrps12> <WnEqf.51541$2k.5252@pd7tw1no> <411tacF1d3s0vU3@individual.net> <d6Yqf.173179$Gd6.77470@pd7tw3no> <413attF1cpb5dU3@individual.net> <64908$43acbfc1$4f9c609$19616@DIALUPUSA.NET>`

```
On Fri, 23 Dec 2005 22:26:05 -0500, "charles hottel"
<jghottel@yahoo.com> wrote:

>Well I have heard of the this concept but I do not pretend to understand it. 
>At one time it was thought that space was filled with and "ether" but I 
>thought that was disproved. 

Actually, the theory of ether made sense as our experiments with other
wave types required a medium.   But the Michelson-Morley experiment
didn't support this common sense view and what math we had to describe
the effects we saw didn't require ether, so we basically just dropped
it as being a useless concept.

> Is this the new ether? When I hear the word 
>"virtual" I tend to think 'simulation' i.e. using one resource to simulate 
>another. Maybe the universe is a humongous holodeck after all.

The word virtual is much older than computers, and its old meanings
still apply.
```

```
three had him having a discussion with a mathematician about how real
God was for him (basically how strongly did he believe expressed as a
percentage likelihood). He scored 94%. If we can not express our faith
as a percentage likelihood of God existing then we can at least express
it as a personal percentage.

As for Monte Carlo, surely spread betting is safest?
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:%X1qf.41941$2k.6087@pd7tw1no...
> Oliver Wong wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> bank on it. Still it has happened.

    Well, I don't like what "Cover Your Ass" implies about my motives for 
believing what I believe, but I suppose that *is* one way of interpreting my 
position. Here's the analogy I could come up with:

    A: What's the weather gonna be like tomorrow?
    B: It's gonna be sunny. I can tell 'cause whenever my knee tingles like 
this, it always ends up being sunny.
    C: It's gonna rain. I used my tarot cards to check, and they say rain.
    D: Snow. Definitely snow. I can feel it in my soul.
    Oliver: I don't know.
    A: Wait, what do you mean you don't know?
    Oliver: I'm not a meteorologist. I don't know what the weather is going 
to be like.
    B: Well, you have to choose SOMETHING.
    Oliver: Why? If I say "I think it's going to [some weather condition]", 
that would be lying, because I don't actually think that. If I want to be 
honest to myself, and honest to those around me, I must say that I don't 
actually know what the weather is going to be like.
    A: Fine, sit on the fence, but I think that's a cowardly position! Me, I 
definitely think it's gonna hail. I mean, smell that air. Doesn't that smell 
like hail to you?
    etc.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Agnoticism (WAS: Making money from Java)

_(reply depth: 52)_

- **From:** "Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>

```
news:8Xfqf.262$km.18@edtnps89...
>

But if one assumes that A asks the question for a reason other than filling 
time, then perhaps an educated answer would be appropriate?
For example, you could have said (say the question is by a farmer suffering 
in a drought):

I am not a meteorologist, but this past Sunday one told me that he had it on 
good authority that the meteoroligist had seen evidence to indicate that 
there was no doubt that it would be sunny if you didn't do the rain dance. 
If you did the rain dance, then it would rain, but only if the rain gods 
thought that you had done it with real integrity.

DU

DU
```

```

"Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:djgqf.45576$6e.21018@tornado.tampabay.rr.com...
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
> news:8Xfqf.262$km.18@edtnps89...
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
> filling time, then perhaps an educated answer would be appropriate?

    Yes, so in this story, let us assume that I know absolutely nothing 
about meteorology (which is pretty close to true).

> For example, you could have said (say the question is by a farmer 
> suffering in a drought):
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> gods thought that you had done it with real integrity.

    Okay, but if I had NOT met an meteorologist this past Sunday, saying the 
above would obviously be lying.

    If you want, we could change the analogy to "What value do you think 
will result if I roll this die?" and me saying "I don't know, but probably a 
value between 1 and 6", "But which value between 1 to 6?", etc.

    The point of the analogy was to illustrate that it is not "fear of being 
wrong" that makes me not believe in any one particular religion (or 
atheism), but lack of information. I say that I do not know the answer as to 
whether or not God exists, because that is the statement which is closest to 
the truth.

    As for educated guesses, I can make statements like the Bible is 
self-contradicting, so I do not think it should be taken literally, but 
otherwise I am unsure as to wether God, if he exists, is anything like how 
he is typically portrayed in Christian religion, for example.

    - Oliver
```

```
Meteorologist = Preacher

again.

>    The point of the analogy was to illustrate that it is not "fear of 
> being wrong" that makes me not believe in any one particular religion (or 
> atheism), but lack of information. I say that I do not know the answer as

> closest to the truth.

than another? Are you leaning on your fence?

Just out of curiousity - you live in a very logical system - if I may...

What kind of weather would you want it do be?

Consider your answer if the above question was not a literal question.


>    As for educated guesses, I can make statements like the Bible is 
> self-contradicting, so I do not think it should be taken literally, but 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```

"Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:ksiqf.45596$6e.1511@tornado.tampabay.rr.com...
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
> news:15iqf.934$km.618@edtnps89...
…[77 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rain Dance = Accept Jesus

    I think you lost me completely here... I'm trying to justify my decision 
of being agnostic. I could have said what you have suggested above, but I 
didn't. And I explained why I didn't. The fact that I could have done 
something else doesn't change the fact that I didn't do that something else, 
and it doesn't change the reasons for my doing what I did.

    So I figure I must have missed the point entirely.

>
>>    If you want, we could change the analogy to "What value do you think 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> again.

    Right. But you and I are different. I maybe be true that YOU think it'll 
be 3 (at that particular moment in time), but it wouldn't be true that I 
think it'll be 3. So I'd say "I don't know", because that's the truth. If I 
said "3", that'd be lying. Which is why I said "I don't know" as opposed to 
"3" (or any other value for that matter).

>
>>    The point of the analogy was to illustrate that it is not "fear of 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Then why use the word "minute" probabilty?

    More specifically, I described the probability of God only liking 
agnostics as being "perhaps minute"; it was a defense tactic to avoid the 
argument "But the probably of God only liking agnostics is so minute!"; I 
admit that it may be minute, but I wanted to emphasize that it was non-zero.

> Is one type of God more probably than another? Are you leaning on your 
> fence?

    Yes. I coincidentally made a reply to Howard on that very topic just a 
few moments ago; the more specific your guesses are (e.g. God only likes 
people born such that when they were born, the universal unix time [seconds 
from the epoch] is a prime number; this implies he hates everyone born 
before the unix epoch), the lower the probability that they are correct.

>
> Just out of curiousity - you live in a very logical system - if I may...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Consider your answer if the above question was not a literal question.

    I don't like snow.

    - Oliver
```

```
"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> did not get to see any evidence.

Correct. This is part of the terrible consequences of mankind's disobedience 
to a holy God, and why it is so important for Christians to tell everyone 
they possibly can about God's free offer of salvation.

>>>> However, there other Scriptures (I'm tired and won't look up all the 
>>>> references now, but I will later if you want) that indicate that God 
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> with many.

It seems you are making a pretty low estimate of what "few stripes" means, 
considering that "many stripes" means eternal suffering. "Few stripes" may 
mean eternal suffering at a lower level, or eternal discomfort or sorrow or 
isolation. I agree that what you describe is the best way to witness when 
possible. But it is better, is it not, to give people at least a chance at 
being saved and having eternal joy and happiness, rather than to simply 
abandon all of them? To "take our goodies and run" so to speak? And what 
about us taking the responsibility of deciding who might be willing to 
believe? Wow! We're accused of being arrogant as it is. Imagine what would 
happen if it got out that we were deciding who was 'worthy' even to hear! I 
shudder at the thought! Only God knows who might believe, and He tells us to 
spread the Gospel diligently, as best we can. And God, unlike us, *does* 
know what the "few stripes" would be. He's certainly in a better position to 
decide. :-)
```

- **References:** `<cvkuo154sjfungkb2jgjmq2q28nl4tu9aj@4ax.com> <1133550230.780919.303800@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <i21kf.47144$6y4.15970@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqd1b$259m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <NBHof.6648$ic1.5375@edtnps90> <LB_of.437$RZ6.247@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
    This post will, for the sake of argument, assume that Judson's beliefs 
(as I understand them) are correct.

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:LB_of.437$RZ6.247@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> tell everyone they possibly can about God's free offer of salvation.

    In this case, the Indians, having never heard of this concept of a 
Christian God, did not actually disobey anyone! If they are punished, it is 
unfairly, and this seems to contradict the image of God being kind and 
loving.

>>    Again, assuming your beliefs are true, then you should ONLY share the 
>> gospel with people who you expect will believe in it. In other words, 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> simply abandon all of them?

    Yes. The best is if everyone goes to heaven. Failing that, second best 
is as few people receive "many stripes" as possible. As you say, some people 
will simply refuse to believe in God, no matter what you say. Certainly we 
should never tell these people about God's rules, to spare them from the 
many stripes. Some people might believe anything you say. Then it is 
beneficial to tell those people to believe in God, for they will receive no 
stripes at all. For everyone in between, you have to figure out for yourself 
whether it is best to tell them or not.

> To "take our goodies and run" so to speak? And what about us taking the 
> responsibility of deciding who might be willing to believe? Wow! We're 
> accused of being arrogant as it is. Imagine what would happen if it got 
> out that we were deciding who was 'worthy' even to hear! I shudder at the 
> thought!

    Don't let the word get out then! Once you're sure someone is willing to 
accept God into their heart, you can tell them about God's rules, and then 
explain why, up until this time, you had hid this secret from them. They 
will surely understand that you were trying to spare them from the "many 
stripes" fate until you were sure you could bring them into the "zero 
stripe" fate; and then you can teach them to do the same, and so only people 
who would be willing to believe in God will know of the rule, and everyone 
will either receive few stripes, or no stripes.

    Alternatively, let the people choose. Tell them that you think that they 
are currently destined to receive "few stripes". If they are willing, you 
can show them how to achieve a "zero stripes" destiny, but if you tell them 
the secret, and it turns out that they are unwilling, they will receive 
"many stripes". Let them decide whether they are willing to hear this secret 
or not, without actually telling them what the secret is. Then no one can 
accuse you of arrogance.

    - Oliver
```

```
"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
>    This post will, for the sake of argument, assume that Judson's beliefs 
> (as I understand them) are correct.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> loving.

I'll let the Bible answer that:

(Romans 1:18-25) "For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all 
ungodliness and unrighteousness of men, who suppress the truth in 
unrighteousness, because what may be known of God is manifest in them, for 
God has shown it to them. For since the creation of the world His invisible 
attributes are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, 
even His eternal power and Godhead, so that they are without excuse, 
because, although they knew God, they did not glorify Him as God, nor were 
thankful, but became futile in their thoughts, and their foolish hearts were 
darkened. Professing to be wise, they became fools, and changed the glory of 
the incorruptible God into an image made like corruptible man-and birds and 
four-footed animals and creeping things. Therefore God also gave them up to 
uncleanness, in the lusts of their hearts, to dishonor their bodies among 
themselves, who exchanged the truth of God for the lie, and worshiped and 
served the creature rather than the Creator, who is blessed forever. Amen."

>>>    Again, assuming your beliefs are true, then you should ONLY share the 
>>> gospel with people who you expect will believe in it. In other words, 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> figure out for yourself whether it is best to tell them or not.

Again, you are assuming a very low "punishment level" for "few stripes." 
Until you know exactly what that punishment is, I don't think your 
conclusion can be justified. :-)

>> To "take our goodies and run" so to speak? And what about us taking the 
>> responsibility of deciding who might be willing to believe? Wow! We're 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> no one can accuse you of arrogance.

Want to bet? :-) Can you imagine what these discussions would look like, if 
any of you folks had an inkling that I was selecting who would get to 
receive a way to have an eternal life of boundless joy and happiness, with 
no pain, no grief, no tears, etc., by *my own decision*? :-)  Anyway, I 
don't have that choice, followers of Jesus have been commanded to spread the 
Gospel. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

- **References:** `<cvkuo154sjfungkb2jgjmq2q28nl4tu9aj@4ax.com> <i21kf.47144$6y4.15970@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqd1b$259m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <NBHof.6648$ic1.5375@edtnps90> <LB_of.437$RZ6.247@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <x3Hpf.9284$ic1.7664@edtnps90> <7u2qf.19463$k76.12837@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:7u2qf.19463$k76.12837@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

    Well, we can use game theory to make some quantative analysis of the 
situation (http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Game_Theory):

First, define some names for values:

fs: few stripes, the "pleasure" of receiving few stripes. Since stripes are 
actually painful, this value is negative (i.e. negative pleasure means 
positive pain).

Ms: Many stripes, the pleasure of receiving many stripes. This is a value 
that is presumably MORE negative than fs.

ns: no stripes, the pleasure of receiving no stripes at all. Presumably, 
this implies going to heaven and may be a very large positive value.

CoB: Chance of Belief. A value between 0 and 1 indicating the probability 
that a particular person will believe in your religion if you explain that 
religion to them.

e0: Expected pleasure if you don't tell them about your religion. This is 
one of the values we want to solve for.

e1: Expected pleasure if you do tell them about your relgion. This is the 
other value we want to solve for.

---

If you don't tell a person about your religion, then the expected pleasure 
they will receive is:

e0 = fs

If you do tell a person about your religion, then the expected pleasure is:

e1 = CoB * ns + (1 - CoB) * Ms = CoB * ns + Ms - CoB * Ms = Ms + CoB* (ns - 
Ms)

Let's assume that going to hell is as painful as going to heaven is 
pleasurable. That is, let's assume that heaven is equal to negative hell (or 
vice versa). I think this is compatible with most people's concept of heaven 
and hell (I figure most people would imagine that heaven is infinitely 
pleasurable and hell is infinitely painful). So we have ns = -Ms

e1 = Ms + CoB * (ns - Ms) = Ms + Cob * (-Ms -Ms) = Ms - 2 * Ms * CoB = Ms 
(1 - 2 * CoB) = 2 Ms (1/2 - CoB)

---

Okay, now assuming we are loving, we want everyone to have the highest 
expected pleasure possible, so we should only tell a person about our 
religion if e1 >= e0. So the minimum Chance of Belief we should require 
before telling them is whatever CoB satisfies the equation e1 = e0, which 
gives us the following equation where we want to solve for CoB:

fs = Ms (1/2 - CoB)
fs = Ms/2 - Ms * CoB
fs - Ms/2 = - Ms * CoB
Ms/2 - fs = Ms * CoB
(Ms/2 - fs)/Ms = CoB = 1/2 - fS/Ms = Ms/(2 *Ms) - (2 * fS)/(2 * Ms) = (Ms - 
2 *fS)/(2 * Ms)

Actually, of these equalities,

CoB = 1/2 - fs/Ms

is probably the most "useful" one.

---

Now just to make some qualitative statements, let's see what happens if Ms 
is infinitely more painful than fs. In that case fs/Ms is equal to zero, and 
so we have

CoB = 1/2

which basically says if there's more than a 50% chance that they might 
believe, we should tell them. Less than 50%, we shouldn't tell them, and if 
it's exactly 50%, then both actions are equally good.

What about if Ms is not infinitely more painful than fs? Then fs/Ms defines 
some positive ratio. If that ratio is 1/2 (i.e. fs is half as painful as 
Ms), then we have:

CoB = 1/2 - 1/2 = 0

meaning we should always tell them about the religion.

---

So what this essentially tells us is that the less painful many stripes is 
in comparison to fewer stripes, or the more terrible fewer stripes is 
compared to many stripes, the more we should be telling others about the 
religion. And when fewer stripes is at least half as bad as many stripes, we 
should tell everyone about the religion. However, if many stripes is 
infinitely worse than fewer stripes, then we only tell those who have a 50% 
chance of believing or higher.

(Which pretty much seemed to be Judson's intuition)

    - Oliver
```

```
> Oliver Wong wrote:

[snip]

>>
>> If you do tell a person about your religion, then the expected pleasure 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> interest in any religion you want to start Oliver.

    After I had worked out the formulas, I googled for "3d graph applet" to 
try to see if I could find a visualization aid. These two hits were 
interesting (requires that you have the Java plugin recognized by your web 
browser):

http://www.cooper.edu/~agicht/3dplot/test.html
http://www.ies.co.jp/math/java/misc/SimpleGraph3D/SimpleGraph3D.html

    Anyway, I had made plotted CoB on the Z axis, fs on the X axis and Ms on 
the Y axis, and the formula is basically:

Z = 1/2 - X/Y

    I ended up not including that in the original post because it was too 
difficult to interpret the results. In particular, you get a discontinuous 
singularity at the coordinates (X=0,Y=0), where coming from the top, Z seems 
to be positive infinity but coming from the bottom, Z is negative infinity. 
At any rate, the graphing applets were thoroughly confused and were drawing 
nonsense.

    I think at one point, to arrive at the above formula, I divided by Ms; 
which I should not be allowed to do if Ms is equal to zero (division by 
zero).

> I don't think this is a probability but a *certainty* - taking the thread 
> title above. I don't think you are making one single penny out of Java, 
> Oliver. You are enjoying yourself far too much discussing, fence-jumping, 
> porno and God knows what else in this archaic Newsgroup of old fogies :-)

    These days actually, I haven't been writing much actual Java code at 
all; it's been mostly designing. They (professors at universities, among 
others) recommend when you're stumped on a problem, to walk away from it for 
a short while, think about something else, and then come back to it. That 
gives your subconcious an opportunity to mull at the problem.

    And posting on c.l.c beats staring at a design document all day, 
wondering what to write next.

    - Oliver
```

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
> 
>     These days actually, I haven't been writing much actual Java code at 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> gives your subconcious an opportunity to mull at the problem.

That's very sound advice they are giving. You can become saturated with 
a problem; you may even produce a half-cocked solution, with an 
awareness that it fits, even though it is cumbersome in implementation.

Go take a break - your choice. Me, it would be something as simple as 
turning over the soil in my veggie plot in the Spring. (A definition of 
Spring in Calgary - the day after Winter and the day before Summer :-) 
). Digging the shovel into the soil doesn't stop your thought juices and 
almost in a casual way, you see the spark of an alternative to what you 
had previously designed. Still not necessarily the *right* solution - 
but you are now going down a more reasonable path.

Jimmy
>     And posting on c.l.c beats staring at a design document all day, 
> wondering what to write next.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **References:** `<cvkuo154sjfungkb2jgjmq2q28nl4tu9aj@4ax.com> <FoZjf.47094$6y4.38133@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <1133550230.780919.303800@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <i21kf.47144$6y4.15970@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqd1b$259m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <NBHof.6648$ic1.5375@edtnps90>`

```

"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
news:NBHof.6648$ic1.5375@edtnps90...

> Again, assuming your beliefs are true, then you should ONLY share the 
> gospel with people who you expect will believe in it. In other words, 
> BEFORE spreading the gospel, get to know the person first. See if they are 
> the type who would likely believe in Christianity. If so, then by all 
> means, share the gospel with them.

I agree, wholeheartedly, and would go one simple step further:  get to know 
the person first *well enough to know what approach is likely to resonate 
with them and what approach will send them screaming into the night*.   It 
is our *obligation* to use the first approach, and when we fail to do what 
is necessary to avoid the second, it *is* on our heads.

> If not, then perhaps it's better you not mention it, so that they may be 
> beaten with few stripes rather than with many.

Or let your overall behavior and demeanor do your preaching for you.

As Chapter 5 of the book "Alcoholics Anonymous" has it:  "If you have 
decided you want what we have, and are willing to go to any length to get 
it, then you are ready to take certain steps."   In that milieu, the same 
applies to choosing a sponsor -- a guide to help you work through the steps 
based on the fact that he or she has gone through them and you can see the 
way they are reflected in his or her behavior.  If your goal in life isn't 
to become even more of a supercilious jerk than you've been thus far, it 
doesn't make a lot of sense to choose as a mentor somebody whose behavior 
strikes you as that of a supercilious jerk.  You choose somebody willing to 
"share our experience, strength and hope with each other, that we may solve 
our common problem[s] and help others to recover", whose behavior convinces 
you that they "walk the talk", and who is moving, and strives to move, in 
the same direction of spiritual growth that you wish to move.

That brings me to John 3:3, which reads in part in my NAB: " ... no one can 
see the reign of God unless he is begotten from above".  If *God* has 
planted the seeds of understanding in another person, he may see God's light 
shining through us, insofar as we strive to *allow* it.  It is not our 
obligation to plant that seed; it is *God's*.  It strikes me as a 
significant error and failing for us to insist that God's light *does* shine 
through us and our behavior as individuals and furthermore to insist that 
failure to see that light is a result of the shortcomings of the observer.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
This is nonsense, Chuck! You guys are already crying foul because people 
that have not heard may be eternally dammed, when we are trying hard to tell 
everybody. Now you say that we should tell fewer people, and that would be 
"more fair?" Sheesh! Gimme a break!
```

- **Message-ID:** `<1135192114.587375.211830@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<Ky2qf.19523$k76.3054@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<dmqd1b$259m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqkbe$2a3t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jDakf.48011$6y4.6486@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <NAjof.9484$kP5.7513@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <ADkof.1728$lv3.1427@clgrps12> <37Bof.31637$Y72.111@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <NBHof.6648$ic1.5375@edtnps90> <Ky2qf.19523$k76.3054@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
[Oliver Wong]:
>>> Again, assuming your beliefs are true, then you should ONLY share the
>>> gospel with people who you expect will believe in it. In other words,
>>> BEFORE spreading the gospel, get to know the person first. See if they
>>> are the type who would likely believe in Christianity. If so, then by
>>> all means, share the gospel with them.

[Chuck Stevens]:
>> I agree, wholeheartedly, and would go one simple step further:  get to
>> know the person first *well enough to know what approach is likely to
>> resonate with them and what approach will send them screaming into the
>> night*.   It is our *obligation* to use the first approach, and when we
>> fail to do what is necessary to avoid the second, it *is* on our heads.

[Judson McClendon]:
> This is nonsense, Chuck! You guys are already crying foul because people
> that have not heard may be eternally dammed, when we are trying hard to
> tell everybody. Now you say that we should tell fewer people, and that
> would be "more fair?" Sheesh! Gimme a break!

What "you guys"?

Never try to teach a pig to sing.  It wastes your time and it annoys
the
pig.

And a family favorite:  Never try to teach your grandmother how to milk
ducks.

Let me try a different tack.  If your goal is to *communicate* a
particular
message, it is incumbent on *you* to ensure that that message is
communicated *effectively*, and techniques like "tell" or "preach" or
"evangelize" or "proclaim" may not be effective to a particular
audience.

As an example:  I have one ear that does not work at all, another ear
that
only works with reasonable effectiveness with amplification, and a
*very*
low tolerance for continuous loud noises.  A street preacher yelling
into a
bullhorn, or for that matter, a minister getting "caught up in the
spirit"
preaching from the pulpit, is not communicating effectively *with me*
if
their volume level exceeds that which my decimated hearing can deal,
and if
they reach that level they are also inflicting physical pain in the
process.
On one occasion in our church, a minister got "caught up in the spirit"
and
began praying over the bread *so loudly* and for *so long* (like, 15
minutes) during preparations for Communion that I had to leave the
sanctuary
to escape the pain he was inflicting and was unable to perform the
music
during Communion as I had been asked and expected to do.  The *physical
pain* his outburst inflicted on me had me in tears, *despite* my
plugging my
ears (as discreetly as I could).

Now, I don't expect *any* minister to know *instinctively* that what he
is
doing causes physical pain.  What I *do* expect is that once he's been
told
that this sort of behavior is painful to some members of the
congregation
and should be curtailed, he would change his behavior.  That has
proven,
alas, in the case of this particular minister, not to be the case, as
he had
been told twice before that this was considered not only extremely rude
but
caused some of the people he was trying to reach *physical pain* and
*physical injury*.

If someone's face turns purple from the effort of screaming at me
trying to
"communicate" the message that God is the God of Love and wants Peace
On
Earth, well, the medium of screaming doesn't match the message.

This is but one example.  To *communicate* the message with someone who
speaks only Navajo, it would not be appropriate to *proclaim* the Good
News
in Tlinkit or Magyar.  To *communicate* the Good News with an audience
of
deaf people, it may not be appropriate for you to *speak* the message.
It
may make *you* feel good that you're Delivering the Message, but it
*is* up
to you to ensure that you deliver the message in a way that
*communicates*
the message *in a way that is effective for the audience*.   If you
drive
someone away by your manner or your actions, that's on *you*, not on
them.

These examples are extreme -- approaching "reductio ad absurdum".  But
they
apply in milder form as well.   I mistrust terms like "proclaim" and
"preach" and "tell" because they all represent a *one-way*
conversation.  I
prefer the idea of "communicating".

And I think in the general case "Unless everything you do and
everything you
believe matches *exactly* what I tell you, you are going to be cast
into a
lake of burning sulfur for an eternity of eternities" is *not* going to
be
perceived by a reasonable person as "Good News".

I've said this before, though I'm not sure whether it was in this
forum.
Some people coming into Alcoholics Anonymous get *really* upset with
the
very idea of accepting the concept of any sort of God and want no part
of
it.  My opinion is that if such a person reaches the point of "There is
a
God, and I'm not it", they have made an ENORMOUS step toward Sobriety,
and
toward the arms of a loving God.   I do *not* believe it is appropriate
to
tell such a person "unless you accept Jesus as the Messiah and as your
personal Savior ..." -- a concept that may be as meaningful to him as
"unless you accept Whatsit as your personal Whoosis ..." -- and I
believe to
do so does a *great deal* more harm than good.  They *may* come to
accept
Jesus as Messiah and as their personal Savior, and they might even come
to
accept the tenets of one or another Christian denomination, but if they
do,
it'll be in God's time, not necessarily in mine or yours.

More importantly, they may never go further than "there is a God, and
I'm
not it", and I personally am *not* willing to say they will burn
forever in
hell for failing to get past that point.  If that concept is sufficient
to
help keep them sober, that might be all that God expects of them at
that
time.  And I have known of people in AA who had reached *exactly* that
position but got drunk after somebody *in AA* told them in no uncertain
terms "Unless you accept Jesus as your savior, today, right now, you
might
as well drink yourself to death for all the value you have to God and
to
humanity".

The nature of one's Higher Power, "which some of us choose to call
God", is
an outside issue in twelve-step programs,  as it should be, for that
very
reason.

     -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> free will, they will be tortured forever and ever.

All have sinned against God. Those who hear about Jesus, and do not believe, 
are condemned because they have sinned but rejected salvation. Those who do 
not hear are condemned because they have sinned against God. Jesus Christ is 
the only human who ever lived who never sinned. It is generally believed 
(there is Scripture to support this) that babies and young children who die 
before reaching an age to know right from wrong are considered innocent, and 
are not condemned. The age when a child reaches this point (which varies 
between individuals, and for some mentally handicapped individuals, may 
never come) is called the "age of accountability". Only God knows when an 
individual reaches this point.
```
