- **References:** `<dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <1134654017.490840.65280@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <v453q1lkb486nlk3fdqv82jr71iqibkiss@4ax.com> <0mBof.31738$Y72.1597@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnv50v$2urr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
> Jesus Christ is the only human who ever lived who never sinned.

> The Roman Catholic Church officially and vehemently disagrees with you on
> that point.

It seems to me that this is because of two points. First the Catholic
church attributes Mary as being a human without sin, and secondly they
claim that Jesus is not a human, being one of the 'gods' in theological
terms makes them an alien in earth people terms.

Being (alegedly) one of the trinity means that he was only given human
_form_ when on earth and, like Hercules (and many other demigods), was
the result of cross-species interbreeding.

What I would be interested to know is what the peoples of 2000, 3000
and 4000 years ago had as a a concept of what they called their various
'gods'. It seems to me that the Egyptians had no problem rationalising
the god-like attributes and god-like powers of the Pharoahs with the
obvious humanness of them. Emperor Chin I was God to his people (and
presumably the devil to his neibours), as was Hirohito. Chin seems to
have been a bit obsessive about the so-called afterlife and had an army
made for him to use to invade 'the next world' when he arrived which he
regarded as being the next country and beyond the Himalayas.

It seems to me that the Jews with their Jehovah (and the Canaanites
with El, and the Hindus with their various lords)  would have given
them a duality of being the 'lord' (as in Baron, warlord) yet known
that they were humans too. In fact it is only in very recent times that
european royalty gave up the reverenge and next-to-godliness that lords
have been granted over the millenia.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 47)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-16T13:12:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dnvaka$pj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <1134654017.490840.65280@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <v453q1lkb486nlk3fdqv82jr71iqibkiss@4ax.com> <0mBof.31738$Y72.1597@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnv50v$2urr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1134765491.604232.127420@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1134765491.604232.127420@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com...
>> Jesus Christ is the only human who ever lived who never sinned.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No, I think that's an oversimplification.

The doctrine of "original sin" ties into it (a doctrine about which I have 
serious misgivings).

According to the doctrine of Immaculate Conception, pronounced ex cathedra 
by Pius IX on 8 December 1854, Mary was *conceived* without sin.  Her 
personal sinlessness throughout her life is generally accepted, but some 
early fathers disagreed.

As I see it, Jesus was not sinless *by nature*, he was sinless *by praxis*. 
The temptations he suffered are meaningless if he was (as is claimed for 
Mary) *fundamentally* incapable of succumbing to them.   If original sin 
there be, I think he bore its stain; that's one of the reasons he chose to 
be dunked:  "Came John baptizing in the wilderness, [and*] proclaiming 
baptism of-direction-changing for [the purpose of] the dismissal of-sins." 
(Mark 1:4, my perhaps-too-literal rendering from the TR).   (*Alford omits 
the "and" and Tregelles considers it doubtful).

His temptations and sufferings are meaningless absent the completeness of a 
truly *human* nature.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 48)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

Chuck Stevens wrote:

> No, I think that's an oversimplification.

Well of course it is, it is the nature of theologins to overcomplexify
things, so anything that I say woul;d be 'oversimplified'.

> The doctrine of "original sin" ties into it (a doctrine about which I have
> serious misgivings).

> According to the doctrine of Immaculate Conception, pronounced ex cathedra
> by Pius IX on 8 December 1854, Mary was *conceived* without sin.  Her
> personal sinlessness throughout her life is generally accepted, but some
> early fathers disagreed.

As do most churches outside of 'The Cult of Mary' as some would call
it. It seems to me that the whole thing is based on a meaningless
distinction between the so-called 'immaculate conception' _of_ Mary and
_in_ Mary.

>  If original sin there be, I think he bore its stain;

Excuse me, but then what is the point of the so-called 'immaculate
conception'. Either Mary was sinless so that Jesus could be without
original sin or he was the target of the immaculate conception so he
was without sin regardless of Mary's situation.

But then I am expecting too much for there to be any sort of logic or
consistency in the mythology.

> His temptations and sufferings are meaningless absent the completeness of a
> truly *human* nature.

Granted, just as Gandi's would have been meaningless if he could wave a
wand and make it all better.
```

Richard wrote:
> Chuck Stevens wrote:
> 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I understand where Chuck the former Catholic is coming from. He is 
unhappy with the Catholic concept of Original Sin. To the best of my 
knowledge there's no direct reference to Original Sin in the Good Book 
that Judson keeps quoting from.

> Excuse me, but then what is the point of the so-called 'immaculate
> conception'. Either Mary was sinless so that Jesus could be without
> original sin or he was the target of the immaculate conception so he
> was without sin regardless of Mary's situation.

I agree Chuck's point of view does appear to negate the above. The point 
of the Immaculate Conception (Initially reluctantly agreed to by the 
Church, became a very late dogma and not announced until 1854) is that 
Mary *was* born without sin so that she could be the *only* parent of a 
sinless Deity - Jesus. As a toss-up Jesus *could* have been born of a 
woman who was *stained* by Original Sin. Still what do you care - it's 
just another fairy tale.

Never been there - Lourdes.

Firstly there's that stupid German-speaking Jew, Franz Werfel (1890 - 
1945). Fleeing the Nazis with his wife they were for a short time in 
Lourdes - he vowed, should he escape, he would write the 'song of songs' 
about a 14-year old peasant girl Bernadette Soubirous who apparently had 
visions of a beautiful lady in 1858, in a dump area where the town threw 
its hospital filth. He kept his promise publishing the book in 1941. It 
went on to become an Oscar winning movie for actress Jennifer Jones in 
1943 as 'The Song of Bernadette'.

Bernadette had 18 visions between February 11 and July 16, 1858, and on 
March 25 the feast of the Annunciation, the lady, dressed in a white 
gown with a pale blue sash, declared "I am the Immaculate Conception", 
in French. She also asked that a church should be built on the site of 
the grotto. The disbelieving parish priest was disturbed as the child 
only spoke a patois. That young girl is now St. Bernadette and her body 
is apparently uncorrupted, although they have used wax to cover the 
features - looks remarkably life-like to me from the photograph. They 
might just let you poke her, to see if she falls to bits like Dracula.

Think on all those ignorant fools who wend their way to Lourdes - not an 
exact number, because of individual visitors as opposed to pilgrimage 
parties. Records show since 1867 - 4,919,000 fools and of those fools 
some 4,000 who received 'miraculous' cures, cancer, TB etc. Built in 
1997 there's one hospice has just hit its millionth resident.

Now there's one to get your teeth into - get some like minded friends to 
go to Lourdes to investigate and dispute those medical figures. So far 
as I'm aware they happily accept doubting medical practitioners.

Just think - all because some greedy Jew wanted to make a buck writing a 
book, this fantastic fairy-tale got big time publicity.

Why stop there - check out those three stupid kids at Fatima around WWI.

I am amazed how much time a complete non-believer seems to spend 
researching things he doesn't believe in.

> 
> But then I am expecting too much for there to be any sort of logic or
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

> As a toss-up Jesus *could* have been born of a
> woman who was *stained* by Original Sin. Still what do you care - it's
> just another fairy tale.

It does seem to me that dogma comes from people sitting around arguing
the toss and then declaring it as dictum. Is it that she _wasn't_
immaculate until 1854 and then she was 'cause the Pope said so ?

But yes, you are correct, I see the stories of his being a demi-god as
a fairy tale.

> so that she could be the *only* parent

Hmmm, did he not have any Y chromosomes then ?

Jesus may well have been a religious activist, primarily acting against
the Roman occupation but I don't believe the deification any more than
I do that of Hercules or Alexander.

> I am amazed how much time a complete non-believer seems to spend
> researching things he doesn't believe in.

What would you have me do ?  Make up my mind after doing no research at
all ? Only research the things that are 'approved' ? Have a list of
books it would be a sin to read (yes I know that is no longer done)?

On Sat, 17 Dec 2005 04:41:51 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> enlightened us:

>Richard wrote:
>> Chuck Stevens wrote:
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There are many references to original sin in both the new and old
testaments.  

The Psalmist says Ps.58:3: "the wicked go astray from the womb, they
err from their birth speaking lies. 
Ps.51:5 David claimed that he was conceived in iniquity."

Job asks the question  "What is man, that he could be pure? And he who
is born of a woman, that he could be righteous?" (Job 15:14)  Job
25:4: "How then can man be righteous before God? Or how can he be pure
who is born of a woman?"

Eph. 2:3: "Among whom also we all had our conversation in times past
in the lusts of our flesh, fulfilling the desires of the flesh and of
the mind; and were by nature the children of wrath, even as others."

Paul states in  Rom 3:10: "As it is written: "There is none righteous,
no, not one" v.12 "They have all turned aside; they have together
become unprofitable; there is none who does good, no, not one."

The Bible says that, "The wages of sin is death." Sin is first and
then the consequence is the payoff. Romans 5:18 -19 says, "through one
man's trespass,  judgment came to all men, for by one man's
disobedience all were made sinners." This Scripture clearly shows sin
is inherited through his (Adam's) disobedience, not ours. The bible
says we are all under the condemnation of sin. The Bible speaks of sin
not as a biological flaw but a spiritual infection that is transmitted
through the reproductive cycle finding itself in all humans corrupting
their nature. Romans 3:23 says, "All have sinned and fallen short of
the glory of God." If one entertains the though that they are born
without sin, then they have the glory of God. The only one who has the
glory of God as their nature is God.


In addition to the the Bible, the Qur'an recounts the story of Adam
and Eve in a similar way. However, the blame of disobedience is either
put squarely on Adam, or both are blamed for the sin, but Eve is never
mentioned to have convinced Adam of the sin. Adam and Eve are forgiven
by God after they repent.

According to Wikipedia, the idea that the sin propagates to their
offspring is categorically refused by the majority of Sunni and Shia
muslims, citing ayahs such as: [6:164] "Every soul draws the meed of
its acts on none but itself: no bearer of burdens can bear the burden
of another," and [2:286] "On no soul doth Allah place a burden greater
than it can bear. It gets every good that it earns, and it suffers
every ill that it earns." There are minor factions, such as the Quran
Alone Muslims, who accept a concept of original sin according to which
every single human has sinned individually before coming to this
earth.

>> Excuse me, but then what is the point of the so-called 'immaculate
>> conception'. Either Mary was sinless so that Jesus could be without
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Christ could have been born through two human parents and His actions
would still be sinless. However, it was for this very reason because
He took on human flesh that He had a virgin birth - to bypass the sin
nature of man and have sinless blood. For the life of man is in the
blood. 

If he was born of both a human father and mother there could have been
no authentic claim to be deity. On the other hand if he had come with
no relation to human parentage then He could not claim to be an
authentic human. The perfect solution worked out from before the
foundation of the world and prophesied in Gen.3:15 "the seed of the
woman" a virgin conception.

Did Jesus need the virgin birth to be sinless? No, he was already
sinless as the Son of God before his birth. It was necessary to
preserve his humanity from the pollution of sin. "For he made Him who
had no sin to be sin for us." (2 Cor.5:21)  Heb.4:15 tells us, "He was
at all points tempted as we are, yet without sin." His temptation was
to prove who He really was.


>Never been there - Lourdes.
>
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>> 

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-


If athletes get athlete's foot, do astronauts get mistletoe?
---George Carlin

Steve

SkippyPB wrote:

ME : >>I understand where Chuck the former Catholic is coming from. He is
>>unhappy with the Catholic concept of Original Sin. To the best of my 
>>knowledge there's no direct reference to Original Sin in the Good Book 
>>that Judson keeps quoting from.
>>

SKIPPY :> There are many references to original sin in both the new and old
> testaments.  
> 
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Thanks for the clarification from a fellow Catholic, Skippy. And back to 
what I wrote :-

"To the best of my knowledge there's no direct reference to Original Sin 
in the Good Book".

Have I got this more or less right ? You were a lapsed catholic, married 
to a Bhuddist whose spirituality made you have a re-think on you own 
Catholicism, to which you returned and became a deacon ?  If the last is 
correct, as a result of studying for the deaconship you no doubt looked 
more closely at Biblical texts with particular emphasis on the New 
Testament.

Now to the average lay-reader I would suggest your references are not 
immediately obvious. Consider all those good ladies who regularly go to 
Rosary sessions. I'd bet they wouldn't pick up on the underlinings of 
your quotes.

Let's try another one on you and Chuck the ex-catholic. Judson's premise 
is based on the fact that the Bible is the Divine Word because God 
instructed/inspired the writers what to write. (It appears that this is 
a focal point for the Pentecostals). My contention, both Old and New 
Testaments are an historical record of events, many passed by word of 
mouth to the authors, as to what happened. In the case of the Jewish 
Bible MANY generational words-of-mouth. We know only too well that in 
the case of the New Testament there were far more documents than those 
that actually finished up in the final set. So the early Christian 
theologians had to be selective, including only those which they felt 
had appeal and rejecting others, for whatever reason.

That doesn't negate the concept that the New Testament isn't God 
inspired. Providing those Early Fathers did it in a spirit of honesty, 
the final edition does represent God's word.

Jimmy
```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1134773919.983470.231910@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...

>>  If original sin there be, I think he bore its stain;
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> was without sin regardless of Mary's situation.

The Doctrine of the Immaculate Conception is purely and solely about 
*Mary's* conception.  Jesus' *conception* was immaculate in the "human" 
sense because it occurred through the actions of the Holy Spirit, and not 
Joseph.   The defense of Mary being sinless through life was so that she 
would be indisputably worthy of being the Mother of God.   Note that I'm not 
stating agreement with these doctrines, I'm just trying to restate that 
which I was told in religious studies about them.  See the relevant subjects 
in the Catholic Encyclopedia for details (e.g., Infallibility, Immaculate 
Conception, Christology, Ex Cathedra).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

Peter Lacey wrote:
> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> identify them on short notice.

I duly checked your two references Peter - they are almost identical 
wording. I still go back to interpretation as I used in my example of 
the trade unionist.

Do some googling on "Jesus and brothers/sisters" and variations on same. 
Get some interesting hits. Some are very obviously Catholic sites; one 
of which I found a bit lame. However if you do want to try and take the 
New Testament text literally you have to do it in time sequence. Follow 
the story of Mary's annunciation by the angel and subsequent promise of 
retained virginity.

If she held to her promise then there was ONLY Jesus and she retains 
that special ranking as the 'Virgin Mary', 'Immaculate Conception', and 
'the Mother of God'.

If she had other children then she is lying and the above three titles 
are meaningless.

Are you a Dan Brown fan ? How about Jesus getting married :-)

Jimmy

Jimmy

- **References:** `<dnv50v$2urr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1134765491.604232.127420@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <dnvaka$pj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1134773919.983470.231910@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <do6sk9$1gs5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <43A70257.532A768C@mts.net> <1135020539.638700.257680@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <xjFpf.29673$2k.18297@pd7tw1no> <43A76D93.9BEBEB05@mts.net> <5D2qf.19583$k76.11555@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <zB3qf.160834$Gd6.105699@pd7tw3no>`

```
On Wed, 21 Dec 2005 03:02:55 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> enlightened us:

>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy

The Catholic Church can trace its roots back to Peter who was its
first pope, although he wasn't called that.  Jesus says to Peter, "
And so I say to you, you are Peter, and upon this rock I will build my
church, and the gates of the netherworld shall not prevail against
it.".[Matt: 16:18]  All other religions can only trace their roots
back to a person with an idea (or an axe to grind with the catholic
church).

The Book of Jude was written after the Epistle of Second Peter. Second
Peter was thought to have been written around 66 AD.  Jude was written
about 67-70 A.D. The reason it is believed it was written after Second
Peter is because Jude quotes (2 Pet. 2:17,18) in this epistle.

As to the identity of Jude or who he is, in Jude 1 he says he is:

    [1]. Servant (or slave depending on who did the translation) of
Jesus Christ.
    [2]. Brother of James.

Also, further information in the Bible one can conclude the following
about Jude:

[1]. An Apostle (Luke. 6:16)
[2]. Brother of Jesus (Matt. 13:55)

The Catholic Church includes the Book of Jude in its Bible.

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-


Can you buy an entire chess set in a pawn-shop?
---George Carlin  
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Steve

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:5D2qf.19583$k76.11555@bignews6.bellsouth.net...

> Jude, the author of the small book of Jude in the New Testament, is 
> commonly thought to be the (half) brother of Jesus.

A great many scholars disagree with that attribution.

Jude also cites explicitly an apocryphal book (1 Enoch), which was one of 
the reasons its inclusion into the Canon was slow in coming, being accepted 
into the Roman canon in the late 4th century (largely through the efforts of 
Jerome and Augustine), and *never* accepted in the Syrian church.

Luther himself discarded Jude (along with Hebrews, James and the Apocalypse) 
as altogether noncanonical, and Lutherans maintained that exclusion for at 
least a century (and in addition excluded 2 Peter, 2 John and 3 John).  In 
the 17th century, Lutheran theologians tended to hold these books to be of 
doubtful, or at least inferior, quality, but by the beginning of the 18th 
they were "back in" (although Hebrews is at the end of the New Testament, 
along with James, Jude and Revelation, in German bibles).

     -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
> Chuck Stevens wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>> give your position greater weight?

Sorry, I had misread/misunderstood what you were saying there, so my reply 
not responsive to what you actually wrote. I do not know of any sin 
attributed to Mary, other than the fact that the Bible clearly declares that 
"all men have sinned." Protestants do not generally believe that Mary was 
'sinless', as Catholics do, but do generally believe that Mary was a very 
good woman, of undisputable character. Protestants would probably say more 
about Mary, if they did not feel that Catholics have overdone it, virtually 
to the point of worship.

> Perhaps because his sect also speaks with the 'gift of tongues' ? Note 
> that to date he hasn't even hinted at that zinger.

Actually, the issue is "spiritual gifts", of which "tongues" is only one 
example. There is disagreement over this issue, even among fellow 
Protestants, and it is not essential to salvation. These nine "spiritual 
gifts" are listed in 1 Corinthians 12:8-10,28.

The Bible clearly teaches that these gifts are real, and Paul talks in 
detail about how these gifts should and should not be used. Pentecostals as 
a group believe these gifts are still active today. Non-Pentecostals who 
also believe this are called "Charismatic's", and there are many of them 
among other Protestants (a.g. Baptists, Methodists), and even Catholics. I 
don't know about Catholics, but non-Pentecostal and non-Charismatic 
protestants generally believe that the spiritual gifts ceased to be active 
after the Bible canon was complete. They base this primarily on a specific 
interpretation of 1 Corinthians 13:8-12, in which "that which is perfect" is 
thought to refer to the Bible. Pentecostals and Charismatics believe "that 
which is perfect" refers to our bodies, lives, knowledge, communication with 
God, etc, once we are with Him in Heaven.
```

Good man - you picked up on the Rat Catchers being charismatics - and 
where did we pick it up from - you modernists. Both John XXIII 1960s 
(Vatican II - see, that wretched 'liberalism' thing again) and JPII in 
1992 have given it their blessing.

The idea leaves me dead cold. I asked the local priest years ago about 
it. We knew there was one parish which held Charismatic meetings and 
apparently speaking in tongues. Not because he 'hated' it - he was like 
me - just wouldn't want to touch it with a bargepole.

Theologically you have to ask yourself - if it was such a good idea,

first Pentecost - why didn't it occur down through the ages - rather 
than leave it to be 'revealed' again in the 20th century ?

As I said I don't 'do churches', so whether or not it is still active in 
Catholicism, or more specifically Calgary, I just don't know.

Watch this space - Skippy is now going to send me a zinger; he speaks

Jimmy

>Judson McClendon wrote:

I might speak it but I can't write either!  And no zinger here...yet
:)

As for the first Pentecost and why didn't it occur down through the
ages.  I believe it did and continues to do so today but in a singular
act.  Also, Catholics are "confirmed" at a young age or, for people
converting to Catholocism, after their baptism.  This act of
confirming or Sacrement of Confirmation, is described in the bible as:

Para. 1315: "Now when the apostles at Jerusalem heard that Samaria had
received the word of God, they sent to them Peter and John, who came
down and prayed for them that they might receive the Holy Spirit; for
it had not yet fallen on any of them, but they had only been baptized
in the name of the Lord Jesus. Then they laid their hands on them and
they received the Holy Spirit" (Acts 8:14-17).

Which I believe was discussed and has been carried forward.  The
Catechism describes it as, "For by the sacrament of Confirmation, [the
baptized] are more perfectly bound to the Church and are enriched with
a special strength of the Holy Spirit. Hence they are, as true
witnesses of Christ, more strictly obliged to spread and defend the
faith by word and deed"

Regards,


Regards,

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> leave it to be 'revealed' again in the 20th century ?

The spiritual gifts are described in the Bible as being very common among 
first century Christians. They have also been present during many great 
revivals since the first century. There has always been a segment of 
Christianity that exercised these gifts. The "Pentecostal Revival" that you 
apparently refer to happened around the world during the period roughly 
1890-1910. There was another "Charismatic Revival" during the 1970's. I only 
came to believe in the current activity of the spiritual gifts after seeing 
and experiencing the actual manifestations of these gifts first hand. For 
anyone who is interested, there are a couple of good books by people who 
definitely did not believe in these gifts, but were "drug kicking and 
screaming", so to speak, into belief. One is "They Speak with Other Tongues" 
by John L. Sherrill, and the other is "Surprised by the Power of the Spirit" 
by Jack Deere. Both are available from Amazon.com.
```

- **References:** `<dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <1134654017.490840.65280@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <v453q1lkb486nlk3fdqv82jr71iqibkiss@4ax.com> <0mBof.31738$Y72.1597@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnv50v$2urr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Zs%of.445$RZ6.226@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <do71vf$1k1r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <08Fpf.29535$2k.18910@pd7tw1no> <_y3qf.20032$k76.19625@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <O54qf.42877$2k.19263@pd7tw1no> <sGiqf.16222$kP5.3271@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:sGiqf.16222$kP5.3271@bignews5.bellsouth.net...

> The spiritual gifts are described in the Bible as being very common among 
> first century Christians. They have also been present during many great 
> revivals since the first century.

Our denomination accepts the validity of these gifts (1 Cor 12:4ff), 
although generally speaking an individual manifestation of the "gift of 
tongues" isn't afforded as much importance if that manifestation isn't 
accompanied by a confirming *interpretation* of the utterance, preferably by 
more than one person in the audience, since if interpretation doesn't occur 
it really isn't possible to discern the message that the utterance was 
supposed to convey.    And in general the manifestations of these gifts are 
taken one-by-one -- for example, the "gift of prophecy" is manifest in an 
individual event of prophesying, not as the "gift of being a 
prophet/prophetess".

One thing that has happened on numerous occasions is that someone will have 
the interpretation of the utterance in tongues -- but because the hearer 
wasn't aware that the speaker was manifesting glossolalia in the first place 
the hearer  doesn't know that the interpretation is *not* common knowledge. 
Our ministry tries to forestall this with a query "Does anyone have the 
interpretation?" when this occurs, and *very* frequently the hearer with the 
interpretation will well and truly blanch before repeating what he/she 
heard.

As it happens, neither of these gifts has shown up in me (not that either 
one is something I particularly covet).

> There has always been a segment of Christianity that exercised these 
> gifts.

Sounds a bit too much like "of Christianity that practiced these gifts" and 
too little like "of Christianity in which those gifts were manifest" for my 
taste.   "Exercizing" the gift of tongues (Webster's Ninth:  "ecstatic, 
usually unintelligible utterance accompanying religious excitation") strikes 
me as missing the point, at the very least.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Bottom post only.

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:jOjqf.16242$kP5.8329@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."

"Allowing" is not the same as "practicing" or "exercizing".   That's my 
point.

I have known people who *actively practiced* speaking in tongues to refine 
the "skill".  In all honesty, these folk didn't have much that I felt was of 
spiritual value elsewhere in their lives; it was largely about trying to 
demonstrate to their congregation how holy they were and was very much an 
"on demand" act on their part.

I have also known people that I think *truly had* the gift of tongues, and 
I'd have to say the ones I have known have "walked the walk".   And a 
meaningful interpretation often accompanied their gift.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>

- **References:** `<dnk8kr$2fpp$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134479141.730598.191240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <5%Anf.4850$w7.4726@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134496171.112258.291400@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <GRHnf.4997$w7.2414@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134591823.187353.61090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <hk1of.8478$kP5.7946@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d1c64$43a0cd92$4f9c602$23399@DIALUPUSA.NET> <1134654017.490840.65280@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <v453q1lkb486nlk3fdqv82jr71iqibkiss@4ax.com> <0mBof.31738$Y72.1597@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <dnv50v$2urr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Zs%of.445$RZ6.226@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <do71vf$1k1r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <08Fpf.29535$2k.18910@pd7tw1no> <_y3qf.20032$k76.19625@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:_y3qf.20032$k76.19625@bignews6.bellsouth.net...

> They base this primarily on a specific interpretation of 1 Corinthians 
> 13:8-12, in which "that which is perfect" is thought to refer to the 
> Bible.

The interpretation aside (and I'd like to see a defense of it, because it 
looks like a bit of a reach to me!), one of my personal semantic "hot 
buttons" is the idea that the Greek word "teleios" (and the Latin word 
"perfectus" that translates it in the Vulgate) both carry the current 
meaning of the English word "perfect" -- that is, "without flaw".

That is not the case.  Flawlessness is not the point of the term; "fully 
worked out" comes closer, and "completion" and "fulfillment" are significant 
features in the semantics of the word.

The same is true of "perfectus", from the past participle of "perficere", 
itself formed of the prefix "per-", through, and the root "facere", to carry 
out, and having the connotation "carried out, completed".

There's a term used in classical music composition to describe such as the 
works of Brahms and Bach:  "through-composed".  That term is closer than 
anything else I've seen in English to carry the connotations appropriately 
associated with both "teleios" and "perfectus".

    -Chuck Stevens
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:Fvynf.4814$w7.442@bignews7.bellsouth.net...

> I absolutely agree with the "show" part. But Christ Himself commanded us 
> to "tell", and one could hardly claim to follow Jesus and ignore His 
> command.

If what you do in my presence doesn't strike me as evidentiary of the sort 
of person I believe I would like to become, what you say will drive me 
further from the perspective you're trying to "sell" than it will to bring 
me to respect it.

> It is unfortunate that so many of us (and I include myself) do this so 
> poorly. We humans are a sorry lot, and I am constantly amazed that God 
> would even bother with us, let alone sacrifice His Son to save us.

I think it's the job of somebody proselytizing to do it *well* and to do it 
*effectively*.  "First, do no harm".

> John Newton, a former slave ship captain who came to Jesus, was so moved 
> by this that he wrote the words to "Amazing Grace", one of the most 
> beloved Christian hymns. (It was the tune to "Amazing Grace" that was 
> played by bagpipes during Spock's funeral in "Star Trek II: The Wrath of 
> Kahn".)

And this citation relates to the subject ... exactly how?

    -Chuck Stevens

On Fri, 2 Dec 2005 16:33:40 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> enlightened us:

>"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>are simply talking, and one happens to be talking to God. Big deal. :-)

This whole thing is ridiculous.  You want your kids to be able to pray
at school then send them to a parochial school.  You don't think that
praying at school is necessary and prefer to teach and practice your
religion in your own home and church/synagogue/temple/mosque, then
send them to public school.  You want to promote your religious
beliefs, do it outside of public school and away from government
supported agencies/buildings.  The constitution guarantees freedom of
religion which also can be construed as freedom from religion.  People
who try and thwart that principle are not much better than the mullahs
in Iran who force everyone there to conform to their vision of Islam.
We don't need that crap here in the USA.

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-


"Can a stupid person be a smart-ass?"  
---George Carlin

"Praying visibly? Why not? What conceivable offense is someone bowing
their head? Praying audibly? Why should a person praying audibly over
their food be any more objectionable than a person having an audible
conversation? Both are simply talking, and one happens to be talking
to God. Big deal. :-)"

You also wrote, "See what I mean about liberals? Should my children
have to leave the school grounds to pray over their food? Richard,
your position is so blatantly biased and unreasonable that I am amazed
you can't see it yourself. Comparing praying over one's food to
playing football, as if they were equally distracting! What a pitiful
example. FYI there are a number of churches who have baseball and
softball teams. Probably some who have football teams too. They just
don't practice inside the sanctuary. :-)"

I have a real problem with people who insist that prayer be allowed in
public school.  That is the basis behind what I wrote.  Your writings
seem to suggest that you support prayer in public school.  If I'm
wrong, than I appologize, otherwise I stand by what I wrote.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 34)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-05T12:25:55-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zA%kf.59802$6y4.52625@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<J5Xjf.5157$Et5.353943@news20.bellglobal.com> <TFXjf.47085$6y4.8198@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <y6Yjf.7656$wf2.836017@news20.bellglobal.com> <FoZjf.47094$6y4.38133@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <1133550230.780919.303800@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <i21kf.47144$6y4.15970@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqd1b$259m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WW3kf.47878$6y4.5280@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5vk3p11ngjff84g5uoolku2cru6nrjlijh@4ax.com> <iXlkf.49275$6y4.19491@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <jpa6p15do5p1eahf85a9au3j3jsnnmmi6f@4ax.com>`

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> enlightened us:
>>"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> don't practice inside the sanctuary. :-)"

What part of what I said equates to "[I] want to promote [my] religious 
beliefs" which is what *you* posted? Christians pay taxes like everyone 
else. Do you think they should not have equal access? Please understand, I 
*AM NOT* advocating that the government participate in, or otherwise solicit 
religion. I am speaking *solely and only* about free exercise of religion, 
either individually or collectively, on private or public property, as long 
as the individuals themselves, and not the government, are the ones making 
the decisions to participate. Should Christian groups not be able to meet in 
public parks? Many civic groups, such as Civitans, meet on school or other 
public property. Christian groups should (and do, by Supreme Court ruling) 
have the same rights. This is all I have addressed in these threads. And in 
none of these recent threads have I even mentioned the subject of Christians 
having the right to promote their religious beliefs. But, okay, since *you* 
brought it up, let's talk about that. :-)

What I want to ask is: what part of "If I can't discuss openly any subject I 
like with anyone I like, as long as the person I am talking with dosn't ask 
me not to do so, then I am not free" do you not understand? Is the 
government going to tell us what and where we can speak? Are you free to say 
to someone you are speaking with in public that you like or dislike some 
particular movie or football team or public personality? Then you should 
also be free to do the same thing about your religious beliefs! What the 
dickens do you think the expressions "free speech" and "freedom of religion" 
mean? Such things should be bound by principles of *politeness*, not *law*! 
When two people are having a discussion, and one of them brings up a subject 
the other does not want to discuss, then the other person should be free to 
decline to discuss that subject. But that is *very different* from a whole 
range of topics being publicly and legally forbidden! If we are going to 
regulate *by law* which subjects we are free to discuss in public, are we 
also going to regulate being disagreeable, or being rude, or being in a bad 
mood in public, *by law* for Heaven's sake? I don't think people are 
stopping to realize how draconian and dangerous your thinking is! Wake up 
people! If we permit such restrictive oppression toward one group today, 
what is your assurance it won't be *your* group that gets pegged tomorrow? 
It is the nature of a free society (and a Very Good Thing) that we are 
exposed to the opinions of many others (as is happening in this newsgroup 
right now). This is the very fabric of how ideas are exchanged among free 
people. Would you choose to dictate which ideas are to be censored in such a 
culture? Apparently so, as long as it is Christian ideas that are censored. 
But before you get too enthusiastic about weilding that censorial pen, you 
should know that *you are not immune*. Your very most cherished personal 
opinions may someday be the target of the very dictatorial authority that 
you cheerfully helped put in place to suppress public Christian expression. 
I really hope that is a Very Sobering Thought!

> I have a real problem with people who insist that prayer be allowed in
> public school.  That is the basis behind what I wrote.  Your writings
> seem to suggest that you support prayer in public school.  If I'm
> wrong, than I appologize, otherwise I stand by what I wrote.

Well, I do and I don't. As I said, I *do not* advocate that teachers lead 
students in prayer, or tell them to pray, or tell them what to pray. But I 
do say that any student who wants to bow their head and pray over their meal 
should be permitted to do so without hindrance, even audibly. Students say 
pretty much anything else they want audibly at lunch, don't they? Don't 
students in public schools often laugh and joke and tease each other, and 
call each other names audibly? Are you for regulating that by law also? In 
addition, if students, on their own, request to have Bible studies or prayer 
meetings in unused rooms after school, they should be permitted to do so, to 
the same degree civic groups would be permitted. US law and the US Supreme 
Court agree with me on both these points, but many schools are openly 
violating those laws, and the student's rights. Apparently you agree with 
that violation of the student's rights, and US law. :-)
```

```

Judson McClendon wrote:
> Christians pay taxes like everyone
> else. Do you think they should not have equal access?

Of course they have equal access, they get to go to school to do
_school_ things.

> I am speaking *solely and only* about free exercise of religion,
> either individually or collectively, on private or public property, as long
> as the individuals themselves, and not the government, are the ones making
> the decisions to participate.

We have this discussion in this country. One of our Menmbers of
Parliment is a Rastafarian (did I mention my grandfather ...) and part
of their religious observence is to smoke cannabis. How would you feel
if a young Rastafarian was allowed to smoke pot in the lunch room under
the same rule that you want to have for your group.

> Should Christian groups not be able to meet in public parks?
> Many civic groups, such as Civitans, meet on school or other
> public property.

How about New Nazi groups ? or Wicken coverns ? or Satanists ? or
Rastafarians ?

> What the
> dickens do you think the expressions "free speech" and "freedom of religion"
> mean?

Fortunately there is also freedom _from_ speech and freedom _from_
religion.

> then the other person should be free to
> decline to discuss that subject.

Unfortunately some people think that their religion is important, or
real, and think that being told to 'bugger off' is merely a test by
'the devil', one that they can overcome.

Would you allow Scientologists, or Jehovah Witnesses, or .. the freedom
to discuss their beliefs with your children ? To invite them to their
'sessions' ?

> Wake up
> people! If we permit such restrictive oppression toward one group today,
> what is your assurance it won't be *your* group that gets pegged tomorrow?

Well, actually it is.  They stopped various groups, such as Witches,
Communists and Fascists, Scientologists, and that was _by_ the
christians, but the rules apply to everyone.

>  Would you choose to dictate which ideas are to be censored in such a
> culture? Apparently so, as long as it is Christian ideas that are censored.

It is not a matter of _what_ it is a matter of _where_. There is _no_
censorship of what you say but only of where you say it.

> But I
> do say that any student who wants to bow their head and pray over their meal
> should be permitted to do so without hindrance, even audibly.

Sure, as long as they don't mind having left overs thrown at them.

> In
> addition, if students, on their own, request to have Bible studies or prayer
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> that violation of the student's rights, and US law. :-)

The decision as to which groups get toi use the facilities is entirely
that of the school board. If they let your group do this, then they may
be lobbied to let every different group in that claims it is a
religion: raelians, suny, shiite, mormans, wickens, bhrmans,
rastafarians, jedi, ..

I am sure that the 'civic groups' have to meet some criteria of
relevence to the school itself.
```

```
In article <zA%kf.59802$6y4.52625@bignews3.bellsouth.net>,
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

[snip]

>What part of what I said equates to "[I] want to promote [my] religious 
>beliefs" which is what *you* posted? Christians pay taxes like everyone 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>meet in public parks?

'Free exercise of religion' on government property... there's the rub, Mr 
McClendon.  It's one thing for two folks to have a chat about how many 
angels can dance on the head of a pin... another for a group to gather to 
sing hymns of praise to their Creator (in multi-part harmony, of 
course)...  and still another to erect a forty foot tall cross with 
flashing red and green lights... and yet each of these is a 'free exercise 
of religion'.  At some point even the most enlightened constabulary might 
say '*You might call it freely exercising your religion by a literal 
demonstrating of Mark.XVI:17-18... but rattlers are dangerous and there's 
young'uns in this park so how about you close up shop, *now*, or come 
downtown and face charges for violating... oh, Municipal Ordinance 123.45, 
Maintaining A Menagerie Without a Permit?'

As was stated in 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/6f0d4734091d086d?dmode=source&hl=en>

--begin quoted text:

You don't ask my tax dollars to go to support your interpretations of 
these admittedly imperfect relatings of what you are calling 'divine' and 
I don't ask your tax dollars to do the same with... whatever I come up 
with, as well.

(Note that when I say 'support' I use it in the broadest sense, including 
but not limited to such things as the wall of a building paid for by your 
taxes is not be used as a base for broadsheets for my church's functions, 
the salary of public officials paid for by your taxes is not to be used 
for their implementing of my religions tenets or mouthing of their 
preachings, the land paid for by your taxes is not to be used for displays 
of my religion's icons.  That would seem to be fair enough, wouldn't you 
say?)

--end quoted text

DD

- **References:** `<AZPjf.7025$Eq5.5412@pd7tw1no> <5vk3p11ngjff84g5uoolku2cru6nrjlijh@4ax.com> <0ls8p1psk03jtvaj4nd0qcrsgl605fb9rc@4ax.com> <QJ%kf.59813$6y4.43015@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`

In article <QJ%kf.59813$6y4.43015@bignews3.bellsouth.net>,
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

[snip]

>I wish parents of school children here in Alabama could get voucher 
>credit for such purposes, as in some states.

In a way, Mr McClendon, public education funds are like insurance 
policies... when enough folks put together a Large Mass of Money it 
behaves differently than discrete, smaller masses do; folks get covered 
for unexpecteds - kidney transplants are not inexpensive - and the 
insurance company employees get to make a living, too.

When such parents as you mention cease to gain benefit from living in a 
society where others have availed themselves of education this 'voucher 
system' might make sense... but until then it is only right and just to 
expect them to pay for that from which they derive benefit.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 36)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-12-05T20:41:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1133844116.793359.42430@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<gb8lf.53738$s92.32183@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<AZPjf.7025$Eq5.5412@pd7tw1no> <5vk3p11ngjff84g5uoolku2cru6nrjlijh@4ax.com> <0ls8p1psk03jtvaj4nd0qcrsgl605fb9rc@4ax.com> <QJ%kf.59813$6y4.43015@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dn2o28$dm1$1@reader2.panix.com> <gb8lf.53738$s92.32183@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote:

> Every time a citizen takes their child out of public school (for which they
> must still pay taxes) and put them into private school, it saves the
> government the cost of educating their children. Is it not fair for them to
> get at least some of those savings back? :-)

That's not true at all. If 30 or 40 are taken out that _may_ reduce the
costs by 1 whole teacher, or it may not, depending on how the classes
are arranged. The capital, management, administration, maintenance, and
many other costs are not reduced at all.

If you decide to walk instead of driving your car you won't get your
'share' of the roading costs paid back to you to pay for shoes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 38)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-06T17:53:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Leklf.155629$yS6.78631@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<AZPjf.7025$Eq5.5412@pd7tw1no> <5vk3p11ngjff84g5uoolku2cru6nrjlijh@4ax.com> <0ls8p1psk03jtvaj4nd0qcrsgl605fb9rc@4ax.com> <QJ%kf.59813$6y4.43015@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dn2o28$dm1$1@reader2.panix.com> <gb8lf.53738$s92.32183@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1133844116.793359.42430@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <zuhlf.56218$s92.38989@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:zuhlf.56218$s92.38989@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
> "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> schools right this minute. Is millions enough to qualify? ;-)

    Richard's point, I think, is that it is not EVERY time a citizen takes 
their child out of school, it saves the government the cost of educating 
their children. As he pointed out, if 30 or 40 children are taken out, then 
you can fire one teacher, and thus the government has saved the salary of 
that teacher, but taking removing 1/40th of the classroom does not result in 
a reduction of 1/40th of the teacher's salary.

    Public schools are always complaining about being underfunded. When you 
compare, for example, the typical computer lab of a private school versus 
that of a public one, their complaints might not be unwarranted. In other 
words, even with millions of students in private school, leading to large 
savings for the government, they still don't have enough money to fund the 
public schools well enough to compete with the private ones.

    The government could refund parents who send their kids to private 
schools, but then they'd have to make up for those costs somehow, presumably 
by charging the parents who sent their kids to public schools more.

    But I think one of the reasons we have public schools at all is so that 
all children can receive an education, regardless of their families' wealth. 
In other words, the people who need public schools the most desperately are 
those who cannot afford to pay for them. Under this light, it doesn't seem 
to make sense to only charge those people who actually make use of public 
schools.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-06T15:02:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37nlf.39$wP3.23@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<AZPjf.7025$Eq5.5412@pd7tw1no> <5vk3p11ngjff84g5uoolku2cru6nrjlijh@4ax.com> <0ls8p1psk03jtvaj4nd0qcrsgl605fb9rc@4ax.com> <QJ%kf.59813$6y4.43015@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dn2o28$dm1$1@reader2.panix.com> <gb8lf.53738$s92.32183@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1133844116.793359.42430@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <zuhlf.56218$s92.38989@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <Leklf.155629$yS6.78631@clgrps12>`

```
"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> not result in a reduction of 1/40th of the teacher's salary.

I agree that is Richard's point, Oliver. But that is an extremely myopic and 
misleading viewpoint. The fact is, millions of students have been pulled 
from public schools and enrolled into private schools. That total 
*enormously* impacts government costs. These millions of parents, as a group 
most definitely have taken an enormous burden from the taxpayers, out of 
their own pockets. They should also be compensated as a group. To suggest 
that this must be evaluated on a single parent basis totally distorts the 
whole picture. This is my point.

>    Public schools are always complaining about being underfunded. When you 
> compare, for example, the typical computer lab of a private school versus 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> public schools well enough to compete with the private ones.

Well, I doon't know about all private schools, but I do know a bit about 
Christian private schools in my area. My own church has a 600 student 
kindergarten through high school. And they would be *delighted* to have as 
tuition say 40% of what the state of Alabama spends per student.

>    The government could refund parents who send their kids to private 
> schools, but then they'd have to make up for those costs somehow, 
> presumably by charging the parents who sent their kids to public schools 
> more.

You're talking only transitory effects. If the state gave vouchers for say, 
50-75% of the cost the state spends on education, the flight from public 
schools would be phenomenal. Virtually every poor and minority student would 
have a chance at a private education. It would take two or three years for 
the private school sector to grow to accomodate them all. Teachers would be 
leaving the public school system in droves, and the good ones would be 
quickly snapped up by the private sector. The bad ones need to be in another 
business, anyway. In a short while, public education would be drastically 
reduced, leaving the state with the remaining 25-50% as a bonus for the 
majority of previous students. And parents would once again have a direct 
say about what their children were taught, by using swift responding 
marketplace dynamics instead of unweildy political methods where many 
different groups are pulling in many different directions. Private schools 
could advertise their teaching philosophies, as private schools do now, and 
parents could pick and choose. The only people who would lose in this are 
those who want to brainwash other people's kids into their own political or 
religious viewpoints. I say Yipee! to that. :-)

>    But I think one of the reasons we have public schools at all is so that 
> all children can receive an education, regardless of their families' 
> wealth.

Of course. But wouldn't it be Great if all students had a change at a 
private education, now mostly the privilidge of the afluent?

> In other words, the people who need public schools the most desperately 
> are those who cannot afford to pay for them. Under this light, it doesn't 
> seem to make sense to only charge those people who actually make use of 
> public schools.

Everybody would still pay taxes for education. Like building roads and other 
infrastructure, everybody in society benefits, even if they don't personally 
use a given public facility. Everyone benefits from a well educated 
populace, even those with no children. The idea is that you don't make 
parents pay *twice* for sending their children to a private school, and 
simultaneously help virtually every child have an opportunity to get a 
private education. Private schools as a group in this country have always 
had better academic standards and success than public schools. Only a very, 
very few public schools, usually in high income areas, can compare with the 
average private school on academics. What could possibly be bad about giving 
virtually every student a shot at that? :-)
```

```
On Tue, 6 Dec 2005 15:02:44 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>I agree that is Richard's point, Oliver. But that is an extremely myopic and 
>misleading viewpoint. The fact is, millions of students have been pulled 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>whole picture. This is my point.

If they should be compensated, why not compensate taxpayers with no
children?    Why not treat all people who don't have children in the
public school system the same?

Because we are paying taxes to turn children into taxpayers.   We
don't want kids to grow up ignorant and incapable of holding jobs. We
are not doing so to be nice to parents.    You aren't paying taxes to
educate *your* children, you are paying taxes to educate *children*.

I've seen private colleges ask for public funds as well - after all,
public colleges get those funds, it's only fair.   But would that
increase the number of educated children?   Not when public colleges
are available.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-06T21:54:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZLnlf.155666$yS6.109227@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<AZPjf.7025$Eq5.5412@pd7tw1no> <5vk3p11ngjff84g5uoolku2cru6nrjlijh@4ax.com> <0ls8p1psk03jtvaj4nd0qcrsgl605fb9rc@4ax.com> <QJ%kf.59813$6y4.43015@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dn2o28$dm1$1@reader2.panix.com> <gb8lf.53738$s92.32183@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1133844116.793359.42430@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <zuhlf.56218$s92.38989@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <Leklf.155629$yS6.78631@clgrps12> <37nlf.39$wP3.23@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:37nlf.39$wP3.23@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yipee! to that. :-)

    Part of the issue with schools is that they need to be near where kids 
live. There exists neighborhoods where it doesn't make any good sense to run 
a private school: The people who live there are simply too poor, so any 
private school you run there will hardly have any customers at all. The 
accounts would then just close down such schools, and open new ones where 
richer kids live.

    This is obviously unacceptable. The government wants to FORCE schools to 
exists even in locations where they would not be profitable. To make up for 
the lack of profit, the government has to give money to the schools.

    "Marketplace dynamics" is a nice idea in theory, but in practice, a lot 
of things get in the way preventing the poor from getting the best bang for 
their buck. Buying groceries in bulk, for example, while making more 
economic sense, is sometimes not feasible for a poor family because they 
cannot afford the upfront investment involved in buying more food.

    For younger kids for whom you wouldn't want to let take public 
transportation alone, you might not be able to afford the gasoline involved 
in driving them to school either. Taking the public transportation with them 
may also be infeasible depending on the schedules of your job.

    I occasionally see supermarkets in poorer neighborhoods actually charge 
MORE money than the supermarkets in rich neighborhoods for the same 
products, taking advantage of the fact that the people living there have 
less opportunity to search for a better deal.

>>    But I think one of the reasons we have public schools at all is so 
>> that all children can receive an education, regardless of their families' 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> private education, now mostly the privilidge of the afluent?

    This assumes that if all public schools closed down, all students (or 
their parents) would be able to afford going to one of the remaining private 
schools. It also assumes that the "cheap" private schools that might pop up 
will give a better education than the previously existing public schools.

    For the poor families who could barely afford sending their child to 
public schools (with the implied costs of buying books and public 
transportation or gasoline fees, and the lost-of-opportunity cost of not 
having the child take on a job to help fund the family), the only private 
schools they could afford, should all public schools close down, would be 
the ones that charged next to nothing. And with almost no income, you can 
expect the salary of the teachers to be extremely low. And so you can expect 
the teachers to be very incompetent.

>
>> In other words, the people who need public schools the most desperately 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> about giving virtually every student a shot at that? :-)

    What I foresee, from having less income for public schooling is less 
money being funded to public schools, meaning more public schools closing 
down, or less competent staff at the public schools, thus resulting in 
students getting a poorer education.

    It seems to me that you think the reason why every student could go to a 
private school if they wanted to, they just don't want to because they'd 
rather go to public school for free. Actually, my impression is that most 
parents, who love their children, want their children to receive the best 
education they possibly can. If they could afford to send their children to 
private schools, they would gladly do so. The problem is that the cannot 
afford it.

    Giving a tax break to the parents who send their children to private 
schools might encourage allow the parents on the border of the "afford/can't 
afford" line to switch over, but for the parents who can barely afford a 
monthly pass to public transportation, a tax break isn't going to make up 
for the significantly higher tuition fee. These families will STILL be send 
their children to public schools, only now there'll be less public schools, 
and the ones that remain will be crappier due to a smaller budget. Not a 
good solution, IMHO.

    - Oliver
```

```

Judson McClendon wrote:

> I agree that is Richard's point, Oliver. But that is an extremely myopic and
> misleading viewpoint.

Your specific point was entirely on a 'single parent'. It seems to be
_you_ that may have been 'myoptic and misleading'.

> These millions of parents, as a group
> most definitely have taken an enormous burden from the taxpayers, out of
> their own pockets.

Tough. You can't choose what taxes you pay and what you don't pay. Many
taxpayers have no children at all and still pay for schools.  Many
people are peaceful and still pay for the war.

>  They should also be compensated as a group.

They aren't a single group. There are thousands of diverse groups.

> To suggest
> that this must be evaluated on a single parent basis totally distorts the
> whole picture.

Then why did you do that ?

> Well, I doon't know about all private schools, but I do know a bit about
> Christian private schools in my area. My own church has a 600 student
> kindergarten through high school. And they would be *delighted* to have as
> tuition say 40% of what the state of Alabama spends per student.

And I would not be surprised to find that they are indoctrinating them
with the sort of mythology and fairy stories that you rant on about
instead of teaching them about the real world.

> You're talking only transitory effects. If the state gave vouchers for say,
> 50-75% of the cost the state spends on education, the flight from public
> schools would be phenomenal.

Well, there's another reason for not doing so.

> Everybody would still pay taxes for education. Like building roads and other
> infrastructure,

What about those who stay at home or only walk or use a helicopter, why
should they pay for everyone else to use the roads, their footwear
should be subsidized.

> everybody in society benefits, even if they don't personally
> use a given public facility. Everyone benefits from a well educated
> populace, even those with no children.

Yes, but we don't benefit from having indoctrinated diverse groups.
Muslim schools teach that only muslims are 'chosen' and only the koran
needs be studied. Baptist schools teach creationist myths and that they
are special. What about a KKK school, or a Scientology school, or a
Raelian school ?

Being well educated means _NOT_ being taught superstitious religious
mythology.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 36)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2005-12-06T10:22:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dn3op5$hia$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<AZPjf.7025$Eq5.5412@pd7tw1no> <QJ%kf.59813$6y4.43015@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dn2o28$dm1$1@reader2.panix.com> <gb8lf.53738$s92.32183@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <gb8lf.53738$s92.32183@bignews6.bellsouth.net>,
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
>> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>government the cost of educating their children.

No, Mr McClendon, it does *not* 'save the government the cost of educating 
their children'; to say this appears to demonstrate an almost willful 
ignorance of economics.

A citizen takes two children out of a school.  This does not require for 
the roof to be removed from over the heads of the remaining students... if 
*enough* citizens take their children out of public schools then yes, one 
can close down a building... but not for 'their child', as you clearly and 
unambiguously state.

A citizen takes three children out of a school.  This does not necessarily 
allow for a teacher to be laid off because now there are only thirty-one 
papers to grade a night instead of thirty-four; the remaining students 
still need to have their papers graded.

A citizen takes four children out of a school... doesn't mean that you can 
therefore fire the janitor because there will be no floors to sweep.

>Is it not fair for them to 
>get at least some of those savings back? :-)

I am saying that teaching a mass of students requires an economy of scale 
to be achieved and maintained, Mr McClendon; as all citizens benefit from 
an educated populace all citizens should be required to pay for it.

DD
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-12-06T08:11:15-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<p3abp1tnnhj5da03e26f034d03175jpacg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<AZPjf.7025$Eq5.5412@pd7tw1no> <QJ%kf.59813$6y4.43015@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dn2o28$dm1$1@reader2.panix.com> <gb8lf.53738$s92.32183@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <dn3op5$hia$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
On Tue, 6 Dec 2005 10:22:29 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>A citizen takes two children out of a school.  This does not require for 
>the roof to be removed from over the heads of the remaining students... if 
>*enough* citizens take their children out of public schools then yes, one 
>can close down a building... but not for 'their child', as you clearly and 
>unambiguously state.

Still, school districts need to have facilities for the number of
children to be taught.    The more children in a school district, the
greater the facilities are needed.    

When I choose not to run a program, it doesn't mean that I my computer
can be smaller anymore than if one student doesn't show up the school
can be made smaller.

But when we have more crowding of resources, we need bigger schools or
computers and we have to pay for them.

(My support for vouchers isn't about paying for people to send their
kids to religious schools - let them pay for that, even if it does
help us.   It's about allowing people without means to opt out of
inferior schools).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 38)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2005-12-06T15:45:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dn4bmu$832$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<AZPjf.7025$Eq5.5412@pd7tw1no> <gb8lf.53738$s92.32183@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <dn3op5$hia$1@reader2.panix.com> <p3abp1tnnhj5da03e26f034d03175jpacg@4ax.com>`

```
In article <p3abp1tnnhj5da03e26f034d03175jpacg@4ax.com>,
Howard Brazee  <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>On Tue, 6 Dec 2005 10:22:29 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>greater the facilities are needed.    

Yes... and no.  I don't remember exactly what it was called but decades on 
back I was taught in a calculus class something I remember as 'the post 
office function', in which for all values (input) in a range the result is 
the same until the input exceeds the bounds said range, at that point the 
result changes and is valid for the next range.  In the example of the 
postage required for mailing a letter:

0 - 1.000...0 oz = n(cents)
1.000...1 - 1.999...9 oz = n(cents) + m(cents)
2.000...0 - 2.999...9 oz = n(cents) + 2(m(cents))
3.000...0 - 3.999...9 oz = n(cents) + 3(m(cents))
...etc.

>
>When I choose not to run a program, it doesn't mean that I my computer
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>computers and we have to pay for them.

Quite so... but in the case of resources such as schools I believe that 
what are usually dealt with are ranges of quantities.

DD
```

- **References:** `<AZPjf.7025$Eq5.5412@pd7tw1no> <5vk3p11ngjff84g5uoolku2cru6nrjlijh@4ax.com> <0ls8p1psk03jtvaj4nd0qcrsgl605fb9rc@4ax.com> <QJ%kf.59813$6y4.43015@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dn2o28$dm1$1@reader2.panix.com> <gb8lf.53738$s92.32183@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <xLelf.1451$kt5.323113@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
On Tue, 06 Dec 2005 06:39:09 -0500, Donald Tees
<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>> Every time a citizen takes their child out of public school (for which they 
>> must still pay taxes) and put them into private school, it saves the 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>nope.

I'm not sure about that.   But schools aren't about "fair" for
individual taxpayers.   Lots of childless people are paying school
tax.    Is that fair?

Schools are about communities investing in their futures.    If
individuals are willing to take on some of this burden for their
children, the result is the same, and all of the other taxpayers pay
less.

We're being taxed for schools because communities need our future
taxpayers to be educated.   If they get educated, we won't have to be
taxed as much for welfare.   If they get educated for "free", then all
the better.    You won't have to take money from me at gunpoint in
order to educate your children.
```

Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Tue, 06 Dec 2005 06:39:09 -0500, Donald Tees
> <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


I am not sure that deliberately shielding a child from every view that
is not identical with their parents is a good thing for the child, or
for the community.

My roomy and I were discussing grandchildren today, and the illness's
that seem to be rife in kindergarden and the first couple of grades.
Seems that kids go to school for the first time, and they all share
germs, get sick, and go home again. If you've raised kids, I am sure you
are familiar with the syndrom.

The thing is, those very illness's are what gives the child a degree of
immunity later in life.

It strikes me that ideas I do not like are somewhat similar. I have no
desire to force my grandkids to be just like me. I want them to be
better, and that means new ideas.

Donald
```

or one class of problem hasn't shown the wide variety of problems that
would make him a good leader of us all.

That's a good point.  Here in Ohio the schools are funded through
levy's applied on your home.  Doesn't matter if you have kids in
private school or no kids at all.  You still have to pay the levy(s).
As my youngest is off to college, I think it unfair that I'm still
charged the levy.  And I thought it unfair when I had her in Catholic
grade and high schools.  But since it is voted on by the community, I
have no recourse but to pay it.  No tax break at the city or state
level either.

SkippyPB wrote:
> 
> That's a good point.  Here in Ohio the schools are funded through
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> level either.

Just for info, slightly different up here. How it works in other 
provinces don't know, others could confirm if different - Oliver for 
Quebec, Donald for Ontario and Peter for Manitoba.

For historical reasons when Upper and Lower Canada, (not sure which was 
which, but Quebec and Ontario) became Canada in 1837 (?) - parts west 
were just prairie - part of the Constitution (British North America Act) 
, while not recognizing a state religion did acknowledge catholic and 
non-catholic for education.

So here in Alberta along with annual city property taxes there is also a 
school tax. Unless you state otherwise your school tax automatically 
goes to the 'public' (non-religious); attest you are a catholic then 
your school tax goes to the 'separate' (Calgary Catholic School Board).
Although both my kids went to the 'separate' system, with due diligence 
my wife confirmed she was a non-catholic (Anglican/C of E). So her 
portion still goes to the 'public' even though we no longer have kids in 
the education system. We could fight it, but the rigmarole.....

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 38)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-06T21:22:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3inlf.155659$yS6.130119@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<AZPjf.7025$Eq5.5412@pd7tw1no> <5vk3p11ngjff84g5uoolku2cru6nrjlijh@4ax.com> <0ls8p1psk03jtvaj4nd0qcrsgl605fb9rc@4ax.com> <QJ%kf.59813$6y4.43015@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dn2o28$dm1$1@reader2.panix.com> <gb8lf.53738$s92.32183@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <blhbp19qeths8u9mqdt0gchfueofjl01r8@4ax.com> <oMmlf.57032$Gd6.29916@pd7tw3no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:oMmlf.57032$Gd6.29916@pd7tw3no...
>
> Just for info, slightly different up here. How it works in other provinces 
> don't know, others could confirm if different - Oliver for Quebec, Donald 
> for Ontario and Peter for Manitoba.

    I'm young enough that I've only recently been unsheltered from issues 
like taxes and such. Not even sure where to go to find out such info (though 
I suppose http://www.gouv.qc.ca/ might be a good start). I have yet to file 
an income tax return, for example.

    From what I understand, Montreal (the city in which I live) used to be 
divided into "boroughs" which had their own individual budgets (not sure 
where the income came from -- home-ownership taxes? Did merchants send their 
sales taxes to the borough head in which they were located? etc.) and so 
"rich neighborhoods" had better... well... everything (libraries, 
snow-plowing services, police, garbage collection, etc.) Recently (as in 
less than 5 years), these boroughs were merged together into a "super-city" 
collectively referred to as "Montreal", much to the protest of the residents 
of the richer neighborhoods.

    Now the rich still pay more taxes than the poor, but everyone gets 
equally good (or equally bad) services. E.g. the snow-plowing service has 
worsened significantly in the rich neighborhoods and improved significantly 
in the poorer neighborhoods.

    I suspect that these once-split-now-merged budgets also went into 
funding the public schools, but I can't confirm it.

    - Oliver
```

Judson McClendon wrote:
> Again, you are misinformed. The Constitution of the United States clearly 
> says it is Treason to espouse the violent overthrow of the United States 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> idealogy here in the United States.

And you are misinterpreting that period of your history. Should all the 
idealists, regardless of political stripe, who took off to support the 
anti-fascists in the Spanish Civil War be classified as Commies (exactly 
as we think of the USSR, NKVD, KGB etc.).

Without being aware of minor details, like the fact as a Russian you 
could disappear into a gulag forever, through the 20s/30s there were 
many young idealists, and certainly some are illustrated in the 
roll-call for the investigations during the McCarthy era. Only young 
myself at the time, I never followed through on specific cases if any of 
them truly were dangerous Commies - some may have been. But I certainly 
get the impression, not the majority - just if you like, misguided 
idealists. (Take the arts - how conceivably could a movie star be a 
'Clear and Present Danger' ?)

Did some of these idealists succumb to Communism - certainly the 
Cambridge Four did - turning MI5/6 on their heads where they fed vital 
intelligence information to the USSR. But note that was the UK not US.

Jimmy

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>> Again, you are misinformed. The Constitution of the United States clearly 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> as we think of the USSR, NKVD, KGB etc.).


Jimmy, I'm not really trying to defend McCarthy, or saying that those people 
you mention should have been branded as Communists. I'm only refuting the 
statement that people in the US had complete freedom to believe and espouse 
as they want. And I did so by pointing out that, when it comes to advocating 
the violent overthrow of the US Government, the US Constitution specifically 
forbids it. Since the US Government, including the Constitution and all 
statutory law, are subject to change peacefully through the democratic 
process, that seems to me to be a very reasonable restriction. Had the 
people of the US a will to do so, we could eliminate the Presidency and 
Congress and go for a Parliamentary government, all legally. The entire US 
Judiciary could be thrown out and rebuilt from the ground up, all legally.
```
