```
"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>    This tactic is called "Appeal to emotion", and is a logical fallacy.

It wasn't a 'tactic', I was simply establishing context. :-)

>> Yet the conventional medical wisdom of whether you should, after a burn, 
>> immediately place your (whatever was burned) into warm or cold water has 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> complicated.

It doesn't matter how complicated the science is. Determining whether burned 
flesh  heals faster if placed in warm or cool water is extremely simple. :-)

>> Once again, a scientific community that cannot determine, after millions 
>> of trials, something as simple and testable as that, defies description.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> free? Therefore, my employees are idiots."

You don't want to believe the Bible if you see flaws in it. Why do expect me 
to believe the scientific community that tells me they know how life formed, 
when I see flaws in their determinations? :-)

>> And you want me to believe these people when they tell me they *know* 
>> life got here without God? Uh, huh. :-)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> to change as new information arrives.

Wow, Oliver, have you been reading these posts? Do you see any *hint* in 
these posts that any of them (or yourself) are even remotely ready to accept 
that the evolutionists, for example, are climbing up the wrong tree? Oh yes, 
*know* is the correct expression, alright. :-)

>    When you meet a scientist, ask him (or her) what is the probability 
> that the Sun will rise tomorrow. If he says 100%, then he is either 
> uneducated as a scientist, or he is simplifying things (perhaps 
> patronizing you). A scientist should always be willing to reinvestigate 
> his beliefs upon new evidence.

Oliver, I really wish what you said there were true, I really do. I'm sure 
you believe it, but it just doesn't work that way.

>> I am familiar with those experiments, and they conclusively show that 
>> dirt can get perhaps as much as a hundred billionth of the way from 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> the result of an intelligent designer versus a fumbling random designer?

All the programs I am familiar with were created by some form of (perhaps 
questionable? :) intelligence. Complex systems can have behavior that is 
unexpected if one is not omniscent. That is a Very Different Thing from 
simplicity changing itself into complexity.

>    One tactic is to point a receiver dish to random points in the sky and 
> try to listen in for a signal. Do the people seeking intelligent life look 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> intelligence.

Well, the key word should probably not be 'complexity' but 'organization', 
or perhaps 'structured'. DNA, for example, is not only highly complex, it is 
highly organized and structured. A sine wave is very simple, but organized 
and structured. Random static is complex, but unorganized and unstructured.

>> The function of a living cell is like billions of tiny nano machines, all 
>> working together busily to sustain life.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> receive?

Sorry, by "working together" I did not mean to imply "cooperatively", but 
that the sum of their effect is to sustain the cell.

>> The way DNA works just boggles my mind at the incredible intricacy and 
>> precision of design.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> our evolutionary ancestors.

I suspect that there is an enormous amount of adaptability built into our 
DNA, that many scientists mistake for evolution when they see it in other 
organisms.

>> No machine made by man even comes close. Yet, you look at that incredible 
>> example of obvious intelligent design, designed by an intelligence vastly 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> one shot by a single creator with a plan and a unified vision.\

A big complex mess is evidence that a big complex something went wrong. :-)

>> There is no question that the academic community is generally hostile 
>> toward the Christian viewpoint. Any Christian who has ever been exposed 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> think the professors couldn't care less what religion you believe in.

Religious prejudice, like racial prejudice, is easy to overlook if you 
aren't on the receiving end. :-)

>    I have trouble believing that all secular colleges on Earth are hostile 
> to Christians, as you seem to be saying.

No, I'm not saying that. I have attended three different colleges and 
universities here in Alabama, and I didn't see a problem. But such 
anti-Christian prejudice is very common, I assure you.

>>> At what point do we cross from the living into the non-living? Again, I 
>>> argue that it's just a question of degree.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> isn't.

I will concede the point. I haven't opened a biology textbook in quite a 
while. :-)

>    I just want to point out that earlier in this post, you ridiculed 
> scientist for claiming they *know* something. Your exact statement was 
> "And you want me to believe these people when they tell me they *know* 
> life got here without God? Uh, huh. :-)"

Well, if these scientists have had personal contact with the Earth at the 
time when life actually got here, then they would have a better basis to 
*know*. :-) As it is, they are dealing with very indirect evidence that can 
(and often does) have many reasonable and competing explainations, and must 
pile assumption on assumption as they combine theories, all along the way. 
Human science has a very poor track record of establishing exactly what did, 
or will, happen when they aren't there to observe and experimentally verify.

Actually, I understand your objection. On the other hand, when one has 
specific and direct knowledge about something, should they fein ignorance, 
or proclaim what they know, even if it isn't in a form others might accept? 
The former is neither honest or satisfying, and the latter can be risky.

>> I can appreciate your reasoning. I have a suggestion to break the logical 
>> dilemma: give God an honest test. If you are honestly open to the 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> only they knew how.

I think a good starting point would be to go someplace private (no point in 
embarrassing myself unless there's a reason :) and start talking to God as 
if he can hear me. (If He's not there, or He won't listen, it's not going to 
matter, and a God who couldn't hear isn't going to do me any good.) I would 
tell God about my doubts, and that I would really like to know if He is 
there, and ask Him to reveal Himself to me so that I could know that He is 
real. It probably wouldn't hurt to throw in some thanks for any good stuff 
He may have sent my way.

I'm embarrassed to admit it, but I have been really ticked at God on 
occasion. I have complained and ranted, even been bitterly resentful and 
sarcastic. I find that I can express my true feelings and doubts to God, and 
He doesn't seem to resent it. At least, he hasn't blasted anything off me 
yet. :-) Once I calm down and get quiet, I can feel His Spirit tugging at my 
heart, as if He is saying "Come on, get over here and let Me give you a big 
hug." How can I resist? :-)
```

```
> But such anti-Christian prejudice is very common, I assure you.

It isn't 'anti christian' it is 'anti fundementalist ignorance'
regardless of which religion. You seem incapable of distinguishing
```

```
Somebody wrote:
> >> Yet the conventional medical wisdom of whether you should, after a burn,
> >> immediately place your (whatever was burned) into warm or cold water has
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> flesh  heals faster if placed in warm or cool water is extremely simple. :-)

Just so everyone knows: my sister-in-law (who works in a medical
practice but is neither a doctor nor a nurse) said to cool the burn in
cold water and then to wrap it in clingfilm. Wrapping in clingfilm
stops oxygen getting to the burn and stops the sensation of pain. BTW,
it does work.

However, if the skin has been burned off then the best advice is to do
nothing to the burn and definitely do not put anything on it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 50)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-17T21:20:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<1134306034.383469.185440@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134319362.237803.306970@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <s4_mf.1530$w7.1446@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <DjAnf.4839$w7.3842@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <aLCnf.146330$y_1.86429@edtnps89> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <qp53q1tur619iqmd93m80ccl64unttcutp@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> What evidence would you accept as evidence that evolution is correct?

Well, I have already logically proven that, if Biblical Creation is correct, 
then any possible physical evidence is useless as a proof. However, for man 
to set up an experiment whereby lifeless dirt formed, all on its own, into 
living cells would go a long way toward convincing me the idea is not an 
outrageous impossibility. :-) Remember, even if man created a living cell in 
a lab through some process, then you still have life being created by 
intelligence, not life forming all by itself, which is what evolution 
presupposes. :-)

After that, then a good description of how things like a human eye or 
bisexual reproduction could evolve would be necessary. The problem is not 
simply the complexity of those things, it is the fact that they would have 
no survival value (perhaps even the reverse) until virtually complete, 
therefore removing any basis for natural selection to work and cause the 
structure to evolve.

Then, as a final touch, explain how love and art and beauty (or sense of 
these things) could evolve. An evolutionary process might come up with lust, 
but why love? Why artistic sense? Why musical sense? In a purely 
evolutionary context, these things make no sense at all. Even if having them 
could be shown to have survival value, they would only work *after* they 
were completely evolved and functional, so that art and music were actually 
produced. Not very logical.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 51)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-12-17T20:33:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1134880390.378798.49500@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<1134306034.383469.185440@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134319362.237803.306970@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <s4_mf.1530$w7.1446@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <DjAnf.4839$w7.3842@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <aLCnf.146330$y_1.86429@edtnps89> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <qp53q1tur619iqmd93m80ccl64unttcutp@4ax.com> <yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
> no survival value (perhaps even the reverse) until virtually complete,

Not true at all. As I previously mentioned (and you probably ignored)
there is in molluscs today examples of every stage required to make an
eye that is like ours, or in fact is better (no blind spot).

Each step from simple light sensitive skin through pits, simple fixed
lens, right to the mobile eye of the octopus serves as an advantage
that suits the needs of the creature.

> Then, as a final touch, explain how love and art and beauty (or sense of
> these things) could evolve. An evolutionary process might come up with lust,
> but why love? Why artistic sense? Why musical sense? In a purely
> evolutionary context, these things make no sense at all.

Just because you do not understand does not make them senseless. 'Love'
certainly has a place, it is directly related to recognition, for
animals to breed they must first be able to recognise their own kind,
whether this be by sight or smell or touch or some other. In fact there
is one whole part of the brain dedicated to recognising 'faces' and
only faces. The corresponding part in the other hemisphere recognises
_everything_else. When one loves another it is because that particular
recognition triggers pleasure receptors in the brain (which are also
triggered BTW by heroin and, in females, chocolate). The evolution of
'love' is the progress from merely recognising the same species, or
particular characteristics, to that of a specific individual leading to
stable pairing, such as in birds, which _is_ an evolutionary advantage
(better survival of young).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

- **In-Reply-To:** `<yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<1134306034.383469.185440@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134319362.237803.306970@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <s4_mf.1530$w7.1446@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <DjAnf.4839$w7.3842@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <aLCnf.146330$y_1.86429@edtnps89> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <qp53q1tur619iqmd93m80ccl64unttcutp@4ax.com> <yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote:
> Well, I have already logically proven that, if Biblical Creation is correct,
> then any possible physical evidence is useless as a proof.
>
> After that, then a good description of how things like a human eye or
> bisexual reproduction could evolve would be necessary.

Not at all difficult, especially as complex eyes have evolved at least
four times already.

> The problem is not
> simply the complexity of those things,

No, they are really very simple and are a long series of simple
enhancements (much like a spaghetti programme) building up over the
aeons. Look at cellular automata; their behaviour and patterns can be
truly mind boggling but they obey only simple rules.

> it is the fact that they would have
> no survival value (perhaps even the reverse) until virtually complete,

Actually, each tiny step in evolution has a survival value. That is why
they get retained.


> Then, as a final touch, explain how love and art and beauty (or sense of
> these things) could evolve.

What is beauty? Can you define it? As for love and lust: lust started
in order to ensure procreation. Love is the mechanism which ensures
that the male hangs around long enough afterwards to ensure that the
osspsring survives to adulthood.

An evolutionary process might come up with lust,
> but why love? Why artistic sense? Why musical sense?

Art and music? It seems that virtually every artistic or musical genius
these days can be diagnosed as showing serious mental flaws
(schizophrenia, autism to name two). These clearly are not good
survival traits but they do ensure the survival of traits within a gene
pool which could possibly contribute to the future survival of the
group.

In a purely
> evolutionary context, these things make no sense at all. Even if having them
> could be shown to have survival value, they would only work *after* they
> were completely evolved and functional,

Not true, their advantage is confered as the traits evolve. Even a
single celled eye (known as ocelli in the insect world) confers
advantage to an individual over a blind member of the same species.
```

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:JL3qf.20086$k76.4760@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
<snip>>
> Why do you say that? I don't know if man will ever be capable of creating 
> life or not. That isn't the issue. But I believe the proposition that dirt 
> formed itself into life is preposterous.

If you accept that the basis of life is a molecule that can reproduce itself 
(DNA), then it should not escape your notice that there are two elements 
that can combine with themselves: silicon and carbon.

If carbon atoms combine with themselves, that is a first step towards 
reproducing themselves. If they combine with other simple substances (like 
hydrogen, just to name one...) and then start hooking up with each other, it 
is possible that eventually structures (like benzene rings, and other 
radicals) will arise from this process. As these structures become 
multiplied it is likely that sequences of them will form and, because of the 
underlying "desire" (propensity, if you prefer...) of carbon to combine with 
itself, these sequences are likely to become duplicated. It is hardly a huge 
leap from this "behaviour" to a molecule that is driven to reproduce itself. 
"A hen is an egg's way of making another egg."

Before you know it, you have complex organic molecules... from soot.

Life from dirt.

No divine intervention; just chemistry and physics over a very long period 
of time.

Preposterous? No more so than the idea of a super hero taking clay and 
breathing "life" into it...


>And that is the issue.

Well, if that is the issue, it isn't much of an issue... :-)

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134319362.237803.306970@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <s4_mf.1530$w7.1446@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <DjAnf.4839$w7.3842@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <aLCnf.146330$y_1.86429@edtnps89> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <qp53q1tur619iqmd93m80ccl64unttcutp@4ax.com> <yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <gamdq1ts42ot5sp0uvk2a9bdn6a8uvnfqh@4ax.com> <JL3qf.20086$k76.4760@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <40srj8F1bnru8U1@individual.net> <1135188623.500797.230460@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> 
> > Preposterous? No more so than the idea of a super hero taking clay and
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> to create superhero. No, wait ....

Doesn't matter whether you believe in God or not.  Or the spaghetti
monster.  Sooner or later, as you probe backwards in time with your
thoughts, you'll arrive at the realization that there must have been a
first "something" which was "always" there.

As an agnostic, I can't deny that "something" might be God, although I
have no reason to think it is so; I'm more comfortable with the idea of
an eternal, infinite emptiness (a vacuum, actually, full of vacuum
energy) out of which various ylems pop into existence (a la Hawking)
which may or may not expand into a universe such as ours.  In another
post I pointed out that since there's been one big bang, it's reasonable
to posit that there have been others.  Given that, there must have been 
SPACE between them - therefore infinite emptiness, rather than
nothingness which suddenly became an infinitesimal somethingness.

Starting off with such a vacuum means that we don't have to worry about
an infinite regress.  If we do it by regress, there are now in existence
an infinite number of things, each of which was produced by and produced
another thing, none of which is less than an infinite number of years
old (except, I suppose, the current one which MIGHT be "nearly"
infinite).  One infinity is better that an infinite number of
infinities.  Although it's possible that there are an infinite number
...

My head's aching.  Cantor - I know how you felt!

PL
```

```

"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mts.net> wrote in message 
news:43A9C2DF.14C9B712@mts.net...
>
> I'm more comfortable with the idea of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> nothingness which suddenly became an infinitesimal somethingness.

    I believe most cosmologists, like Hawking, are feel that it is a very 
important point to stress that the "Big Bang" is the expansion of space 
itself, and not, for example, the expansion of a fireball within space. In 
the beginning, when big bang was just a tiny particle about the explode, it 
was not a tiny particle within a vast and infinite space; that tiny particle 
is space itself!

    They want to emphasize that what is "outside" the Big Bang is NOT empty 
space, because that would imply that space existed outside of the Big Bang, 
which is not the case. Space itself was constrained to that tiny little 
point, and space itself has expanded into the large proportions we see 
today.

    Furthermore, there is no time without space. That's another point 
Hawking stresses. So if there WERE multiple Big Bangs, it does not make 
sense to say one occured "before" or "after" the other, because there is no 
such thing as time outside of each particular big bang.

>
> Starting off with such a vacuum means that we don't have to worry about
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> ...

    The current universe's age is estimated to be on the order of 10-15 
billion years old, and even the value "100 billion years" is still a lot 
smaller than "infinitely many years". The reason we can estimate the age of 
the universe is because we see that the universe is expanding, and so if we 
"run the clock backwards", we can see how long ago it was before the 
universe must have been nothing more than an infinitely small spec (when the 
Big Bang occured).

    There are a few extra details (e.g. that gravity is curving space-time, 
that the expansion of the universe wasn't a constant speed, but accelerated 
and decelerated over time, etc.), but that's the basic gist of it.

    - Oliver
```

```
"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mts.net> wrote in message 
news:43A9C2DF.14C9B712@mts.net...
> Richard wrote:
>>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> first "something" which was "always" there.

Not necessarily, Peter. I am persuaded by the Big Bang theory and have done 
much reading and given it considerable thought.

If time began as the space expanded, then what existed before the beginning 
of time is meaningless.

If it came from a point singularity, then the existence of that point 
started when time started.

If it came from a quantum field fluctuation in multi-dimensional hyperspace, 
then that fluctuation occurred at the moment time began.

Certainly, as I "probe backward in time with MY thoughts" I do NOT come to 
"the realization that there must have been a
first "something" which was "always" there."

If all the energy (matter) in the universe is considered, it comes to zero. 
(That is why antimatter annihilates matter). Everything came from nothing. 
The imbalances that may have led to the creation of our universe have more 
to do with virtuality than with reality.

I also believe that within the next 20 years this whole process will be much 
better understood than it is presently.

Neither Time, God, nor any other dimension were relevant BEFORE the first 
event.

Pete.
```

```
>
> If all the energy (matter) in the universe is considered, it comes to 
> zero. (That is why antimatter annihilates matter).

    When matter and anti-matter come in contact, they transform into energy. 
So if the universe were compose of an equal amount of matter and anti 
matter, and if we brought all of that together, we'd end up with a large 
amount of energy. Assuming there is no such thing as "anti-energy", this 
doesn't result in a zero sum.

    I hadn't considered this before, so I'm not sure how (or if) the big 
bang theory addresses the issue of a non-zero sum. Guess I have another item 
to add to my weekend-research checklist.

    - Oliver
```

- **References:** `<mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134319362.237803.306970@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <s4_mf.1530$w7.1446@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <DjAnf.4839$w7.3842@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <aLCnf.146330$y_1.86429@edtnps89> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <qp53q1tur619iqmd93m80ccl64unttcutp@4ax.com> <yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <gamdq1ts42ot5sp0uvk2a9bdn6a8uvnfqh@4ax.com> <JL3qf.20086$k76.4760@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <40srj8F1bnru8U1@individual.net> <1135188623.500797.230460@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43A9C2DF.14C9B712@mts.net> <40ufi2F1c0rt8U1@individual.net> <42Aqf.1798$m05.1599@clgrps12>`

```

"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
news:42Aqf.1798$m05.1599@clgrps12...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> energy.

Not exactly. (Energy is released; they are annihilated, not transformed)

> So if the universe were compose of an equal amount of matter and anti 
> matter, and if we brought all of that together, we'd end up with a large 
> amount of energy. Assuming there is no such thing as "anti-energy", this 
> doesn't result in a zero sum.
>
There is "anti-energy". It was shown by Dirac to exist and a positron (for 
example) is comprised of it.

The original equations of Relativity show that the energy of a particle that 
has mass M and momentum P is given by:

E^2 = M^2C^4 + P^2C^2

Which reduces to the well known: E = MC^2 when the momentum is zero.

Because the more familiar equation comes from taking the square root of the 
full equation, E can be + or -

Of course most people discount the - side, as the only answer we are 
obviously interested in, is the positive root.

Dirac (being a genius) did not take this obvious step but instead considered 
the implications. When energy levels are calculated in the relativistic 
version of  Quantum Mechanics, there are two sets: one all positive, 
corresponding to MC^2, and one all negative, corresponding to MINUS MC^2.

Electrons ought, according to the theory, to fall into the lowest unoccupied 
energy state and even the highest negative energy state is lower than the 
lowest positive energy state. So what do the negative energy levels mean, 
and why didn't all the electrons in the universe fall into them and 
disappear?

Dirac's answer hinged upon the fact that electrons are fermions and that 
only one electron can go into each possible state (two per energy level; one 
with each spin). It must be, he reasoned that electrons don't fall into 
those negative states because all those states are full. What we call "empty 
space" is actually a sea of negative energy electrons! He didn't stop there. 
Give an electron energy and it will jump up the ladder of energy states. So, 
if we give an electron in the negative energy state some more energy, it 
ought to jump into the real world and become visible as an ordinary 
electron. To get from the state of -MC^2 to +MC^2 clearly requires an energy 
input of 2MC^2, which, for the mass of an electron is about 1MeV and can be 
provided quite easily in atomic processes or when particles collide with 
each other.

The negative energy electron, promoted into the real world, would be normal 
in every respect, but it would leave behind a "hole" in the negative energy 
sea, the ABSENCE of a negatively charged electron. Such a hole, said Dirac, 
ought to behave like a positively charged particle (the absence of a 
negatively charged particle in a negative sea ought to show up as a positive 
charge). In 1936 Andersen won the Nobel prize for detecting the exact 
particle with the right mass and charge (positron) that Dirac had predicted 
from the above.

Any particle can, in principle, be produced by the Dirac process above, 
directly from energy, provided it is always accompanied by production of its 
antiparticle counterpart, the "hole" in the negative energy sea.

Although Physicists today have more erudite explanations for particle 
creation, the rules are fundamentally the same. When a particle meets its 
anti-particle it "falls into the hole", liberating energy 2MC^2 and 
disappearing. (Not so much in a puff of smoke, but in a burst of gamma 
rays.).

The released energy (photons) could interact to create new electrons and 
anti-electrons or other particles, or can move back across the Planck 
threshold (or not). The effect is a 'balance' of negative and positive 
energy. I referred to this as "zero".

I stand by it.

(Some of the above is paraphrased from "In Search of Schrodinger's Cat" by 
John Gribbin.)

Pete.

>    I hadn't considered this before, so I'm not sure how (or if) the big 
> bang theory addresses the issue of a non-zero sum. Guess I have another 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:41236bF1ct6kvU3@individual.net...
>
> There is "anti-energy". It was shown by Dirac to exist and a positron (for 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> into them and disappear?

    I'm having trouble accepting this last part. If negative energy states 
exist, then there are either an infinite number of negative energy states, 
or there are a finite number of them (tautology). If there are an infinite 
number of negative energy states, the electrons should just be "sinking" 
forever, and we would never see one.

    If there are a finite number of negative energy state, then there must 
be a state which is the "lowest of all", which, when the electrons reach, 
stop sinking. This lowest energy state has some number, say, for 
example, -42. Whatever number that state has, it seems really arbitrary, and 
rather than calling it, for example, -42, why not just call it the zero 
state?

    In other words, to avoid arbitrariness, there should either be zero 
negative energy states, or there should be infinitely many of them.

    [Nothing new added beyond this point, save my signature]

    - Oliver

>
> Dirac's answer hinged upon the fact that electrons are fermions and that 
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> John Gribbin.)
```

```
"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
news:tvVqf.12543$m05.11486@clgrps12...
>
>
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> negative energy states, or there should be infinitely many of them.

No, why should it be any different from the known states (which are not 
infinite) that electrons can configure themselves?

I think you may be falling into the same trap as the old "blackbody 
radiation" conundrum (see Boltzmann and Planck). It is important to realise 
that energy states are not continuous and therefore cannot be infinite. This 
was what led to the foundation of quantum theory. Energy exists as discrete 
packets (quanta), that's why blackbodies don't emit infinite radiation.

I don't intend to argue it here (this is the wrong forum, anyway :-)) but I 
wanted to explain what I meant by suggesting that all of the energy and 
matter in the universe sums to zero, and that is where it came 
from...nothing. :-)

What we see is material in different states... if you have covered electron 
tunnelling you will know that energy can be "borrowed" from virtuality (as 
long as you pay it back before the universe notices you took it :-)). If it 
was all "re-integrated" into a single state (somewhat akin to the first 
nanoseconds of the Big Bang, and the goal of GUTs) we would end up with the 
nothing we started with...

Pete.

[No more below]
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message
> > "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> example) is comprised of it.

I thought this smelled a little off. A positron (posited by Dirac and
proved to exist by others) is an anti-matter particle composed of
energy which will annihilate with a matter particle releasing energy.
There is no anti-energy; the result of annihilation is energy.

Wikipedia: The positron is the antiparticle or the antimatter
counterpart of the electron. The positron has an electric charge of +1,
a spin of 1/2, and the same mass as an electron. When a positron
annihilates with an electron, their mass is converted into energy in
the form of two gamma ray photons. (See electron-positron annihilation)

>
> Dirac (being a genius) did not take this obvious step but instead considered
> the implications. When energy levels are calculated in the relativistic
> version of  Quantum Mechanics, there are two sets: one all positive,
> corresponding to MC^2, and one all negative, corresponding to MINUS MC^2.

Or e=imc**2  ?

Does that mean that we could be living in an imaginary universe?
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1135628578.058239.193080@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com...
>
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, that particular interaction is so and I said it ends up in a cloud of 
gamma rays (photons).  My statement that the positron is comprised of 
anti-energy is incorrect; at the time I learned this, that was the agreed 
understanding. More recent work has shown it is not so. However, Quantum 
theory predicts that said photons will behave in a number of ways, including 
popping out of existence or combining into electrons again. And none of this 
denies the existence of negative energy.

Negative energy is a concept that is required by Quantum Mechanics and the 
Uncertainty Principle.

A quick search shows the following links:

http://www.physics.hku.hk/~tboyce/sf/topics/wormhole/wormhole.html
http://www.physics.nmt.edu/~raymond/classes/ph13xbook/node150.html (this one 
includes the Relativistic equation I quoted which forms the basis for the 
deduction of negative energy)
http://jnaudin.free.fr/html/gsea21.htm
http://www.cheniere.org/references/negative%20energy.htm (this one bears out 
my point regarding everything equating to zero)

I did not search or read any of the above before today, and did so only in 
response ot your post.

My point in posting any of this was that it is possible to get something out 
of nothing. In fact, the "something" "remembers" that it came from nothing, 
and could be collapsed back into nothingness (at least, theoretically).

My proposition was that if all the energy and matter in the universe was 
paired with it's anti-equivalent, the result would be zero. Oliver's 
objection was that there is no such thing as negative energy; I think that 
has been shown to be false, there is.

>>
>> Dirac (being a genius) did not take this obvious step but instead 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No, it doesn't mean that. But we certainly could be living in an imaginary 
universe... :-)

Pete.
>
```

```
Defaultuser wrote:
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
> <snip>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Interesting that you developed above theme, because I'm still on the 
tack 'Nothing' doesn't become 'Something'. Bear with me, see if my lousy 
description puts some of you on to the right track, then having got the 
correct name, go to pbs.org and Nova and do a search on the topic by name.

Unfortunately I came into the TV programme during about the last 25%, 
roughly a month or two back. Taking something like a small branch on a 
tree, or another example might be a strand of patterned seaweed, using 
magnification, you can get closer into the object and what is revealed 
are a set of repetitive steps, the same structure appearing more 
minutely, over and over again. Chances are what I was looking at were 
coloured computerised graphic patterns, and it was the colouring which 
initially attracted me. Obviously the more powerful the microscope the 
more depth one could get into - probably to the point of infinity - I 
wasn't considering this topic at the time - but perhaps there just isn't 
infinity to this process. If we think 'humans' we would tend to suggest 
there is an infinity - but is that 'assuaged' by the fact that this 
process of infinity is generated from the mother's womb or the male 
sperm or both, going back to endless previous generations.

It seems to me, that our so called pure scientific thought is derived 
from our own human historical experience. We tend to see time as being 
finite, the sun rises, the sun sets, the earth cycles with other planets 
and we have seasons etc. For a human 'convenience' we have built the 
concept of time to indicate a Beginning and an End. Not only that, but 
how do we place the Ice Age, Bronze Age, Iron Age without some time 
check-book. Time was further compartmentalised into calendars, hence the 
fact that the old Jewish calendar gives us Hannakuh on the same day as 
Christmas this year.

With due regard to pure scientists like Einstein and Hawking, I see the 
word 'theory' being used to explain current concepts. Are they still 
purely theory or are they provable, but only by a 'leap of faith', in a 
scientific context. An honoured man has got his logic and mathematics 
right, so he must be right. On the basis that he is right, now we 
advance further with another extension of scientific thought.

Back to the tree twig or seaweed, unless proven otherwise, it looks to 
me like hard evidence for an infinity and that there never was and never 
will be a Beginning and an End. Things, shapes might look very different 
in a zillion years from now, but still no nearer to a finite conclusion.

I searched PBS thinking Evolution might give me a lead to what I had 
been watching. No luck - but I did find the following under that 
ubiquitous title - FAQ :-

11. Does evolution prove there is no God?  	

   	No. Many people, from evolutionary biologists to important religious 
figures like Pope John Paul II, contend that the time-tested theory of 
evolution does not refute the presence of God. They acknowledge that 
evolution is the description of a process that governs the development 
of life on Earth. Like other scientific theories, including Copernican 
theory, atomic theory, and the germ theory of disease, evolution deals 
only with objects, events, and processes in the material world. Science 
has nothing to say one way or the other about the existence of God or 
about people's spiritual beliefs.


Jimmy
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41fogmF12afhmU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <s4_mf.1530$w7.1446@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <DjAnf.4839$w7.3842@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <aLCnf.146330$y_1.86429@edtnps89> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <qp53q1tur619iqmd93m80ccl64unttcutp@4ax.com> <yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <gamdq1ts42ot5sp0uvk2a9bdn6a8uvnfqh@4ax.com> <JL3qf.20086$k76.4760@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <40srj8F1bnru8U1@individual.net> <1135188623.500797.230460@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43A9C2DF.14C9B712@mts.net> <40ufi2F1c0rt8U1@individual.net> <42Aqf.1798$m05.1599@clgrps12> <41236bF1ct6kvU3@individual.net> <1135628578.058239.193080@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <41bep6F1e5j00U1@individual.net> <1135701669.486720.26320@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <ljfsf.3728$bd.1943@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <ujnsf.90462$2k.14349@pd7tw1no>`

```
 
Jimmy,

I responded to this because it needed response :-)

Obviously, I strongly disagree with your position, but please bear in mind I 
totally respect your right to hold it and I don't think you are "wrong"; 
just that it is an individual thing and what works for you simply doesn't 
for me...

Discussions in CLC should never be too serious.

Comments below...

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:ujnsf.90462$2k.14349@pd7tw1no...
> Defaultuser wrote:
>> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>
And the "right track" would be...? :-)

> Unfortunately I came into the TV programme during about the last 25%, 
> roughly a month or two back.

What TV program? What topic?  Need more information here for those of us 
outside North America. I have some excellent DVDs from pbs.org sponsored by 
Nova. One of my favourites is the DVD of the programs made around Brian 
Greene's Book "The Elegant Universe". It describes many of the topics we 
have been discussing and, like the book itself, is easily assimilable by 
anyone remotely interested.

> Taking something like a small branch on a tree, or another example might 
> be a strand of patterned seaweed, using magnification, you can get closer 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> was the colouring which initially attracted me.

This is a well known phenomenon and represents the Mandelbrot set.  It is 
covered by a simple algorithm based on a simple equation and these sets can 
be generated on a computer. Some of them are extremely beautiful.

> Obviously the more powerful the microscope the more depth one could get 
> into - probably to the point of infinity - I wasn't considering this topic 
> at the time - but perhaps there just isn't infinity to this process.

No, it is far from infinite. Democritus used the process you are describing 
more than two millenia ago, and he didn't even have a microscope! He 
reasoned that if he could cut something in half and keep on doing that (in 
effect, magnifying it by two each time he cut it), he should eventually 
arrive at a particle so  small it could not be divided. This would be a 
fundamental building block of Nature. He named it with the Greek word 
"atom", meaning "indivisible", but a Kiwi named Ernest Rutherford upset his 
apple cart and split it in the early 20th century.  That doesn't mean 
Democritus was "wrong"; it just means there was more...

Today, we have progressed to the point where we recognize that there ARE 
fundamental particles and we understand they arise from fluctuations in an 
energy field that was created some 10 -20 billion years ago. People 
sometimes say: what if the quarks are splittable? They might be. (It is 
considered unlikely, but so was splitting the atom until Rutherford did 
it.). Even if they were, we know the process is not infinite. It stops at 
the Planck length. I sometimes visualise the H bar  as a bistro that sits on 
the border between reality and virtuality. Across the border is meaningless 
in this universe. Although by the time you reach the H bar you are 
unthinkably small (several trillion times smaller than an atom), you are 
still finite.

> If we think 'humans' we would tend to suggest there is an infinity - but 
> is that 'assuaged' by the fact that this process of infinity is generated 
> from the mother's womb or the male sperm or both, going back to endless 
> previous generations.
>

I don't understand this. When I think 'humans' I think "latecomers"... been 
here no more than 3 million years... that's a few seconds on the clock of 
the Earth's 5 billion years. 'Humans' to me certainly does not suggest 
'infinity'... The dinosaurs were here for over 200 million years and 
cockroaches for around 600 million (no wonder the little buggers are 
impossible to eradicate... :-)). There are no "endless" previous 
generations; it is a finite line. The DNA you carry today did not come from 
somewhere in infinity. Just because you don't know the details of every 
generation of it, doesn't mean it is infinite. It is demonstrably finite. 
(There was a time in the Earth's history when there were no humans on it.) 
Similarly, there will be a time when humanity no longer exists. Even if we 
managed to disperse ourselves throughout the universe we will still be 
nailed by it's eventual heat death, just like all other living creatures (if 
there are any) at that time. When the stars go cold and the night closes in, 
none of our kind will witness it. It is a long way off, but nothing like 
'infinity'...(30 billion years max...)

The line of Adam's seed is not infinite in the backwards or forwards 
direction...

> It seems to me, that our so called pure scientific thought is derived from 
> our own human historical experience.

It isn't "so called"; the scientific method is a stated set of principles. 
Certainly, it is based on logic and experience, rather than faith. "So 
called" is incorrect and pejorative in this context.

>We tend to see time as being finite, the sun rises, the sun sets, the earth 
>cycles with other planets and we have seasons etc.

Agreed. That fits the observations.


>For a human 'convenience' we have built the concept of time to indicate a 
>Beginning and an End.

It is observably so. Time is more than a concept. The cycle of "creation - 
survival - destruction" is demonstrable in all things, at all levels. All 
the great religions of the world recognize it; the Bible talks of creation 
and destruction, the Hindus have separate Gods to handle it (Brahma the 
Creator; Shiva the Destroyer), and so on. The concept of Beginning and End 
is demonstrable in Nature and far from being a 'human convenience'. 
(Actually, I find it bloody INconvenient when I have to get up in the 
morning and go to work... :-))

>Not only that, but how do we place the Ice Age, Bronze Age, Iron Age 
>without some time check-book. Time was further compartmentalised into 
>calendars, hence the fact that the old Jewish calendar gives us Hannakuh on 
>the same day as Christmas this year.
>
So? We recognize that time passes and mark it's passing. What is your point 
here?

> With due regard to pure scientists like Einstein and Hawking, I see the 
> word 'theory' being used to explain current concepts. Are they still 
> purely theory or are they provable, but only by a 'leap of faith', in a 
> scientific context.

If you disagree with the "theory" of gravitation, it will not enable you to 
fly unassisted.

Scientists don't take "leaps of faith" (well, good ones don't...). They 
observe and postulate, based on their observations. Those postulates are 
then tested by experimentation and new data is acquired. From this, new 
postulates are made, and so on. Every so often, something is confirmed 
beyond all reasonable doubt and passes into accepted science.

The problem has been that science is suffering from its own success. During 
the last 80 years more knowledge has been acccumulated than in the previous 
3 million. When you talk about the word 'theory' being used currently, that 
is because some of these 'current concepts' don't fit the traditional 
classical ideas that have been with us for thousands of years. How can a 
scientist explain quantum weirdness in a form that people can assimilate, 
with brains that have evolved to deal with three dimensions and cause- 
effect timelines? How do we cope with phenomena that deny logicality? What 
about things that behave contrary to normal experience? What about 
experiments that cannot be guaranteed to be repeatable, but only have a 
probability of being so? All of these ideas are new and exciting and 
scientists are only just coming to deal with them.

When we find that some of these things look like they may not be "True" but 
we cannot prove them "False" either, we are forced to call them 
'theoretical' until more evidence arrives that helps us understand better. 
In some cases, we don't need to understand in order to be able to use the 
knowledge acquired. We certainly don't know what particles and energy are 
doing when we are not watchng them, or even if they exist or not at that 
time, but that doesn't stop us building microwaves, lasers, computers, cell 
phones, satellite communications and other high tech electronic 
applications. The fundamental probabilistic laws apply. We know a certain 
number of electrons will tunnel through a substrate and create a transistor, 
even though we don't know which ones or at what time.

My point is that we don't need EXACT knowledge any more for something to be 
scientific. In fact, the Uncertainty Principle tells us there are some 
things we are simply not allowed to know or measure, except within certain 
ranges, based on probablilty. (However, that doesn't stop us searching for 
more understanding of why things behave the way they do.)

>An honoured man has got his logic and mathematics right, so he must be 
>right.

Or, a Bishop or Cardinal has a Doctorate in Divinity, and is seen to be a 
pillar of the church, so HE must be right...  The argument from authority is 
no more appropriate in a scientific discussion than it is in a religious 
one. It is just as wrong in both places. It isn't about WHO's right; it 
should be about WHAT's right.

>On the basis that he is right, now we advance further with another 
>extension of scientific thought.
>
No, that is a mis-statement of the scientific method. His "rightness" (and 
the completeness thereof) has to be verified by experiment before proceeding 
on the basis that he is right. If the experiments support the theory, then 
there is a good basis for proceeding; if they don't, we learned something. 
Often it is just as important to know what ISN'T so, as it is to know what 
IS so...

> Back to the tree twig or seaweed, unless proven otherwise, it looks to me 
> like hard evidence for an infinity and that there never was and never will 
> be a Beginning and an End.

And I have taken some pains to argue against this and support the exact 
opposite. There is no infinity, there was a beginning, and there will be an 
end. The universe is finite and knowable. It is only the church that seeks 
to prevent us from knowing it, because the church relies on mystery for its 
very existence (what thinking person could support religion otherwise?)

I understand that you will never accept this, and your "unless proven 
otherwise" is simply a weasel clause because there is no proof on earth that 
would make you change your mind :-). That's OK; this is a friendly 
discussion with no chance of changing minds anyway :-).

Only the Bible says "World without end". That is a comforting thought for 
people who never had the luxury of an education, and never learned to think 
(and, more importantly, question) for themselves.


>Things, shapes might look very different in a zillion years from now, but 
>still no nearer to a finite conclusion.
>
It won't be a zillion years, but neither of us will see it out :-)

> I searched PBS thinking Evolution might give me a lead to what I had been 
> watching. No luck - but I did find the following under that ubiquitous 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Precisely. Evolutionists are not out to discredit the church. Darwin lived 
in a deeply religious society. It would have been crazy and political 
suicide to come up with a theory that proved church teaching was wrong. The 
manuscript lay unpublished in his desk for a number of years before he had 
the courage to publish it. He feared the backlash from the established 
churches. And he was right. Even today, fundamentalists see Evolution (or 
anything that contradicts the words of the Bible) as an attack on the 
chiurch and God. Just look around this very thread....

Personally, I have not traded belief in God for belief in science. I accept 
neither on faith. My investigations over decades into the fundamental 
questions I posed as a teenager have led me to believe that God is extremely 
unlikely and definitely unnecessary in my everyday life. I have had, and am 
having, a thoroughly enjoyable life in the expectation that when it ends 
that will be it.

It doesn't frighten me, and I don't lean on my Imaginary Friend in times of 
need. Neither do I blame Him for the ills of this world or the fact that 
things are not perfect, and there is injustice. Instead, I seek to do what I 
can and encourage others to do what they can to make it a better place. We 
simply can't wait for God :-)

If I believe in anything, it would be personal responsibility. (Which 
immediately rules out Christianity... :-))

Pete.
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
>  
> Personally, I have not traded belief in God for belief in science. I accept 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I somewhat question the above.  While I accept that "god" in the
traditional sense is extremely unlikely, I have come to the belief that
spiriual values of some sort *are* a requirement for a happy life. From
everything you have said to date, I rather expect that you have found
the same thing.

That has also been born out by my observations ... those that I see
around me leading happy lives have all adopted (for whatever reason)
some set of values that I would deem "spiritual". That is, they are
based on conceptual entities such as "love", or "justice", or
"fairness". Those that I have observed that do not have such a value
system tend to do less well.

So my question to you is this "Do you have a set of values that are
man-made, and defined by yourself", and, as a secondary question, 'Are
such values "real", or only imaginary?'

Donald
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-12-28T09:46:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gtf5r1hbomnmc8c63qnsrovc4qu2rfs3j0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<42Aqf.1798$m05.1599@clgrps12> <41236bF1ct6kvU3@individual.net> <1135628578.058239.193080@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <41bep6F1e5j00U1@individual.net> <1135701669.486720.26320@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <ljfsf.3728$bd.1943@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <ujnsf.90462$2k.14349@pd7tw1no> <41fogmF12afhmU1@individual.net> <bmysf.5121$l87.372596@news20.bellglobal.com> <spd5r1tdjek0mgpod4m6mc48ga7ba8svdj@4ax.com> <FSysf.1333$Yk2.173887@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
On Wed, 28 Dec 2005 11:15:33 -0500, Donald Tees
<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>> To me those are all secondary to "are your values good?".   Do you
>> increase the amount of love and joy in the world?   
>
>Only if you assume that "love and joy" are good.

We all have basic assumptions.    Mine are that hate & unnecessary
pain are bad.   Love and joy are good.   

If you find someone whose basic assumptions are different, we will
have to define them before we can have meaningful conversation.   

There have been societies that have taught that hate and/or pain are
good - where they help make one strong.    The primary value is strong
is good.

And some that define "good" as what helps one survive.   The primary
value is survival is good.

In these cases, we can still evaluate whether the values are "good"
without evaluating whether they are Truth.

When the primary definition of "good" is Truth do those other values
become secondary.    Some scientists have this definition, as well as
many religious people.
```

```
"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message 
news:bmysf.5121$l87.372596@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> the same thing.

That is hardly a fair argument, Donald. I say I don't believe in God; you 
say that if I experience love it is spiritual and I therefore have SOME kind 
of spiritual belief.

Let me make my position clear:

1. I do not believe in a supreme being.
2. I do not believe in an afterlife. (This one is quite enough for me and I 
have spent my life living it. This approach may have been suggested by the 
fact that I nearly died at age 14 and resolved then and there to make the 
most of  whatever time was thereafter afforded to me... Whatever the reason, 
I can assure you that religion, spirituality, and concepts of God, had 
nothing to do with it.)
3. Being human, I certainly experience the gamut of emotions that humans are 
subject to. I see this as part of my humanity, not part of my 
"spirituality". I have seen animals exhibit the same emotions even though 
they are not supposed to have souls according to accepted religious 
teaching. I understand and discard the arguments about anthropomorphism and 
behaviourism, people reflecting their own feelings into animal behaviour. I 
know enough about psychology to not do this. Desmond Morris says cats 
present their arses to humans as a sign of submission. They don't really 
"love" you... I discussed this with the cat I had at the time and she 
thought it was extremely amusing. Her comment was that Desmond Morris 
wouldn't know where his own arse was, let alone what she did with hers...
4. If I present evidence that shows I am getting along fine without 
spirituality, religion or God, and you then say that if I am doing all right 
it must be because I have spiritual values, all you have done is make my 
evidence fit your own preconceived ideas. I cannot argue it, because you 
will not accept what I am saying as being what I say it is.


>
> That has also been born out by my observations ... those that I see
> around me leading happy lives have all adopted (for whatever reason)
> some set of values that I would deem "spiritual".

The important thing here, Donald,  is the personal pronoun in the above. YOU 
deem it spiritual. That is because you require it to be so in order to fit 
comfortably into YOUR world view. I'm not saying you're wrong; we all do 
this to some extent. However, in MY world view I do not require spirituality 
in order to be compassionate or kind or decent or anything else. I am that 
way because it makes sense and it is just as easy as being evil. People are 
basically good, and this nonsense about original sin is just that, nonsense.


>That is, they are
> based on conceptual entities such as "love", or "justice", or
> "fairness".

Yes, agreed. I have those concepts in my world view, and hold them in high 
regard. But it isn't "spiritual".

> Those that I have observed that do not have such a value
> system tend to do less well.
>
Sure, that may be fair.

> So my question to you is this "Do you have a set of values that are
> man-made, and defined by yourself", and, as a secondary question, 'Are
> such values "real", or only imaginary?'

Answer question 1: Yes. They are not carved in stone and have evolved over 
years based on interaction and experience. They have arisen as answers to 
important questions which I laid out in my teen years. The process of 
getting answers, continues. (Some of the questions are extremely difficult 
and may never have complete answers in my lifetime. I'm OK with that; the 
search keeps me interested, and progress has been made. I am much more 
satisfied now than I was at 13... :-)).

I consider personal growth to be an extremely important and ongoing process, 
so I periodically review my beliefs and consider whether they are still 
relevant or need modification. Fortunately, I live in a place that provides 
many ideal settings for quiet reflection and contemplation.

You might consider this "spiritual"; I don't. Maybe we are playing with 
words, I honestly don't know.

(I don't do this every day; I have a life. But when I do need to do it, this 
environment is ideal. For me, my little visits to these special places 
probably serve the same purpose that going to church serves for many people 
reading this. I love to drive into the country at night and drop the hood on 
the car. Away from the light pollution, the stars look like you can touch 
them and I always come home mentally refreshed and buzzing. Is it 
"spiritual" to be aware there is more in this universe than Mankind and 
planet Earth? I don't think so, but many would disagree and it is certainly 
arguable.)

Answer second question:  The values and ethics I live by are certainly 
pretty "real" for me. However, I accept the possibility that all existence 
may not be "real". Until the reality or otherwise is thoroughly established, 
I use values that help me deal with the "apparency" I have to interact with 
on a daily basis.

There is a fighting chance this discussion may become serious, so I'll end 
it here :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 68)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IwTsf.5825$l87.554431@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9PJsf.1651$Yk2.227490@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <qp53q1tur619iqmd93m80ccl64unttcutp@4ax.com> <yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <gamdq1ts42ot5sp0uvk2a9bdn6a8uvnfqh@4ax.com> <JL3qf.20086$k76.4760@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <40srj8F1bnru8U1@individual.net> <1135188623.500797.230460@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43A9C2DF.14C9B712@mts.net> <40ufi2F1c0rt8U1@individual.net> <42Aqf.1798$m05.1599@clgrps12> <41236bF1ct6kvU3@individual.net> <1135628578.058239.193080@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <41bep6F1e5j00U1@individual.net> <1135701669.486720.26320@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <ljfsf.3728$bd.1943@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <ujnsf.90462$2k.14349@pd7tw1no> <41fogmF12afhmU1@individual.net> <bmysf.5121$l87.372596@news20.bellglobal.com> <41git9F1eh722U1@individual.net> <9PJsf.1651$Yk2.227490@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:

>>
>>You might consider this "spiritual"; I don't. Maybe we are playing with 
>>words, I honestly don't know.
>>

The crux of the matter, I believe.

If we define "spiritualism" as a belief in super-hero's that epitomize
specific value systems, along with comic-book level personalizations of
such, then it becomes so devalued as to be worthless IMO. That has
always been my main beef with the organized churches.

If we define "spiritualism" as that part of ourselves that deals with
abstract concepts, as opposed "reality" in the sense of concrete chunks
of rock, then the development of spiritual values becomes rather more
important.

From what you wrote, I think you see words like "love" as refering to
emotional reactions that take place on a physical level as side effects
of our animal nature. I can relate to that definition, but also think
that "phrases like "the power of love" refer to something a little more.
There is a concept of fairness, or treating each another as if we were
in their shoes, that goes beyond simple emotional reaction. The
evolution of those concepts is really a social/political evolution that
profoundly affects the world we live in, on a daily basis.

To the point on fact, that I can say quite honestly "I believe in good
and evil". Extraopolaqting that to "I believe in the power of god and
the devil" is somewhat of a jump, but as you say, largely semantic.

I am a product of my times. I turned 13 in January of 1960. I heard then
"that all you really need is love", and I have not changed my mind a lot.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 69)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-12-30T14:03:32+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41jfb8F1f2c0oU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <qp53q1tur619iqmd93m80ccl64unttcutp@4ax.com> <yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <gamdq1ts42ot5sp0uvk2a9bdn6a8uvnfqh@4ax.com> <JL3qf.20086$k76.4760@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <40srj8F1bnru8U1@individual.net> <1135188623.500797.230460@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43A9C2DF.14C9B712@mts.net> <40ufi2F1c0rt8U1@individual.net> <42Aqf.1798$m05.1599@clgrps12> <41236bF1ct6kvU3@individual.net> <1135628578.058239.193080@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <41bep6F1e5j00U1@individual.net> <1135701669.486720.26320@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <ljfsf.3728$bd.1943@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <ujnsf.90462$2k.14349@pd7tw1no> <41fogmF12afhmU1@individual.net> <bmysf.5121$l87.372596@news20.bellglobal.com> <41git9F1eh722U1@individual.net> <9PJsf.1651$Yk2.227490@news20.bellglobal.com> <IwTsf.5825$l87.554431@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
 

"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message 
news:IwTsf.5825$l87.554431@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Donald Tees wrote:
>> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No argument on that, agreed.

I think we should use the word "spirituality", because ""spiritualism" 
smacks of  table tapping, ectoplasm, and ouija boards :-)

> If we define "spiritualism" as that part of ourselves that deals with
> abstract concepts, as opposed "reality" in the sense of concrete chunks
> of rock, then the development of spiritual values becomes rather more
> important.
>

Hmmm... this is very clear and I have given it quite a bit of thought. I 
agree that the fact that we are capable of abstract thought deserves some 
special consideration, but I'm not sure it is spiritual. The questions it 
raises are interesting: Where did our intelligence come from? Are we the 
only organisms capable of abstract thought? Does this ability demonstrate 
that we are spiritual beings?

These questions are exactly what the God Squad need to reinforce their 
position. Here's an imaginary debate between the God Squad (GS) and the 
Secular Sources (SS),  SS being the positions of Science, Rational 
Philosophy,  and those who are not part of the GS, namely, me...:-)

Some of the arguments are necessarily incomplete, so if anyone reading would 
like to add to the positions for either side, please feel free to do so. I 
stress again, this is for amusement only and is not intended to disrespect 
or denigrate the beliefs of any individual or group in the forum.

INTERLOCUTOR: Where did our intelligence come from?

GS: It was breathed into Man by God so that he might have dominion over all 
the earth. The "Divine Spark" idea...

SS: Intelligence evolved as a survival mechanism, allowing us to decide 
whether to fight or flee and to evaluate the best options for either of 
those courses of action. Speech evolved through the need to work together 
when hunting in groups, and once speech was possible, the experience of each 
generation could be passed to the next so that the knowledge base grew and, 
eventually, people started to think about things other than hunting... :-)

INTERLOCUTOR:  Are we the only organisms capable of abstract thought?

GS: Absolutely. It requires a soul. God has given us this ability so that we 
can decide moral questions and exercise our free will. He wants us to 
worship Him of our own volition.

SS: It is impossible to know whether species which we cannot communicate 
directly with, exercise abstract thought. Does a cat "think" about catching 
a bird or is it instinctive? What's the difference? How can we know that the 
same cat which chases anything that moves, doesn't "think" about birds and 
mice when it isn't playing?  It is convenient (for Man) to decide that all 
"lower animals" are simply stimulus/response mechanisms without the ability 
for higher thought. But how do we explain the dog that drags its master from 
drowning in the river (leaving safety and risking its own life to do so), or 
the cat that is in tune with the emotions of its human and comes and purrs 
on the lap when the human is feeling down? It is simply impossible to know 
what goes on in the heads of other species to a point where we can 
definitively say they are not capable of abstract thought or emotion. (It 
should be noted in passing, that while God may want us to worship Him of our 
own volition, the penalties for NOT doing so are severe :-))

INTERLOCUTOR: Does this ability demonstrate that we are spiritual beings?

GS: Yes. It requires a soul. Therefore it is a manifestation of our 
spirituality.

SS:  Possibly, but, in my opinion, unlikely, and definitely not proven. "I 
think, therefore I am." Does it deny or confirm the "soul"? Depends what you 
mean by soul... If you mean the Divine Spark installed by God, I don't think 
so. If you mean the essential "me", the "awareness of being aware", the "Fat 
Controller" in the Central Station of my brain, then we can talk.

It does not require divine intervention for thought processes to evolve into 
higher forms, culminating with the ability to create abstract ideas, 
question things, investigate, and finally arrive at a central point from 
which these processes emanate: "me"..."I AM". (Interesting that God used 
these same words to describe Himself to Moses, if the Bible is to be 
believed. Could it be that the author of this document instinctively 
realised that this is the essence of being? Or maybe he was actually 
inspired by God :-) The debate continues...)

I believe emotions are a (very important) by-product of this process. We 
operate at at least two levels (conscious and sub-conscious) and emotions 
sometimes allow us to over-ride the higher thought functions. Uncontrolled 
they are dangerous, but so is higher thought; you can design nuclear power 
stations or make atom bombs...your choice.

I find it awesome (in the true sense of the word) that in the short space of 
around 3 million years a bipedal life form can evolve from basic instincts 
and living in trees, to the point where it can question the very 
architecture of the Cosmos and seek to understand the motivations for its 
own behaviour. (But I don't see that as evidence for the existence of 
God...)

Maybe belief in God was a necessary part of that growth process. For me (and 
an increasing number of people), it isn't any more.  I'm not "anti-God" (I'm 
certainly anti-religion...because it has an abysmal track record of 
exploitation and deliberately inhibiting growth and independent thought), I 
just don't see the need, and the evidence fails to persuade me. It's time 
Mankind "put aside childish things" and started taking responsibility for 
life on Earth.

We are responsibile for the condition we are in.

<end of "debate">

While the above is necessarily superficial, I think it gives an insight into 
how one person approaches these issues. As I am fond of sayng, the questions 
are every bit as important as the answers. Stop questioning and you might as 
well die. Accept the answers of the church (or science, or the guys you 
drink with down the pub, or anyone else...) without question, and you might 
as well die

And it is, hopefully, amusing and interesting.


> From what you wrote, I think you see words like "love" as refering to
> emotional reactions that take place on a physical level as side effects
> of our animal nature.

Well, yes, but I've never considered it in those terms. I believe we 
transcend our "animal nature" through the exercise of higher thought (as 
described above).

(Sometimes it is good fun to NOT transcend our animal nature, as you will 
know if you have ever been in a foodfight, wallowed in mud, continued 
drinking after the flag in your head has turned on to tell you to stop, or 
had wild monkey sex with a willing partner... :-).  (WARNING: attempting all 
of these on the same day, could injure both your mental and physical 
health...) The important thing is to have the choice and to know when to 
exercise it. Higher thought gives us this. I see emotions as a "balance" for 
our higher thought. Sometimes the heart must rule the head, but generally, 
it shouldn't. Resolving this is all part of being human.


> I can relate to that definition, but also think
> that "phrases like "the power of love" refer to something a little more.

So do I.

> There is a concept of fairness, or treating each another as if we were
> in their shoes, that goes beyond simple emotional reaction.

It is higher thought combined with emotion; the two are not always mutually 
exclusive.


> The
> evolution of those concepts is really a social/political evolution that
> profoundly affects the world we live in, on a daily basis.
>
Absolutely. Agreed.

> To the point on fact, that I can say quite honestly "I believe in good
> and evil". Extraopolaqting that to "I believe in the power of god and
> the devil" is somewhat of a jump, but as you say, largely semantic.
>
I don't "believe" in either of them. I see outcomes that are positive or 
negative and all relative. If I believed in God, I'd have to believe in the 
Devil and I find that ludicrous. However, I can quite understand how many 
people are comfortable with this dichotomy, and the semiotic and logical 
processes behind it have been woven into our society for several thousand 
years. It is hard to step outside a circle as old as that.

> I am a product of my times. I turned 13 in January of 1960. I heard then
> "that all you really need is love", and I have not changed my mind a lot.

I also was strongly influenced by those times. Still am. However, I believe 
that love alone is NOT enough. It's like marriage. I have been married twice 
(both times for love) and I came to the realization that love is not enough, 
in and of itself, to meet the dynamics of long term human personal 
relationships. The danger is that love allows one partner to become 
dominant. Look at the battered women who stay because "I love him"... I do 
not wish to devalue love by this discussion; it is certainly the most 
powerful and positive single emotion and amazing things have been 
accomplished by it, but it is not the total solution.

Nothing is.  We are comlex creatures with many facets; we need many things 
in order to be "happy". The person who says: "I only need my <whatever>..." 
is deluding themself. We need physical and mental basics, as well as the 
emotional ("spiritual" if you must :-)) nurture, for us to feel complete and 
"happy". And it is an individual thing. What works for me may not work for 
you and vice versa. That is why tolerance and compassion are essential.

Celebrate the diversity of humanity; revel in it.

And respect it.

Pete.



>
> Donald
>
```

```
On Fri, 30 Dec 2005 14:03:32 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>I think we should use the word "spirituality", because ""spiritualism" 
>smacks of  table tapping, ectoplasm, and ouija boards :-)

That is a common response when someone else uses a word that has
meaning for us - in a way that we reject.

So let me look at some definitions.    I suspect we need to learn what
is meant when we use words with these many diverse emotional meanings.

http://www.onelook.com/?w=spirituality&ls=a
Quick definitions (spirituality)

# noun:   concern with things of the spirit
# noun:   property or income owned by a church 

http://www.onelook.com/?w=spirit&ls=a
Quick definitions (spirit)

# noun:   a fundamental emotional and activating principle determining
one's character
# noun:   any incorporeal supernatural being that can become visible
(or audible) to human beings
# noun:   the vital principle or animating force within living things
# noun:   the general atmosphere of a place or situation and the
effect that it has on people
# noun:   an inclination or tendency of a certain kind
# noun:   the state of a person's emotions (especially with regard to
pleasure or dejection) (Example: "He was in good spirits")
# noun:   animation and energy in action or expression
# noun:   the intended meaning of a communication
# verb:   infuse with spirit (Example: "The company spirited him up")
# Word origin info is available. 

http://www.onelook.com/?loc=pub&w=spiritual

Quick definitions (spiritual)

# noun:   a kind of religious song originated by Blacks in the
southern United States
# adjective:   concerned with or affecting the spirit or soul
(Example: "A spiritual approach to life")
# adjective:   lacking material body or form or substance (Example:
"Spiritual beings")
# adjective:   concerned with sacred matters or religion or the church
(Example: "Lords temporal and spiritual")
# adjective:   like or being a phantom (Example: "Spiritual tappings
at a seance")
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1135902170.241666.81240@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Thanks Alastair. I appreciate the support (I'm probably out on a limb here 
:-)), but I found the above just silly :-). It's no wonder I have little 
faith in Psychiatrists...  That is the most dull, unimaginative, 
uninspiring, stupid, "definition" of love I have ever seen.

You might even call it "soulless" :-)

It could be seen as a cheap shot  (perish the thought :-)) to criticize 
without offering an alternative, so here's one possibility....

<one possible subjective definition of "love">

A powerful emotional state that has many different forms, but fundamentally, 
arises from the cognition that there is a connectivity between humans, and 
even all life forms, that transcends words, culture, belief, skin colour or 
any other differentiation, and whispers to us that we are "not alone".  It 
manifests itself in many ways and is reinforced by physical contact 
(hugging, kissing, stroking, touching, sex, or even just spending time in 
the presence of...). It is fed by compassion, lust, sympathy, empathy, 
understanding, joy, and laughter, (there may be others; it depends on 
individuals), but it always involves the recognition of connectedness to the 
object of affection. It enriches both the giver and the receiver, and life 
would simply be "existence" without it.

</one possible subjective definition of "love">

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 71)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-01-01T12:09:32+13:00

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:NKAsf.212161$Gd6.31575@pd7tw3no...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>>  Jimmy,
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
My point was that all life on this planet had a start point; furthermore the 
planet itself had a start point (best estimates around 5 billion years ago 
( I believe it was a Monday :-))), and even the universe (and time itself) 
had a start point (much more difficult to estimate but believed to be 10 - 
20 billion years ago.  There is not, and never has been, infinity in nature. 
Even space is finite (but boundless). Poets see "infinity in a grain of 
sand" but scientists don't.

The concept of an infinite all-powerful deity is a creation of Man in 
response to his own needs. That is why we are "created in His image" and 
that is why He behaves like a terrible two year old (because the people who 
were around writing about Him at that time respected force, and vengeance 
and retribution were woven into their society. To this day, many Arab and 
Israeli families take blood oaths for revenge if something happens to one of 
their family. Vendetta is alive and well in the Middle East and the God of 
peace and love is seen as a wimp... They WANT the OT God and use the OT to 
justify many outrageous acts against each other.)

It may take several thousand more years before the message gets through that 
it is MAN's inhumanity to MAN, not God's or any other imaginary being.

The only hope I see is education, the raising of awareness, the questioning 
of beliefs held dogmatically for thousands of years, the acceptance of 
personal responsibility for the condition people find themselves in.

It won't happen overnight.

Pete.
```

```
> The concept of an infinite all-powerful deity is a creation of Man ...

Actually it is not '_a_ creation', but is dozens or hundreds of
separate and conflicting creations, each in its own locality, some of
multiple deities, some have passed into disuse replaced by conquest.

> (because the people who were around writing about Him at that time
> respected force, and vengeance and retribution were woven into their
> society.

Alternately it is because the person(s) it is based on was vengeful and
controlled his subjects and his enemies with force and retribution
(similar examples may be found in living memory).

> the acceptance of
> personal responsibility for the condition people find themselves in.

Unfortunately one of the attractions of many religions, such as muslim,
is that 'Allah has written' so that everything is predetermined. This
means that whatever hapens it is 'not my fault'.
```
