```
Richard wrote:
> > The concept of an infinite all-powerful deity is a creation of Man ...
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> means that whatever hapens it is 'not my fault'.

I think that you are being a little unfair on our muslim brethren. I
shall state my ignorance up front: I have not read the Koran nor any
other Islamic text. I do believe that their concept of 'God willing'
does not refer to  predetermination but that it refers to Allah's will
being expressed. Predetermination, correctly expressed by you, denies
freedom of choice. 'God willing' permits freedom of choice but means
that the outcome is in Allah's hands. If you make the effort then you
can certainly effect the outcome (as long as Allah permits it).
```

- **References:** `<qp53q1tur619iqmd93m80ccl64unttcutp@4ax.com> <yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <gamdq1ts42ot5sp0uvk2a9bdn6a8uvnfqh@4ax.com> <JL3qf.20086$k76.4760@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <40srj8F1bnru8U1@individual.net> <1135188623.500797.230460@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43A9C2DF.14C9B712@mts.net> <40ufi2F1c0rt8U1@individual.net> <42Aqf.1798$m05.1599@clgrps12> <41236bF1ct6kvU3@individual.net> <1135628578.058239.193080@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <41bep6F1e5j00U1@individual.net> <1135701669.486720.26320@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <ljfsf.3728$bd.1943@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <ujnsf.90462$2k.14349@pd7tw1no> <41fogmF12afhmU1@individual.net> <NKAsf.212161$Gd6.31575@pd7tw3no> <41gjthF1enan5U1@individual.net> <1135815542.970866.295880@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <1135902500.197907.235490@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
> Predetermination, correctly expressed by you, denies
> freedom of choice. 'God willing' permits freedom of choice but means
> that the outcome is in Allah's hands. If you make the effort then you
> can certainly effect the outcome (as long as Allah permits it).

As in all religions there are sects that interpret things one way and
others that interpret things completely differently, and then some that
just seem to make it up as they go along.

<quote>Predestination does not contradict with the above statement. It
refers to God's foreknowledge of what choices are going to be made. In
a famous Qudsi Hadith, the Prophet Muhammed (Peace and Blessing of
Allah be upon him) said that when a person is in the womb, an angel
will write his/her earnings, lifespan, deeds, and whether he/she will
be happy or unhappy. This is a foreknowledge of how much people will
earn, how long will they live, whether they are going to do good or
bad, and whether they will be happy or unhappy as a result.</quote>

So this particular bit of dogma is that you can 'choose freely' but
Allah already knows what that choice will be and what the outcome will
be so you can only 'freely choose' from the one that Allah already has
written down.

If one makes 'poor' choices: get drunk, drive wildly, kill people, then
that's OK because Allah already had that down in his plan so it is not
one's fault, it is merely fulfilling destiny and therefore is a good
thing.

That's the great thing about religions, just choose what suits and make
up the rest, it's not like its real or anything.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6oywf.80038$6K2.40461@edtnps90>`
- **References:** `<mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <DjAnf.4839$w7.3842@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <aLCnf.146330$y_1.86429@edtnps89> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <qp53q1tur619iqmd93m80ccl64unttcutp@4ax.com> <yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <gamdq1ts42ot5sp0uvk2a9bdn6a8uvnfqh@4ax.com> <JL3qf.20086$k76.4760@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <40srj8F1bnru8U1@individual.net> <1135188623.500797.230460@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43A9C2DF.14C9B712@mts.net> <40ufi2F1c0rt8U1@individual.net> <42Aqf.1798$m05.1599@clgrps12> <41236bF1ct6kvU3@individual.net> <1135628578.058239.193080@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <41bep6F1e5j00U1@individual.net> <1135701669.486720.26320@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <ljfsf.3728$bd.1943@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <ujnsf.90462$2k.14349@pd7tw1no> <41fogmF12afhmU1@individual.net> <NKAsf.212161$Gd6.31575@pd7tw3no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:NKAsf.212161$Gd6.31575@pd7tw3no...
> I'll do a search on 'Mandelbrot set' and see if that turns up something 
> more specific. Actually I was hoping some bright spark would provide the 
> name of what I was looking at :-)

    What you mentioned, zooming in on seaweeds and seeing a self-similar 
structure which when itself zoomed in upon, reveals more self-similar 
structures, is called "fractal". The Mandelbrot set is one of the most 
famous fractals in Mathematics. Other popular ones include the Julia set and 
the Koch Snowflake.

    I don't know too many practical applications of fractals other than 
video games. They're used to generate realistic looking terrains, trees, 
galaxies (for space games), etc. They give a nice 
semi-structured-and-yet-semi-random look that shows up often in nature.

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal

    - Oliver
```

- **References:** `<mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <s4_mf.1530$w7.1446@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <DjAnf.4839$w7.3842@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <aLCnf.146330$y_1.86429@edtnps89> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <qp53q1tur619iqmd93m80ccl64unttcutp@4ax.com> <yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <gamdq1ts42ot5sp0uvk2a9bdn6a8uvnfqh@4ax.com> <JL3qf.20086$k76.4760@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <40srj8F1bnru8U1@individual.net> <1135188623.500797.230460@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43A9C2DF.14C9B712@mts.net> <40ufi2F1c0rt8U1@individual.net> <42Aqf.1798$m05.1599@clgrps12> <41236bF1ct6kvU3@individual.net> <1135628578.058239.193080@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <41bep6F1e5j00U1@individual.net> <1135701669.486720.26320@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <ljfsf.3728$bd.1943@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
 

"Defaultuser" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:ljfsf.3728$bd.1943@tornado.tampabay.rr.com...
>
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
:-) good point...

> As a basis is has an "uncertainty principle"....it requires a level of 
> mathematics that is studied by post doctoral mathematics, physics and 
> cosmology students....and is changing constantly as new things are 
> "discovered".
>
At least the study is continuing. What we see with religion is that once the 
obvious "answer" is attained, no further study is required. There is no 
arguing with the will of God.

> I don't know that I find the human interpretation of measuring and 
> analysing any more accurate on the scientific level than I do the 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> issues.   So, great minds are wrong, just like great books....

I think "wrong" is a little harsh... "not sure" or "still under 
investigation" might be fairer... And sometimes (especially when it comes to 
weird things like quantum theory), more than one solution may be "right" 
(or, at least, "most probable so far") and concrete concepts of "right" and 
"wrong" (Aristotle again... ) are actually meaningless...
>
> I've read that there are 11 dimension, but that their might be 12, but 
> then there might be as many as we need......and as for what was their 
> before the big bang?.....the same things that's North of the North Pole, 
> obviously.

The number of dimensions arise out of extending the geometric approach to 
space/time. It was found that when extra dimensions were added, many of the 
problems and infinities simply disappear. It's like the added understanding 
that adopting a three dimensional viewpoint gives to a crumpled two 
dimensional piece of paper. Something  that has only ever existed on the 
surface cannot appreciate why it experiences forces in some places (as it 
goes up and down "hills"), because such a creature could not conceive of the 
third dimension. Similarly, our brains are adapted to deal with three 
visible dimensions (look at the trouble we have understanding time in this 
context, never mind 6 more tightly curled up dimensions existing at every 
point in space/time...) The maths of it are not really that difficult (I 
claim no particular mathematical adroitness but got my head around this with 
a pretty basic understanding of vectors and matrices), however, there are 
"rules" about how many dimensions are required to explain the standard model 
and the particle zoo; we cannot just take any number and decide that will 
do... I would recommend Michio Kaku's "Hyperspace - a journey to the tenth 
dimension" as being an excellent book that describes these concepts and 
extends the Riemannian approach in a logical way. It also deals with the 
topology of multi-dimensional space/time in a fascinating and refreshing 
manner. Kaku is an excellent writer on these things.

As for M theory... I think the M stands for "Maybe..." :-)

Pete.
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1135380490.052403.82660@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
>
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete - Time is an artefact of space. Space can exist without time.

Oh really? And for how long would that be? :-)

If it exists for 1 billionth of a picosecond, it still has time attached to 
it. It CANNOT exist without time and I really don't care what your authority 
is... :-) The word "exist" implies a time dimension. Only things which DON'T 
exist, have no such dimension.

> Apparently we perceive time as an artefact of three dimensions (sorry -
> see Scientific American in the last yeay).

Whaddaya mean "we"...:-)?  And whaddaya mean by "artefact"?  Certainly if 
you perceive three dimesnions, you require tme in which to do it. It is true 
that space (three dimensions), and time are inextricably intertwined, and we 
may be simply arguing about the word "artefact", but if that is your 
meaning, then it contradicts your statement above that space can exist 
without time..

> However, it is only a theory
>  :-)

Looks like a very bad one to me, and although I have the utmost respect for 
SA, it is no more infallible than the Bible :-)

Pete.
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:414p31F1coilcU3@individual.net...
>
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> which DON'T exist, have no such dimension.

    I think we won't find a section of our universe which is composed of 
space but not time. If such a thing were to exist, it would have to exist 
outside of our universe (where "outside" here means both outside of our 
space and outside of our time).

    For example, "after" our universe ceases to exist, a new universe could 
"come into existence", which had no time. There would just be matter 
arranged in space, and the matter would "never" move. "After" that, another 
universe could come into existence, this one being composed of both time and 
space. Words like "after", "never", etc. are not appopriate here, because 
they talk about timelessness, but hopefully the meaning is clear.

    Once Alistair brought it up, I realized that I am not aware of any 
scientific theory which supports the idea that space requires time, though I 
believe there are some which support the idea that time requires space. I 
vaguely recall a discussion that time is actually a "derived" concept, and 
the "base" concepts that make up the universe are space and velocity.

    - Oliver
```

```
"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
news:wXywf.80084$6K2.54082@edtnps90...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>    - Oliver
Space and time are related in very much the same way that energy and matter 
are. However my basis for sayng this is philosophical, rather than physical.

If you consider matter to be energy that is moving "slower" than the speed 
of light, and energy to be what matter converts to  when it approaches the 
speed of light (both gross but "useful" oversimplified distortions of 
physics), then you can imagine time arising out of space and space arising 
out of time. Relativity certainly implies that time is derived from 
movement, and movement certainly implies space.

It is all interesting speculation; for myself, I cannot conceive space and 
time as being anything other than interrelated.

(Gotta stop now as I have no more space or time :-))

Pete.
```

```
In article <42hj02F1if80kU1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>> space and outside of our time).

[snip]

>Space and time are related in very much the same way that energy and matter 
>are. However my basis for sayng this is philosophical, rather than physical.

Hmmmm... energy, matter and time all have units of measure but I don't 
know of a measure of space which cannot be applied to matter 
(absence-of-space)... but that's neither here nor there.

When you speak of the relation between energy and matter, however, there's 
a little equation that was generated by a Swiss patent-clerk in 1905 that 
says the two are the same, insofar as terms of a valid equation which are 
on either side of the '=' sign are the same... 'The mass of a body is a 
measure of its energy-content' and all that.

[snip]

>It is all interesting speculation; for myself, I cannot conceive space and 
>time as being anything other than interrelated.

Back in the days before I had to work for a living I formulated 'Time is 
the label applied to the purported sequentiality of events'.

DD
kk
```

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:dq0cbq$460$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <42hj02F1if80kU1@individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

A friend of mine who is a Scientologist quotes L Ron Hubbard as: "Time is a 
postulate that space and particles will persist."
(I have some very interesting conversations with this guy and his wife; 
usually we call it a draw :-))

That's interesting and kind of agrees with your definition regarding 
'sequentiality'.

Of course we know that there is no theoretical reason why Time can't run 
backwards (it is symettrical mathematically), and some particles are even 
considered to move in negative time (pi mesons and tachyons, for example), 
but the laws of thermodynamics and consequent entropy cause the 'arrow' to 
move 'forward' (according to Hawking...)

Oops... out of time again... :-)

Pete.
```

```
>Of course we know that there is no theoretical reason why Time can't run 
>backwards (it is symettrical mathematically), and some particles are even 
>considered to move in negative time (pi mesons and tachyons, for example), 
>but the laws of thermodynamics and consequent entropy cause the 'arrow' to 
>move 'forward' (according to Hawking...)

If all time went backwards, how could we tell?
```

```
I remember reading someplace about the phenomenon of watching a typical 
war movie backwards... something about how men lay dying in their 
comrades' arms and suddenly the injuring shrapnel was sucked out of them 
by explosions that re-formed into bombs which rose into the bellies of 
airplanes which were moving contrary to their aerodynamic design.  The 
airplanes then returned to their home fields, where these bombs were then 
unloaded from their bays, put into trucks that also moved contrary to 
their aerodynamic design and brought to a factory where they were 
dis-assembled so that they would never harm anyone else.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 68)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-01-12T07:06:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4cocs11d70actdrbig37sh12i963i1adql@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <42kbs4F1imfjbU1@individual.net> <8a9as19cd4qg016ps33f1bjld09jhbvldg@4ax.com> <42moi2F1j4jnrU1@individual.net> <dq5ivl$ihh$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
On Thu, 12 Jan 2006 12:44:37 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>>The universe would move from chaos to a more ordered state. That is 
>>observably not true. (See "Entropy")... If it was, my bed would make itself 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>dis-assembled so that they would never harm anyone else.

An old SF story has a character who hopes his war wound isn't the
result of tripping on a tent stake.    The end of the story occurs
after him and his dad are sitting around, then they go to the hospital
where they meet his mother for the first time.

But if time really flowed backwards - who's to say that our memories
aren't also flowing backwards?   We still remember things from the
past - even if that's the direction we're moving.   Everything else is
based upon the past, and memory is just what was created from the
past, so our "now" would be the same as if memory was moving forward.

And since "now" is all we have, that's what we have to work with.
```

```
> "everything is my fault and I'm so responsible I now
> need to spend loads of money so I can gain the power to do something about
> it..." ;-)

"""   The book argued that the brain was analogous to a computer with
two independent memory banks, the Analytical (conscious) Mind and the
Reactive (subconscious) Mind. The latter is full of "engrams"
(traumatic memories), which interfere with the operation of the
Analytical Mind and cause personality problems and ailments such as
asthma and arthritis.

During "auditing" (therapy), the "pre-clear" (patient) can "run"
(relive) and thus conquer the traumatic experiences. Once "clear," the
patient would have super powers: total recall, high IQ, perfect health,
and for all I know X-ray vision. Except for the inflated payoff, the
parallels to Freudian analysis are obvious, including the high hourly
fees you later had to pay to learn this stuff at Scientology centers.
"""

http://www.straightdope.com/classics/a4_134.html

But the main thing is: don't annoy them.  If they tell their 'church'
that you are anti-scientology then there will be burnings on your front
lawn and law suits   ;-)

BTW Did you hear about the Agnostic branch of the KKK ? They come and
burn a huge question mark on your front lawn.
```

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1137090977.466386.95740@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
>> "everything is my fault and I'm so responsible I now
>> need to spend loads of money so I can gain the power to do something 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 Well, first off, I'm not ('anti' I mean...)

I try not to make judgements about religions (or cults, or beliefs, or 
whatever...)

In NZ we have everything... I wear a ring that was 'purified' by White 
Witches... (No, I don't believe that nonsense, but why offend them when they 
were trying to do me a favour?... And who knows? Quantum Theory indicates 
there may be 'connection' between all things (well,  all matter and energy 
anyway...))

 One of my team was complaining today that computers 'had it in' for her. 
I've certainly met people who hate the technology and have nothing but 
trouble with their computers. (Maybe it is a circular argument; maybe there 
is some quantum fluctuation that places a tiny part of the fields that 
constitute 'us', into the field of a printed circuit board... it remains 
unproven. Until it is proven one way or the other, I'll try not to close my 
mind to these weird possibilities...:-)

I may not approve of religion in general, but I certainly am not 'anti' any 
specific one. (No, not even Jehovah's Witnesses... I've even been known to 
invite them in (once, on a very slow day in Australia... :-))

> BTW Did you hear about the Agnostic branch of the KKK ? They come and
> burn a huge question mark on your front lawn.
>
Hahaha! I liked that... :-)

Do you know what happens if you cross a Jehovah's Witness with a Hell's 
Angel?

You get someone who knocks on your door and tells YOU to fuck off...

Pete.
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message
> news:wXywf.80084$6K2.54082@edtnps90...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Suppose we consider a volume of space in which time does not exist. A
complex organism, such as a human being, may evolve and occupy that
volume of space. Such an organism may remember a past and may be able
to predict a future without the existance of the fourth dimension. Such
an organism may conceive of a fourth dimension even though that
dimension does not exist and there is only 'now'.

That is how I can say that time is not a real dimension. However, it is
only a debated topic and is not widely accepted. But then the (digital)
universe may in fact be virtual and not real.
```

```
Mr Maclean, language begins to break down under such circumstances.

measurement of time in his request.  Difficult thing to ignore, it seems.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 65)_

- **From:** "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2006-01-11T06:12:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1136988756.015434.172030@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<dq0ium$dm3$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <wXywf.80084$6K2.54082@edtnps90> <42hj02F1if80kU1@individual.net> <1136900927.606227.97620@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dq0ium$dm3$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <1136900927.606227.97620@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>,
> Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

I'll grant that the language is difficult but the concept is simple. I
also have difficulty discussing religion without using capitals for the
start of God and Bible (although I deliverately did so at times during
this thread). Even as an atheist I am not consistent in my approach to
what I view as unimportant but deeply ingrained basic habits.

If all that exists is now does not make memories impossible and
predictions false.

Back to the one/two/three dimensional worlds. If a one dimensional
being perceives a dark point expand and break into two points which
move apart, move back together and then become a singular point, then
he may deduce the existence of a dark circle (and a second dimension)
having passed through his uni-dimensional world.

A two-dimensional creature may perceive that the dark point expands to
a dark circle before contracting back to a dark point again. That
creature may deduce the existence of a sphere  and a third dimension.

Because we have memories and can look forward to a happy retirement
does not mean that time exists. It may mean that we have a concept of
something that does may not exist.

Anyway, this is still academic and I look forward to the time when
academia settles the argument.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<Oi9xf.53080$km.35363@edtnps89>`
- **References:** `<dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <wXywf.80084$6K2.54082@edtnps90> <42hj02F1if80kU1@individual.net> <1136900927.606227.97620@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dq0ium$dm3$1@reader2.panix.com> <1136988756.015434.172030@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1136988756.015434.172030@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
>
> Back to the one/two/three dimensional worlds. If a one dimensional
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> creature may deduce the existence of a sphere  and a third dimension.

    I think most creatures of dimensionality N "perceive" an image in 
dimension N-1. Of course, the only samples I have to work with are 3 
dimensional creatures (namely life on earth), but it seems like a reasonable 
conclusion when you think of perception (or vision anyway) as being mostly 
the result of receiving rays (of light) that reflect against matter.

    For example, us, being 3D creatures, receive via each eye, a 2D image of 
the world around us (or directly in front of us, anyway). Most people have 
at most two eyes, so we can receive up to two images, and we can use the 
minor differences between each to try to infer some information about depth, 
but we don't "see" in 3D, in the sense that we can't see the backside of an 
object. If you're looking at a standard 6 sided dice, and you see the side 
marked "6", you will not be able to see the side marked "1" simultaneously.

    Similarly, imagine a 2D creature on a sheet of paper, and you draw a 
circle onto that sheet. The 2D creature cannot see the "other side" of that 
circle. He only sees the side facing him. So this 2D creature would probably 
receive a 1D image, which is a line. Perhaps the 2D creature will have 2 (or 
more) eyes, and can then infer some depth information about this line, and 
decide that what he sees is probably a circle, though he cannot see the 
backside, so he cannot know for sure until he walks around and checks it 
from all angles.

    Now, instead of a sheet of paper, imagine the 2D creature lives on a 
flat Euclidean, Cartesian, infinite 2D plane that travels through our 3D 
universe. The creature is only capable of moving along the plane, having no 
organs to allow it to travel along a 3rd axis. Let's say we push a sphere 
through the plane, very slowly, so that the creature has time to move around 
the sphere, and inspect it from all angles. At any one point in time, the 
creature is still receiving only a 1D image, but using the power of 
abstraction and his mind, he can determine that the 2D slice that he is 
seeing is a circle. As we push the sphere through the creature's plane of 
existing, it would seem that a tiny circle appears out of nowhere, grows for 
a while, and then shrinks again, and finally disappears again.

    If this were to happen to me, the first thing to jump into my mind is 
not "There must exist other dimensions" but rather "I guess matter is not 
conserved after all." Occam's Razor and all that.

    - Oliver
```

```
>

Not 'false', likewise impossible.  Memory, by definition, is concerned 
with that which 'has been'; if all that exists is 'now' then 'has been' 
cannot exist and memory would seem to be impossible and likewise with 
prediction ('will be', by definition, does not exist because all that 
exists is 'now').

'Flatland' (Abbott, 1884) covers this, I believe.

[snip]

>Because we have memories and can look forward to a happy retirement
>does not mean that time exists. It may mean that we have a concept of
>something that does may not exist.

I am not sure I agree or disagree with you here; I would need to know what 
you are using as criteria for the condition you postulate as 'existence'.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 67)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2006-01-11T14:17:36-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<43C567E0.68BEAABC@mts.net>`
- **References:** `<dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <1136900927.606227.97620@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dq0ium$dm3$1@reader2.panix.com> <1136988756.015434.172030@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dq3fdn$cn6$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
> Not 'false', likewise impossible.  Memory, by definition, is concerned
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >


In fact, it's possible for time to pass for a person - a conscious
person - without it causing memory.  I have experienced this spooky and
counterintuitive state.  There's a drug - a derivative of a popular
tranquillizer - which prevents experience being bound into the brain
structure which contains our memory.  It's used after operations when
the patient is conscious and rational, but in pain or other serious
discomfort, so that the attending staff can communicate with him without
his having a sense of time passing and therefore not being oppressed by
the length of time he's hurting.  The result is that while you're there
and being intelligible, you don't remember it!  You might say that you
weren't thre at all!  Having experienced it I can tell you that not
being aware of pain (in this way) is well worth not remembering it!

Most of the problems encountered in the discussion of time come about
because language doesn't really allow for the distinction between
personal/subjective time - we perceive time passing - and objective time
- which is the perhaps non-existent succession of events outside our own
consciousness.  Thus: I can go into the past and change something which
(logically) ought to prevent the human race from happening.  That's to
say: I'm going to do it tomorrow, which means that it has already
happened, which means that I can't do it.  Subjectively, the situation
is yet to be; objectively, it either has or hasn't happened and what I
plan to do is irrelevant.  Whatever has happened, objectively, is
already fixed in time, regardless of how my subjective sense of time
orders it.  I don't think it's possible, even if time travel is, to
reverse one's own time flow vis a vis the objective time flow.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 68)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()

```
In article <43C567E0.68BEAABC@mts.net>, Peter Lacey  <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>person - without it causing memory.

A person?  I know I do that with wind... sorry, couldn't resist.

[snip]

>Thus: I can go into the past and change something which
>(logically) ought to prevent the human race from happening.  That's to
>say: I'm going to do it tomorrow, which means that it has already
>happened, which means that I can't do it.

I believe this is a variant of the 'Grandfather Paradox': you 'goe back 
in time' and kills your grandfather before he can participate in the 
conception of your father.  Since your father was not conceived he did not 
exist to conceive you, since you were not conceived you did not exist... 
so how does that-which-did-not-exist kill your grandfather?

>Subjectively, the situation
>is yet to be; objectively, it either has or hasn't happened and what I
>plan to do is irrelevant.

As mentioned in another posting - if time can be made to 'run in reverse' 
then the universe becomes rigidly deterministic; nothing can be random.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 68)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-01-12T14:58:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m8uxf.53717$km.11784@edtnps89>`
- **References:** `<dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <1136900927.606227.97620@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dq0ium$dm3$1@reader2.panix.com> <1136988756.015434.172030@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dq3fdn$cn6$1@reader2.panix.com> <43C567E0.68BEAABC@mts.net>`

```

"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mts.net> wrote in message 
news:43C567E0.68BEAABC@mts.net...
>
> In fact, it's possible for time to pass for a person - a conscious
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> being aware of pain (in this way) is well worth not remembering it!

    I've never heard of this before. Do you know the name of the drug? Do 
you know (e.g. were told) whether, during those moments of 
intelligibility-but-non-remembering, that you were aware of currently being 
in a state of pain?


>
> Most of the problems encountered in the discussion of time come about
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> orders it.

    From what I understand of the theory of relativity, an objective 
chronological ordering of events does not exist. Here, I'm defining event as 
some hypervolume in space and time. That is, an event could span a 
continuous amount of time, or just exist at a single point in time, and an 
event could span a continuous amount of space, or just exist at a single 
point in space. Since I assume space-time is 4 dimensional here, I used the 
term hypervolume.

    So given two events A and B, and two observers travelling at different 
velocities (preferrably both near the speed of light, but in different 
directions) with respect to the events, the two observers may disagree on 
whether event A occured before event B or the other way around.

> I don't think it's possible, even if time travel is, to
> reverse one's own time flow vis a vis the objective time flow.

    If time travel were possible, it might be possible for the traveller to 
continuously move to the instant "just before" the current instant, at the 
same rate at which that prior instant turned into the current one, so that 
the traveller perceives time to flow in the reverse direction.

    What this means depends on the mechanics of the time travel involved. 
Does the traveller simply project his consciousness around in time, so that 
as he travels backwards, his body gets younger? Or does he actually move the 
matter that makes up his body through time as well? If the latter, how is 
conservation of matter handled? Does an equivalent amount of matter a the 
destination-time of the journey get transported to the source-time? Or does 
some matter get obliterated at the destination-time (like in the Terminator 
system of time travel)? Etc.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 70)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-01-13T01:27:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dq6vl7$4js$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <1137101437.003563.306290@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dq6i5s$q92$1@reader2.panix.com> <1137105304.217221.214160@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1137105304.217221.214160@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>,
Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>there is NOW and MEMORIES of NOT NOW.

I seem to handle that without too much difficulty, Mr Maclean, as it 
represents what I call the world around me.  What I have difficulty 
reconciling is your posited universe where 'all that exists is now' (your 
words) and in such a universe anything other than 'now' existing.  If all 
that exists is 'now' then 'not now' and memory cannot exist.  Consider:

'Imagine a universe where everything is cottage cheese.  Now if you take 
your hand...'

'Wait a moment.  If everything is cottage cheese then neither I nor my 
hand exist, all that exists is cottage cheese.'

'I think it is perfectly clear. You can not handle an existence in which
ONLY COTTAGE CHEESE and THAT-WHICH-IS-NOT-COTTAGE-CHEESE.'

Only 'now', Mr Maclean... no past or memories, no future or predictions.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 71)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-01-13T15:34:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6MPxf.79762$AP5.13644@edtnps84>`
- **References:** `<dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <1137101437.003563.306290@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dq6i5s$q92$1@reader2.panix.com> <1137105304.217221.214160@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dq6vl7$4js$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:dq6vl7$4js$1@reader2.panix.com...
>
> 'Imagine a universe where everything is cottage cheese.  Now if you take
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Only 'now', Mr Maclean... no past or memories, no future or predictions.

    I can think of one way for a universe, without time but with memories, 
to exist depending on how relaxed you allow me to get with the definition of 
"memories"...

    Imagine an intelligent creator (not nescessarily so omnipotent that he 
can overcome logic) who chose to create a timeless universe which is an 
exact replica of the state of the universe at this very moment. There would 
be a planet whose inhabitants refer to as "Earth", there would exist humans 
sitting a computers, reading newsgroup postings, and some of them might be 
in the process of thinking. Others will be in the process of accessing their 
memories. Since this universe is timeless, the states will never change. 
I.e. the people who are thinking will "never" finish their thoughts. The 
people who are retrieving their memories will "never" actually retrieve 
them. The people who have "already" retrieved their memories will have those 
memories stuck in their minds "forever".

    One could argue that since this universe has no past, these things in 
the people's mind aren't memories at all. Or perhaps they are memories, but 
implanted by the creator. They are memories of events that "never happened" 
(or which happened in an alternative universe, one *with* time). Or one 
could argue that memories are nothing more than a byproduct of the state of 
the neurons in one's brain, and as long as the neurons are indeed in a valid 
state (as designed by our ID), then yes, memories exist.

    It's similar to considering someone in the process of speaking. The 
particles of air have certain values for momentum... That is, they "want" to 
move, if only time existed. Does this mean sound, defined as a fluctuation 
of pressure in a medium, exist? Is it still a wave, if it is frozen in time?

    If we choose to define sound as the perception of the fluctuations in 
pressure, rather than the fluctuations themselves, at what point does it 
become sound? As soon as the eardrum organ detects the change? During the 
period in which the ear drum is communicating with the brain? During the 
period in which neurons in the brain are communicating with each other about 
the signals from the eardrum? After the mind has already acknowledges the 
presence of this sound?

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 64)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-01-10T15:27:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XnQwf.73038$OU5.31017@clgrps13>`
- **References:** `<dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <DjAnf.4839$w7.3842@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <aLCnf.146330$y_1.86429@edtnps89> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <qp53q1tur619iqmd93m80ccl64unttcutp@4ax.com> <yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <gamdq1ts42ot5sp0uvk2a9bdn6a8uvnfqh@4ax.com> <JL3qf.20086$k76.4760@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <40srj8F1bnru8U1@individual.net> <1135188623.500797.230460@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43A9C2DF.14C9B712@mts.net> <40ufi2F1c0rt8U1@individual.net> <1135289360.886960.119420@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <4123jkF1b3mvlU3@individual.net> <1135380490.052403.82660@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <414p31F1coilcU3@individual.net> <wXywf.80084$6K2.54082@edtnps90> <42hj02F1if80kU1@individual.net> <1136900927.606227.97620@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1136900927.606227.97620@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> Suppose we consider a volume of space in which time does not exist. A
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

    I think there are some very large conceptual problems with a volume of 
space without time surrounded by a volume of space *with* time. First, let's 
assume a discrete change from time-ness to timeless-ness, and a well defined 
border between the two regions of space. Any matter (e.g. the atoms that 
make up the matter that composes the organism) attempting to enter the 
region would be unable to do so, because to do so would change the contents 
of space at that point, and change implies the existence of time. So if that 
point of timeless space had matter, that matter could never be moved, and so 
you could never, for example, push that matter aside to insert your finger. 
If that point contaiend empty space, that empty space could never be filled. 
So what would happen if you tried to touch this empty space? Would you feel 
something pushing back to stop you? It seems to simply not make sense.

    A more "realistic" situation would be a continuous transition between 
time and timelessness. For example, well outside the boundaries of the 
timeless space region, time progresses "normally", but as we approach, time 
seems to progress more and more slowly, until at some point it the 
progression of time reaches zero, and we are in the timeless-region of 
space. Like Zeno's paradox, an organism trying to enter this region of 
timeless-ness could always get closer, but never actually reach that point.

    Isn't this what is thought to happen near a black hole anyway?

    - Oliver
```

```

"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
news:XnQwf.73038$OU5.31017@clgrps13...
>
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>    - Oliver
Three comments on a very lucid and thought provoking post... :-)

1. Space, by definition, is space, so 'empty space' is technically redundant 
and space is never 'empty' anyway...there are quantum fluctuations in the 
virtual and real fields.

2. I can't accept space without time; I can accept void or nothingness 
without time, but space is actually 'something'.

3. Xeno's paradox is demonstrably incorrect. Ask anyone who ever walked past 
a tortoise going in the same direction :-)

Pete.

PS yes, time is believed to stop at the event horizon of a BH. (There is 
still some debate about this...)


>
```

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
> news:1136900927.606227.97620@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> space without time surrounded by a volume of space *with* time.

Sorry Oliver, I did not mean a volume of space without time existing in
a volume of space with time. What I was thinking of was a universe
without time. Sorry, my sloppy use of English.


> First, let's
> assume a discrete change from time-ness to timeless-ness, and a well defined
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> something pushing back to stop you? It seems to simply not make sense.

Aside from my comment above, there is the belief in science that some
parts of the universe are bubbles of space running at different rates
of time compared to our local space. I think that was one of the
explanations for our being able to see parts of the universe purporting
to be older than the universe or at least outside of our light cone
(sorry but we are reaching the limits of my knowledge).

As to borders around such bubbles of space, I think it unlikely that
there would be a rigid defined border, rather a gradient and that
probably compressed in the direction of travel. A gradient would be
passable. But that is academic as I am happy living in three
dimensions.

>
>     A more "realistic" situation would be a continuous transition between
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> timeless-ness could always get closer, but never actually reach that point.

I wonder what would happen to Zeno's paradox if time does not exist?
Perhaps the hare would race past the tortoise with ease?

>
>     Isn't this what is thought to happen near a black hole anyway?

I always thought that it was the observers' perception of the falling
object that it perpetually falls in to the hole but never quite gets to
the event horizon. I think that the object does fall in and at
increasing speed.


If time does not exist, could I argue that speed limits have no meaning
in law?

> 
>     - Oliver
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1136900927.606227.97620@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I certainly can't close down your argument, Alastair. (It is a bit like the 
tree in the forest falling over... does it make a sound if no one is there 
to hear it? Logic tells us it must do, but it really depends what you mean 
by 'sound'. If you consider 'sound' to be the arrival of certain nerve 
impulses in a certain part of the brain, then it doesn't make a sound. If 
you consider 'sound' to be a wave motion of air, with an amplitude and 
frequency within a certain range, then it might indeed make a 'sound'. )

The fact that no-one's occupying your space and cannot therefore be aware of 
time passing, does not preclude it passing :-) It depends on what your 
definition of 'time' is. And that is the nub of the discussion.

I'm not persuaded by your argument, but I don't have enough authoritative 
data to refute it, so I just have to agree to disagree :-) I guess my 
concept of Time is largely based on intuition and faith... :-)

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<gamdq1ts42ot5sp0uvk2a9bdn6a8uvnfqh@4ax.com> <JL3qf.20086$k76.4760@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <40srj8F1bnru8U1@individual.net> <1135188623.500797.230460@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43A9C2DF.14C9B712@mts.net> <40ufi2F1c0rt8U1@individual.net> <1135289360.886960.119420@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <4123jkF1b3mvlU3@individual.net> <1135380490.052403.82660@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <414p31F1coilcU3@individual.net> <wXywf.80084$6K2.54082@edtnps90> <rhf7s1lgprp7r3ginemsn3f7pi9s3picfc@4ax.com>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:rhf7s1lgprp7r3ginemsn3f7pi9s3picfc@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 09 Jan 2006 19:37:00 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> primacy over the other.

    Good question. I looked it up, and while I haven't finished reading all 
of these pages, these seem to address the issue.

This first page claims that both space and time are derived from a simpler 
concept which he calls "tise".
http://www.spaceandtimeareone.com/

<quote>
Space and time are great inventions of the mind to help it comprehend the 
world, at least in the scale range of our experience. But what could be the 
source in nature that led to the evolutionary development of these two 
tenacious and extremely useful perceptions? I have hypothesized that a 
single entity in nature , which, for convenient reference, I will call 
"tise," underlies both of these perceptions. Tise is all of what we perceive 
as space and time. Tise is comprised of infinitesimally small quanta, which 
I will call "tisons." I introduce the words "tise" (from TIme SpacE) and 
"tison" reluctantly, but necessarily, because tise is conceptually very 
different from the spacetime of modern physics.
</quote>

This page claims that both space and time are derived from the simpler 
concept of velocity.
http://www.reciprocalsystem.com/nlst/nlst06.htm

<quote>
An important consequence of the progression of space-time is that unit 
velocity, one unit of space per unit of time, is the condition of rest in 
the physical universe, the datum from which all activity begins. We are so 
accustomed to measuring from the mathematical zero that this concept of a 
finite velocity as the neutral condition will no doubt seem odd on first 
consideration, but it is not without precedent.
[...]
In this neutral condition, each unit of space is exactly like all other 
units of space and each unit of space is equivalent to a unit of time that 
is exactly like all other units of time. A unit of elapsed time, a unit 
movement in time, is equivalent to a unit movement in space, hence all 
locations in space-time are moving away from all other locations at unit 
velocity. Since space-time is motion, in the most general sense of that 
term, its measure is speed or, as this speed manifests itself in a spatial 
or temporal reference system, velocity. Just as we measure space in 
centimeters, or some similar unit, and time in seconds, we measure 
space-time in centimeters per second-in velocity terms. Unit velocity is not 
only the measure of the progression of space-time; it is the measure of 
space-time itself. Space-time is a motion: a progression. Aside from this 
ceaseless progression, a universe in the neutral condition would be one vast 
domain of perfect uniformity in which nothing ever happens and nothing could 
happen.
</quote>

    - Oliver
```

```
On Sat, 17 Dec 2005 21:20:16 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>After that, then a good description of how things like a human eye or 
>bisexual reproduction could evolve would be necessary. The problem is not 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>structure to evolve.

The ability to sense light and dark does not have survival value?
Specialization in reproduction starts with one part of a plant
producing a seed, and another part producing the trigger.

>Then, as a final touch, explain how love and art and beauty (or sense of 
>these things) could evolve. An evolutionary process might come up with lust, 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>produced. Not very logical.

It certainly is a survival value to decide that a woman with big hips
is beautiful, of that a man with big muscles is handsome.   It is a
survival value to learn to discern differences in different sounds -
and to accept rhythmic sounds as normal - clashing sounds as
dangerous.   It is a survival value to be willing to stick around
after mating - so that your progeny survives.   It is a survival value
to care for your progeny long enough for them to survive and
reproduce.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 51)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-20T19:53:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cjZpf.13519$wg4.2943@edtnps84>`
- **References:** `<1134306034.383469.185440@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134319362.237803.306970@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <s4_mf.1530$w7.1446@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <DjAnf.4839$w7.3842@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <aLCnf.146330$y_1.86429@edtnps89> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <qp53q1tur619iqmd93m80ccl64unttcutp@4ax.com> <yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
>
> Well, I have already logically proven that, if Biblical Creation is 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> what evolution presupposes. :-)

    Evolution does not predict that lifeless dirt, all on its own, will form 
into living cells. If such a thing happened, then it would be very good 
evidence that our current theories of evolution are wrong! Rather, what 
evolution DOES predict is that carbon atoms in some kind of soup (I don't 
know the details, but I suspect it would contain water and sugar, among 
other things; we could probably look it up if we really wanted to), and 
electricity and sunlight together could eventually form living cells.

    A problem with using "something complex cannot come into existence all 
by itself" as a proof for intelligent design is that it ends up in an 
infinite recursion. Let's assume that the ID is correct: Life is too complex 
to happen by itself, so there must be something (let's call that thing "God 
A") to have created it. Then surely, God A is much more complex than all 
forms of life on earth. And since we've assumed as an axiom to our belief 
system that complex things cannot come into existence all by itself, that 
means God A cannot have come into existence all by itself. So something must 
have created it. Let us call that thing God B. Well, surely that thing is 
yet even more complex, and something must have created it. If you continue 
this process, you have an infinite series of Gods, each one more complex 
than the last, and thus it becomes more and more implausible that they 
existed without being created.

    The further you take this particular version of the ID theory, the more 
implausible it becomes.

>
> After that, then a good description of how things like a human eye or 
> bisexual reproduction could evolve would be necessary.

    Others have already provided benefits of the eye. Here's a benefit of 
bisexual reproduction that I haven't seen mentioned yet:

    Bisexual reproduction promotes a heterogeneous population, which makes 
the population as a whole more resilient to diseases. When a all members of 
a species are identical, and a virus comes into existence (whether by God or 
by evolution) which targets that species, then all members of the species 
are equally vulnerable, and if the disease is fatal, all the members of the 
species will die. If each member is slightly different, then the disease 
will have varying effectiveness against each of its members, and even if the 
disease is fatal, there is a higher chance that some members of the 
population will survive the disease.

> The problem is not simply the complexity of those things, it is the fact 
> that they would have no survival value (perhaps even the reverse) until 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> lust, but why love?

    Again, love has already been explained. I'll try to tackle art and 
music:

> Why artistic sense? Why musical sense? In a purely evolutionary context, 
> these things make no sense at all. Even if having them could be shown to 
> have survival value, they would only work *after* they were completely 
> evolved and functional, so that art and music were actually produced. Not 
> very logical.

    What is considered beautiful and musical actually varies from culture to 
culture, and over time. Western concept of music, for example, with its 12 
tones in a chord, and 4/4-oriented time signature, is somewhat unique, and 
other regions do no use the same basis for deciding what sounds are 
considered musical and what sounds are not. Even over time, within the 
context of one population (western humans), which chords were considered 
harmonious versus atonal has changed.

    What seems to be common among music of almost all cultures, as someone 
has pointed out, is that it has low entropy in the mathematical sense. In 
laymens term, it has "patterns" that repeat itself through the sequence of 
sounds. The same thing applies with art: it plays with the part of our brain 
that recognizes patterns.

    Pattern recognition is one of the greatest strengths of the human mind, 
and it is of huge importance to our survival. Pattern recognition is 
considered to one of the most fundamental aspect of intelligence. Almost all 
research in AI is centered around finding ways to get computers to recognize 
more and more sophisticated patterns.

    A useful pattern to notice, for example, is that after eating, you are 
less hungry; When your arm is in pain, jerking it away quickly usually stops 
further pain from occuring. These patterns are hardwired into almost all 
animals. An individual mammal seems to have the ability to learn new 
patterns over its life (unlike any particularly individual insect, for 
example). Humans seem to be able to recognize the most complex patterns of 
all known animals.

    We happen to enjoy patterns (e.g. music and art) for the same reason 
that we enjoy sex: Because it causes us to look for more instances of it, 
which is generally good for the survivability of the species. Or to put it 
the other way around, those who didn't care for sex/patterns didn't actively 
go seek them out, and now they've died without descendants.

    - Oliver
```

- **References:** `<1134319362.237803.306970@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <s4_mf.1530$w7.1446@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <DjAnf.4839$w7.3842@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <aLCnf.146330$y_1.86429@edtnps89> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <qp53q1tur619iqmd93m80ccl64unttcutp@4ax.com> <yx4pf.713$RZ6.678@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <cjZpf.13519$wg4.2943@edtnps84>`

```
On Tue, 20 Dec 2005 19:53:44 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
wrote:

>    Others have already provided benefits of the eye. Here's a benefit of 
>bisexual reproduction that I haven't seen mentioned yet:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>population will survive the disease.

His complaint wasn't that bisexual reproduction is useful - his
complaint was that he didn't see how it could come about in
evolutionary steps.

Sex didn't start with animals though.   We can see simpler examples of
triggers used for reproduction - for instance when a forest fire
enables lodge pole pines to germinate.   An intermediate step between
single and dual sex species could be when a plant keeps some genetic
material separate until germination time was appropriate.  This could
develop into a 2 sexed plant (they do exist), and further
specialization causes one part of this to whither after
cross-pollinization was possible and its benefits could dominate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion

_(reply depth: 53)_

- **From:** "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
> news:1135125071.284841.109250@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>     - Oliver

I've done  a search with AV and found the following:

"Mating type regulation of sexual reproduction in Coprinus


Lorna Casselton, University of Oxford, Oxford, UK
Mating compatibility in the basidiomycete Coprinus cinereus is
determined by multiallelic genes at two complex loci termed A and B.
These genes regulate a developmental programme that converts the
asexual monokaryon into a fertile dikaryon. It is estimated that there
are some 160 versions of A and 79 versions of B which would generate
more than 12000 cross compatible mating types in nature. We have a
fascinating biological problem in trying to resolve how such large
numbers of A and B specificities are derived and how it is that so many
versions can interact to trigger the same developmental pathway. The A
locus contains several genes that encode multiple versions of two
dissimilar homeodomain proteins. Specific combinatorial interactions
between these proteins promotes the A-regulated part of the pathway; we
are interested in why certain pairs of proteins can heterodimerize but
others cannot. The B locus also contains several genes of different
classes; preliminary data indicate that these play a role in signal
transduction. Rare mutations that result in self-compatibility arise
within the A and B loci and molecular analysis of these provides novel
insights into the function of the genes. "



Rather than sexes, mycologists refer to mating types. Usually as a and
b. However,
http://www.teagasc.ie/research/reports/crops/4148/eopr-4148.htm
contains a reference to the A2 mating type. But this appears to be the
second mating type.

Beyond  that I give up. I remember the three-sex-types from my GCE "A"
level biology. However, I no longer have the various books that I used
to have to be able to prove my point conclusively.
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
>>    Maybe the science behind what should be done after a burn is 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> simple. :-)

    You claim it is simple, but you avoided the implied question of "Well, 
if it's so simple, then what do YOU think we should do?"

    Alistair post implies that the best thing to do is cold water if the 
skin has not been burned off, but to do nothing if the skin HAS been burned 
off. Your question above, implies that there's only two possible answers: 
"warm water" or "cold water", and to figure it out might simply involve 
trying both and seeing which works better. As you can now see, that 
expectation is overly simplistic and naive. There may be several other 
variables that a professional doctor would consider that have not been 
mentioned in Alistair's post.

<as an aside to why that may be the case>
    I'm pretty sure that people in the medical profession are often 
hesistant to give medical advice to people who have not received the proper 
training, because mistakes can be fatal, and people are sometimes 
overconfident about their capabilities. Some forms of surgery might be 
routine to a doctor with 40 years experience. It's simple for HIM/HER. 
Doesn't mean he's willing to talk you through it over the telephone for you 
to perform yourself, for example.
</as an aside to why that may be the case>

>>    What are the alternatives to science for treating a skin burn? Does 
>> the Bible have something to say about what to do in those cases? Did 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> formed, when I see flaws in their determinations? :-)

    I have two friends (well I have more than that, but this next anecdote 
is only going to be about two of them). They have both misled me at one 
point in the past. Nothing big, and I'm sure it happens all the time. I've 
probably misled them too. One of them, let's call him Michael, would admit 
to being wrong.

    Mike: "Sorry, I thought that really would fix your car engine. I guess I 
don't know as much about mechanics as I thought."

    The other, let's call him Patrick, would stubbornly push through any 
lies he's caught in.

    Pat: "No, YOU said the solinoid was broken, so I changed the solinoid. 
You never said anything about a spark plug. Yes you did! YES! I SPECIFICALLY 
heard you say solinoid. Fuck it man. You think you're so smart? Fix your own 
damn car then!"

    I'm still friends with both of them, but now I've learned that I should 
take everything Pat says with a grain of salt, whereas I can afford a lot 
more trust to Mike.

    Nobody's perfect. Science has been wrong before, religion has been wrong 
before. The difference is that Science is typically willing to admit to its 
mistakes, and address them, while religion typically is not.

    (Incidentally, Mike is Christian and Patrick is not.)

>
>>> And you want me to believe these people when they tell me they *know* 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> tree? Oh yes, *know* is the correct expression, alright. :-)

    When you say "these posts", if you're talking about the posts on this 
newsgroup, yeah I've been reading them. I don't know how many of the posters 
here are "scientists" though; I figure most of us are programmers (whether 
active or retired) here. =)

    But in my experience, science never pretends to *know* anything. They 
merely give observations, and theories explaining what might cause these 
observations. Note that scientists do not refer to evolution as "The Fact Of 
Evolution"; usually they'll refer to it as "The Theory Of Evolution", and in 
fact, they will admit that the theory is under development, which means that 
at some point in history, there were some flaws in it.

    Scan through the tone of the article for evolution of Wikipedia, for 
example http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolution (emphasis mine):

<quote>
The *DEVELOPMENT* of the modern *THEORY* of evolution began with the 
introduction of the concept of natural selection in a joint 1858 paper by 
Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace. This theory *ACHIEVED A WIDER 
READERSHIP* in Darwin's 1859 book, The Origin of Species.
[...]
This work was groundbreaking, and overturned *OTHER EVOLUTIONARY THEORIES*, 
such as that advanced by Jean Baptiste Lamarck. Because of its potential 
implications for the origins of humankind, the theory has been at the center 
of many social and religious *CONTROVERSIES* since its first inception
</quote>

    See? They acknowledge that the it's a theory, that it's under 
development, that at first not many people believed in it, but now more do, 
that there were other evolutonary theories, and that there are controversies 
surrounding the theory.

    At the time of writing, I did a search for the term "proof". It does not 
appear on that page. In fact the substring "proo" does not appear, and 
"prove" appears only twice: "earlier scientific hypotheses which have been 
disproven" and "which argues for the improvement of human heredity".

    Scientists do not claim to have "proved" evolution to be true. They 
provide "evidence" in the scientific meaning of the term. I.e. "This leads 
us to believe that...", and NOT "This shows without a doubt that..."

    As for my own posts, yes, I think I HAVE expressed that I am willing to 
accept the possibility that evolutionists may be barking up the wrong tree. 
Take for example, the post you replied to. I wrote:

<quote>
the "angle in triangle adds up to 180" statement is only true IF you accept 
those 5 assumptions.
</quote>

    In other words, we (used to) believe that the angles add up to 180 
degrees, but our belief is based on 5 assumptions which are not absolutely 
certain to be true (and we now have evidence that one of them is actually 
false).

    I also wrote the following in that same post:

<quote>
The nice thing about
the scientific process is that it encourages scrutiny, and is willing to
change itself when it finds out it is wrong. This is in contrast to many
religions, which prefer blind faith and are willing to deny logical
consistency in favour of maintaining their initial beliefs.
</quote>

    And:

<quote>
That's
strawman again, because scientists DO have theories about how life got here,
and those theories ARE under development, and they are subject to change as
new information arrives.
</quote>

    So in other words, I am perfectly willing to admit to the possibility 
that the theory of evolution is wrong. Science has been wrong before, and 
it'll probably be wrong again. There's no reason to believe that evolution 
is immune to this. So *know* is very much an inappropriate term to emphasize 
when speaking of how Scientists feel about their theories.

>
>>    When you meet a scientist, ask him (or her) what is the probability 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> you believe it, but it just doesn't work that way.

    From the scientists I've encountered and spoken with, it IS true. Maybe 
you should visit Montreal, Quebec, Canada; meet some friendlier scientists.

>>
>>    The idea that "complexity" must come from "intelligence" is a 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> simplicity changing itself into complexity.

    My point is that you seem to think that complexity is a sign of 
intelligence. I claim that it's the other way around.

[snip]

> A big complex mess is evidence that a big complex something went wrong. 
> :-)

    Yes, and DNA is believe by many biologist to be a big complex mess. 
Human anatomy as well (lots of useless organs, etc.)

>
>>> There is no question that the academic community is generally hostile 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> aren't on the receiving end. :-)

    For all they (the universities and professors) knew, I could have been 
Christian. But they never knew, because they never asked me. And they never 
asked me, I suspect, because they never cared. Being Christian does not make 
one a "visible minority". Being Asian does make one a visible minority, but 
again I did not notice any racial discrimination against me in university.

>
>>    I have trouble believing that all secular colleges on Earth are 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> anti-Christian prejudice is very common, I assure you.

So how do you justify your original claim?

<quote>
Any Christian who has ever been exposed to a secular college or university 
environment knows this.
</quote>

[snip]

>>    I just want to point out that earlier in this post, you ridiculed 
>> scientist for claiming they *know* something. Your exact statement was 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> risky.

    Well, I guess we could end this whole discussion with the assumption 
that the following dialog took place:

    "*I* know that God exists."
    "Okay, but we don't care whether or not *YOU* know. We want to know how 
can *WE* know if God exists?"
    "Well, you can't. Or I don't know. I guess..."
    "Alright. End of discussion then?"
    "I guess so."

>
> I think a good starting point would be to go someplace private (no point 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> for any good stuff He may have sent my way.

    Maybe I'll give it a shot over the weekend. I don't promise I'll 
nescessarily come back and report my findings here on CLC though.

    - Oliver
```

- **References:** `<43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134306034.383469.185440@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134319362.237803.306970@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <s4_mf.1530$w7.1446@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <DjAnf.4839$w7.3842@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <aLCnf.146330$y_1.86429@edtnps89> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <wIjof.876$lv3.506@clgrps12>`

```
>  See? They acknowledge that the it's a theory, that it's under
> development, that at first not many people believed in it, but now more do,
> that there were other evolutonary theories, and that there are controversies
> surrounding the theory.

'Evolution' is not a theory, it is observable and can be demonstrated,
for example adaptaion, for example breeding of dogs, for example new
strains of flu.

The 'theories of evolution' are what the _causes_ are. Lemark's theory
was that aquired characteristics are inheritable. Darwin's theory is
that evolution is primarily driven by natural selection. Others include
sexual selection.

In the same way there is a 'Theory of Gravity' This does _not_ mean
that 'gravity is only a theory' and that we may float away at any time,
the theory is about the _causes_ of gravity.

> Scientists do not claim to have "proved" evolution to be true.

Evolution is observable. For example the world millions of years ago
was full of reptiles and now it is full of mammals, the explanation of
this is that over time species evolve where this gives them an
advantage they increase at the expense of those that are a
disadvantage.  What they haven't 'proved' is that natural selection
_alone_ is sufficient. Personally I think there is something to be said
for a part of this being down to sexual selection.

You probably think the Dinosaurs were drowned in the flood. Well,
duhhh.

> They
> provide "evidence" in the scientific meaning of the term. I.e. "This leads
> us to believe that...", and NOT "This shows without a doubt that..."

Yes, a typical religionist viewpoint. You require an adamant statement
such as the bible gives you. Well remain ignorant then.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Religion and Science (WAS: Making money from Java)

_(reply depth: 51)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-15T21:42:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qrlof.1874$lv3.282@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134306034.383469.185440@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134319362.237803.306970@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <s4_mf.1530$w7.1446@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <DjAnf.4839$w7.3842@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <aLCnf.146330$y_1.86429@edtnps89> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <wIjof.876$lv3.506@clgrps12> <1134678788.549508.261350@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1134678788.549508.261350@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>>  See? They acknowledge that the it's a theory, that it's under
>> development, that at first not many people believed in it, but now more 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> strains of flu.

    Evolution *IS* a theory. Much like, as you say later in this post, that 
gravity is a theory. Gravity has been observed as well. The problem lies in 
the definition of the term "theory" (or rather, in the perception of the 
definition of that term).

    "Yesterday, I let go of a ball, and it fell to the ground." That is a 
fact, or an observation. Assuming we can trust our sense, our mind and our 
memory, we "know" it is true.

    Now we might form theories about WHY the ball fell to the ground. One 
theory might be that the earth emits graviton particles can cause an 
attractive force between the ball and the earth. Another theory might be 
that the mass of the earth causes space-time around it to curve, and the 
ball wishes to move into a lower energy state. Sometimes two theories can 
seem very different but end up being equivalent to each other. Another 
theory might be that lightwaves from the sun *push* the ball downwards.

    "Tomorrow, when I let go of the ball, it will fall to the ground." That 
is a prediction, and we do NOT know it will be true. Some of our theories 
may support the prediction, some of the theories may make other predictions.

    Now we can come up with experiments to support our theories. We might, 
for example, drop the ball in a dark room. Or use a flashlight to "push" the 
ball upwards, away from the ground.

    After performing the experiments, we have *new* observations, from which 
we can refine our theories. The ball still dropped in a dark room, which 
means that light waves are not *nescessary* to cause a ball to fall. Note 
that this does not prove the inexistence of light waves, and it does not 
prove that light waves were not perhaps partially responsible. Further 
experiments may reveal that lightwaves DO exert a force, and that we can 
build "solar sails" to ride on these waves.

    The ball also still dropped when we had a flashlight try to push the 
ball upwards. This leads us to believe that the force applied by light 
waves, if any, is negligeable compared to the force of graviton particles or 
to the force applied by space-time curvature.

    As our theories mature, we may become more confident in them, and thus 
more confident in the predictions they make (e.g. "I bet you 50 bucks that 
if I let go of a ball tomorrow, it'll drop to the ground.") But we can never 
be 100% sure about it. At some point in the future, we may find a flaw in 
one of our theories.

    It's not right to say that evolution is "untrue" because it is a theory, 
but it is equally wrong to say that evolution is "true" because it is a 
theory. We may have a very high confidence in our theory of evolution, but 
we can never be sure that it is true.

[snip]
>
>> Scientists do not claim to have "proved" evolution to be true.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

    Evolution has apparently been *directly* observed. See section 5.0 of 
http://www.talkorigins.org/faqs/faq-speciation.html

    Scientists have observed new species of plants coming into existence. 
Pests are widely known to develop immunity to pesticides (e.g. ants, weeds, 
etc.) In fact, if you google for "evolution has been observed", you'll see 
literally millions of hits (17.4 million when I tried it), the first 30 of 
which, at least, all seem to be relevant.

    - Oliver
```

```
> Evolution *IS* a theory.

No. Evolution is an observable phenomenon. There are theories about
what causes it, about which ancestral forms begat later species, about
why certain characteristics are advantageous. Of course, if you study
the world on your knees with your eyes closed then you will have
difficulty observing anything.

> Much like, as you say later in this post, that gravity is a theory.

No. Gravity is not a theory. There are theories about what causes it.

>"Tomorrow, when I let go of the ball, it will fall to the ground." That
> is a prediction, and we do NOT know it will be true.

Some things in science are so certain that we raise them to the level
of 'laws'. You will find, if you look, that gravity is one of these.
Not only the fact that gravity is certain, but the strength of it and
how it varies with distance.  Gravity is not a 'theory' though certain
interactions with light are theories and black holes have not, for
obvious reasons, been directly observed, so they remain theoretical.
```

_(reply depth: 53)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SSDof.2075$ic1.1491@edtnps90>`
- **References:** `<43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134306034.383469.185440@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <mTWmf.1499$w7.1305@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134319362.237803.306970@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <s4_mf.1530$w7.1446@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dqcrp1hlglpf18tg9lmn3bbfqgofvo63st@4ax.com> <DjAnf.4839$w7.3842@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <aLCnf.146330$y_1.86429@edtnps89> <98Lnf.6905$kP5.4674@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <Jk_nf.256568$ir4.37132@edtnps90> <AA3of.8507$kP5.2767@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <wIjof.876$lv3.506@clgrps12> <1134678788.549508.261350@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <qrlof.1874$lv3.282@clgrps12> <1134714620.655734.55080@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1134714620.655734.55080@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>> Evolution *IS* a theory.
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> obvious reasons, been directly observed, so they remain theoretical.

    I think we're just arguing about the definitions of terms here, and 
there is no substantial different between what you believe and what I 
believe with respect to the content of this post. That being said, I was 
curious as to what the different was between a "Law" and a "Theory" in the 
scientific meaning of the words, so I looked it up on Wikipedia. Given, 
Wikipedia is not nescessarily the final authority on such definitions, but 
it seemed as good a starting point as any.

<quote>
Physical laws are distinguished from scientific theories by their 
simplicity. Scientific theories are generally more complex than laws; they 
have many component parts, and are more likely to be changed as the body of 
available experimental data and analysis develops. This is because a 
physical law is strictly empirical. It is a summary observation of things as 
they are. A theory is a model that accounts for the observation, explains 
it, relates it to other observations, and makes testable predictions based 
upon it. Simply stated, while a law notes that something happens, a theory 
attempts to deal with why or how it happens.
</quote>

    So to me, there is the "Law of Gravity", which is an empirical 
observation: Matter seems to exert attractive forces to other matter, and 
this force is proportional to the mass of the matter, and inversely 
proportional to the distance seperating the two units of matter. Then there 
are theories of gravity. One such theory might involve graviton particles; 
another theory might involve space-time curvature. Maybe these two theories 
are equivalent.

    Then there is the "Law of Evolution", which is an empirical observation: 
new species seem to come into existence that are slight modification of 
existing species. And then there are theories of evolution. Perhaps this is 
a non-law, in that it doesn't exibit the "general properties of physical 
laws" listed on the Wikipedia page, but that may be because evolution is not 
so much physics as it is biology.

    But given the above definition of "law" and "theory", it seems like we 
do not "promote" something from the status of "theory" to that of "law" just 
because we have more confidence in the latter. Rather, they seem to be 
addressing two seperate facets of reality.

    - Oliver
```
