```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
> Skippy, how many English translations of the mass have you gone through in 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> But you can still be a *believer*.


Different English translations don't seem an issue to me. What puzzles me is 
why Catholics used to be discouraged, if not forbidden, from reading the 
Bible. John Tyndale was even burned at the stake for making the first 
English translation of the Bible. How does one say they believe in the 
Bible, yet forbid or even discourage others form reading it? It makes me 
think the motivation came more from human lust for power than any Christian 
motivation. It doesn't surprise me when non-believers see such things and 
question the validity of Christianity.

My objection is that they often see only these things, while ignoring the 
vast number of true Christian acts of love, kindness, charity and sacrifice 
for others. Anyone can stand up and claim "I'm a Christian!", proceed to 
live completely in opposition to what Jesus taught, then the word accepts 
without question their claim of being a Christian, instead of judging their 
claim against their acts. If I claimed to be a socialist, but acted entirely 
like a capitalist, or vice versa, people would justifiably believe my 
actions over my claim. What Jesus actually taught is there for anyone to 
read. Anyone can act out of character on isolated occasion, but anyone's 
claim of being a Christian should be taken as specious, when they continue 
to live and act in a manner entirely different from what Jesus taught, 
rather than bashing the whole of Christianity. At least that would be 
intellectually honest.
```

- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Bpkf.51276$6y4.13244@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <43923888.E48F76B3@mts.net> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134306865.212753.114390@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <1134415273.500876.207870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dnkm05$2niv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Itlnf.98582$Eq5.59918@pd7tw1no> <sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
>
> Different English translations don't seem an issue to me. What puzzles me 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Christianity. At least that would be intellectually honest.

    I think the problem is that you seem to equate "Christian acts" with 
"acts of love, kindness, charity and sacrifice for others" and 
non-Christians don't.

    That's not to say that non-Christians nescessarily equate "Christian 
acts" with "acts of hate, meanness, greed and... whatever the reverse of 
sacrifice is". Rather, the concept of Christian-behaviour and 
loving-behaviour seem to be completely orthogonal to each other.

    When we (I say "we" because I happen to be a non-Christian) see someone 
acting in a kind and loving matter, we don't immediately think "Wow, that 
person is acting like a Christian." Rather, we think "Wow, that person is 
acting in a kind an loving matter". And if that person happened to be 
Christian, it's considered incidental to, rather than the cause of, their 
good behaviour.

    Jesus Christ may have told people to behave kindly, but he certainly 
wasn't the first person to come up with that concept. So when someone 
performs a kind act, we don't call it a "Christian act", we call it a "kind 
act".

    Even if what Jesus taught is out there for anyone to read, why should I 
care? If I want to be a good person, I already know how to be one. I don't 
need to read Jesus' teachings to find out, and anyway there are plenty of 
other sources on how to be a good person. If I am interested in Jesus' take 
on this in particular, it is not because he's offering some advice on being 
good that hasn't been said anywhere else; it would be because I am 
interested in Christ himself. Hence how Christianity and "Goodness" can be 
two completely seperate concepts.

    I think people are bashing on Christians because it's the "biggest" 
religion these days (sort of like bashing on Microsoft because they're the 
richest), but really the complaint is about religions in general. The people 
who bash on religions don't like the idea of believing in something 
absolutely, such that if contrary evidence were to come up, a true believer 
would discard the evidence and continue believing whatever it is he 
believes. Most religions seem unwilling to adapt themselves when evidence 
comes up contradicting one of their beliefs. AFAIK, only Buddhism has an 
officially state policy of changing itself when it contradicts with 
scientific evidence.

    - Oliver
```

```
On Tue, 13 Dec 2005 17:34:41 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
wrote:

>    I think the problem is that you seem to equate "Christian acts" with 
>"acts of love, kindness, charity and sacrifice for others" and 
>non-Christians don't.

It's an expression that has been common in Christian societies for
some time.   I haven't seen any statistical analysis that shows that
other members of religions nor even atheists are more lax at doing
charitable acts though.   Muslims are required to give to beggars, and
many Jews believe that they are graded by God by their actions here on
Earth.

Still, I encourage all to do what Christians describe as "Christian
behavior".

On the other hand, it certainly isn't an atheistic ideal to wish
someone an eternal future of unimaginable torture.   That evil seems
limited to a many followers of a limited number of religions.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
> On the other hand, it certainly isn't an atheistic ideal to wish
> someone an eternal future of unimaginable torture.   That evil seems
> limited to a many followers of a limited number of religions.

Howard, ever since Jesus, Christians have been dedicating their lives and 
fortunes to spread the good news of Jesus Christ, so that people *would not* 
suffer an eternity of suffering. Countless Christian missionaries have given 
their lives to this very purpose, and most Christians give to missionary 
organizations for this purpose. This hardly equates to "wish someone an 
eternal future of unimaginable torture" now, does it? :-)  In this case, 
there is a vast difference between believing something will happen and 
wanting that something to happen. Jesus Christ gave His own life to provide 
a way for each of us to avoid it happening to us.
```

```
On Tue, 13 Dec 2005 20:44:02 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>> On the other hand, it certainly isn't an atheistic ideal to wish
>> someone an eternal future of unimaginable torture.   That evil seems
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>a way for each of us to avoid it happening to us.

Finding examples of people who do not want their beloved God who has
the ability to stop such unimaginable torture to allow such doesn't
change the fact that "many followers" do appear to enjoy the concept
of Hell.

You can't deny that there are many people who have cursed or otherwise
wished Hellfire on others.

I can't imagine anything more hateful and evil than wishing that any
being to be tortured beyond all understanding forever and ever without
hope of parole.

Close behind is anybody accepting that even one person will suffer
such a fate without doing everything possible to stop it.   Heck, the
end of the world would be a great improvement - fewer souls born to
end up with such a terrible fate.

There is nothing anybody can do that approaches the misery that
believers in Hellfire expect for the vast majority of people.

What would you think of your father if he stood by while some bully
tortured your sister - when your father could easily say "stop", and
the bully would stop?

Now replace "father" with "Father" in that above question.
```

```
> the fact that "many followers" do appear to enjoy the concept of Hell.

Each religious group has its own different belief system. Judson makes
the mistake of believing that 'all true christians' are exactly like
himself, but that is probably because he avoids anything that does not
support his views.

For example there are the 'Exclusive Bretheren' who believe ('proof' is
in the bible) that there are only a fixed small number that will be
'saved' and that is them, they have the numbers and don't want anyone
else to join.  So Judson goes to hell regardless of how dedicated he
is.

so Judson goes to a special hell reserved for Baptists.

Perhaps James can illustrate the Catholic view on whether the Pope is
the only way to salvation.
```

```
news:P34of.8513$kP5.6831@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> thought anything else.

I seem to recall a message in which you wrote that Roman Catholics agree 
with you on the issues of "faith alone" and "grace alone", which are 
prerequisite premises to being correctly called Christian.

All the evidence at hand leads me to believe "sola fide" and "sola gratia" 
are specifically Reformation positions, and stem directly from Luther, and 
are rejected by the Roman Catholic church.

Thus, as I read your premise:  (1) All Christians must believe in salvation 
by faith alone through grace alone.  (2) Roman Catholicism teaches  that 
salvation is by faith alone through grace alone.  Therefore (3) Roman 
Catholic doctrine is a Christian doctrine.

Whether (1) is true or not, (2) is definitely false.

And if  (1) is factually true then no Roman Catholic (or for that matter 
Eastern Orthodox) group has the right to call itself Christian, by your 
rules.  Is that sort of arrogance appropriate behavior for a Christian? 
Does God really play "Simon says ..."?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> are rejected by the Roman Catholic church.

I believe the apparent discrepance here is essentially semantical. From your 
comments about James 2 and so forth, I suspect that Catholic Doctrine is 
concerned with the issue James was, that Christians envision someone 
becoming a Christian without understanding the importance of works. People 
had apparently misunderstanding Paul's comments about "faith apart from 
works" as meaning there is no need for works. However, as James points out, 
if you have the faith that God wants (unlike demons, who believe), then that 
faith will produce works. There is no discrepancy between Paus and James, 
Paul is simply speaking about what it takes to be saved, in alegal sense, 
James is talking about the Christian experience. Works contribute nothing, 
nada, zip toward obtaining salvation. But if a person is really saved, then 
they are indwelt by the Holy Spirit, Who *will* produce works. Denying the 
works means denying the indwelling, ergo works are necessary, but as fruit 
of salvation, not in obtaining it. This *has* to be the case, for salvation 
is also by grace, and grace is not grace if there is works, as Paul says. If 
the Bible is the inerrent Word of God, then you must interpret Scripture in 
a way that is consistent with major and minor context. Any other 
interpretation of these Scriptures here produces conflict with other 
Scripture. This interpretation does not.

> Thus, as I read your premise:  (1) All Christians must believe in 
> salvation by faith alone through grace alone.  (2) Roman Catholicism 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Whether (1) is true or not, (2) is definitely false.

The Bible clearly and defiinitely teaches (1). Draw your own conclusions 
about the rest. :-)

> And if  (1) is factually true then no Roman Catholic (or for that matter 
> Eastern Orthodox) group has the right to call itself Christian, by your 
> rules.  Is that sort of arrogance appropriate behavior for a Christian?

To simply relay what the Bible clearly says is not arrogance.

Do you think God reads Roman Catholic doctrine to decide whether individuals 
are saved? :-) Every person receives Jesus or not as an individual. Groups, 
such as denominations, are a man created thing. The Bible declares all 
Christians (true believers in Jesus) to be "one". When the Bible declares 
that we are saved by grace through faith, apart from works, that is the end 
of the issue. Paul clearly says that and James did not contradict it, he was 
clearly writing to stave off a particular wrong interpretation of Paul. 
Anyone who looks at Scripture trying to decide which part to believe is 
missing the boat. If the Bible is not God's Word, then it is foolish to have 
a religion based on it. If the Bible is God's word, then it is entirely 
consistent. If we don't see the consistency, we should study until we do see 
it. Until that time, we must put aside interpretation of that area that 
seems to be in conflict.

> Does God really play "Simon says ..."?

Not as far as I know. I don't think God plays games of any sort. He is 
apparently entirely serious about anything He says. :-)
```

- **References:** `<lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <1134415273.500876.207870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dnkm05$2niv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Itlnf.98582$Eq5.59918@pd7tw1no> <sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <RCDnf.146343$y_1.35785@edtnps89> <j22up1l7975en7ises32kh1ckieglsqqea@4ax.com> <vDLnf.7043$kP5.3641@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9kd0q1psli1hur0pcv1pld5dn12u26q11d@4ax.com> <1134587227.400842.107150@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <P34of.8513$kP5.6831@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <dns7dp$16ep$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Mi6pf.732$RZ6.42@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:Mi6pf.732$RZ6.42@bignews7.bellsouth.net...

>> Thus, as I read your premise:  (1) All Christians must believe in 
>> salvation by faith alone through grace alone.  (2) Roman Catholicism 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> The Bible clearly and defiinitely teaches (1).

The words of Jesus in Matthew 16:27 and 25:33 are pretty clear declarations 
to the contrary.  When there's a perceived inconsistency between Paul's 
words and Jesus', as a Christian I tend to put more weight on Jesus' and 
treat Paul's (in this case, in conjunction with James') as carrying more 
weight.

I see "Fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom" with minimal wording 
differences in the KJV at Psalm 111,10; Proverbs 1:7; Proverbs 9:10; and 
Sirach 1:14.  The way I've always understood it is through the following 
progression of analogies:

At one level of maturity, the child will do the dishes after dinner because 
he fears punishment if he doesn't.

At another level, the child will do the dishes after dinner to get a reward.

At another level, the child will do the dishes out of love because Mom asked 
him to.

At another level, the child will do the dishes out of love without being 
prompted, as a matter of course.

I believe the last is a "higher level" of spiritual maturity than the first. 
BUT:  the kid still has to do the dishes.

And no matter how much he proclaims that he loves his parents, if he doesn't 
do the dishes his actions (in this limited scenario) belie his words.

>  Draw your own conclusions about the rest. :-)

I have already demonstrated by citation from the Catholic Encyclopedia that 
(2) is CONTRARY to Roman Catholic doctrine.   Your arguments about semantics 
don't cut it, given that it is specifically the issue of the word "alone" in 
the phrase "faith alone" that they don't agree with, and it is specifically 
the use of the word "alone" in conjunction with "faith" that Luther 
*introduced*.   I think the Roman Catholics are right in this respect. 
You're the one that came to erroneous conclusions, both about the 
universality of acceptance of "sola fide" among Christians and its 
acceptance as a matter of faith by the Roman Catholic Church in particular, 
as part of your declaration that this is a required tenet for a Christian, 
stated *not* as a matter of opinion but as a matter of *fact*.

I've said before:  I do not believe God plays a game of Simon Says.

> To simply relay what the Bible clearly says is not arrogance.

To claim that "sola fide" is the only possible interpretation of the Bible, 
to claim (by omission) that Jesus' words are irrelevant to the point, and to 
state categorically that the Roman Catholic Church and all of its 
theologians have been wrong on the subject for the last two thousand years 
strikes me as a *bit* on the arrogant side!

> Do you think God reads Roman Catholic doctrine to decide whether 
> individuals are saved? :-)

Do you think God says "What people do and how they behave is irrelevant, 
only what they believe" in deciding whether individuals are saved?  I think 
Matthew records Jesus as stating otherwise.

> Every person receives Jesus or not as an individual.

Every person is judged by God based, I believe, on what they *understand*, 
and how they put that understanding into practice.  Judgment is *not* 
synonymous with condemnation.

And having lived with condemnation all my life, I am now at the point where 
I do the best I can from day to day to keep my thoughts and my actions 
focused toward my understanding of proper attitude and behavior, and am 
prepared to face my Maker and His judgment of my strengths, my weaknesses, 
my understanding, my misunderstandings, my accomplishments and my 
transgressions.  What the outcome will be is whatever the outcome will be at 
the time; I don't focus on either the reward or the punishment, only in 
doing the best I can to stay in alignment with what I believe  God's will 
for me to be on a daily basis, not out of fear but out of love.  Sometimes I 
succeed, sometimes I don't, and when I don't, I regret it and work to avoid 
it, again, out of love, not out of fear.  Whether that's good enough for God 
the Father is up to Him, and I'm willing to accept what comes, be that 
eternal bliss, eternal torture, eternal oblivion, or anything else.    And 
yes, I rely on Jesus in the process.   I do not believe that reliance is in 
vain because of my position on "sola fide".

       -Chuck Stevens
```

_(reply depth: 57)_

- **From:** "charles hottel" <jghottel@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-19T22:42:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5f3f4$43a77dbe$4f9c629$16961@DIALUPUSA.NET>`
- **References:** `<lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <1134415273.500876.207870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dnkm05$2niv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Itlnf.98582$Eq5.59918@pd7tw1no> <sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <RCDnf.146343$y_1.35785@edtnps89> <j22up1l7975en7ises32kh1ckieglsqqea@4ax.com> <vDLnf.7043$kP5.3641@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9kd0q1psli1hur0pcv1pld5dn12u26q11d@4ax.com> <1134587227.400842.107150@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <P34of.8513$kP5.6831@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <dns7dp$16ep$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Mi6pf.732$RZ6.42@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <do71lt$1jnq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
>
>> To simply relay what the Bible clearly says is not arrogance.
>

First of all it could be arrogant if you quoted the Bible out of context.

Secondly you are not just quoting the Bible and leaving us to come to our 
own understanding/interpretation, hopefully under the influence and 
inspiration of the Holy Spirit.

In addition to quoting the Bible you are giving us your interpretation of 
what it means.  Bible truth and your interpretation of it are NOT the same 
thing. Many well meaning people have studied the Bible seeking truth and 
come to many differing interpretations. Your interpretation is NOT the only 
possible true one. Stating your Bible beliefs strongly with confidence is 
NOT the same thing as, someone doing the same thing as regards COBOL.  You 
get reliable and consistent feedback from a good COBOL compiler and once you 
publish your results other people can try to duplicate those results and 
decide for themselves if you were correct. Even then people argue endlessly 
about the use of periods and other such things. With the Bible there are 
many different translations being used and each person does not have the 
same reproducible feedback experience. Even a single person may read the 
same passage at different times and have a different experience. I find this 
to be the best use of the Bible for myself. It seems to reflect back to me 
those things that I am most in need of at the time (not that I always want 
to hear it!).  Bible interpretation is just NOT that clear cut. I do not 
think that it is unfair if others disagree with your interpretation or even 
for them to call your interpretation arrogant, as long as they give their 
reasons. It is not meant as a personal attack.

You seen to hold very many strong beliefs. One of them seems to be a belief 
about your own beliefs, i.e. a metabelief: you believe that once you have 
formed a belief that it is very correct as though actually a fact, and you 
are extremely reluctant to reconsider that decision. There is a reason that 
faith is called faith and not called fact. No matter how strong a persons 
faith is there is always some probability that it could be wrong.  Generally 
there is less evidence for things accepted on faith than for things accepted 
as fact. Even facts are not true with 100 percent probability. Take Newton's 
laws for example. They enabled many correct predictions and for hundreds of 
years evidence piled up affirming their correctness.  Yet today we know that 
there are situations in which they are inaccurate i.e. wrong. I personally 
do not trust myself to come to 100 percent correct beliefs that are good for 
all time. I prefer to keep and open mind and to be flexible enough to modify 
my beliefs when I receive new information that indicates that I should do 
so. That is what works for me but I presume it does not work for you or 
indeed for many others. Scientists also can have this same problem. Some do 
not like it if you call evolution a theory because they say there is so much 
evidence that supports it. But just like Newton's laws evidence can never 
prove a theory true with 100 percent probability, but  facts can disprove a 
theory or at least suggest it must be modified.  I regard evolution as much 
less well established than Newton's laws but that is just me. Even if a 
person believes in creation it seems quite clear that since that creation 
evolution has occurred.

I have noticed that the field of religion (preachers) and the medical field 
(doctors) and some others attract a personality type know as "I'm ok you're 
not ok". They read the Bible, interpret it and declare "The Creed" that 
consists of what they consider the most important points. You must believe 
this creed to become a member of their religious club (church). Today there 
are many denominations (divisions) in just the Christian part of the world. 
I have even heard one group refer to the other groups as non-believers even 
though ALL the groups mentioned believe in Jesus Christ as the means of 
salvation!  People even kill each other because of these divisions. Just to 
be clear, I am not accusing you of having this personality type as I 
certainly do not know you well enough to reach any such conclusion. You do 
say you are not associated with a particular denomination but your mention 
of the word "rapture" leads me to believe that you have been somewhat 
influenced by one.

The gospel has often been referred to as the "Good News" for mankind, but 
your interpretation certainly makes it sound like extremely "BAD NEWS" for 
many if not most. In your view Indians and infants who died without ever 
know Jesus Christ are condemned to hell and dogs and other beloved pets die 
forever and never make it to heaven.  You might as well say "Indians ate 
dogs and dogs do not have cloven hoofs so Indians must go to hell".  Of 
course the Bible does NOT say that IMNSHO. The Bible does not say directly 
what happens in this situation, it is only your interpretation. I find your 
view less than compassionate and yes even arrogant. If I were asked the 
question, I would say that I do not think the situation is covered in the 
Bible except only that God is a loving, compassionate and just Being and 
therefore I trust in Him to handle situations like this justly and 
appropriately. Of course it is just my opinion, one among millions, and the 
value you receive is in proportion to what you have paid for it! I for one 
would not find heaven to be heaven  if others who had no chance for 
salvation, as well as some of my loved ones were suffering eternally in 
hell. Even if hell were defined without fire and brimstone but as eternal 
exclusion from the presence of GOD,  it would not seem just to me.  Any 
method of salvation that is exclusive and not available to all who are in 
need of it, is inherently flawed in my opinion. Also it will not be heaven 
for me if my dog is not there. If God is love and all love comes from Him 
then certainly the love that comes from a beloved pet will not disappear 
forever. I just had to have my dog put to sleep Nov. 28th so I may not be 
very objective about this matter, but I found your statement about pets to 
be extremely hurtful even though you may not have meant it that way. I think 
you say a lot of things without carefully considering their effect on 
others. While I am at, just to let God know, I am not interested in living 
in a golden city paved with streets of gold. I would prefer a log cabin in 
the woods by a mountain with a stream and/or lake nearby and with my dog of 
course. I am glad that programming has worked out well for you and I suggest 
that you avoid sales as a profession because even though I am sympathetic to 
your position I find your salesmanship of the good news severely lacking 
IMO.

Since you take the Bible so literally I am curious about a couple of things.

Do you think the days of creation in Genesis are literal days as we know 
them now. If so please explain how that could be when the sun was not 
created until after the third day.

Also what day do you keep as the Sabbath? Sunday or the original Jewish 
Saturday Sabbath ( actually sundown Friday evening until sundown Saturday 
evening, since the creation days each consisted of an evening and a 
morning)? If Sunday is the new Sabbath how do you explain that? After all, 
the keeping of the Sabbath is one of the ten commandments i.e. part of the 
law which supposedly cannot be changed. If it (the law) can be changed then 
why is it necessary for the wages of sin to be death and why is a Savior 
necessary? Why not simply change the law instead. After all Jesus said He 
came not to change the law but to fulfill the law and that not one dot of 
the 'i' or one cross of the 't' would ever be changed.

One last thing. Thou shalt not kill. Are there any circumstances where you 
think it is alright to kill? How about if you think the killing will save 
lives in the long run? How do you know with 100 percent certainty that it 
will save lives? Would you stake your eternal salvation on making the right 
decision? Would it be alright to kill Nazi soldiers to save Jews from the 
concentration camps? I seem  to recall from another thread that you support 
the Iraq war (correct me if I am wrong), do you think any of the killing 
going on there is justified? Either "thou shalt not kill" is absolute truth 
or it isn't or is it as I believe Mr. Wong stated, a command is not 
necessarily true or false. I guess I am a lot less certain about things than 
you seem to be. Well if each of us has our own way that works for us I guess 
that is what matters.  Or is it what God thinks that really matters? Wow 
this mind reading thing is exhausting, very error prone and not productive. 
Certainty, certainty where o where are you?

Well it is up to you whether you want to answer or not as I am sure you are 
growing weary of this discussion and I am asking simply out of curiosity.

Well this took longer than you can possibly imagine as I am a poor typist 
(slow and lots of spelling errors) so I will try to end on a more upbeat 
note with a spiritual comment from "Frank and Earnest" from today's comic 
page: "The last time I had a salad and a rotisserie chicken, I tossed and 
turned all night".

To docdwarf: pet killer for me and when you find yourself with the "I just 
can't resist urge" that is the time that you most need to resist;-)
```

```
On Mon, 19 Dec 2005 22:42:48 -0500, "charles hottel"
<jghottel@yahoo.com> wrote:

>You seen to hold very many strong beliefs. One of them seems to be a belief 
>about your own beliefs, i.e. a metabelief: you believe that once you have 
>formed a belief that it is very correct as though actually a fact, and you 
>are extremely reluctant to reconsider that decision. There is a reason that 
>faith is called faith and not called fact. 

Apparently "faith" wasn't an issue in the time of Abraham.    Religion
was accepted the same as any other part of life.  If God talks to you,
you don't need faith.

But in a world where we expect evidence for cases of science and
justice and other life issues - but don't see evidence for the
supernatural, that the church has switched gears and proclaimed that
religion is all about faith.

It's not about love.   It's not about doing good things.   It's not
about following the Law.   It's about believing without evidence.
(Believing with evidence isn't "faith").

If you believe the right way without evidence, you are one of us and
will be rewarded.   If you believe the wrong way without evidence, you
are one of them and will be punished beyond all understanding forever
and ever.

What could be more monstrous than this creed?
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<do9cec$30i2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <1134415273.500876.207870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dnkm05$2niv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Itlnf.98582$Eq5.59918@pd7tw1no> <sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <RCDnf.146343$y_1.35785@edtnps89> <j22up1l7975en7ises32kh1ckieglsqqea@4ax.com> <vDLnf.7043$kP5.3641@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9kd0q1psli1hur0pcv1pld5dn12u26q11d@4ax.com> <1134587227.400842.107150@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <P34of.8513$kP5.6831@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <dns7dp$16ep$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Mi6pf.732$RZ6.42@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <do71lt$1jnq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5f3f4$43a77dbe$4f9c629$16961@DIALUPUSA.NET>`

```
"charles hottel" <jghottel@yahoo.com> wrote in message 
news:5f3f4$43a77dbe$4f9c629$16961@DIALUPUSA.NET...

Addressee not cited, so I'll jump in with some responses:

> Do you think the days of creation in Genesis are literal days as we know 
> them now. If so please explain how that could be when the sun was not 
> created until after the third day.

The word "yowm" (Strong's #03117) in Hebrew can be interpreted as "day", and 
the "evening and morning" part would lend support to that interpretation, 
but it can also be interpreted as "a period of time", "a year", or even "an 
era".   Insisting that the Bible says that the period before the separation 
of the sky and the water (the formation of planets) was *exactly*, or even 
*approximately*, 86,400 of what we now call "seconds" might just be 
overstating what the original author intended.

So, no, I for one don't think the wording in the Masoretic Text absolutely 
and unambiguously requires that the only possible time period being 
described as "day" is what we consider "day" now.  The original Hebrew word 
is nowhere near that precise.

In addition, there's a whole bunch of form shifts in the verbs in this part 
of Genesis, shifting back and forth from "instantaneous" to "continuous" 
action.  I've never seen this fully explored, nor have I seen a way to 
convey the complexity and richness of these shifts in English 
satisfactorily.  One translation I know of begins something like "When God 
was beginning to create the heavens and the earth, the earth being without 
form and void, ...", but I'm not sure that covers it completely.

> Also what day do you keep as the Sabbath? Sunday or the original Jewish 
> Saturday Sabbath ( actually sundown Friday evening until sundown Saturday 
> evening, since the creation days each consisted of an evening and a 
> morning)? If Sunday is the new Sabbath how do you explain that?

Technically, Sunday isn't the Sabbath, it's the Lord's Day.  The convention 
of celebrating both Resurrection and Pentecost on the first day of each week 
by "breaking of bread" is covered specifically in Acts 20:7, mentioned in 1 
Corinthians 16:1-2, appears to be implicit in Colossians 2:16 (which 
distinguishes it from the Sabbath), and the term "Lord's day" itself is used 
in Revelation 1:10.   I'm not at all convinced we should be enjoined from 
observing *both*.

> After all, the keeping of the Sabbath is one of the ten commandments i.e. 
> part of the law which supposedly cannot be changed.

Acts 15 (Council of Jerusalem).  It is not necessary for Gentile Christians 
to observe the *Jewish* laws.

> One last thing. Thou shalt not kill.

Most modern translations have "murder", not "kill", and Strong's seems to 
support that distinction.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
 
Thanks for posting that, Chuck.

I was surprised to find a great deal of congruence in our positions.

Some comments below...

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
news:do71lt$1jnq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
<snip>
> And having lived with condemnation all my life, I am now at the point 
> where I do the best I can from day to day to keep my thoughts and my 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> my transgressions.

I was sorry to see the opening clause; you have obviously recovered well and 
are dealing with it.

I was fortunate enough to have been raised in a close, loving, family where 
I received encouragement and direction, rather than condemnation. (I realise 
how lucky this was, and never take the love and wisdom of my parents (now 
both departed) for granted.)

Like you, I also do the best I can. The only difference is our motivations, 
and I don't think yours is "wrong". (It is wrong for me, but that's OK...)

Like you, I will be happy to give account at "God's great Judgement seat" 
(Kipling) if required to. (However, I don't expect to have to, so it has no 
bearing whatsoever on my behaviour in this life.)

> What the outcome will be is whatever the outcome will be at the time; I 
> don't focus on either the reward or the punishment, only in doing the best 
> I can to stay in alignment with what I believe  God's will for me to be on 
> a daily basis, not out of fear but out of love.

The only difference here is our motivation. The results are the same.You 
behave well through love of God; I do so through love of Man. I want a 
better world. (I'm sure you do too...)
.
> Sometimes I succeed, sometimes I don't, and when I don't, I regret it and 
> work to avoid it, again, out of love, not out of fear.

Slight difference here... I won't entertain shame, blame or regret. I see 
them as wasted emotions. When I screw up, I do everything I can to make it 
right. Take responsibility for what I did, do whatever I can to fix it and 
that's it. Move on, no grudges held, no recriminations, accusations, 
criticisms. I see no point in regretting what cannot be changed; learn from 
it. For the same reason I see no point in assigning blame (and I covered 
that at length in the "Management wars" thread here.) And shame is OK as a 
learning aid, but should not be revelled in... :-)) I work to fix things and 
learn from mistakes because it makes me a better person and helps me grow. 
Not because it is what God wants. But I do not dismiss ANY motivation that 
leads to better behaviour from someone.


>Whether that's good enough for God the Father is up to Him, and I'm willing 
>to accept what comes, be that eternal bliss, eternal torture, eternal 
>oblivion, or anything else.

Wow! That is a lot closer to my position than I would expect from a 
committed Christian...:-) My respect for you grows.


>  And yes, I rely on Jesus in the process.   I do not believe that reliance 
> is in vain because of my position on "sola fide".
>

Neither do I. If there is any justice in the Christian position I believe 
you will be fine... :-)

Pete.
```

```
Thanks for the response, Pete!

There's only one piece that I think requires further discussion:


>> Sometimes I succeed, sometimes I don't, and when I don't, I regret it and 
>> work to avoid it, again, out of love, not out of fear.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> right. Take responsibility for what I did, do whatever I can to fix it

That's the critical part.

> and that's it. Move on, no grudges held, no recriminations, accusations, 
> criticisms. I see no point in regretting what cannot be changed; learn 
> from it.

Try not to do it again, though!  "We do not regret the past, nor wish to 
shut the door on it."

> For the same reason I see no point in assigning blame (and I covered that 
> at length in the "Management wars" thread here.) And shame is OK as a 
> learning aid, but should not be revelled in... :-))

Yes.  I spent many years with that.

> I work to fix things and learn from mistakes because it makes me a better 
> person and helps me grow.

I agree.  And I think the proof that one is becoming a better person is, as 
they say, in the pudding.

> Not because it is what God wants. But I do not dismiss ANY motivation that 
> leads to better behaviour from someone.

And I've already cited Scripture supporting the idea that this was what's 
important anyhow.

>>Whether that's good enough for God the Father is up to Him, and I'm 
>>willing to accept what comes, be that eternal bliss, eternal torture, 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> committed Christian...:-) My respect for you grows.

"God grant me the serenity to accept the things I cannot change; the courage 
to change the things I can, and the wisdom to know the difference."  I can't 
do better than the best I can do.  As long as I strive to do that, the rest 
is beyond my control.  I can't change other people, places or things.  What 
I can change is me.   And I strive to do that, on a daily basis, in my case, 
as I said, praying *only* for the knowledge of His will for me and the power 
and the willingness to carry it out.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-12-21T12:03:01+13:00

- **References:** `<lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <1134415273.500876.207870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dnkm05$2niv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Itlnf.98582$Eq5.59918@pd7tw1no> <sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <RCDnf.146343$y_1.35785@edtnps89> <j22up1l7975en7ises32kh1ckieglsqqea@4ax.com> <vDLnf.7043$kP5.3641@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9kd0q1psli1hur0pcv1pld5dn12u26q11d@4ax.com> <1134587227.400842.107150@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <P34of.8513$kP5.6831@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <dns7dp$16ep$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Mi6pf.732$RZ6.42@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <do71lt$1jnq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
> When there's a perceived inconsistency between Paul's words and Jesus', as 
> a Christian I tend to put more weight on Jesus' and treat Paul's (in this 
> case, in conjunction with James') as carrying more weight.

This illustrates a fundamental difference in the way you and I look at 
Scripture. You see the Bible as the words of different people, some more or 
less 'right' than others. I see the Bible as a *coherent whole*, penned by 
some 40 authors, but all divinely orchestrated. When I see apparent conflict 
in Scripture, that tells me that one of two things is true: either there is 
a text/translational problem or I do not understand the complete context. As 
time passes, more and more of these apparent conflicts have been explained, 
as diligent study has uncovered reasonable contexts in which the 'conflicts' 
can be seen to be in harmony. One example is that a completely harmonious 
chronology of the Gospels has been constructed, in which all the events 
depicted in the Gospels can be seen to occur in chronological harmony 
without conflict, where once this did not appear to be the case. To me, this 
progressive clarification and removal of apparent conflicts serves to 
validate my position. :-)

>> To simply relay what the Bible clearly says is not arrogance.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> thousand years strikes me as a *bit* on the arrogant side!

I can't help that. :-) The fact is, if you take the interpretive course that 
you say that the Catholic Church has done, you will unavoidably do damage to 
the doctrine of salvation by grace alone, because grace, by very definition, 
and by clear and unambiguous Scriptural declaration, precludes works. Either 
salvation is by grace alone *and* faith alone, or it is neither, and the 
Bible contradicts itself. There simply is no other valid logical position. 
What you see to be a conflict between Paul and James on this issue can 
easily be explained by understanding that it from two perspectives, Paul 
from a strictly legal definition (which is what I am discussing), and James 
who is concerned with the practical issue of believers thinking works are 
not important. Given the unavoidable conflict and damage resulting from one 
interpretation, and the very reasonable and consistent result from the other 
interpretation, this is why I am so adamant on the issue. There is simply no 
reason to purposely choose an interpretation that leads to conflict.

>  Judgment is *not* synonymous with condemnation.

It depends on the particular word being translated as "judgment". In some 
places the original language means 'judge' in the sense of 'evaluate'. In 
other places the original language means 'judge' in the sense of 'condemn'.
```

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

I don't have a Strongs handy, but here is Vines New Testament on 
"judgement":

judgment

1. krisis (krivsi" , (2920)) primarily denotes a separating, then, a 
decision, judgment, most frequently in a forensic sense, and especially of 
Divine judgment. For the variety of its meanings, with references, see 
Condemnation, B, No. 3.

Notes: (1) The Holy Spirit, the Lord said, would convict the world of (peri, 
in respect of), i.e., of the actuality of, God's judgment, John 16:8, 11. 
Cp. 2 Thess. 1:5. (2) In Rom. 2:5 the word dikaiokrisia, "righteous 
judgment," combines the adjective dikaios, righteous, with krisis, the two 
words which are used separately in 2 Thess. 1:5.�

2. krima (krivma , (2917)) denotes the result of the action signified by the 
verb krino�, to judge; for its general significance see Condemnation, B, No. 
1: it is used (a) of a decision passed on the faults of others, Matt. 7:2; 
(b) of judgment by man upon Christ, Luke 24:20; (c) of God's judgment upon 
men, e.g., Rom. 2:2, 3; 3:8; 5:16; 11:33; 13:2; 1 Cor. 11:29; Gal. 5:10; 
Heb. 6:2; Jas. 3:1; through Christ, e.g., John 9:39; (d) of the right of 
judgment, Rev. 20:4; (e) of a law-suit, 1 Cor. 6:7.

3. he�mera (hJmevra , (2250)), a day, is translated "judgment" in 1 Cor. 
4:3, where "man's judgment" (lit., 'man's day,' marg.) is used of the 
present period in which man's mere judgment is exercised, a period of human 
rebellion against God. The adjective anthro�pinos, human, belonging to man 
(anthro�pos), is doubtless set in contrast here to kuriakos, belonging to 
the Lord (kurios, a lord), which is used in the phrase "the Day of the 
Lord," in Rev. 1:10, "The Lord's Day," a period of Divine judgments. See 
Day.

4. gno�me� (gnwvmh , (1106)), primarily a means of knowing (akin to 
gino�sko�, to know), came to denote a mind, understanding; hence (a) a 
purpose, Acts 20:3, lit., '(it was his) purpose;' (b) a royal purpose, a 
decree, Rev. 17:17, R.V., "mind" (A.V., "will"); (c) judgment, opinion, 1 
Cor. 1:10, "(in the same) judgment;" Rev. 17:13, "mind;" (d) counsel, 
advice, 1 Cor. 7:25, "(I give my) judgment;" 7:40, "(after my) judgment;" 
Philm. 14, "mind." See Mind, Purpose, Will.�

Notes: (1) In 1 Cor. 6:4, A.V., krite�rion, a tribunal, is rendered 
"judgments" (R.V., "to judge," marg., "tribunals"). See Judge, B, No. 3, 
Note (1). (2) In Rom. 1:32, A.V., dikaio�ma, an ordinance, righteous act, is 
translated "judgment" (R.V. "ordinance"); in Rev. 15:4, "judgments" (R.V., 
"righteous acts"). (3) In Acts 25:15, A.V., katadike�, a sentence, 
condemnation, is translated "judgment" (R.V., "sentence"). Some mss. have 
dike�. See Sentence. (4) In Phil. 1:9, A.V., aisthe�sis, perception, 
discernment, is translated "judgment" (R.V., "discernment"). (5) In Acts 
21:25, in the record of the decree from the Apostles and elders at Jerusalem 
to the churches of the Gentiles, the verb krino� (see Judge, B, No. 1), is 
translated "giving judgement," R.V. (A.V., "concluded").

B. Adjective.

hupodikos (uJpovdiko" , (5267)), brought to trial, answerable to (hupo, 
under, dike�, justice), Rom. 3:19, is translated "under the judgement," R.V. 
(A.V., "guilty").�
```

- **References:** `<lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <1134415273.500876.207870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dnkm05$2niv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Itlnf.98582$Eq5.59918@pd7tw1no> <sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <RCDnf.146343$y_1.35785@edtnps89> <j22up1l7975en7ises32kh1ckieglsqqea@4ax.com> <vDLnf.7043$kP5.3641@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9kd0q1psli1hur0pcv1pld5dn12u26q11d@4ax.com> <1134587227.400842.107150@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <P34of.8513$kP5.6831@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <dns7dp$16ep$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Mi6pf.732$RZ6.42@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <do71lt$1jnq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <bG4qf.20411$k76.18201@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <doc44m$1jls$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <pejqf.16233$kP5.1938@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:pejqf.16233$kP5.1938@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> "judgement":
....

The numbers are actually the same as Strong's.

#2920, "krisis", is primarily about the act of deciding between, not about 
preordained condemnation.

#2917, "krima", is about the results of that decision, usually negative 
("sentence" in the judicial sense is included in its semantics).

#2250, "hemera", is a metaphoric use in this context.

#1106, "gnome", is more appropriately "reason", "knowledge" (e.g., 
"gnostics").

#5267, "hupodikos", is used exactly once in the KJV and is translated 
"guilty" there.

The root of the words I was thinking of is #2919, "krino", determine, 
resolve, prefer, form an opinion.  #2917, "krima" is the *result* of a 
judgment, most commonly in the negative sense.  #2920, "krisis", is the act 
described by "krino".  #2921, "kriterion", is the means of determining among 
alternatives, the matter being judged.  #2922, "kritis", and #2923, 
"krites", seem to be synonyms:  an arbiter; an administer of justice.  And 
#2924, "kritikos" is one skilled in discerning.

The primary meaning of "krino" and its derivations is *deciding*, and only 
in the case of "krima" do I see it having a fundamentally negative 
connotation.

For all the 28 instances of "krima" that Strong reports, I would contend 
that there are 5 among the cases in which it does *not* have a negative 
connotation:  John 9:39 (where the NAB has "I came into this world to divide 
it"); Acts 24:25 ("As Paul talked about uprightness, continence and the 
coming judgment ..."); Romans 11:33 ("How inscrutable are his judgments, how 
unsearchable his ways!"); Hebrews 6:2 ("instruction about baptisms and 
laying-on of hands, resurrection of the dead and and eternal judgment"); and 
1 Peter 4:17 ("The season of judgment has begun, and begun with God's own 
household.").  I agree that the remaining instances of "krima" have the 
connotation of the imposition of punishment.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:gwLnf.7012$kP5.3135@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
>> AFAIK, only Buddhism has an officially state policy of changing itself 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's interesting, I didn't know that about Buddhists.

    The Dalai Lama wrote the following as a foreword to the book 
"Destructive Emotions: How Can We Overcome Them?"

<quote>
I have often said that if science proves facts that conflict with Buddhist 
understanding, Buddhism must change accordingly. We should always adopt a 
view that accords with the facts... However, a clear distinction should be 
made between what is not found by science and what is found to be 
nonexistent by science. What science finds to be nonexistent we should all 
accept as nonexistent, but what science merely does not find is a completely 
different matter. An example is consciousness itself. Although sentient 
beings, including humans, have experienced consciousness for centuries, we 
still do not know what consciousness actually is: its complete nature and 
how it functions.

With the ever-growing impact of science on our lives, religion and 
spirituality have a greater role to play in reminding us of our humanity. 
What we must do is balance scientific and material progress with the sense 
of responsibility that comes of inner development. That is why I believe 
this dialogue between religion and science is important, for from it may 
come developments that can be of great benefit to mankind.
</quote>

Taken from http://mednews.stanford.edu/stanmed/2005fall/dalai-lama.html

    - Oliver
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> question the validity of Christianity.

Quite simply - can't answer that one; can only make a possible 
observation - I've got my eyes wide open Richard. (Chuck with his 
background or Skippy might chip in).

First having translated from original Greek texts into Latin the latter 
became sacred documents with a given consistency. (Whether there was a 
Latin Bible Version I, followed by Version II etc., I just don't know - 
and frankly, I don't care either). The clerics felt that the 'faithful' 
(the uneducated peasants, if you like), should have an interpreter - 
hence half way through the catholic mass the priest would give a homily 
in the vernacular, quoting from the Bible, Old and New.

I'm not aware that any were 'forbidden' or even discouraged from reading 
the bible - assuming they had a good grasp of Latin. But just as the 
Vatican of the times reacted unfavourably to the thoughts of Galileo, 
same reaction set in with Bible translations. The latter probably with 
some *slight* justification, i.e. were the translations being offered in 
the vernacular, true interpretations. It's worth remembering that Henry 
VIII, England's Protestant 'Apostle' was not happy with translations 
into English.

I just re-read you - yes I think it possibly did come from a lust for 
power. Knowledge (hidden), is power. Did you ever see the movie starring 
Sean Connery as a medieval monk, 'The Name of the Rose' - that 
illustrates it very well.

Similarly, I have nothing but contempt for bean-counters I have met in 
my computing career, who being Luddites or power-seekers, attempted to 
do snow jobs to hide facts - to either not have things computerized or 
to try and ensure that systems got designed to their particular 
double-entry 'beliefs'.

Still nothing changes - with the same closed minds, as we have seen from 
this and associated threads, having arrived at a conclusion, there are 
some that think poor old Joe McCarthy should be canonized to become St. 
Joseph :-)

Jimmy
```

- **Message-ID:** `<dnncat$1a94$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <43923888.E48F76B3@mts.net> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134306865.212753.114390@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <1134415273.500876.207870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dnkm05$2niv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Itlnf.98582$Eq5.59918@pd7tw1no> <sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <_GEnf.100562$ki.98036@pd7tw2no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:_GEnf.100562$ki.98036@pd7tw2no...

> First having translated from original Greek texts into Latin the latter 
> became sacred documents with a given consistency. (Whether there was a 
> Latin Bible Version I, followed by Version II etc., I just don't know - 
> and frankly, I don't care either).

There were early Latin versions, but St. Jerome's effort to go back to the 
best and oldest Greek texts of his time (ca. 347AD - 11/30/420) in the 
Vulgate lead to the fact that that translation actually represents an 
earlier version of the New Testament than most of the Greek versions, until 
the discovery and availability of the Great Uncial Codices.

> I'm not aware that any were 'forbidden' or even discouraged from reading 
> the bible - assuming they had a good grasp of Latin.

They were, however, discouraged from reading the orignal Greek, Hebrew and 
Aramaic.

>  But just as the Vatican of the times reacted unfavourably to the thoughts 
> of Galileo, same reaction set in with Bible translations.

The Douai translation was produced in 1568 in response to the Protestant 
translations, which were often used in polemics and which the Church held 
contained anti-Catholic biases.  The Council of Trent (1565) held that it 
was the *Latin Vulgate* that was inspired; this position seems to have been 
recanted by Pius XII (as I indicated elsewhere in this thread).  The 
Confraternity translation of the Old Testament (to accompany its published 
revision of the New, 1941) had begun before Divino Afflante Spiritu, but 
shelved in favor of the original New American Bible translation.

> The latter probably with some *slight* justification, i.e. were the 
> translations being offered in the vernacular, true interpretations.

In particular, there were a fair number of anti-Catholic remarks in the 
margins of published translations of the Bible back in this period.  That's 
one of the reasons the Church elected to produce its own.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<dnncat$1a94$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134306865.212753.114390@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <1134415273.500876.207870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dnkm05$2niv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Itlnf.98582$Eq5.59918@pd7tw1no> <sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <_GEnf.100562$ki.98036@pd7tw2no> <dnncat$1a94$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
> news:_GEnf.100562$ki.98036@pd7tw2no...
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Thanks for the background Chuck.

Judson - I did a check-up on Tyndale. Not the first to suffer at the 
burning stake.

John Wycliffe (1324 - 1384) did the first translation into English 
between 1381-84 which was distributed in the UK by the Lollards. Died in 
his bed. That didn't stop the then Pope from exacting his revenge. He 
had John dug up and his bones burned !

Henry VII (1457 - 1509). Henry Tudor became king in 1485 having defeated 
Richard III (Richard Duke of York) at Bosworth (ROYGBIV - the way we 
remembered the colour spectrum at school, 'Richard of York Gave Battle 
In Vain'). He had seven children. Seeking an alliance to counter the 
French his eldest son Prince Arthur, (1486 - 1502) was betrothed and 
married to Catherine of Arragon. Arthur died shortly after his marriage.

Catherine of Aragon (1495 - 1536), youngest surviving daughter of 
Ferdinand and Isabella of Spain, betrothed to Arthur, (she was 3 he was 
2) and  married Arthur in 1501. She was betrothed some 14 months after 
Arthur's death to his younger brother Henry VIII and they were married 
in 1509. Subsequent annulment/divorce in 1533.

Henry VIII (1491 - 1547), King in 1509. Prior to the marriage to 
Catherine both the Spanish and English royal families sought a papal 
dispensation that the 'remarriage' of a widow who was Henry's 
sister-in-law did not conflict with the biblical Leviticus text. This 
was also based on Catherine's assertion, which she continued to make 
right up until her death, that her marriage to Arthur had not been 
consummated.

Henry was almost 18 when he became king, young, athletic and 
good-looking, not the corpulent mess we normally visualize. Seems 
Catherine had some 6 or 7 pregnancies only the 5th resulting in the 
birth of Princess Mary (Bloody Mary) - wasn't for want of trying, was it ?

Henry obsessed with obtaining a male heir, and bearing in mind the path 
his father took to the throne, cast his eye around the Court. Catherine 
was now 42 - and certainly in that age very probably incapable of 
further conception. He saw Anne Boleyn (Bullen) a relative of one of his 
former mistresses. So now he sought an annulment of the marriage from 
the Pope, giving his Chancellor Cardinal Wolsey, the tricky task of 
achieving it. Henry 'had got religion', and realized the error of his 
ways in having married his brother's widow - apparently Leviticus says 
such a marriage 'will be childless'. Well hardly, since his first wife 
had given birth to the Princess Mary !

Initially kept in the dark Catherine appealed to her nephew, Charles V, 
the Holy Roman Emperor, who could put 'the squeeze' on the pope. The 
political and legal debate continued for six years but came to a head in 
1533 when Anne Boleyn became pregnant (with the future Elizabeth I).

.................
William Tyndale (1494 - 1536). Around this time Tyndale becomes one of 
the background players, already having established himself as a 
theologian. He had already made some English clerics very uncomfortable 
and eventually sought refuge in Hamburg in 1524. The first copies of his 
translations were circulated in England in 1526.

While the English church authorities could make no case for the Bible 
not being published in the vernacular, they nevertheless were very 
uneasy with the outcome. Tyndale's New Testament carried a great deal of 
comment. Tunstall and Archbishop Warham denounced it; so did Thomas 
More, who was against every aspect of Luther's Reformation. Wolsey 
demanded Tyndale's arrest as a heretic.

In 1530 Tyndale wrote 'The Practice of Prelates' which was a criticism 
of Henry's divorce, opposing the grounds on which the annulment was 
sought. This ensured the wrath of Henry who asked the Emperor Charles V 
to have Tyndale seized and returned to England. He went into hiding 
until he was betrayed, strangled and burned in 1536.

..................
Back to Henry VIII -
Only one solution - 'if you can't join them, then beat them'. He had 
already dumped Wolsey because of his ineffectiveness replacing him by 
Thomas More as his Chancellor, and Thomas Cranmer, (1489 - 1556) as 
Archbishop of Canterbury. Henry introduced the Act of Supremacy in 1534 :-

"Albeit, the King's Majesty justly and rightfully is and oweth to be the 
supreme head of the Church of England, and so is recognised by the 
clergy of this realm in their Convocations; yet nevertheless for 
corroboration and confirmation thereof, and for increase of virtue in 
Christ's religion within this realm of England, and to repress and 
extirp all errors, heresies and other enormities and abuses heretofore 
used in the same, Be it enacted by authority of this present Parliament 
that the King our sovereign lord, his heirs and successors kings of this 
realm, shall be taken, accepted and reputed the only supreme head in 
earth of the Church of England called Anglicana Ecclesia,...........etc."

He then presented his statesmen/bishops with the above Act, "Sign 
here..... or else'. Chancellor Thomas More and Bishop of Rochester John 
Fisher took the 'or else' option - and had their heads lopped off in 
1535. Meanwhile Henry allowed his 'trustworthy nobles' to share in the 
spoils - the dissolution of the monasteries.

1539 - Now Supreme Head of the Church, Henry had a change of heart about 
theology and via Cranmer now introduced the Great Bible, produced by one 
of Tyndale's followers, Myles Coverdale.

.............................................................
I was aware of the name Tyndale which for me has about the same 
significance as the catholic or Douai/Douay version of the bible. It was 
researching Tyndale that I find his life occurs during the Henrican 
period; I had assumed 'having been burned at the stake', this might have 
been one of Mary's victims.

Was he burned at the stake for religious beliefs - a definite YES ! 
However read above - he also managed to piss off his sovereign lord with 
his criticisms of the first divorce and through the Tudor dynasty that 
was a big-time crime exceeding anything you could come up with in 
theology. I got lucky, the first or second source made reference to the 
criticism of Henry. So now I back-check on others sites, where in the 
21st century I see red flags like 'Romist' or 'Church of Rome' - those 
are already sending me a signal - in what is supposed to be an age of 
ecumenism. (If nobody is getting the message we are quite happy if you 
use the word 'Catholic' when referring to us). I look further, they all 
stress Tyndale's martyrdom, but just ONE mentioned he pissed off the king !

..........................................................................
If there's one salutary lesson from the above - don't mix religion and 
politics. Using that very annoying phrase, which however does seem 
pertinent here - 'What goes around, comes around' - the Founding Fathers 
in the United States were adamant your Constitution should be framed 
specifically to separate Church and State. Perhaps more accurately they 
should have been referred to as your 'Wise Fathers'. So to complete the 
loop - next time you and your Pentecostal brethren want to do a holding 
of hands with a quick prayer over a cup of coffee in a shopping mall - 
might just be more discrete to do the prayer thing in the cleaner's 
closet for brooms and dustpans - then get back to the coffee table.

PS: I'm almost reluctant to admit it, but I found a reference to 
'Catholicism for Dummies' - can you just imagine Richard's reaction when 
he reads this :-)

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Religion

_(reply depth: 52)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-12-15T05:18:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F07of.111139$ki.24485@pd7tw2no>`

- **References:** `<3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134306865.212753.114390@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <1134415273.500876.207870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dnkm05$2niv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Itlnf.98582$Eq5.59918@pd7tw1no> <sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <_GEnf.100562$ki.98036@pd7tw2no> <dnncat$1a94$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <qdMnf.107856$Gd6.102984@pd7tw3no> <1134600025.234235.243040@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Alistair wrote:
> James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Martyrdom usually implies 'dying for one's faith'. That's not the case 
with Thomas.

He and Henry II were good boozing and whoring buddies. Henry wanted to 
control the hierarchy, so with great reluctance Thomas was fast-tracked 
to become the Archbishop of Canterbury. Once wearing the Canterbury 
mitre he too 'got religion' and sided with the bishops particularly with 
reference to Church Courts as opposed to Crown Courts. It all gets very 
complicated - but Thomas was challenging Henry's authority as king.

In sheer exasperation it is claimed Henry uttered the words, "Who will 
rid me of this troublesome priest ?". Thy wish is but my command - four 
of his knights/barons took him up on his comment, went off to Canterbury 
  and murdered Thomas.

Hardly grounds for martyrdom - nevertheless he was eventually canonized.

Jimmy
```

```
Alistair wrote:
> James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Not the first by any means, and probably not the last. Knowing of the 
'Confessions of St Augustine' (of Hippo/Algeria/Carthage), went checking 
to see if I could get the dirt on him. I imagined a life of complete 
debauchery. Apparently that's not quite the case from the following, but 
he sure had his oats before getting religion :-

http://ccat.sas.upenn.edu/jod/augustine.html

I can't immediately think of modernist examples - but possibly somebody 
else can.

Not quite in the same vein - think of Saul/St. Paul of Tarsus, Turkey. 
Essentially a Jewish quisling, (Hellenized Jew and Roman citizen). With 
the blessing probably of the Sanhedrin, accompanied Roman 
Christian-hunters on their expeditions. Whether one accepts or not that 
he had a vision on the road to Damascus, he sure had a complete change 
of heart and became the Apostle of the Gentiles.

Jimmy
```

```
re Thomas a Beckett:

This quote, stating that TaB is officially one of the top 10 worst
Britons of all time, comes from BBC news:


<QUOTE>"The "greedy" Thomas Becket, Archbishop of Canterbury, was
nominated by Professor John Hudson, of St Andrews University, as the
12th century's worst villain.
"He divided England in a way that even many churchmen who shared some
of his views thought unnecessary and self-indulgent," he said.
"He was a founder of gesture politics.
"Those who share my prejudice against Becket may consider his
assassination in Canterbury Cathedral on 29 December, 1170, a fittingly
grisly end." </QUOTE>
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
> Henry 'had got religion', and realized the error of his ways in having 
> married his brother's widow - apparently Leviticus says such a marriage 
> 'will be childless'.

Actually, the Mosaic Law, of which Leviticus is a part, teaches quite the 
reverse. When a man died, leaving a childless widow, his eldest brother was 
instructed to marry the widow and have children with her. This was to give a 
home for the widow, and to provide her with children who would be heirs to 
her dead husband (inheritance was through the male line). Jesus was asked a 
trick question about this (Matthew 22:23-33). Even before Moses, a man named 
Onan was killed by God for using coitus interruptus to prevent his brother's 
widow, whom he had been forced to marry by his father Judah, from bearing 
heirs to his brother (Genesis 38:8-10). Sexual activity that avoids 
conception is still called "Onanism".

> Was he burned at the stake for religious beliefs - a definite YES ! 
> However read above - he also managed to piss off his sovereign lord with 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tyndale's martyrdom, but just ONE mentioned he pissed off the king !


Thanks, Jimmy, that was very interesting and informative. :-)
```

- **Message-ID:** `<dnna1a$18s8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Bpkf.51276$6y4.13244@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <43923888.E48F76B3@mts.net> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134306865.212753.114390@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <1134415273.500876.207870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dnkm05$2niv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Itlnf.98582$Eq5.59918@pd7tw1no> <sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

> Different English translations don't seem an issue to me. What puzzles me 
> is why Catholics used to be discouraged, if not forbidden, from reading 
> the Bible.

"Sola scriptura" was never part of Roman Catholic doctrine, and the church 
feared that for the laity to read scripture *alone* without the proper 
training to understand the traditions that underlay the Church's teachings 
would lead to heresy.

> John Tyndale was even burned at the stake for making the first English 
> translation of the Bible.

Among other things.

> How does one say they believe in the Bible, yet forbid or even discourage 
> others form reading it?

Answered above.  Both Tradition and Scripture (which includes what 
Protestants know as the Apocrypha, BTW) are the underpinnings of Roman 
Catholic doctrine, and the premise behind the Pope's right to declare 
doctrinal matters like the Assumption and the Immaculate Conception of the 
BVM, to say nothing of the doctrine of papal infallibility.

> It makes me think the motivation came more from human lust for power than 
> any Christian motivation. It doesn't surprise me when non-believers see 
> such things and question the validity of Christianity.

Such is the conclusion to which you choose to come.  Others might walk 
different cause-and-effect logical paths.  I've seen *lots* of actions taken 
by those who have, and have exercized, the right to call themselves 
Christians that could easily lead me to believe that if those actions are 
representative of the fruits of being Christian, then I want no part of it, 
and those actions are a whole lot more offensive than maintaining "tradition 
and scripture" as a fundamental premise for nigh onto two millenia.

>               Anyone can stand up and claim "I'm a Christian!", proceed to 
> live completely in opposition to what Jesus taught, then the word accepts 
> without question their claim of being a Christian, instead of judging 
> their claim against their acts.

More to the point, Judson:  Anyone can stand up and claim "I'm a Christian", 
and truly believe that he's living in absolutely perfect harmony with what 
Jesus taught, regardless of how he actually behaves and how well his 
behavior maps onto *my* understanding or *your* understanding of what Jesus 
taught.  All *I* can say about such a person is "If *his understanding* of 
Christianity is correct, then I want no part of Christianity".   I am more 
likely to be of the opinion that such actions are not indicative of a 
Christian perspective *as I understand it*, and based on my own conscience I 
would elect not to engage in them.

> What Jesus actually taught is there for anyone to read.

And interpret as best he can.  The danger is in telling somebody *else* what 
*the correct* interpretation is, and conclude that *they* need to change 
*their* behavior to match your expectations and your interpretation.

> Anyone can act out of character on isolated occasion, but anyone's claim 
> of being a Christian should be taken as specious,

Anyone who makes a big noise about being Christian invites intense scrutiny 
of their behavior at the outset.   Let the behavior speak for itself.    If 
someone "sees the spark" in you, they'll ask your perspective.

> when they continue to live and act in a manner entirely different from 
> what Jesus taught, rather than bashing the whole of Christianity. At least 
> that would be intellectually honest.

Well, again, an example.

I know people who claim the Gospels are full of Jesus' condemnations of 
homosexual activity, but I've looked high and low, and the only *clear* 
references I find are in the Old Testament and the (genuine and attributed) 
writings of Paul.

I also know of several denominations within Christianity (in particular 
within Protestantism) that have no problem with gay people as active members 
of the congregations and even, in some cases, in the clergy.

Jesus' references to Sodom and Gomorrha, in context, indicate *to me* that 
the heinous sin that the residents of that city was about *inhospitability 
to strangers* (Matt. 10:14-15, 11:20-24; Mark 6:11; Luke 10:8-10).  Luke 
17:29 is the only other reference to Sodom in the Gospels, and it's silent 
on the subject.

That doesn't mean I choose to engage in homosexual behavior, but I think 
those who held up signs at Matthew Shepard's funeral reading the likes of 
"God hates fags" and those who count the days that he's been in hell, are 
engaging in some pretty hateful and non-Christian behavior.   In fact, you 
wanna know the sort of thing that gives Christianity a bad name?  Check out 
the website of the Westboro Baptist Church of Topeka Kansas: 
www.godhatesfags.com .

I believe the constitution *guarantees* these folks the right to decree what 
being a *real* Christian is.  It grants me and you the same right.  I'm 
going to have to trust *my* conscience rather than *their* press releases in 
determining whether or not I agree that Katrina was clearly God's wrath on 
New Orleans and is therefore cause for rejoicing.

The same applies to *my* understanding of the Bible -- and I've done a fair 
bit of studying of it -- and *your* understanding of the Bible.  I have the 
right to come to my understanding, and *I* do not grant *you* (or anyone 
else) the right to claim that my understanding is inadequate in God's eyes. 
The refinement of my understanding of Scripture is between me and God and 
those *I* choose as my guides in that area.

To make the direct or implicit claim that you have a better grasp on *any* 
subject -- to say nothing of "*I* am the one that *really* understands the 
Bible, whereas you, you poor schlub ..." is behavior that does not strike me 
as evidentiary of the fundamental humility the New Testament leads me to 
believe characterizes Christians.

By the way, it looks to me like the audience for the Great Commission in 
Matthew 28:19 was eleven people.  It's not clear to *me* that when Jesus 
directed the *apostles* to do something that that direction applies in the 
same way, to the same degree, to *me*.   You may claim that right for 
yourself, but doing so that leads me to the reasonable conclusion that you 
rank yourself spiritually with the Apostles.

I'm reminded of the joke from the twelve-step community relative to Step 4: 
"I've done a searching and fearless moral inventory of myself, and have come 
to the conclusion that my excessive humility is my greatest flaw."

    -Chuck Stevens
```
