```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> Jesus' references to Sodom and Gomorrha, in context, indicate *to me* that 
> the heinous sin that the residents of that city was about *inhospitability 
> to strangers* (Matt. 10:14-15, 11:20-24; Mark 6:11; Luke 10:8-10).

I think the problem was that they wanted to get a little *too* friendly, 
Chuck. :-)

> That doesn't mean I choose to engage in homosexual behavior, but I think 
> those who held up signs at Matthew Shepard's funeral reading the likes of 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> www.godhatesfags.com .

I'm not even going to that website, the URL is enough. The Bible declares 
that God loves all people ("It is not God's will that any should perish"). 
Though the Bible does condemn the sin of homosexuality, the Bible condemns 
all sin. The Bible also teaches that we should hate all sin, but we should 
love the sinner. Hating a person because they practice a particular sin is 
not Biblical. On a practical level there are some sins that can be more 
damaging to a person or a family than others. From what I have read and 
heard from people who have been involved in them, homosexuality and 
involvement in the occult seem to be two of the most difficult things to 
'break free', and stay free, from. But I don't think God looks at sin the 
way people often do, considering one sin a little thing and another sin a 
big thing. God's standard is absolute perfection. No human other than Jesus 
ever lived up to that standard, the rest of us are simply sinners, we can 
take God's way out, or perish.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 50)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-14T09:52:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dnpm44$2mp7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134306865.212753.114390@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <1134415273.500876.207870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dnkm05$2niv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Itlnf.98582$Eq5.59918@pd7tw1no> <sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dnna1a$18s8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o6Mnf.7170$kP5.5404@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:o6Mnf.7170$kP5.5404@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> I think the problem was that they wanted to get a little *too* friendly, 
> Chuck. :-)

I view the Genesis 19 story (and its nearly-exact parallel in Judges 19) as 
analogous to prison rape, and it's about power trips, showing the visitor's 
who's in charge.  More on other passages below.

> Though the Bible does condemn the sin of homosexuality,

What exactly do you mean by "the sin of homosexuality"?   As I see it, a 
celibate homosexual is no less nor more a sinner than a celibate 
heterosexual.   The adjective "homosexual" has only one definition in 
Webster's Ninth:  "of, relating to, or characterized by a tendency to direct 
sexual desire toward another of the same sex", and the noun "homosexual" has 
no separate definition.  The *primary* definition of "homosexuality" is "the 
quality or state of being homosexual", and only secondarily is used to 
connote sexual *activity*.

And I've taken the time to study closely all of the passages that I've heard 
described as condemning homosexuality and homosexual activity in all its 
forms in the Bible, in numerous translations and in the original languages: 
besides Genesis 19 and Judges 19 cited above, I know of Leviticus 18:22 and 
20:13; Deuteronomy 23:18-19; Isaiah 3:9;  Romans 1:26-27; 1 Corinthians 6:9; 
and 1 Timothy 1:10.

And while each of these (with the probable exception of Isaiah 3:9) can 
indeed be taken to discuss individual acts within, or aspects of, sexual 
activities and relationships between two people of the same sex, I do not 
see any of them, taken individually or collectively, describing, much less 
condemning, the entire spectrum of intimate relationships involving same-sex 
sexual activity.

A specification of avoidance of a particular homosexual act as a matter of 
ritual purity among Jews is not a condemnation of homosexuality.  A 
condemnation of temple prostitution, specifically whether same-sex or 
opposite-sex, is not a condemnation of homosexual activity in particular.  A 
condemnation of idolatry and the practices engaged in by pagans is not a 
condemnation of homosexual activity as such.  A condemnation of the cultural 
acceptance of the rich and powerful man having a "kept boy" who exchanges 
sexual favors for the right to "mix" with the rest of the rich and powerful 
is not a condemnation of homosexual activity.

I am aware of no other passages that can be taken to relate to homosexual 
activity or even the state of being correctly characterized as homosexual.

I am aware that Acts 15:29 does *not* include it in the list of things that 
Christians are to avoid (with specific reference to the ritual purity 
proscriptions in Leviticus).

Besides these, what passages do you have in mind?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 51)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-14T14:07:19-06:00

- **Message-ID:** `<CV_nf.8439$kP5.8270@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134306865.212753.114390@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <1134415273.500876.207870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dnkm05$2niv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Itlnf.98582$Eq5.59918@pd7tw1no> <sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dnna1a$18s8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o6Mnf.7170$kP5.5404@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <dnpm44$2mp7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
> proscriptions in Leviticus).

Sin is of the heart, Chuck (Matthew 5:21,22,28). Jesus said that to hate is 
equivalent to murder, and to have lust is equivalent to adultery. Would the 
same thing not apply regarding homosexuals? I don't think God forbids 
homosexuality simply for ritual purity. Homosexuality is declared as "an 
abomination" and "a perversion", right along with sex with animals. It 
appears to me that God considers these things to be perversions of His 
natural order, and as such are forbidden. God provides a legitimate outlet 
for sexual desires, and that outlet is within a marriage between a man and a 
woman. I see no implication anywhere in the Bible that God is in favor of 
any kind of sexual activity outside normal heterosexual marriage.

On the other hand, we all have sins. I'm not sure why homosexuality has been 
so vehemently targeted. But even something so common today as divorce has 
long been a sore point among Christians. Many churches have made poor 
divorced women virtual (even literal) outcasts for divorcing unfaithful 
husbands (or vice versa), making divorce a virtual Unpardonable Sin. 
Fortunately, this intolerance for divorced people is less common today, 
among Protestants at least, and many churches have support programs for 
divorcees.
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> Sin is of the heart, Chuck (Matthew 5:21,22,28). Jesus said that to hate is
> equivalent to murder, and to have lust is equivalent to adultery. Would the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

It seems to me that you have ignored Chuck's arguments and considered
explanations of the scriptures whereby he pointed out that only one
passage among many referring to homosexuality actually was in
condemnation. All other passages seemed to consider the circumstances
surrounding the homosexuality to be the sin and not the homosexuality
itself.

>
> On the other hand, we all have sins. I'm not sure why homosexuality has been
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> divorcees.

Interesting that you bring up divorce. The Catholic church has
condemned divorce amongst the common man and yet insists that any
married would be priests abandon their wives before training as a
priest. Read the 'Vicars of Christ' by Peter de Rosa. He covers the
notion of celibacy in detail (apparently priests were once allowed to
be married but were not allowed to indulge themselves with their wife.
They were allowed to indulge with boys and housekeepers (I'm not
referring to the recent events in Boston)).

> --
```

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dnq9hs$vl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134306865.212753.114390@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <1134415273.500876.207870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <dnkm05$2niv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Itlnf.98582$Eq5.59918@pd7tw1no> <sxBnf.4852$w7.4489@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <dnna1a$18s8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o6Mnf.7170$kP5.5404@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <dnpm44$2mp7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <CV_nf.8439$kP5.8270@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <1134600824.260758.276870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1134600824.260758.276870@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...

> Interesting that you bring up divorce. The Catholic church has
> condemned divorce amongst the common man and yet insists that any
> married would be priests abandon their wives before training as a
> priest.

Only in that portion of the Church known as the "Latin Rite".  A number of 
groups that had departed during the Western Schism rejoined the church, and 
retained the custom of the married priesthood with Rome's concurrence; I 
believe Ukrainian and Maronite Rite priests (among others) are allowed to be 
married, and to marry, and they're under Rome's authority, but not "Latin 
Rite".

Despite the most-recently-deceased pope's protestations to the contrary, all 
the evidence I've seen is that the celibate priesthood is a *custom* that 
arose out of the need for priests to avoid the distractions of family so 
that they can devote themselves to church duties.   The defense that neither 
Jesus nor any of the Apostles appear to have been married and thus priests 
must follow their example came, I believe, much later.  It's been during my 
lifetime that this convention has escalated from "church custom" into the 
realm of  "doctrine", largely through the efforts of JPII.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
> condemned divorce amongst the common man and yet insists that any
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> referring to the recent events in Boston)).

The Catholic church does not require priests in all countries to be 
celibate. Many (if not most) priests in Africa, for example, are married by 
sanction of the church. I've never understood the Catholic injunction 
against priests and nuns being married. The Bible makes it clear that Peter 
was married, Jesus even healed Peter's mother-in-law. If the Pope's 
authority is handed down from Peter, and Peter was married and carried his 
wife around with him, why not priests and nuns? Puzzling.
```

```
Alistair wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Unfortunately this is one yet again where Judson goes off half-cocked. 
Go googling if interested looking for other sources, non-catholic which 
may counter this article, but try this one for starters on Celibacy from 
the on-line Catholic Encyclopedia :-

http://www.newadvent.org/cathen/03481a.htm

It lays down the principles for the Western Church and differentiates 
from the Eastern Church/Rite, pus later in the article talks about the 
Oriental Church.

Lost my good Scottish friend late October and appear to have lost a good 
Romanian friend as well. Haven't seen him walking his dog since May. The 
latter has been in Canada since late seventies having escaped from 
Romanian Communism. To the best of my knowledge he was an agnostic but 
did tell me that Romanian Orthodox priests were encouraged to get 
married, particularly where they would be 'stationed' at remote 
villages. However, he also said that such married priests were never 
elevated to the hierarchy - bishops as such were always appointed from 
celibates/monasteries.

The wikpedia has a good article too.

There's an article by Ray Groswith MA M.Div, didn't read it through, but 
starts off with a statement like, "With the acute shortage of Catholic 
priests..........". Perfectly true, nearest church to me is served by a 
Philippino priest. However, once again about a week back in my local 
paper - there are two or three seminaries down in the States that follow 
the old Latin rite of the mass in Latin, chucking incense around, 
Gregorian chant etc... Funny thing is, they don't have any recruitment 
problems - they have long waiting lists of young men eager to get in.

Jimmy
```

```
"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> regarding homosexuals" unless the Bible literally states that.

We're talking principles here. If there is a Biblical principle (there is) 
that says "having in your heart the desire to do sin A is as bad as actually 
doing sin A" then that principle would also apply to sin B so that "having 
in your heart the desire to do sin B is as bad as doing sin B." This is 
perfectly reasonable and logical. In fact, if you read where Jesus express 
the principle (Matthew 5:21,22,28), it is very clear from the context that 
he is intentionally stating such a principle, therefore directly implying 
the corrrelation. The Bible is not a simple 'rule book', it is much more 
involved with teaching principles. This is more evident in the New Testament 
than in the Old Testament. :-)
```

```
"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> convolutions? The way you did it now, it just seems like a sick joke."


God tried that with Israel, as I pointed out in another post. But in 40 
years of God's undeniable immediate presence (plagues on Egypt, parting of 
the Red Sea, cloud by day, glowing cloud by night, manna every morning, God 
going into Moses tent in the form of a cloud, Moses face glowing when he 
came out, the ground opening up to swallow rebels, etc.) there were (IIRC) 
10 major rebellions. What makes you think people would listen to more direct 
evidence today? People believe God did not create us, even when there is no 
other reasonable explanation for life (autogenesis). People believe what 
they want to believe. Jesus points this out in the story of Lazarus and the 
rich man. I used to find that hard to believe, but I have witnessed enough 
with my own eyes to see that it is true.
```

- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4ebkf.48013$6y4.2744@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4391E491.4F910527@mts.net> <9Bpkf.51276$6y4.13244@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <43923888.E48F76B3@mts.net> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134306865.212753.114390@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <stAnf.4842$w7.1758@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:stAnf.4842$w7.1758@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> God tried that with Israel, as I pointed out in another post.

    To say that God "tried" something and failed implies that he is not 
omnipotent. If he did he IS omnipotent, then by definition, whatever it is 
he tried must have succeeded. Whatever state the world is in right now, God 
wanted it that way (or he doesn't care).

> But in 40 years of God's undeniable immediate presence (plagues on Egypt, 
> parting of the Red Sea, cloud by day, glowing cloud by night, manna every 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> listen to more direct evidence today?

    Again, why can't God just tell us directly? Why can't there be little 
angels on every living creature's shoulders following us around all day, 
reminding us to check both ways we cross the street, etc.? We still have 
free will, in the sense that we can still disobey the angels on our 
shoulders, but now we have a direct, unambiguous communication with God, and 
there is no chance for misunderstanding.

    Even if people don't accept the existence of God, they will have to 
accept the existence of these angels, if EVERYONE has one, and we can see 
and hear and speak with them, and touch them. The people who refuse to 
believe will continue to refuse to believe, and too bad for them. The people 
who are willing to change their minds given sufficient evidence will now 
believe in God. And the people who always did believe in God will continue 
to do so. It seems like a no-lose situation to me.

    To go back to the analogy, Alistair could have sent a letter 80 years 
before the woman's birth, a postcard 40 years before, a telegram 20 years 
before, and then show up at the girl's 8th birthday and told her just before 
she blows out the candles, "Check both ways before you cross the street". If 
at age 30, she STILL gets into the car accident, and Alistair were around, 
we'd still be questioning him "Why didn't you just tell her 5 minutes before 
the accident? Why play these mind games with her?"

> People believe God did not create us, even when there is no other 
> reasonable explanation for life (autogenesis).

    Since your writing this, I've made a post addressing this issue.

> People believe what they want to believe. Jesus points this out in the 
> story of Lazarus and the rich man. I used to find that hard to believe, 
> but I have witnessed enough with my own eyes to see that it is true.

    I'm not familiar with this story, so I can't comment on it.

    The point is that God is omnipotent, so he can do anything. If he can do 
anything, why did he give such a crappy warning? God is omniscient, so he 
knows everything. If he knows everything, then he knew the warning wouldn't 
work. So why did he gave a warning which he knew would be ignored? And if he 
knew we were going to ignore it, why are WE to blame?

    I'm going to take Alistair's analogy again, but this time I'm going to 
make myself the main character instead of Alistair, because I don't want to 
portray him as being a cruel person.

    So let's pretend now that the woman is deaf and a friend of mine (so I 
know she's deaf), and she's no longer crossing a street, but rather walking 
through a laboratory. At one point, over the PA system, a voice announce 
that the particle acceleration beam is about to start, so people should 
clear the area. The woman, being deaf, didn't hear it. I know she's deaf, 
and but for whatever reason, I decide to shout at her "Hey, they're starting 
the particle acelleration test. You better get out of there." She's facing 
the other way though, so doesn't hear or see me shouting. The voice over the 
PA system does its countdown from 30 seconds. "Hey, I'm warning you. They're 
going to fire the beam. It'll kill you if you don't move," I say, knowing 
well in advance she will ignore my warning (obviously, because she's deaf). 
The PA system is counting down from 5 seconds now. "Okay, don't say I didn't 
warn you" I conclude.

    The beam fires, killing her instantly.

    I knew she would ignore my warning, and I had the power to, for example, 
run up to her, tap her on the shoulder to get her attention and tell her to 
leave the room. But I didn't. I chose to just let her die.

    If an impartial audience were watching this sequence of events, do you 
think they would really believe "Well, Oliver gave her warning, but she 
ignored it" or would they believe "What a cruel person that Oliver was; he 
had the power to save her life, but chose not to... and not only that, but 
he had to nerve to pretend it was all her fault anyway!"

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 46)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-14T10:50:09-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M0Ynf.8401$kP5.7101@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4ebkf.48013$6y4.2744@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4391E491.4F910527@mts.net> <9Bpkf.51276$6y4.13244@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <43923888.E48F76B3@mts.net> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134306865.212753.114390@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pm_mf.1532$w7.312@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Johnf.203501$Io.98244@clgrps13> <stAnf.4842$w7.1758@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <AVDnf.146344$y_1.13306@edtnps89>`

```
"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> God wanted it that way (or he doesn't care).

You are using a too restricted definition of "tried", Oliver. Being 
omniscent, God would never have to "try" something for Himself to know the 
result. God "tries" (or "tests") people for the purpose of demonstrating the 
nature of their hearts, so *we* will know. This is why God "tested" Abraham 
by asking him to kill his only son, so by Abraham's willingness to complete 
this terrible request, we can see the amazing extent of Abraham's faith. 
Abraham's great faith is why God chose Abraham to be the father of His 
chosen people.

>> But in 40 years of God's undeniable immediate presence (plagues on Egypt, 
>> parting of the Red Sea, cloud by day, glowing cloud by night, manna every 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> and there is no chance for misunderstanding.

As I carefully pointed out, God has already tried being overwhelmingly 
obvious, and it did not produce faith. The problem is in the human heart, 
not in God's methods.

>    Even if people don't accept the existence of God, they will have to 
> accept the existence of these angels, if EVERYONE has one, and we can see 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> continue to do so. It seems like a no-lose situation to me.

Let me digress for a moment, or my response to what you said may unduly 
provoke some believers. There are several different schools of thought among 
Christians about how to interpret prophetic Scripture. Some believe that all 
prophetic Scripture should be interpreted symbolically, while others (e.g. 
the vast majority of Protestants) believe that prophetic Scripture should be 
interpreted just like any other Scripture; that is, literally unless the 
context clearly indicates otherwise. The descriptions below come from this 
literalist or "Dispensationalist" view, and I present them purely FYI as 
context for my comments below, not as any kind of argument. Interpreted as 
described above, here is a brief description of the "End Time" prophetic 
events as described in the Bible. At the end, I'll make some comments that 
you may find interesting, regarding what you say above.

Sometime in the (probably not too distant) future, there will be an event 
called "The Rapture" (from the Latin, meaning "catching away"), in which all 
Believers will be instantly taken to be with Jesus in heaven (1 
Thessalonians 4:16-17).

The Rapture will be closely followed by a seven year period called the 
"Tribulation". Most of the book of Revelation, Matthew 24 & 25, and many 
other Scriptures, are concerned with this period. This will be a time of 
great suffering in which God brings severe judgment against the corrupt 
world systems. Very early in this period will be the rise of the prophetic 
figure called the "Antichrist", who sets himself up to be God. The last half 
of this period are particularly severe, and are called the "Great 
Tribulation". Jesus said that this period will be cut short, otherwise 
nobody would be left.

The end of the "Great Tribulation" is punctuated by the "Glorious Return" of 
Jesus Christ, in which He puts a stop to the battle of "Armageddon", casts 
the Antichrist (and his "False Prophet") directly into the Lake of Fire, has 
Satan bound for 1000 years, renovates the earth, rebuilds the temple in 
Jerusalem, and establishes a Personal Theocracy based in Jerusalem.

At the end of the thousand year "Millenial Period" Satan will be unbound 
temporarily, he will foment a rebellion against God, which will be instantly 
crushed, the world will be destroyed by fire, the "Great White Throne 
Judgment" will occur, Satan and all the other baddies will be thrown into 
the Lake of Fire, followed by death and Hell, then will come an eternity of 
peace, love and righteousness for everybody else. The End.

In the above "Mellinial Period", God appears to be arranging a scenario very 
similar to what you describe. My opinion is that God will do this, at least 
in part, to answer precisely the question you propose. Jesus Christ will be 
bodily present and reign as absolute King of Kings. All Christians will be 
present in 'glorifed' bodies, acting as "priests and kings" in Jesus' reign. 
During this period, peace, justice and righteousness will be universal, and 
sin will be swiftly and justly punished. Nobody will break the rules with 
impuity. Nobody will be an athiest, because God is present and clearly 
represented by Jesus Himself and all Believers in visible form. The Bible 
says that any child who dies before 100 years old will be thought to be 
cursed, so there will be health and longevity. This is the period when the 
Bible says the swords will be beaten into plowshares, the lion will lay down 
with the lamb in peace, and a child will play with an 'adder' with no harm. 
But even after 1000 years of this, when Satan is released, he will 
apparently have no trouble in fomenting a rebellion. If the above scenario 
is correct, then it would serve to demonstrate that even a thousand years of 
continuous and obvious presence and activity by a loving and good God will 
not ensure that people will believe. Anyway, I enjoy speculating on such 
things. :-)

>    To go back to the analogy, Alistair could have sent a letter 80 years 
> before the woman's birth, a postcard 40 years before, a telegram 20 years 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> minutes before the accident? Why play these mind games with her?"

It has been my personal experience with God that, once He tells me 
something,or gives me an answer, He expects me to accept it, and that be the 
end of it. Apparently God is quite willing to warn us, often many times, but 
He doesn't appear to feel obligated to pound us incessantly with warnings.

>    The point is that God is omnipotent, so he can do anything. If he can 
> do anything, why did he give such a crappy warning?

Why do you call the warning 'crappy'? Just because you choose not to believe 
it? Must it be tatooed onto your flesh, in order to not be 'crappy'? :-)


> God is omniscient, so he knows everything.
> If he knows everything, then he knew the warning wouldn't work. So why did 
> he gave a warning which he knew would be ignored? And if he knew we were 
> going to ignore it, why are WE to blame?

What makes you say it doesn work? It works for me, and millions of other 
believing Christians. What makes you so difficult to convince? Maybe that's 
why YOU are to blame, eh? :-)

>    I'm going to take Alistair's analogy again, but this time I'm going to 
> make myself the main character instead of Alistair, because I don't want 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> he had to nerve to pretend it was all her fault anyway!"

An impartial audience would conclude that your analogy is incorrect. :-) In 
your scenario, your friend did not hear, and was completely unaware of, the 
warning. But those who have been exposed to what the Bible says (even if 
only in a NG SIG :) are not unaware of the warning, for they have heard it, 
but choose to ignore it or disagree with it. The Bible makes it clear that 
God is looking for people who will have faith in Him. Faith, like love, is 
something you must give willingly. People who refuse to believe unless proof 
is crammed down their throats are not looking to have faith in God. They 
don't want to have faith in God, and would only acknowledge God grudgingly 
after His presence was forced upon them. If you are in the water drowning, 
and somebody says "Here comes a lifesaver", are you going to ask for formal 
proof before reaching for it? I don't think so. :-)

Okay, so what about people who haven't heard? That's why Jesus gave His 
followers the Great Commission, to "go into all the world and spread the 
Gospel (good news)", and why there have been countless Christian 
missionaries. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 46)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-14T21:43:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:M0Ynf.8401$kP5.7101@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote:

>>>>    ... People might ask Alistair "Why didn't you just tell her 
>>>> directly, right before crossing the intersection, instead of going 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> His chosen people.

    You're mixing two definition of "tried" here. One is "to attempt to do 
something (and perhaps failing)" and the other is "to test someone", as in a 
trial.

    When you say "God tried that with Israel", I'm pretty sure you meant the 
first definition, of attempting to do something. The other definition 
doesn't make sense in the context.

    And like you say, an omnipotent God does not "try". He either does 
something, or he doesn't do something. He either saved the people of Israel, 
or he did not. And if he did not, why not?

    This story about Abraham... If I understood it correctly, a bunch of 
people see a guy called Abraham kill his only son, and then claims that "God 
made him do it", and so they choose to follow him as their leader? I suppose 
I might follow him too, out of fear that he kills me next! Every now and 
then a news story comes out about someone killing someone else because he 
heard voices telling him to do so. I generally believe that these people are 
considered "crazy". If said person claim that the voice was not just any 
voice, but actually the voice of God, does this make him a spiritual leader?

>
>>> But in 40 years of God's undeniable immediate presence (plagues on 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> not in God's methods.

    Again, there is the problem with the word "try". Either God makes 
himself overwhelmingly obviously to everyone, or he does not. And if he does 
not, why did he choose to make himself obscure or unclear to some people? 
Does he love those people less?

>
>>    Even if people don't accept the existence of God, they will have to 
…[68 more quoted lines elided]…
> enjoy speculating on such things. :-)

    The different I see between this prophecy and my description is that in 
my description, God creates an avatar of himself for each living thing. In 
the Prophecy, there is only one Jesus, and he will live in a temple in 
Jerusalem, so not everybody will have access to him. Thus some people may 
not be around to hear the messages of God, should he send out any.

    It baffles me that someone so powerful (God) would use such a clumsy 
method of communication, if his intent was to actually communicate with 
people. He could avoid all these problems with mis-translations, or 
misrepresentation, etc. by simply placing the messages directly into our 
conscious thoughts.

>
>>    To go back to the analogy, Alistair could have sent a letter 80 years 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> with warnings.

    The point is that God already knows whether we will listening to his 
warnings or not. So why does he bother warning the people whom he knows will 
not listen to him? Just so he can say "See? Told you so..." afterwards?

>
>>    The point is that God is omnipotent, so he can do anything. If he can 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'crappy'? :-)

    The warning is crappy because it was intentionally crafted in such a way 
so as to cause people to ignore it. God is about to create a warning to give 
to someone. In the moments before creating it, being omniscient, he knows 
"Well, that guy's not gonna heed this warning I'm creating. What the heck, 
I'll send it anyway. I could have sent a BETTER warning, one that he would 
actually heed... but I just don't feel like doing that."

>> God is omniscient, so he knows everything.
>> If he knows everything, then he knew the warning wouldn't work. So why 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> that's why YOU are to blame, eh? :-)

    I'm not convinced, therefore it didn't work on me. There's two 
possibilities here: Either God knew it wasn't going to work, or he didn't 
know. If it's the former, if God knew it wasn't going to work, then my 
questions above stand: "Why did he give a warning which he knew I would 
ingore? If he knew I was going to ignore it, why am *I* to blame?"

    If it's the latter, then God is not omniscient. I personally have no 
problem with this. God didn't know his warning wasn't going to work, so it 
wasn't his fault; no hard feelings, right? But Christians seem to be 
convinced that God *IS* omniscient, so God must have known, which leads 
right back to the same questions above: "Why am *I* to blame?"



>>    I'm going to take Alistair's analogy again, but this time I'm going to 
>> make myself the main character instead of Alistair, because I don't want 
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> it, but choose to ignore it or disagree with it.

    Okay, let me make the analogy slightly more "correct" then. The woman is 
in some theoretical physics laboratory, and there are a few people dressed 
in lab coats standing around. Suddenly, some of them become very agitated 
and shout out "Hey, the particle-accelerator beam is about to fire! Come 
stand next to me, where the safety zone is!" Others shout "No, don't listen 
to them! They aren't even real scientists. Stand next to me. *I* am in the 
safety zone." The woman looks around and doesn't see any flashing red lights 
or hears any sirens, so she doesn't know what these men are talking about.

    Meanwhile, I'm sitting in the control room monitoring all of this. And I 
do know that the particle beam is about to fire because I actually pulled 
the lever that starts its activation. And the reason some of the scientists 
know is because I've sent them messages on their pager.

    Now I also have a microphone and could announce over the loudspeaker 
that the test is commencing, and indicate where the safety zone is. But I 
chose not to.

    Instead, I want to test the faith of that woman. I've told the "true" 
scientist to call her over to the safety zone. Unfortunately, there are a 
few fake scientists in the room as well calling her to dangerous zones.

    But if she is truly devout, she will be able to determine which is which 
and choose the correct location to stand, right?


> The Bible makes it clear that God is looking for people who will have 
> faith in Him. Faith, like love, is something you must give willingly. 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> forced upon them.

    You seem to be implying that people who want proof before believing in 
things are somehow anti-God. Why can't there exist people who want proof 
before accepting something, and when presented with proof for the existence 
of God, gladly accept it?

    The people who want proof before accepting something are "fair" in the 
sense that they want proof before accepting theorms in Math and Science as 
well. They don't blindly accept, for example, that 3 + 6 = 83. They would 
want to see a proof for that first. The difference is that there ARE proofs 
for certain theorems in Math and Science. There is, so far, no proof for the 
existence of God.

    So these people believe those Math and Science theorems for which there 
exists proofs. These people don't believe in God (though that does not 
nescessarily mean they believe that God does not exist), and these people 
don't believe in the Math and Science theorems which have failed to be 
proven.

> If you are in the water drowning, and somebody says "Here comes a 
> lifesaver", are you going to ask for formal proof before reaching for it? 
> I don't think so. :-)

    The problem with this analogy is that the danger of drowning is usually 
apparent, whereas the danger of eternal damnation is not. Here's a better 
(in my opinion) analogy:

    You're walking down the street minding your own business when a man runs 
up to you with a syringe saying "You've just been exposed to a deadly 
biological weapon, and I have the only cure. Let me inject this into you."

    Are you going to let him, or are you going to want some evidence that 
the stuff injected into you is going to do more good than harm? I suspect 
that latter.

    - Oliver
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> Judson McClendon wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> the future. God is therefore guilty.

    I (half) believe Alistair is correct here, but in my philosophy, free 
will is really only an illusion anyway (regardless of whether or not God 
exists). I'm a believer in "strong AI" 
(http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strong_AI) which means that I believe that our 
apparent self-consciousness and "intelligence" is just a byproduct of 
physical laws and their consequences of the matter that makes up our brain. 
I.e. I don't believe there's some sort of "magic" or mysticism going on that 
causes our mind to be a seperate entity from our brain.

    What does it mean to say "I control what I think" (which is essentially 
the definition of having free will)? Is it really true that I'm controling 
my thoughts right now? And what IS the nature of my thoughts? AFAIK, my 
thoughts are just the emergent behaviour (the "symptoms" if you will) of 
electrical nerve impulses moving around in my head. The behaviour of these 
pulses are dictated by the movement of electrons and the chemical 
interactions. I don't control my thoughts anymore than I control the 
electromagnetic waves being emitted by my monitor right now.

    That doesn't mean that I don't believe people shouldn't be responsible 
for their actions. If someone commits a crime, and we try to punish them, 
that person might counter "but it wasn't my fault! The electrons in my brain 
made me do it!" While that may be true, from a pragmatic point of view, 
punishment has been empirically shown to discourage undesired behaviour. So 
we punish them anyway; not because it's their "fault" (a meaningless term, 
from this philosophy), but because it stops it from happening again. (I 
know, this philosophy of mine is not a popular one).

    Anyway, to go back to the topic of God's responsibility (or lack 
thereof) for the mess we're in...

    I'm not too familiar with the bible, but from my recollection, it starts 
with "In the beginning, there was nothing" and then proceeds to say that God 
created everything.

    In that sense, God is ACTIVELY responsible for everything that results, 
because, being omniscient, he knew the consequences of everything he did, 
and everything that exists now is a direct result of his initial 
creation-act.

    - Oliver
```

```

Oliver Wong wrote:
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
> news:1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> made me do it!"

In a deterministic universe this would be true as the consequences of
any initial state would be known and reproducible (in theory). However,
we live in a non-deterministic universe where the uncertainty principle
prevents us from being able to reproduce precisely any one state and
where, given any set of initial starting conditions, we can not predict
the future. I sort-of think that makes sense.


> While that may be true, from a pragmatic point of view,
> punishment has been empirically shown to discourage undesired behaviour. So
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>     - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 44)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-13T18:32:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PsEnf.146354$y_1.67386@edtnps89>`
- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4ebkf.48013$6y4.2744@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4391E491.4F910527@mts.net> <9Bpkf.51276$6y4.13244@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <43923888.E48F76B3@mts.net> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1134306865.212753.114390@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <lcXmf.1502$w7.604@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134321423.450307.326540@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <xdhnf.203499$Io.67299@clgrps13> <1134491827.518815.18790@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1134491827.518815.18790@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> Oliver Wong wrote:
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> the future. I sort-of think that makes sense.

    I've read Roger Penrose's book, The Emperor's New Mind, and I'm not sure 
if I fully understood it (so perhaps I will completely misrepresent 
Penrose's intentions here), but it seems that Penroses believes that the 
universe is deterministic, but uncomputable. He believes that the 
consciousness that arises out of our brain has something to do with physical 
processes we don't understand yet (he suspects it has something to do with a 
unifying theory of quantum-gravity). For what it's worth, he does NOT 
believe in Strong AI.

    The uncertainty principle says that we cannot know the exact position 
and momentum of a given particle, but that doesn't nescessarily mean that if 
two particles were exactly in the same state and environment, they wouldn't 
behave identically. If they DO behave the exact same way, then the universe 
IS deterministic.

    But either way, whether the electrons in my brain are deterministic or 
underministic, it STILL isn't my fault; the electrons in my brain STILL made 
me do it! (Unless you believe in the existence of a "soul", and that this 
soul has some effect on the behaviour of the undeterministic particles, in 
which case, it isn't my electrons' fault either, it's my soul's fault!)

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2005-12-14T10:10:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <xdhnf.203499$Io.67299@clgrps13> <1134491827.518815.18790@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <PsEnf.146354$y_1.67386@edtnps89>`

```
In article <PsEnf.146354$y_1.67386@edtnps89>,
Oliver Wong <owong@castortech.com> wrote:

[snip]

>    But either way, whether the electrons in my brain are deterministic or 
>underministic, it STILL isn't my fault; the electrons in my brain STILL made 
>me do it!

Ummmmm... Mr Wong, where does 'me' exist in your statememt?  Just curious; 
in modern parlance it is often said of a body which no longer registers 
electroencephalographic (EEG) activity, or 'flatlines' that 's/he's no 
longer in there'.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 46)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-14T20:07:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IX_nf.256583$ir4.214345@edtnps90>`
- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <xdhnf.203499$Io.67299@clgrps13> <1134491827.518815.18790@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <PsEnf.146354$y_1.67386@edtnps89> <dnor3i$qqi$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:dnor3i$qqi$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <PsEnf.146354$y_1.67386@edtnps89>,
> Oliver Wong <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ummmmm... Mr Wong, where does 'me' exist in your statememt?

    Your question is known to philosophers as the famous "Mind-Body 
problem". (And so I won't pretend to be able to answer it in a satisfying 
way).

    I should have said "The electrons in my brain made my body do it!" to 
avoid the whole "which part of this is me?" question. This also nicely 
reflects the belief that the body is just a machine, albeit a very complex 
one.

> Just curious;
> in modern parlance it is often said of a body which no longer registers
> electroencephalographic (EEG) activity, or 'flatlines' that 's/he's no
> longer in there'.

    Yes, when there is no more such activity, the person is typically 
considered "legally dead". I wasn't addressing the legal aspects of life and 
death in my post, but rather the philosophical ones.

    - Oliver
```

```
Actually my question is known to me as the 'Mind-Mind problem'; what you

>(And so I won't pretend to be able to answer it in a satisfying

Too late... now folks can ask 'where's the rest of me?'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>

- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <PsEnf.146354$y_1.67386@edtnps89> <dnor3i$qqi$1@reader2.panix.com> <IX_nf.256583$ir4.214345@edtnps90> <dnrgnr$5ue$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
> Oliver Wong <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> wont to ask where 'you' exists in that statement, as well.

    I suppose the question of what is "me" and what isn't is more a matter 
of linguistics than philosophy. When you wave your hand in my direction and 
say "That, there, is Mr. Wong", you are probably referring to the collection 
of matter that makes up my body. So "me" is a convenient pronoun to refer to 
this collection of matter.

[snip]

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()

```
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:dnrgnr$5ue$1@reader1.panix.com...
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>of linguistics than philosophy.

Some might say sociology/anthropology as well, Mr Wong... how does one 
learn to differentiate between that-which-is-called 'self' and 
that-which-is-not?  Consider the horrors which might be found in the 
punishment of banishment when what some folks call 'a group' is necessary 
for the survival of what some folks call 'an individual'; when the Human 
Beings say 'you are no longer a part of us' then a logical response might 
be to go out into the forest, sing a Death Song and be done with it all.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 53)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-12-19T08:50:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rgldq1hffnom1v7gh3p934u5lakr7rtd75@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <f9kof.1408$lv3.1341@clgrps12> <dnufbm$fq1$1@reader1.panix.com> <6KEof.125461$Gd6.74433@pd7tw3no> <dnv5ei$8ie$1@reader1.panix.com> <1134836305.609124.111650@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 17 Dec 2005 08:18:25 -0800, "Alistair"
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>Isn't that the new holy grail in AI? Apparently, IIRC, only humans and
>dolphins have been assessed as being self-aware. Chimps and apes seem
>to be only semi-self-aware.

People say the silliest things in attempts to seem special.   I've
seen people claim that only people dream (obviously untrue), only
humans blush (can't tell - but I've seen embarrassed animals), that
only humans kill for fun, or kill their own kind.      Time and again,
observation and testing do not support these claims.

How do you define "self aware"?    Recognizing themselves in a mirror?
Recognizing their voice?  Recognizing their name?  

 How do you define "semi-self aware"?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 54)_

- **From:** "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2005-12-19T11:47:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1135021677.553705.6610@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<rgldq1hffnom1v7gh3p934u5lakr7rtd75@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dnufbm$fq1$1@reader1.panix.com> <6KEof.125461$Gd6.74433@pd7tw3no> <dnv5ei$8ie$1@reader1.panix.com> <1134836305.609124.111650@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <rgldq1hffnom1v7gh3p934u5lakr7rtd75@4ax.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 17 Dec 2005 08:18:25 -0800, "Alistair"
> <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>  How do you define "semi-self aware"?

This has been covered in Scientific American recently. Recognition of
one's own image in a mirror and subsequent recognition of a change
(such as a painted mark on a dolphin's head). Semi aware seems to be
about recognizing that the object in the mirror is 'special' perhaps by
the image duplicating the subject's movements but without the subject
being fully aware that the image is of the subject. At least that is
how I recall the article.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 54)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-21T18:55:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pyhqf.754$km.514@edtnps89>`
- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <f9kof.1408$lv3.1341@clgrps12> <dnufbm$fq1$1@reader1.panix.com> <6KEof.125461$Gd6.74433@pd7tw3no> <dnv5ei$8ie$1@reader1.panix.com> <1134836305.609124.111650@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <rgldq1hffnom1v7gh3p934u5lakr7rtd75@4ax.com>`

```
    I have a dog. This post is anecdotal evidence only.

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:rgldq1hffnom1v7gh3p934u5lakr7rtd75@4ax.com...
>
> People say the silliest things in attempts to seem special.   I've
> seen people claim that only people dream (obviously untrue),

    There seems to be evidence that my dog dreams. That is, while my dog is 
(apparently) sleeping, she will move her limbs around and bark in her sleep.

> only
> humans blush (can't tell - but I've seen embarrassed animals),

    As far as I can tell, my dog is shameless.

> that
> only humans kill for fun,

    Depends on where you draw the line between "for fun" and "by instinct". 
My dog seems to "enjoy" attacking squirrels, and it doesn't seem to be 
because of a desire to eat them.

> or kill their own kind.

    As far as I know, my dog hasn't killed another dog yet (though I do not 
doubt your claim).

>
> How do you define "self aware"?    Recognizing themselves in a mirror?

    I had a previous dog which would become very agitated when he saw his 
reflection in a mirror, and would bark angrily at it for hours on end. My 
current dog seems completely uninterested in her reflection.

> Recognizing their voice?  Recognizing their name?

    My current dog doesn't seem to recognize her name; rather she recognizes 
tones of voice. That is, the actual syllables I say don't seem to matter so 
much as how I say them.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 55)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2005-12-21T19:06:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<doc92u$iu5$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1134836305.609124.111650@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <rgldq1hffnom1v7gh3p934u5lakr7rtd75@4ax.com> <pyhqf.754$km.514@edtnps89>`

```
In article <pyhqf.754$km.514@edtnps89>,
Oliver Wong <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
>    I have a dog. This post is anecdotal evidence only.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>(apparently) sleeping, she will move her limbs around and bark in her sleep.

My dog's name is Killer - nobody has objected - and she will do something 
while sleeping on my lap that I've heard folks from the southern part of 
the United States of America refer to as 'chasing rabbits'.  It goes 
something like... twitch... twitch twitch... wrf... brf wrf... kick kick 
kick kick (in a running pattern)... brf... sssssiiiiggggghhhhhh.

Now Killer is a pug, the result of a couple of thousand years' worth of 
selective breeding to be an indoor dog, a pampered lap dog, and nothing 
more... which raises the question of 'what *is* she 'seeing'?'

[snip]

>> Recognizing their voice?  Recognizing their name?
>
>    My current dog doesn't seem to recognize her name; rather she recognizes 
>tones of voice. That is, the actual syllables I say don't seem to matter so 
>much as how I say them.

As nobody else - to the best of my knowledge - has represented it, either 
before or since:

http://www.w3.org/2005/Talks/0623-sb-IEEEStorConf/farside3.gif

DD
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>
>Oliver Wong wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>targets.

It's known as "superpredation" and is caused by a glitch in the
instincts of animals that normally prey upon solitary targets. If
presented with a large number of immediately reachable prey animals,
eg a fox in a hencoop, the instinctual activity of "see prey, kill
it..." becomes overloaded and, to use a computer similie,  gets into a
loop.
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
>
> You pose a couple of interesting questions there, and I wonder if you have 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> makes sense.

[snip]

    This probably doesn't answer Pete's question though, because as I 
understand it, he DOESN'T assume God is as the Bible describes Him.

    To me, this is like someone asking "How do I change the desktop 
background on my computer?" and someone posting as an answer "Well, let's 
assume your computer works via magic little elves. Then, the way to get the 
desktop to change is to stick candy canes into your disk drive until the 
elves are satiated, at which point you can speak into your microphone your 
request to change the desktop."

    That works if the computer really IS powered by magic little elves, but 
won't work if they're not.

    Pete, at best, is not convinced that God is as the Bible describes Him, 
so any explanation which depends on the assumption that the Bible is 
accurate probably won't answer his question satisfactorily. Instead, there 
should be an answer which doesn't nescessarily depend on the bible being 
true. The answer could perhaps, however, somehow derive that the bible is 
true, thus leading to the same conclusion you presented above.

    To convince a rational person of something, you must start off with a 
set of assumptions that both parties can agree with, so assuming apriori 
that any one particular religion is "correct" won't work.

> This cannot be overstated: GOD'S OBVIOUS DAILY PRESENCE DID NOT PRODUCE 
> FAITH IN MEN! Why?

    According to the Bible anyway. I'm relatively confident that if God were 
to perform miracles of biblical proportions today, we would readily believe 
in Him. And these days we have video cameras and the Internet, and the 
"Google Video" service, so that records of God could be saved forever.

    We need not fear that people would accuse the video recording of being 
trick photography, as we could simply ask God to perform some miracle that 
is guaranteed to convince all viewers, present and future, of His existence. 
Being omniscient, he knows what miracle will satisfy these conditions, and 
being omnipotent, he is capable of performing it.

    - Oliver
```

- **Message-ID:** `<439DB914.8F559EDC@mts.net>`
- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4ebkf.48013$6y4.2744@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4391E491.4F910527@mts.net> <9Bpkf.51276$6y4.13244@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <43923888.E48F76B3@mts.net> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <GXgnf.203497$Io.123871@clgrps13>`

```
Oliver Wong wrote:

>     We need not fear that people would accuse the video recording of being
> trick photography, as we could simply ask God to perform some miracle that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>     - Oliver

Don't be so sure!  There's a sci-fi book called "The day the sun stood
still"; actually three stories by three authors with the same premise:
that God finally gave in to the eternal pestering for an inarguable
miracle and caused the sun to cease moving across the sky for (I think)
12 hours, with no negative consequences.  The result?  Exactly what
you'd expect.  Everybody in the world acknowledged the miracle but
interpreted it in his/her/their own fashion, fashions which completely
cancelled each other out, and in the end the miracle accomplished
precisely nothing - not even proving the existence of God!  I found it
very striking that three authors who were turned loose on the same
project, presumably without instructions and without contact with each
other, should come up with the same denouement.  Not encouraging.

PL
```

```
"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mts.net> wrote in message 
news:439DB914.8F559EDC@mts.net...
> Oliver Wong wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> other, should come up with the same denouement.  Not encouraging.

    If God is omniscient though, he should have known that freezing the Sun 
in the sky for 12 hours would not convince some people.

    I couldn't imagine any miracle that could be videotaped that would 
convince EVERYONE that God exists, which is why I didn't explicitly name 
what miracle God should perform in the post above. However, if such a 
miracle exists, surely God would know what it is, and could perform it.

<anecdote>
    Back in highschool, we had a physics professor who claimed that science 
has progressed far enough that if God were to come today and try to 
demonstrate some miracle, it would always be possible, in principle, for 
humans to replicate the miracle.

    "What about if he shot lighting from his hands?"

    "We can have a sufficient understanding of electricity to artificially 
produce lightning too."

    "What if he caused a solar eclipse?"

    "He could have saw when the next solar eclipse were to happen, and 
conveniently wait until then to make his appearance, and claim that the 
eclipse was his own doing"

    Etc. The professor, Mr. Moffat, said he had discussed this problem with 
his peers and he was certain that there really was nothing that could be 
done by God that couldn't also be done as a (potentially very expensive and 
elaborate) magic trick.

    So then I said "So let's say I'm God and I can do anything I want, and I 
want to make you believe that I'm God."

    "Yes..."

    "So I'll just re-arrange your mind so that you believe I'm God."

    "..."
</ancdote>

    An omnipotent being will ALWAYS find a way. Or else, by definition, they 
are not omnipotent.

    - Oliver
```

```
On Sat, 10 Dec 2005 06:48:18 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>If God were to simply remove all suffering, 
>that means all the direct and indirect consequence of sin, would He not by 
>that very act, undermine what He has said about sin, that the consequences 
>of sin are death? 

I can accept that a good, loving Father had as the consequences of sin
being death.   Nothing evil about an omnipotent being allowing one of
His children to die.   Nor in allowing one to suffer in order to grow
and become better.

It is when people worship an omnipotent being who they believe allows
even one of His children to be tortured beyond all understanding
forever and ever without hope of parole, that I despair.

What could possibly be more evil than that?
```

```
Judson,
As you took the trouble to respond, I have commented on your response below. 
I would've done so sooner, but simply haven't had the time.
Thanks for the response.
Comments below...

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> us all the answers.

You got that right. In my opinion, it raises more questions than it answers. 
But that is probably to be expected when you consider all the various 
authors and the time periods it was written across. If all the authors were 
inspired by the same God, He appears to have changed his mind a lot.

There are lots of things I don't understand about God. But
> it would have to be a pretty small and insignificant god that a human 
> could completely understand, would it not? :-)
>
Why? I have known women who I couldn't understand, but they were neither 
small nor insignificant... :-)


> You pose a couple of interesting questions there, and I wonder if you have 
> considered those questions from God's viewpoint, assuming God as the Bible 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
OK.

> Okay, why does God seem to hide Himself? Well, what has happened in the 
> past when He did not? Remember, we're assuming the Biblical account is 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> they could be in God's presence without being destroyed.

This is where I have my first problem. I don't believe in "sin". (I believe 
there are outcomes that are undesirable, because they make life harder for 
us all, but the universe has no knowledge of "good" or "evil" and doesn't 
care. (Neither should it...). There are simply events that occur at all 
levels. Randomly sometimes, and consequentially other times. In every case, 
the considerations about these events are imposed by humans, not by nature.)

If God was so Holy that He couldn't even be in the presence of sin, and 
given that, as the world He created unfolded, there was going to be a fair 
bit of it about, it would seem a flaw in His capability if He wasn't able to 
be near it.

> So how did our original parents respond to God's clear and regular 
> presence? They waited until (from Adam and Eve's perspective) God was not 
> immediately present, then they both disobeyed God's one and only rule, and 
> ate the forbidden fruit.

It was a stupid rule that was made up entirely to tempt them anyway. God 
could easily grow another apple; why was He so concerned about Adam 
acquiring "knowledge"? Could He have felt threatened by Man? Unlikely. So 
why the rule about the tree? It had to be to see if His created people were 
obedient. And they weren't. They were curious. The same curiosity that to 
this day drives people to probe and investigate and find out more about the 
nature of things. God's behaviour over this says more about God than it does 
about Adam and Eve. Why would an all powerful supreme being want to be 
worshipped by people who were totally obedient, subservient, unimaginative, 
and unquesioning? He could have populated the world with IBM salesmen if 
that was what he liked. :-)

>Okay, so God ran them out of Eden. Adam and Eve, now being sinners, would 
>perish in God's immediate presence, so out of mercy God backed off from 
>immediate contact with them.

Oh, like He was unable to contain their sin or be unaffected by it Himself?

> And how did that work? Cain, the first human being ever born became a 
> murderer and killed his brother Able. (That would be "Abel"... his 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> A loving God would not require propitiation.


>This trend continued until, in apparent disgust at the rampant evil of 
>mankind, God destroyed the entire human race with a flood, except for the 
>righteous man Noah, and seven other people.

And He didn't foresee this outcome when He took the dust and created Adam? 
If He did, then what was the point?

>Even after that dramatic judgment, people continued to be evil.

That is not proven. SOME people behaved badly. God's definition of "bad" was 
that they didn't live in fear and trembling of Him. (Personally, I don't 
believe people are now, or have ever been, evil. We are certainly flawed and 
imperfect, but a new born baby is not evil and bad behaviour is acquired, 
not inherent.)

>So God selected a man of great faith, Abraham, and through him God started 
>a people whom God intended to deal with in a more direct manner.

Or so they tell us...

I've never understood why God would have a chosen race and if He did, why 
don't they rule the Earth? Instead, for thousands of years they have had 
nothing but trouble and strife, persecution, and suffering. Even today, they 
are still havng trouble just trying to hang on to the land they claim God 
gave them. Small wonder that some of them are becoming disillusioned with 
the official religion.

>After 400 years, Abraham's descendents the Jews were slaves and many were 
>worshiping the gods of their captors, so God sent Moses to deliver them 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>except for the Sabbath.

I can't imagine living on the same food for forty years, even if it was 
magic mushrooms dropped by a space pixie. Wouldn't you think that a God who 
could do all those outrageous things to the Egyptians could have varied the 
diet a bit?


>There were no atheists among the Jews in those days, because God's presence 
>was astoundingly obvious.

And you know this.... how? Who's to say that one or two didn't buy into the 
mass hallucination?

>What was the result? Only a few months after the parting of the Red Sea, 
>God spoke to the whole nation from Mt. Sinai. This scared the Jews so much, 
>they asked Moses to deal with God for them. So when Moses went up Mt. Sinai 
>to hear what God had to say, by the time Moses returned in 40 days, the 
>Jews had already made a golden calf and were worshiping it!

When the Cat's away... they probably elected Moses to go up the mountain 
because he was the least fun and most likely to spoil the party. Once he was 
gone they let the good times roll...

>These people had just witnessed the most fantastic series of supernatural 
>events since the Creation, they had seen God's undeniable presence every 
>single day, and they were worshiping a gold calf!

They had spent forty years wandering around in a very inhospitable place 
(I've been there) waiting for their leader to get the word from God and 
never knowing when that word would come, living on food that was tedious in 
the extreme, keeping all the rules required by their faith, and having very 
little fun. I don't blame them one bit for wanting to kick their heels up.


> This cannot be overstated: GOD'S OBVIOUS DAILY PRESENCE DID NOT PRODUCE 
> FAITH IN MEN!

Probably because familiarity breeds contempt. He could have looked in 
occasionally... Besides, he never communicated with them on a regular basis 
so it was hardly surprising they were all terrified when He finally did.

Why? The only answer I can think of is Jeremiah 17:9: "The
> heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately wicked; who can know 
> it?"

Jeremiah was a miserable old fart. Only his mother could love him and the 
bible does not record whether she did or not. He was EXACTLY the sort fo 
person who would delight in the austere worship of a tyrannical God as this 
would be his only chance of having any effect on the people in his 
community. He couldn't make friends so he decided to instill fear instead.

> Men simply have evil hearts, and they do not believe or disbelieve in God 
> because of proof or lack of proof, but because of what men want in their 
> hearts.

No they don't. If you look around you can see heroism and kindness every day 
in people from all faiths, cultures, and walks of life. People are NOT 
basically evil. They just get screwed up by having to cope with a complex 
world and learning the wrong lessons sometimes. Furthermore, these wrong 
lessons are often passed on to children, not born in them.

I don't disbelieve in God because my heart is wicked. I disbelieve in God 
because my reason and intuition tell me so, and because I have no necessity 
for God in my life. (I can also explain how the universe works to a 
satisfactory level without needing a supreme being to make it so...) I have 
coped with adversity and danger, even been in peril of my life, and never 
asked God for help. I'm still here, and I live a pleasant (though somewhat 
quieter now) life which gives me great enjoyment. I do the best I can to 
help my fellow man, not because I wish to accrue Brownie points in heaven, 
or because I fear burning in eternal torment (I notice God's pretty big on 
this blood and torture stuff; I'm not...), but because it simply makes sense 
to do so. It is just as easy to be "good" as it is to be "bad" and doing so 
makes life a bit easier all round.


>I believe this clearly illustrates why simply dazzling us with miracles 
>*does not* accomplish what God apparently wants, and that is for us to have 
>faith in Him.

I think the God you are describing is insecure and needs help. (These are 
axiomatically NOT Godlike qualities, so it throws the whole belief system 
into question.)

>One other illustration while I'm on this point.When thinking about the 
>story Jesus told of Lazarus and the rich man (Like 16:19-31), I used to 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>records, would it make you a believer? :-)

Yes, certainly. It would make me a believer in the power of the mind and the 
human defence systems that evolved over three million years to ensure we 
stay healthy. If the mind is clear and untroubled, there is no reason why 
these systems won't do their job. I can't also rule out the efficacy of 
prayer, inasmuch as a group of people focussing on a commn goal and 
intensely wishing it so, MAY have some effect. Modern science teaches us 
that everything is connected at some level, so I certainly wouldn't dismiss 
the power of prayer. (I would argue that a group of people all simply 
focussing on healing the sick person might attain the same goal; and this, 
in and of itself, probably won't effect a cure. It certainly does no harm.)

 The reason we get sick is because the protective systems that took millions 
of years to evolve have been overtaken in the last geological blink of an 
eye by technology and intensive farming that these systems haven't yet had a 
chance to adjust to. We eat food that is full of chemicals, drink water that 
is not pure, and are bombarded 24/7 by electromagnetic waves that we really 
don't know the full effects of, because they haven't been around long enough 
for us to really do definitive studies on them. The "miracle" is not that 
people get better unexpectedly sometimes, but that any of us survive more 
than a few decades.

>
> So, why do so many people suffer, if there is a God, and He is good?

I believe Buddhism addresses this exact question, but removes the reference 
to God.


>You're not the only one to pose this question, many Christians have asked 
>the same thing, particularly when enduring great suffering. If you were a 
>believer, I would recommend C.S. Lewis' book "The Problem of Pain" but this 
>book might not be satisfying to a non-believer.

No, it isn't for this non-believer. Likewise Bertrand Russell and Malcolm 
Muggeridge. (I did enjoy the Chronicles of Narnia as a kid and saw the newly 
released movie (made in New Zealand) last week. His allegory is a bit too 
heavy handed for me now, as an adult, but the special effects alone made the 
film worth a visit. I have a feeling Disney may struggle to get their $200 
million back, but I hope not...)

> I will take a stab at it, but this is a very difficult subject, and I'm no 
> expert. Again, for the sake of argument, let's assume the God of the 
> Bible.

I've stayed with you so far... :-)

> I believe the answer lies in understanding three things, one that God 
> decided to give man free will, two that our time in this world is short, 
> particularly when compared to the eternity that follows, and three that 
> there really is a Satan out there and his purpose is "to kill, steal and 
> destroy" (John 10:10).

Fortunately, as an atheist, I don't have to believe in Satan any more than I 
do in God. I find the whole concept of absolute good and absolute evil just 
laughable. I have to say, though, that simply as a character, Satan is a lot 
more interesting than God.


>When God created man, He gave man dominion over the earth. But when man 
>(Adam and Eve) listened to Satan and sinned, man, through Adam, abdicated 
>his dominion to Satan. Since that time, Satan has been "the ruler of this 
>world" whom Jesus has defeated (John 12:31), and who will be eventually 
>thrown into the Lake of Fire for eternity.

Well, my information is that he is not averse to fire, so that should be 
fine... :-)


>The Bible doesn't spell out precisely what powers Satan has to work 
>mischief here, but it is apparently significant.

I'm sure you believe my posts here are inspired by him... Who knows? If you 
believe something, it may become real for you.

If it helps... I hereby publicly renounce Satan and all his minions, and 
refuse him dominion over me. (The mischief I indulge in here sometimes, is 
not Satanic, it simply comes from being afflicted by a sense of humour...)

>Were diseases created by Satan (e.g. by mutating harmless bacteria & 
>virus), for example? We can only speculate. But because of man's fall, God 
>cursed the earth (Genesis 3:17-19). I have often wondered if what we 
>perceive and express as "Murphy's Law" (Anything that can go wrong, will go 
>wrong) is actually God's curse at work? :-)

Once you create a "whipping boy" it is pretty easy to ascribe all bad things 
to him. I still believe in randomity.

> Anyway, man has sinned against God, and there are direct and indirect 
> consequences of that.

How? I personally don't bear the Old Boy any grudge. Just exactly how did I 
"sin" against Him?


>There are also the specific consequences of the sins themselves (e.g. 
>adultery leads to divorce).

Really? And I thought it was incompatibility... (I have been married twice; 
neither marriage ended because of adultery on either side.)


>And all the while, Satan causes all the mischief he can, helped by hosts of 
>fallen angels whom the Bible calls demons. At the very least, Satan tempts 
>and encourages men to evil acts.

I think the problem I have here is the same one I have with Christianity in 
general.

There is no RESPONSIBILITY.

The Devil made me wicked, but it's OK because Jesus died for me...

Soory, no disrespect to those who follow this faith, but it simply doesn't 
work for me. I take responsibility for the things I do; the good, the bad, 
and the ugly. When I screw up I try to make it right, and whatever I do, I 
certainly learn by it.


>Now, against this background, God has limited Himself by the decisions He 
>has made, such as to give man free will, and dominion over the earth, which 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> -- 

It is a valiant attempt, Judson, and I respect your sincerity. 
Unfortunately, I am not persuaded. The reasons for this are not obstinacy or 
the Devil hardening my heart, it is just that the case is predicated on the 
Bible being true and authoritative. I don't accept that.

In the end, it comes down to faith. While I respect yours (and everybody 
else's) I smply don't share it.

Pete.
```

```
> That was an obvious parable.   Knowledge of Good and Evil apparently
> is what makes us religious animals.

The parable is that 'aquiring knowledge' not authorised by 'God', or
more especially by the priesthood, leads to undersirable outcomes. You
must not read or listen to anything that does not come from the
priesthood, and/or you must ignore it lest you, too, be cast out.

Fundementalists (of all religions) seem to do a particularly good job
at this, perhaps it is necessary to do so in order to remain so rigidly
believing. I would think that it is difficult to maintain faith in a
single religion if one actually learnt about some of the hundreds of
others that have rigid followers.

It is like in WWII the german population was banned (with prejudice)
from listening to the BBC lest they start to think that their god
(adolf) and his scripture writer (goebbels) were not _the_truth_.
```

```
>That is not proven. SOME people behaved badly. God's definition of "bad" was
```

```
On Fri, 16 Dec 2005 12:12:23 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>They had spent forty years wandering around in a very inhospitable place 
>(I've been there)

then.
```
