```
On Fri, 16 Dec 2005 12:12:23 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>Fortunately, as an atheist, I don't have to believe in Satan any more than I 
>do in God. I find the whole concept of absolute good and absolute evil just 
>laughable. I have to say, though, that simply as a character, Satan is a lot 
>more interesting than God.

Lots of fantasy has Satan as a character.   He's interesting.   There
are various interesting versions of Hell.   I kind of like the myth
that Satan is trying his best to be better than God, with a better
heaven than heaven - he's just not as competent at it as God is.

I haven't seen any fantasy depiction of heaven that looks like a place
that I would be willing to spend a million years at.   And a million
years is nothing compared to eternity.

Nothing wrong with death, at least compared with living forever.
```

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **Date:** 2005-12-16T19:32:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZCEof.2095$ic1.600@edtnps90>`
- **References:** `<dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4ebkf.48013$6y4.2744@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4391E491.4F910527@mts.net> <9Bpkf.51276$6y4.13244@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <43923888.E48F76B3@mts.net> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <40ebioF1aa2vkU1@individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:40ebioF1aa2vkU1@individual.net...
>
> I have to say, though, that simply as a character, Satan is a lot more 
> interesting than God.

    From a purely storytelling/narrative point of view, the problem with God 
as a character is that he suffers from what is often referred to as 
"Superman syndrome". The character has no flaw, and so is uninteresting to 
most readers.

    One portrayal of Satan that I find particularly appealing is the "I'm 
just doing my job, but everyone hates me" archetype. There are many variants 
on this theme; for example, Satan and God are doing a "good cop, bad cop" 
routine, but actually both of them want people to generally act good and go 
to heaven.

    The nice thing about writing fiction in which Satan is a character is 
that it cannot be said to be an inaccurate portrayal of Satan. If (s)he 
appears kind, loving and friendly throughout the entire story, it may be a 
case of deception, for example, for the purpose of some greater plan (which 
laid outside the scope of the story). This is not true about God; there 
exists certain portrayal of God which can considered "inaccurate" because it 
is widely believe that God would not stoop to deceiving people about 
Himself.

    - Oliver
```

```
>as a character is that he suffers from what is often referred to as 
>"Superman syndrome". The character has no flaw, and so is uninteresting to

We don't see God in Gaiman and Pratchett's _Good Omens_, but there is
a point where an Angel and a Demon were talking and the Angel is
wondering about the situation - saying something to the effect that
God had this funny smile when this situation (Armageddon) was being
discussed - implying that the big battle between good and evil might
not be quite as the angels and devils believed.

Fun stuff.
```

```
Here's an antic thought:

Taking the Biblical story of the Garden of Eden seriously: I'd say that
Satan has to be as immortal as God himself.  The serpent in that story
is not specifically identified as Satan but is usually accepted to be. 
In that case, somewhere in the world is an immortal snake (or perhaps
sea-serpent) which can talk and which can therefore tell us exactly what
happened!

PL
```

_(reply depth: 43)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-18T20:28:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rSopf.1751$k76.1602@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4ktjp1l7pjsdfidtn66748n3cak1t65q7n@4ax.com> <vMnmf.466$w7.160@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <3vvn2lF188adkU2@individual.net> <76Amf.3687$MA2.1527@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <40ebioF1aa2vkU1@individual.net> <ZCEof.2095$ic1.600@edtnps90> <sd66q1t3oht3ihg9jf6na7h7ehoesn75cm@4ax.com> <43A35334.768AF6C9@mts.net>`

```
"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>
> Taking the Biblical story of the Garden of Eden seriously: I'd say that
> Satan has to be as immortal as God himself.  The serpent in that story
> is not specifically identified as Satan but is usually accepted to be.

Among Christians it is common to speak of God being "eternal", having no 
beginning or end. God is a "spirit". Created beings such as Satan have a 
beginning. It seems to me that the Bible describes all spiritual beings 
(e.g. Satan and other angels, fallen or not) as having no end of existence. 
Angels are "spirit beings". Man is a created being with a body, soul and 
spirit (no, I don't want to quibble about the precise definitions of these 
:-). Genesis says that God "breathed" His spirit into Adam and made him a 
"living soul". In both the Hebrew Old Testament and Greek New Testament, the 
word for "breath", as in the breath you breathe, is also used for "spirit". 
The spirits of men will also exist without end. It appears that the "spirit" 
is the essence of life that never ceases to exist.

Animals (Ecclesiastes 3:21) are said to have a "spirit" that goes down to 
the ground. Apparently that means they do not continue to exist after 
physical death.
```

- **References:** `<1132756229.533877.277650@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <Z7kjf.44667$6y4.17321@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <b2ljf.3756$Et5.224858@news20.bellglobal.com> <yMljf.44677$6y4.43720@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <438E2529.647B6DE1@mts.net> <11ou69ibsdf8s06@news.supernews.com> <ldduo11tgjo6tlt11vb3k1qdhelmd4ehi7@4ax.com> <amPjf.47033$6y4.4957@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <OlQjf.2377$ki.1407@pd7tw2no> <bWYjf.47091$6y4.12872@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4390A849.BFEA168D@mts.net> <r83kf.47826$6y4.274@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4ebkf.48013$6y4.2744@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4391E491.4F910527@mts.net> <9Bpkf.51276$6y4.13244@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <43923888.E48F76B3@mts.net> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <439A18D3.5D58C0EC@mts.net>`

```
"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>> >
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> God.

Your words:

>> > The Bible, after all, is supposed to be God's word.  As such, there is
>> > no way it needs to suffer from all sorts of human faults.  I can't
>> > imagine how God would want it any other way.

If you aren't trying to interpret the mind of God, that what was the purpose 
of your statement "I can't imagine how God would want it any other way"? If 
you point was to simply express your inadequacy to understand God's reasons, 
then I agree. But it seems very clear to me, from that and the rest of your 
comments, that you were attempting to refute the Bible being God's Word 
because it doesn't match the personal intellectual reasoning you are trying 
to applying to God. The problem, it seems to me, is that you aren't allowing 
for the possibility that God has perfectly good reasons for wanting it that 
way. When dealing with an omniscent, omnipotent God, we aren't in a position 
to make a determination of these issues through our own understanding. We 
would have to base any such reasoning on what God has told us about Himself. 
Since I believe the Bible is God's Word, I would base my own opinions on 
that. If you believe the Bible isn't God's Word, then you need a much better 
point on which to reason that just your human understanding. This is my 
point. :-)

Consider this. Leaving aside questions of the supernatural for a moment, 
consider this. Mathematics is as close to an absolute as anything we know. 
But is 2+2 *always* = 4? Could there be a universe in which 2+2= something 
else? I suspect most people would believe even God could not change such 
things, but I'm not so sure. We are so bound up and limited by our existence 
in this universe, how can we even imagine what parameters would involved in 
creating a universe from nothing? Can you picture 4 dimensions in your mind? 
How about 25 dimensions? If God did create this universe, then He has no 
problems with any of that. How could we possibly have any idea what the 
reasoning of such a God would be, apart from revelation from God Himself? 
Anything we came up with on our own would be *more futile* than a microbe 
speculating about the nature of a man, and deciding what things would be 
reasonable for a man to do or not do, and why.

>  I've just given my opinion.  And I notice you STILL  haven't
> attempted to answer the question.

I'm sorry, details sometimes get lost in these long threads. Which question 
is that? :-)
```

- **Message-ID:** `<1134308443.431201.227430@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<cXpmf.482$w7.96@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<ldduo11tgjo6tlt11vb3k1qdhelmd4ehi7@4ax.com> <amPjf.47033$6y4.4957@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <OlQjf.2377$ki.1407@pd7tw2no> <bWYjf.47091$6y4.12872@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4390A849.BFEA168D@mts.net> <r83kf.47826$6y4.274@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4ebkf.48013$6y4.2744@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4391E491.4F910527@mts.net> <9Bpkf.51276$6y4.13244@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <43923888.E48F76B3@mts.net> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <439A18D3.5D58C0EC@mts.net> <cXpmf.482$w7.96@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote:
> Consider this. Leaving aside questions of the supernatural for a moment,
> consider this. Mathematics is as close to an absolute as anything we know.
> But is 2+2 *always* = 4? Could there be a universe in which 2+2= something
> else?

2+2 always = 4. You are quoting an example from mathematics which has
conventions defined in which, Intel chips aside, always will produce
the result of 4. In another universe there may be other conventions
whereby 2+2=3 (or 5) and not 4. In another universe there may be no
concept of God or Allah...

> I suspect most people would believe even God could not change such
> things, but I'm not so sure.

Assuming that there is or was a God, then an omnipotent being could
bring about the initial conditions necessary to ensure that 2+2=3 with
ease.

> We are so bound up and limited by our existence
> in this universe, how can we even imagine what parameters would involved in
> creating a universe from nothing?

Not at  the moment, but we are working on it. Currently, it is thought
that this universe is just one in a long series of universes some of
which would be of a nature that we could not possibly contemplate and
understand them. Which brings us to one trick that you have missed
here: the Cosmologic Anthropic Principle.

> Can you picture 4 dimensions in your mind?

Easy. Up/down, left/right, forwards/backwards, and time. Although there
is some debate as to whether time is a dimension or just an abstraction
of the other three.

> How about 25 dimensions?

I thought there were only 11.

> If God did create this universe, then He has no
> problems with any of that.

This is unproven. To use a motor car I do not need to know the
intricacies of the internal combustion engine. God does not need to
understand the principles of the method of Creation (is it object
oriented?) merely how to use them.

> How could we possibly have any idea what the
> reasoning of such a God would be, apart from revelation from God Himself?
> Anything we came up with on our own would be *more futile* than a microbe
> speculating about the nature of a man, and deciding what things would be
> reasonable for a man to do or not do, and why.

You're deliberately ham-stringing yourself here. Because we do not
currently understand the motivation of a supreme being does not prevent
us from attempting to understand Him/Her/It. How do you know that we
can not understand God unless you try to understand Him/Her/It.
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>> If God did create this universe, then He has no
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> oriented?) merely how to use them.

If there is only God and nothing else, which is what the Bible declares was 
the original state of affairs, then the concept of God 'applying principles' 
apart from Himself is meaningless. If the Bible is correct, then God *is* 
all principle.

>> How could we possibly have any idea what the
>> reasoning of such a God would be, apart from revelation from God Himself?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> can not understand God unless you try to understand Him/Her/It.


Well, I certainly think we should want to understand God, or 'a god', as you 
would put it. But if the God of the Bible does exist, and everything else 
that exists was created by Him, as the Bible declares, then He entirely 
transcends anything we know or can imagine, being limited here in this one 
tiny portion of this universe, and limited by our finite minds. On what 
basis, or using what example, would you suggest that we, finite man, could 
know infinite God on our own? Perhaps we're simply hashing opinions here, 
but it seems to me that being able to understand (comprehend) that there is 
such a thing as infinity is quite a long way from being able to 
understanding (comprehend) infinity itself. And if the Bible is true, then 
God could (metaphorically) "hold infinity in His hand" since He created 
infinity along with everything else associated with this universe. Without a 
universe, even the concepts of time and infinity are meaningless. Are you 
suggesting that finite, physical man could somehow on his own "get his 
intellect around" these issues and understand a spiritual God Who (before 
Creation) existed alone, apart from *anything* else? I'm sorry, but the 
expression "biting off more than one can chew" comes to mind here. :-)
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> > Judson McClendon wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> all principle.

Just to be picky, the Bible states "in the beginning...."  of what?
Time, the Universe, what? If it said "in the absolute beginning.." To
use this thread as an example, it has a beginning but there were other
threads before it; and other newsgroups. Is the Biblical beginning an
absolute start to everything and if so: What preceded it?

>
> Well, I certainly think we should want to understand God, or 'a god', as you
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> know infinite God on our own?

If a God existed then I doubt if any man could Know God but that should
not stop any man from attempting to understand their supreme being.
After all, if you can not understand God in any part then there is no
point to existance (for any religious person).

> Perhaps we're simply hashing opinions here,
> but it seems to me that being able to understand (comprehend) that there is
> such a thing as infinity is quite a long way from being able to
> understanding (comprehend) infinity itself.

I have an absolute proof that infinite does not exist. In an infinite
universe there would be any infinite of Doc Dwarfs. Surely that is too
horrible to comprehend and therefore (adopts ostrich pose) proves
infinite does not exist?

Failure to make the effort to understand infinite condemns you to be
bound to the finite for all time.

> And if the Bible is true, then
> God could (metaphorically) "hold infinity in His hand" since He created
> infinity along with everything else associated with this universe.

The expression would lead to the consideration that, in having a hand,
God could not be infinite as he has distinct bounds. But I know what
you meant.

> Without a
> universe, even the concepts of time and infinity are meaningless.

Excepting where a (mythical) God existed.

> Are you
> suggesting that finite, physical man could somehow on his own "get his
> intellect around" these issues and understand a spiritual God Who (before
> Creation) existed alone, apart from *anything* else? I'm sorry, but the
> expression "biting off more than one can chew" comes to mind here. :-)

Yes I am suggesting that. At least the finite man should trouble
himself to make the effort if he does truly believe in God. Isn't that
what monks are supposed to do when they meditate upon the face of God?

> --
> Judson McClendon      judmc@sunvaley0.com (remove zero)
> Sun Valley Systems     http://sunvaley.com
> "For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Religions

_(reply depth: 44)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-12-15T19:13:13+00:00

```
Alistair wrote:
> James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I'm trying desperately to get this away from the thread title "Java" 
:-). Of course we could stick with Java if Oliver is prepared to use 
some of his abstract classes to create an instance of a 'God'. Hell ! He 
could even have a crack at Multiple Inheritance, and different 
attributes/properties - then we could all be happy.

I agree the two can dove-tail but not if you try and apply scientific 
logic. If you like, religion is not dissimilar to art (painting) - which 
may leave a lot of people just 'cold'; similarly music. I personally can 
get a lot of joy from art or music - it just doesn't make sense to try 
and define them in scientific terms.

Well we know in the case of art that there is an element of geometry - 
if you can see two sides of a rectangular object then both sides can't 
be shown to be horizontal. With music, the element of sound, 
reverberation etc., all explained scientifically, but not an explanation 
of the emotion one can feel for music.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 41)_

- **From:** "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

- **In-Reply-To:** `<dnn1v9$cok$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<OlQjf.2377$ki.1407@pd7tw2no> <1134308443.431201.227430@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <tBXmf.1505$w7.392@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134493231.926875.300290@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dnn1v9$cok$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <1134493231.926875.300290@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>,
> Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> asking 'what precedes 'the first'?'.

I am reasonably committed to the big bang theory (I know it has
problems) but I can still ask "What preceded the big bang?" There is a
theory that this universe is just one in a long series of universes
linked by big bangs and big crunches. The usual belief is that all
information about one universe is destroyed in the big crunch and that
we can not know what preceded the big bang. I suspect that time will
show that not all information is destroyed at the end of one universe
any more than that black holes destroy information. Therefore, to me at
least, it seems reasonable to ask what kind of beginning was this and
what existed/happened before the beginning. Maybe what I am getting at
is something along the lines of: "Who or what created God?"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 42)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-13T19:24:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CdFnf.146369$y_1.51501@edtnps89>`
- **References:** `<OlQjf.2377$ki.1407@pd7tw2no> <1134308443.431201.227430@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <tBXmf.1505$w7.392@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134493231.926875.300290@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dnn1v9$cok$1@reader2.panix.com> <1134499245.930105.283760@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1134499245.930105.283760@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
>
> I am reasonably committed to the big bang theory (I know it has
> problems) but I can still ask "What preceded the big bang?"

    I made a post about this elsewhere in this thread.

> There is a
> theory that this universe is just one in a long series of universes
> linked by big bangs and big crunches. The usual belief is that all
> information about one universe is destroyed in the big crunch and that
> we can not know what preceded the big bang.

    I'm not a cosmologist, but it's hard for me to imagine a form of 
information that can exist without space-time. It is said that there is no 
space-time before the big-bang, so I wonder if truly any information at all 
could escape a big crunch.

> I suspect that time will
> show that not all information is destroyed at the end of one universe
> any more than that black holes destroy information.

    Actually, from what I understand, black holes DO destroy information; 
everything except mass, anyway. The way I heard the story, physcists first 
postulated that nothing can ever escape from a black hole, and so therefore 
black holes never emit anything (hence the "black" in black hole). However, 
observations showed some black holes actually decreasing in mass over time. 
I think this is where the suddenly popularity of worm-hole theories came 
from; the thought was that the matter must be escaping into some alternate 
universe or something.

    But then they realized that what was happening was that spontaneously a 
particle of matter and anti matter would appear near a black hole 
(spontaneous matter/anti-matter particle creation happens all the time, 
apparently), and that sometimes the anti-matter particle would get sucked 
into the black hole (and thus annihilate a small part of it), while the 
matter particle would escape, thus giving the "illusion" that the black hole 
was emitting matter.

    Thus what is "escaping" from the black hole is purely random noise, and 
contains no information about the original matter that has actually 
collapsed into the hole.

> Therefore, to me at
> least, it seems reasonable to ask what kind of beginning was this and
> what existed/happened before the beginning. Maybe what I am getting at
> is something along the lines of: "Who or what created God?"

    I attended a lecture where the speaker claimed to have come up with a 
logical proof for the existence of God. Long story short, his proof was that 
there had to exist some initial thing, and that thing is God.

    If you follow that line of argument, then by definition, nothing created 
God. Here, the definition of God is "the first thing that created something 
which eventually resulted in our universe coming into existence".

    I didn't like his "proof". He takes something -- maybe I call it "Big 
Bang", and he calls it "God" -- shows that it exists, and then says "There, 
I've proved that God exists".

    He showed SOMETHING exists, and he chose to call it "God". But to claim 
having found a "logical proof of the existence of God" is very misleading, 
in my opinion. By the same token, I can prove the inexistence of God: Let's 
call my iPod "God". Oh, but wait, I don't actually own an iPod. Therefore, 
the term "my iPod" refers to something which doesn't exist. There, I've 
shown "God" doesn't exist.

    - Oliver
```

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
> news:1134499245.930105.283760@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>     I made a post about this elsewhere in this thread.

I admit to having skipped chunks of this ng thread.

>
> > There is a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> could escape a big crunch.

A friend once argued that the universe is digital, either on or off. It
filled a few hours down the pub. A bit is information, albeit not what
one would expect from a whole universe being destroyed and then
rebuilt. I have wondered if clumpiness of matter is an artefact of the
finishing state of the previous universe, ie at  the time of
destruction the universe is clumpy and this may be preserved in part
into the new universe.

>
> > I suspect that time will
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> universe or something.

The decreasing mass of black holes is due to 'evaporation' as material
is ejected in plumes from the poles. Stephen Hawking has recently
retracted his previous stance regarding the destruction of information
in black holes (and paid up on a long standing bet at the same time)
and concedes that information is conserved in a black hole. As to worm
holes, they are popular because it looks as if matter transporters and
faster than light drives will remain pure science fiction.

>
>     But then they realized that what was happening was that spontaneously a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> was emitting matter.

There was a theory about matter spontaneously being generated in a pure
vacuum from 'vacuum energy'. I haven't heard of matter generating
sponatneously near black holes.

>
>     Thus what is "escaping" from the black hole is purely random noise, and
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

A commonly held fallacy. My favourite proofs are:

1. The Bible says....
2. The Cosmologic Anthropic Principle.

I also like: 'How can you exist without believing in God? You must
believe in something!'
```

```
>
> Oliver Wong wrote:

>>
>>     Actually, from what I understand, black holes DO destroy information;
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> and concedes that information is conserved in a black hole.

    You are right. I knew of Hawking's bet, but I had forgotten the topic of 
the bet. From what I recall, though, Hawking lost on a "technicality" 
(though he paid whatever it was he had wagered; I recall it being a 
subscription to Penthouse or something like that), and then reformulated his 
bet with a new wording. I can't find a citation for this though. So as it 
stands, I am currently unsure as to what the belief is with regards to 
things escaping black holes (will perhaps do some more research on this over 
the weekend).

>
>>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> sponatneously near black holes.

    I don't know much about spontaneous generation of matter/anti-matter 
pairs, but I thought that was the core idea behind how black holes could 
seemingly emit something (anything). Again, I can't find a good citation for 
this, though Wikipedia vaguely alludes to it: 
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hawking_radiation

<quote>
Physical insight on the process [of Hawking Radiation] may be gained by 
imagining that (particle-antiparticle) radiation is emitted from just beyond 
the event horizon. This radiation does not come directly from the black hole 
itself, but rather is a result of virtual particles being "boosted" by the 
black hole's gravitation into becoming real particles.
</quote>

    - Oliver
```

- **References:** `<OlQjf.2377$ki.1407@pd7tw2no> <1134308443.431201.227430@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <tBXmf.1505$w7.392@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134493231.926875.300290@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dnn1v9$cok$1@reader2.panix.com> <1134499245.930105.283760@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <CdFnf.146369$y_1.51501@edtnps89> <1134504488.512436.275330@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <oJGnf.146382$y_1.48262@edtnps89> <1134512682.313508.239140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1134512682.313508.239140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com...
>
> Oliver Wong wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> This is beginning to sound like black magic to me.

    Well, I found a citation that specifically mentions matter/anti-matter 
pairs forming near black holes:

http://imagine.gsfc.nasa.gov/docs/features/news/29dec04.html

<quote>
Just outside the event horizon of a black hole, virtual particles may be 
created for a very short amount of time from quantum fluctuations in the 
vacuum of space. These particles are created in matter-antimatter pairs. If 
this occurs close to the event horizon, one of the members of the pair can 
fall into the black hole, while the other remains outside. This produces a 
"radiation" from the black hole that Hawking predicted.
</quote>

    - Oliver
```

- **References:** `<OlQjf.2377$ki.1407@pd7tw2no> <1134308443.431201.227430@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <tBXmf.1505$w7.392@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134493231.926875.300290@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dnn1v9$cok$1@reader2.panix.com> <1134499245.930105.283760@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <CdFnf.146369$y_1.51501@edtnps89> <1134504488.512436.275330@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <oJGnf.146382$y_1.48262@edtnps89> <1134512682.313508.239140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <62%nf.256585$ir4.200506@edtnps90> <1134601274.619367.317670@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1134601274.619367.317670@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> Oliver Wong wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> black magic to me.

    Conversation of matter/energy is still conserved: the vacuum energy in 
space is converted to matter temporarily (on the order of Planck-time) and 
usually these matter/anti-matter pair just immediately recollide back into 
each other to turn back into energy again. But sometimes a black hole eats 
one of the pair up, and so it looks like matter (or energy) is being 
radiated away from the black hole.

    As for "there is some belief that pure vacuums can spontaneously 
generate particles", I believe there is a "reproduceable" (assuming you have 
the proper equipment) experiment that you can perform that provides evidence 
for this belief. If you have the budget and the desire to try it yourself, I 
believe you can read on how to perform the experiment by googling the 
"Casimir effect".

    - Oliver
```

```
In article <1134504488.512436.275330@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>,
Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

[snip]

>I also like: 'How can you exist without believing in God? You must
>believe in something!'

A response to this might be 'I try to keep my beliefs simple... for 
example, I believe that all the electrons in all the atoms in all the 
molecules in all... errrrr, both my legs will *not* shift into 
configurational space when I next try to walk across the room... and this 
belief allows me to stand up and move with a bit less fear of my legs' 
suddenly disintergrating and the rest of me falling, painfully, to the 
ground.'

DD
```

```
>

Three different situations, Mr Maclean... in the first ('I have trouble 
convincing myself that I exist') perhaps one needs to ponder what is it 
that needs to be convinced (Descartes) or what is it that is attempting to 
do the convincing; in the former case to even say 'cogito' requires an 
'ego' and in the latter... well, somebody's sure doing something, might as 
well be the 'I'.

The second and third cases appears to require proof of a logical negative 
('this is not a holographic simulation/I am not dreaming if (x) can be 
demonstrated') and such proofs can get a bit... dicey.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 42)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2005-12-14T10:30:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dnos7l$4h2$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<OlQjf.2377$ki.1407@pd7tw2no> <1134493231.926875.300290@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dnn1v9$cok$1@reader2.panix.com> <1134499245.930105.283760@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1134499245.930105.283760@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>,
Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>linked by big bangs and big crunches.

'What preceded the big bang', Mr Maclean, is not, I would say, the same as 
asking 'what precedes 'the first'.  The big bang was an event, 'the first' 
is a condition.

>Therefore, to me at
>least, it seems reasonable to ask what kind of beginning was this and
>what existed/happened before the beginning. Maybe what I am getting at
>is something along the lines of: "Who or what created God?"

No need for that ('creation of God') at all, Mr Maclean, as that's one of 
the 'omni-' attributes... along with omnipotent, omniscient, 
omnibeneficent there's omnipresent, remember?  Such a condition is defined 
into Him/Her/It and it makes things so much easier.

Years ago I was shown a hymn that was based on Maimonides' thirteen 
principles of faith; it contained a poetic construction based on that 
beginning/first word with which Genesis opens (in Hebrew):

Rishon v'ayn rayshis l'rayshi-soh   (or, in a different dialect)
Rishon v'ayn rayshit l'rayshi-toh

First  and'not (a)beginning to'firstness/beginning-his

Maimonides, as we all know - he said, preparing to introduce things which 
he was uncertain of peoples' knowing - attempted, among other things, to 
reconcile Aristotle to the revelatory nature of the Bible ('God 
appeared/said/did').  Aristotle, as we all know, messed about a bit with 
the concept of 'The Unmoved Mover', that which can originate a cause 
without a prior cause... and here might be where things break down.

Science assumes that every effect must have a cause, every condition must 
have a prior; the attributes of the Divine, as given above, are, by 
nature, without cause... first and there's no beginning to his 
'firstness', that's all there is.

You see, Mr Maclean?  This is not science... this is religion and poetry.

DD
```

```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1134493231.926875.300290@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>

    I'm going to get a pick picky here too. ;)

> Is the Biblical beginning an
> absolute start to everything and if so: What preceded it?

    The same question is asked of Stephen Hawkins when he presents his 
theory for the Big Bang. He claims that time and space and inseperable 
concepts. Without space, there is no such thing as time. And before the Big 
Bang, there was no space. So there was no time before the Big Bang. So 
people ask "Well, what happened before the Big Bang?"

    To which Hawkins replies "That's like asking what's one mile north of 
the North Pole. The question is meaningless."

>
> I have an absolute proof that infinite does not exist. In an infinite
> universe there would be any infinite of Doc Dwarfs. Surely that is too
> horrible to comprehend and therefore (adopts ostrich pose) proves
> infinite does not exist?

    QED, though rather that proves that the universe is finite, rather than 
that the concept of "infinity" doesn't exist (what does it mean for a 
concept to exist anyway?)

>
> The expression would lead to the consideration that, in having a hand,
> God could not be infinite as he has distinct bounds. But I know what
> you meant.

    There are an infinite number of negative numbers, but there is a 
distinct bound: 0 (non-inclusive). You can extend this into 2D by taking all 
the points on the cartesian plane whose y value is negative, and you end up 
with an infinite 2D surface with a 1D boundary. You can extend the idea for 
an infinite 3D volume with a 2D surface, and so on.

    In all the above examples, the size of the boundary was infinite along 
with the size of the object being bounded. It's also possible to have a 
finite boundary with an infinite sized object. Here's how in 2D: Draw a 
circle. The circle is the boundary, and it's finite. Everything outside the 
circle is thing being bounded, which is infinite in size.

>
> Yes I am suggesting that. At least the finite man should trouble
> himself to make the effort if he does truly believe in God. Isn't that
> what monks are supposed to do when they meditate upon the face of God?

    I figure if God really didn't want us to try to understand Him, He 
wouldn't have given us the curiosity that drives us to understand Him.

    - Oliver
```

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

- **References:** `<OlQjf.2377$ki.1407@pd7tw2no> <bWYjf.47091$6y4.12872@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4390A849.BFEA168D@mts.net> <r83kf.47826$6y4.274@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4ebkf.48013$6y4.2744@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4391E491.4F910527@mts.net> <9Bpkf.51276$6y4.13244@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <43923888.E48F76B3@mts.net> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <439A18D3.5D58C0EC@mts.net> <cXpmf.482$w7.96@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134308443.431201.227430@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <tBXmf.1505$w7.392@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134493231.926875.300290@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1134493231.926875.300290@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...

> Just to be picky, the Bible states "in the beginning...."  of what?

If you want to get picky, it starts something like "brshyt bera' 'LHYM", and 
while the interpretation "in the beginning" has been traditional since the 
days of the Septuagint ("en arche", which John 1:1 I believe consciously 
echoes), it's not entirely clear that that's the only, or the only 
appropriate, opening.  One translation I know of runs something like "When 
God began creating the heavens and the earth ..." and I think the Hebrew 
comes closer to supporting that than it does the LXX.

> Time, the Universe, what? If it said "in the absolute beginning.." To
> use this thread as an example, it has a beginning but there were other
> threads before it; and other newsgroups. Is the Biblical beginning an
> absolute start to everything and if so: What preceded it?

In this case, the answer is "God", and v.3 provides a detail as to *how*.  I 
personally see no conflict between the first explicit step of creation and 
Einstein's e=mc**2.

I'm also not one to insist that the Torah was transmitted intact without a 
single letter changed from Sinai all the way through to the time of Herod. 
I find composition and compilation over a much longer period of time (and 
the Jahwist, Elohimist, Priestly and Deuteronomic aggregate authorship 
theories) much more likely.

There are those who would insist that such a view makes me not only not a 
Christian, but beyond that, *anti*-Christian.   Sigh.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** "charles hottel" <jghottel@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-14T21:55:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<26faa$43a0db18$4f9c602$23726@DIALUPUSA.NET>`
- **References:** `<ldduo11tgjo6tlt11vb3k1qdhelmd4ehi7@4ax.com> <amPjf.47033$6y4.4957@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <OlQjf.2377$ki.1407@pd7tw2no> <bWYjf.47091$6y4.12872@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4390A849.BFEA168D@mts.net> <r83kf.47826$6y4.274@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4ebkf.48013$6y4.2744@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4391E491.4F910527@mts.net> <9Bpkf.51276$6y4.13244@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <43923888.E48F76B3@mts.net> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <idilf.56223$s92.48841@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vqqidF16g774U1@individual.net> <RxZlf.195998$Io.442@clgrps13> <43987AEC.D06B40B3@mts.net> <Wlmmf.443$w7.410@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <439A18D3.5D58C0EC@mts.net> <cXpmf.482$w7.96@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <1134308443.431201.227430@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <tBXmf.1505$w7.392@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
Well it seems that finite men can understand  something about infinite sets 
and Georg Cantor even discovered a hierarchy of infinities. Donald Knuth in 
his book "Things a Computer Scientist Rarely Talks About" discusses 
finiteness and clearly shows that just being finite is not much of a 
restriction. He states that the word "infinite" occurs only three times in 
the KJV of the Bible and only one of those is about God (God's understanding 
is said to be infinite).  Quoting from page 172:

"But now I realize that infinity is not necessarily even one of God's 
attributes. I'm quite willing to grant God might indeed be infinite, and God 
might have the power to examine infinitely many possibilities in an instant. 
But even the ability to deal with finite numbers, on the order of Super K, 
is more than enough to inspire awe.

Moreover I don't think theologians can legitimately disagree with me on 
this. To say that God's abilities are not infinite, but limited by 
quantities like Super K, is not a realistic limitation at all. Such a 
limitation cannot contradict the Bible or any other sacred text, because 
natural language has no words to distinguish meaningfully between such 
unimaginable large magnitudes."

Well there is a lot of interesting material in this book. He discusses a 
sampling technique that you can use to gain insight into a complicated 
subject. For those who do not trust any Bible translation other that their 
own, he discusses how to translate Bible verses without knowing Hebrew or 
Greek.  This is the method he used in writing his book "3:16 Bible Texts 
Illuminated". He also discusses aesthetics and calligraphy, what he learned 
about God and theology and the difference between the two. Finally there is 
a section on God and computer science. Computer programmers as creators of 
new universes, computational complexity as a way to approach the questions 
of free will and omnipotence and other concepts of computer science that may 
give insights about divinity.

Well there may be as many interpretations of what the Bible says as there 
are human beings that have read it.  Anyway it seems that those with an 
interest and or belief in the Bible are unfairly characterized when 
described as "uneducated myth believers". If Donald Knuth is uneducated then 
we all are.



"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:tBXmf.1505$w7.392@bignews7.bellsouth.net...

<snip>

> Are you suggesting that finite, physical man could somehow on his own "get 
> his intellect around" these issues and understand a spiritual God Who 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:cXpmf.482$w7.96@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
>
> Mathematics is as close to an absolute as anything we know. But is 2+2 
> *always* = 4? Could there be a universe in which 2+2= something else? I 
> suspect most people would believe even God could not change such things, 
> but I'm not so sure.

    "2+2=4" is true by definition. Someone came up with the concept of an 
atomic quantity, and called it "1". Someone (perhaps the same person) then 
came up with an atomic incremention of the previous quantity; a quantity 
which is bigger, but for which there is no intermediate value, and called it 
"2".

    The "primitive" concept of addition could have been discovered 
empirically (e.g. "if I had 2 apples, and then I have another 2 apples, I 
end up with 4 apples. Is this always true? Yes, it does seem to always be 
true. I guess I can abstract the concept to '2+2=4'"). Our modern concept of 
addition is slightly different in that we now have concepts of zero, 
rational and irrational numbers, complex numbers, etc. all of which didn't 
exist until (relatively) recently.

    Another society might have (coicidentally) also called the quantity 
after 1 "2", and they might have (coincidentally) also chosen the symbol for 
addition to be "+", and the symbol for equality to be "=", but for whatever 
reasons, they chose the symbol for the quantity after the quantity after 2 
to be written as "3". So they would write "2+2=3". But they would 
conceptually mean the same thing as us, and there would exist some whole 
value between '2' and '3' so that '3' would be the fourth number in their 
counting system.

    Similarly, there might exist a society which uses a base-3 system 
instead of a base-10 system like we do, so they would write "2+2=11". But 
again, conceptually, they still mean that 2+2 is equal to the fourth value 
in the counting system, and there must exist exactly one value between '2' 
and '11' (which is '10' in this case).

    The only way God could change this is if He were to cause it so that 
logic does not hold, or if he were to change one of the axioms on which 
Mathematics is based (I believe that the concept of addition relies on an 
axiomatic zero, so God could perhaps change the concept of zero so that 
"2+2!=4" and yet maintain a logically-consistent universe, but I cannot 
currently think of a way to do this yet).

    - Oliver
```

```
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
>> ("...sometimes difficult, perhaps even impossible to express *exactly*
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> express the same statement in two different languages.

As it happens, so do I... :-)

Oliver,

This is the second post of mine which you have taken seriously, when it was
meant to be tongue-in-cheek.

I really can't discuss these things too seriously in a forum such as
this.The only bit I was really serious about was the need for a blood
sacrifice, and the position that, as God is infinite and we are not, there
is no way to understand Him, other than to accept His inspired Word. (The
Bible). (I have problems with any approach that forbids free thinking.)

Just bear in mind that my posts here are purely for entertainment; if there
is loose logicality, that is because I'm being flippant.

What I really think was laid out in the Post that Jimmy alluded to.

No minds will be changed in this thread. (they don't get changed on COBOL,
never mind deep philosophical matters :-)), and the entrenched positions are
really a matter of personal choice, so the only real reason I could see for
posting here would be for fun. (And perhaps an innate sense of mischief...).
I know there are people here (in this thread) who ARE serious and that's OK
too...

I'm just not one of them.

>
>    It is not sufficient to prove Judson's statement to be false by a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, I agree it was a well-stated case.

>    To show that this is false would require some intricate proof that
> gives a mapping for every statement in every language to some statement in
> every other language. Probably not feasible.

But it cannot be proven to be infeasible. And it is certainly true that
translators (both Human and computer) make a living by attempting to do just
that.

And you wouldn't have to map to every other language... If it can be
translated with equivalent meaning in ONE other language the case is lost.
>
>    To "prove" that the statement is true, one would only need to give an
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
OK, I'll have a go...

It might be possible using nicknames which are understood in English to have
various connotations: "Nancy" for a wimpy or Gay man, "Buster" or "Spike"
for a hard man, and so on...

"You will need to toughen up and take care of yourself..."
"OK, so I'll change my name..."Nancy" Jones puts the washing in the machine;
"Buster" Jones tells someone else to take it out and put it on the line..."
"Hahaha! I don't think "Buster" will suit you at all..."

Language simply represents concepts (that's why new, mainly English, words
are coined to keep up with new technological and scientific inventions and
advances. These words in either original or modified form, find their way
into other languages, not because they are "untranslatable", but because
re-using them saves inventing new words in the target language. The French,
of course DO invent a new French word for each case, but by the time they
have done so, the original English has established itself so no-one uses the
new French one... :-))

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 37)_

- **From:** "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2005-12-13T09:28:47-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
In article <40i845F1agn0jU1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Exactly. Gaze on it and tremble... :-)

Be careful how far one takes that analogy, Mr Dashwood.  If I recall 
correctly the Ceremony of Manhood for some who hold the Old Testament dear 
includes demonstrating the ability to read from the original texts and 
then delivering an exegesis on what has been read (the Jewish customs of 
'reading the parasha' and 'making the bar mitzvah speech', the latter 
beginning with the traditional formulation of 'Today I am a man.')

(a common gift on this occaision, a few decades back, was a fountain pen, 
leading to the joke about the 13-year-old, exhausted and bashful, starting 
his speech with 'Today I am a fountain pen.')

At any rate... nowhere is one enjoined to reproducing the constructs of 
the text and one is most strongly enjoined against rewriting them in a 
different style; the Sacred Texts are *not* to be changed.

DD
```

```
On Sat, 17 Dec 2005 15:16:27 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
>At any rate... nowhere is one enjoined to reproducing the constructs of 
>the text and one is most strongly enjoined against rewriting them in a 
>different style; the Sacred Texts are *not* to be changed.

Babbling again aren't you?

http://www.bidstrup.com/bible.htm
The Deuteronomist and the Second Great Revision, With the Rise of the
Temple State and the Third Great Revision 
742-600 B.C.E.
It is at least a century after the first books of the Pentatuch was
written that the gods of the Old Testament are harmonized into a
singular being, this having been done by the third major writer of the
Old Testament books, a writer (or more probably group of writers)
called by scholars, "D" the Deuteronomist. If we are going to have a
monotheistic religion here, we can't go around having two competing
gods, so something must be done. The tribes of Israel and Judah had a
choice to make, and Joshua warned them that Yahweh was a jealous god.
Which god would it be? In essence, there was no difficulty making a
choice. Yahweh was the more powerful, having demonstrated his power by
intervening on their behalf in Egypt, and in the desert at Sinai. The
choice was easy. It was Yahweh.

So the second great revision of Judaic religion has happened. In the
original Pentatuch, written in the 8th century B.C.E., there isn't a
clearly monotheistic statement to be found, but by the time of the
writings of the Deuteronomist, a century or so later, the
Deuteronomist has Joshua threatening the Israelites and making sure
they became monotheistic under threat of being destroyed. The
Deuteronomist pulls off this neat harmonization of two competing gods
by having the Israelites reminded that their fathers had promised
Yahweh that he would be their god, and so they made him their elohim,
their high God. So now, Elohim, who originally was the king of the
gods of Fertile Crescent, is now Yahweh, the god of Israel. If you
have two conflicting gods, its a neat trick to just get rid of the
conflict by declaring they're the same being.4
```

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:3vvn9bF17q871U3@individual.net...
>
> This is the second post of mine which you have taken seriously, when it 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> is loose logicality, that is because I'm being flippant.

    I'm not sure how to take your "you have taken seriously" comment... 
While I'm not laughing as I write these posts, I'm also not trying to 
convert people to/against religion either (as can be seen, perhaps, by my 
jumping back and forth between "sides" of this argument). As someone pointed 
out earlier, I just like thinking about this kind of stuff.

    I think what I'm doing is like those articles you occasionally see 
written by scientists explaining how it's feasible for Santa Claus to 
deliver toys to all the children all over the world in one night. They point 
out that it's not simultaneously night all over the world, so Santa can 
actually do one time zone at a time, and how the rate at which he travels is 
well below the speed of light, etc. It's sort of a "Let's actually consider 
this seriously for a moment and see how far we can take it" type of train of 
thought.

[snip]
>
>>    To show that this is false would require some intricate proof that
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> that.

    Yes, but I think the computers are only doing a subset of the full 
problem. E.g. they may be using look-up tables and only capable of 
translating pre-known sentence structures.

> And you wouldn't have to map to every other language... If it can be
> translated with equivalent meaning in ONE other language the case is lost.

    If the claim is that there exists some statement in some language that 
is difficult to translate in some other language, then you do need a mapping 
for every other language. I believe this claim is closer in spirit to 
Judson's original claim, because it avoids the difficulty of 
sibling-languages (e.g. Quebec French and France French) for which it might 
be very easy to provide a mapping between every statement in one language to 
another.

>>    In Japanese, there are multiple pronouns for the first person 
>> singular.
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Hahaha! I don't think "Buster" will suit you at all..."

    Perhaps I am biased, but I claim that this does not faithfully reproduce 
the original meaning. To me, changing one's name has all sorts of other 
connotations not present in changing the pronoun used to refer to oneself. 
In just changing the pronoun, you are only changing how you see yourself, 
whereas in changing the name, you are sort of forcing others to accept and 
acknowledge the change. E.g. the character telling his mom "Stop calling me 
Nancy! My name is Buster now!"

    Changing the name also implies to me a much more radical change (e.g. it 
brings up images of going to court to apply for new ID cards, etc.) and 
perhaps dual-identities, or secret identities. If the story went that way, 
the character might, 15 years later, encounter a mysterious man who says "I 
know who you REALLY are... Nancy..." and all the melodrama that follows.

    Changing the pronoun is much more subtle, and might actually happen 
commonly in Japanese society as people grow older (e.g. "boku" when a child, 
"ore" when an rebelious adolescent, and then "watashi" when entering into 
the mid-life, etc.) (or maybe it doesn't happen, I don't know Japanese 
culture well enough to be sure, but I have trouble picturing a 5 year old 
child referring to himself as "ore", so I figure it MUST change at some 
point) so it might not be seen as such a big deal. A character may even 
change the pronoun depending on who he's speaking to (e.g. when speaking to 
the Emperor of Japan versus when speaking to an annoying drunkard at a pub).

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jIklf.155638$yS6.68052@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<1132756229.533877.277650@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <9d2b9$438bd9a3$45491c57$12306@KNOLOGY.NET> <EF5jf.652377$1i.549767@pd7tw2no> <Go6jf.38533$Y82.6701@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <438CF9AE.831AF4B4@mts.net> <mdbjf.662053$tl2.246460@pd7tw3no> <Z7kjf.44667$6y4.17321@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <b2ljf.3756$Et5.224858@news20.bellglobal.com> <yMljf.44677$6y4.43720@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <438E2529.647B6DE1@mts.net> <11ou69ibsdf8s06@news.supernews.com> <ldduo11tgjo6tlt11vb3k1qdhelmd4ehi7@4ax.com> <amPjf.47033$6y4.4957@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <OlQjf.2377$ki.1407@pd7tw2no> <bWYjf.47091$6y4.12872@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4390A849.BFEA168D@mts.net> <r83kf.47826$6y4.274@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <dmqjq8$29p3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4ebkf.48013$6y4.2744@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <4391E491.4F910527@mts.net> <9Bpkf.51276$6y4.13244@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <43923888.E48F76B3@mts.net> <wT8lf.54020$s92.31937@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net...
>
> There was this infinite all powerful Being who decided He might as well 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> CLC... maybe He has... maybe this is it...

    If there exists an omnipotent being (nevermind for now whether we want 
to call this being "God" or something else), then any train of (formal, ) 
logical thought we might want to follow quickly becomes uninteresting: This 
being can do anything. If we try to prove that the being could not do 
something because it would lead to some sort of logical contradiction, we 
just have to pull out the original assumption that the being is omnipotent 
and thus can defy logical consistency.

    Omnipotence also implies omniscience, because if the being wanted to be 
omniscient, then that being could cause itself to become omniscient. And I 
think any sufficiently intelligent omnipotent being would always choose to 
become omniscient, because it would then guarantee that the every action or 
decision the being makes from then on is optimal (in a game-theory sense).

    So an omnipotent being is not so interesting to think about. More 
interesting is to consider an omniscient being who is not omnipotent. 
Omniscience does not nescessarily imply omnipotence: If there existed a way 
for the being to "make itself omnipotent", then the omniscient being would 
obviously know it, and could do exactly that; but it's also possible that 
given the circumstances, it turned out to be impossible for the being to 
make itself omnipotent (and the being would also know that, if it were the 
case).

    Now assume an omniscient being, who is not omnipotent, and who has 
desires. That is, the being wants some condition to be true, but it is not 
yet true (if the being wants something to be false, that is equivalent to 
wanting its negation to be true). Assuming it is possible for the condition 
to be ture, being omniscient, the being would know what it would have to do 
to cause that condition to become true.

    Perhaps part of what it has to do is speak with Judson.

    As such, it's perfectly reasonable for a being to know in advance 
everything Judson is going to say, know in advance everything it will say in 
responce, and know in advance how Judson will react, etc. and yet the being 
would still do it anyway. Such actions would not nescessarily be useless.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 34)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-06T20:47:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1Omlf.155656$yS6.10887@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<OlQjf.2377$ki.1407@pd7tw2no> <3vl8n2F169mq1U1@individual.net> <jIklf.155638$yS6.68052@clgrps12> <i3obp1pmuul6eeojvp7r2t5pl9eegebipp@4ax.com> <dn4nvs$hfg$1@reader2.panix.com> <7aqbp1tgc8136hdc3thi11d1d197opkkds@4ax.com>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:7aqbp1tgc8136hdc3thi11d1d197opkkds@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 6 Dec 2005 19:15:08 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nothing's too late for the omnipotent.

    This is exactly the sort of trouble you run into when trying to reason 
logically about an omnipotent being, which is why I usually avoid doing so.

    The omnipotent being might wonder whether or not it is more fun to be 
omniscient or not, and so chooses to temporarily become omniscient to find 
out the answer. If the being then finds out it's actually more fun to be 
omniscient, then it may choose to continue to do so (or it may choose not 
to, whatever) and that's pretty much the end of this discussion.

    But if the being finds out it's more fun to not know, it can then force 
itself to forget everything it learned while omniscient.

    But then it would wonder again whether it was more fun or not to be 
omniscient, and re-enable the omniscience again, ending up in an infinite 
loop.

    But if this were the case, it would realize, while omniscient, that the 
infinite loop would occur, and if it wanted to avoid this loop (assuming 
that this loop was not the very essence of the "fun" it was looking for), it 
could do so too, perhaps by causing itself to forget everything it learned 
and, additionally, arranging fate so that the thought "Hmm, I wonder if it 
would be more fun to be omniscient or not" would never enter its head.

    So now the being is omnipotent, not omniscient, and it would never occur 
to it to wonder to itself whether it would be more fun to be omniscient or 
not, and it would never realize that it was through its own doing that this 
thought would never enter into its head.

    - Oliver
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Tue, 06 Dec 2005 20:47:57 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> made and why.   No need to go whole hog.

Or when you personally get up there 'it' could say to you,

"I am not an 'it' I am IT. In fact I'm not IT nor am I He or She but ME. 
And as to forgetting, I forget nothing. That computer language down 
there you insisted on calling Cobol. Do you think that was a mere human 
thought that out. I put the idea into the head of one of the DoD people 
- COBOL. So here's a very large scroll and a quill that you can dip into 
gold ink. Now start writing 10,000 times. It is called COBOL. Repeat 
after me, it is called COBOL.........."

Then ME looks over ME's shoulder, "Well done thou good and faithful 
servant", as ME looks kindly at Bill K. "You tried real hard to keep 
them on track about C-O-B-O-L. Still I have to say William, I wasn't too 
thrilled when you referred to ME as being a She. I know, I know, I 
allowed feminism to happen. But even my tolerance has limits".

Jimmy
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:30vbp1t7e6md1mu31rab94q187oqo1dgcr@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 06 Dec 2005 20:47:57 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> made and why.   No need to go whole hog.

    True, but perhaps the "why" would reveal to it what it was that was 
forgotten. It might have to place a mind-block on itself so that it could 
not decude the "what" from the "why", or it might simply remember the 
choice, but forget the why and the what together. But then it would have to 
trust itself that it made the right choice, since it can no longer remember 
why it had made that choice (perhaps it can satiate itself with the memory 
that when the choice was made, it was omniscient at the time, so surely it 
must have been the best choice, whatever that choice was).

    - Oliver
```

```
Oliver Wong wrote:

Oliver, no wonder you like the abstractness of Java :-) I think I 
followed you though....

>     As such, it's perfectly reasonable for a being to know in advance 
> everything Judson is going to say, know in advance everything it will say in 
> responce, and know in advance how Judson will react, etc. and yet the being 
> would still do it anyway. Such actions would not nescessarily be useless.

That's Judson's belief above as it is mine. But don't ask me to try and 
explain it logically. Often I find myself saying "How could He allow 
9/11 to happen ...", or Darfur, or the Tsunami etc... Or on another tack 
- if He already knew Judas Iscariot was going to betray Jesus and 
subsequently hang himself, why did He allow either event to happen. We 
in our respective ways have what we call 'Faith', even though I am no 
longer a church-goer.

What Judson doesn't get is that some/many people do not have 'Faith' 
whether it be Christianity or some other religion. Agnostics - like St. 
Thomas when told that Jesus had arisen, "Show me.......". Prove it 
logically then I might, just might have a change of thought.

Atheists. People who independently have thought it through for 
themselves and applying tangible earth-bound logic, reject the whole 
notion of any supreme being, regardless of which branch of religion - to 
them it is completely contra to scientific fact. Somewhat reluctantly, 
at my request, Pete Dashwood spelled out his particular path and I 
understand.

Even within that 'Faith' that Judson and I share - I can still pick up 
differences. In response to Peter Lacey, Judson suggested (a) If there 
is no such a thing as a God, then none of us is harmed, and (b) If there 
IS a God, you poor suckers that didn't believe, you are condemned to 
damnation. Well (b) is the one with which I take exception. That is just 
not my belief nor a tenet of my religion, Catholicism; if Chuck can dig 
it out - a quote from a pre-Reformation Pope, and the Vatican in the 
centuries that have followed, hasn't changed that pope's dictum one iota.

Judson you are on a hiding to nothing proselytizing your religion here. 
I don't know how 'Making money from Java' took this route. I think 
initially some definition of conservatism, which in turn became 
religious conservatism and then we are off to the races - and nothing 
gets resolved.

I'll take money you aren't going to get one convert. Two months back, 
walking the dogs, saw two young Mormon missionaries - easy to spot by 
their 'uniforms' - dressed soberly in black while the rest of the world 
goes casual. "Hello", I said, "Two young Mormen". We chatted and I 
finally asked, "What sort of success rate do you have on making 
converts". One was already primed. "One in 10,000 if we are lucky".

Let's get back to the political aspect if you like, the rights of humans 
to express religion in their own way within a democratic society - we 
can all understand that - but start quoting chapter and verse from the 
Jewish Bible or the Christian New Testament - your score card will be zero.

Jimmy
```

```
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

> I did not indend or purposely try to direct the thread to this point. I was 
> only trying to answer challenges and questions, and it wound up here. If you 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> very hostile anti-God/anti-Christian comments made.

Fair enough. That's probably how it went.
> 
> You may be right. I did have the priviledge of leading a person to Jesus as 
> the result of an exchange back in the 90's in another newsgroup, which 
> started when a few people began lambasting me for my SIG. He emailed me and 
> asked "How can I get to know this Jesus?"

Nicely put, "I did have the privilege". Any success with him ? So you've 
been at this lark before in other NewsGroups where your byline got 
attacked. (Mischievous Judson :-) ). Well I doubt your above convert was 
Alistair, but he was the atheist who DEFENDED your right here to use 
your byline.

> 
>>Let's get back to the political aspect if you like, the rights of humans 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> if you folks are. :-)

Yes I think we've all  pretty well drained both. Still you are welcome 
to chip in when I add something to OT: McCarthyism. Meanwhile get that 
journalist daughter of yours to do some independent research by 
googling. There's oodles of stuff if you can be impartial and try and 
sort the wheat from the chaff.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 36)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-12-11T10:26:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CoYmf.1519$w7.1296@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<yMljf.44677$6y4.43720@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <X9olf.252$wP3.100@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <kTolf.52294$ki.37607@pd7tw2no> <1134309763.012354.283060@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dnhc6b$mjb$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
> Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> respond to *everything*, I know.


Sorry, I must have missed those proposals. My view on this is simple. Just 
as in all civil freedoms, the State has no business abridging religious 
freedom, as long as that freedom causes no harm to others. 'Harm' *does not* 
include one's not liking religious views being expressed, or simply being 
exposed to the fact of the exercise of those freedoms, such as seeing it 
happen in a public place (e.g. prayer). But that doesn't mean I want the 
State pushing religion, either.

On a side issue, I am *definitely against* what are called "hate crime" 
laws, because they amount to making personal opinions a criminal issue. 
People should be punished because of their actions, not their feelings and 
opinions. The crime is the business of the law, the hate isn't. Is it better 
to kill someone because you think they're ugly, or because you think it's 
fun, than because you don't like their race or sexual practices? I hardly 
think so.

Is that a sufficient response?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making money from Java

_(reply depth: 37)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2005-12-12T01:18:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dnij50$n3v$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<yMljf.44677$6y4.43720@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <1134309763.012354.283060@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dnhc6b$mjb$1@reader2.panix.com> <CoYmf.1519$w7.1296@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <CoYmf.1519$w7.1296@bignews7.bellsouth.net>,
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
>> Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>State pushing religion, either.

Here's the situation, Mr McClendon... as originally posted in 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/ab8c49fbfa814779?dmode=source&hl=en>

--begin quoted text:

'Free exercise of religion' on government property... there's the rub, Mr 
McClendon.  It's one thing for two folks to have a chat about how many 
angels can dance on the head of a pin... another for a group to gather to 
sing hymns of praise to their Creator (in multi-part harmony, of 
course)...  and still another to erect a forty foot tall cross with 
flashing red and green lights... and yet each of these is a 'free exercise 
of religion'.  At some point even the most enlightened constabulary might 
say '*You might call it freely exercising your religion by a literal 
demonstrating of Mark.XVI:17-18... but rattlers are dangerous and there's 
young'uns in this park so how about you close up shop, *now*, or come 
downtown and face charges for violating... oh, Municipal Ordinance 123.45, 
Maintaining A Menagerie Without a Permit?'

As was stated in 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/6f0d4734091d086d?dmode=source&hl=en>

--begin quoted text:

You don't ask my tax dollars to go to support your interpretations of 
these admittedly imperfect relatings of what you are calling 'divine' and 
I don't ask your tax dollars to do the same with... whatever I come up 
with, as well.

(Note that when I say 'support' I use it in the broadest sense, including 
but not limited to such things as the wall of a building paid for by your 
taxes is not be used as a base for broadsheets for my church's functions, 
the salary of public officials paid for by your taxes is not to be used 
for their implementing of my religions tenets or mouthing of their 
preachings, the land paid for by your taxes is not to be used for displays 
of my religion's icons.  That would seem to be fair enough, wouldn't you 
say?)

--end quoted text

--end quoted text

[snip]

>Is that a sufficient response?

Given what has been cited above... perhaps so, perhaps no.

DD
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>> You may be right. I did have the priviledge of leading a person to Jesus 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nicely put, "I did have the privilege". Any success with him ?

Thank you, it is a privelege. Yes, we kept in contact for over a year, but I 
haven't heard from him in quite a while now. At the time he lived in New 
Jersey. None from these recent posts, but in the past I have gotten numerous 
replies from Christians who expressed appreciation for my (admitedly flawed) 
defense of our common beliefs in a hostile setting.

> So you've been at this lark before in other NewsGroups where your byline 
> got attacked. (Mischievous Judson :-) ).

Only when attacked. :-) I am constantly amazed at how hostile people can get 
toward someone who is simply declaring in print that God loves them. I could 
maybe understand, if I was collering them on the street. I am also amazed at 
how people will ignore my getting blasted repeatedly for my SIG, or a simple 
statement about religion or the Bible or God, then cry "Foul! Foul!" when I 
merely respond to what they say. It is difficult to explain such hostility, 
apart from spiritual influence.

> Well I doubt your above convert was Alistair, but he was the atheist who 
> DEFENDED your right here to use your byline.

Hardly, but his defense is noted, and appreciated. :-)  Please realize that 
I do not equate athiest with evil.

> ... Meanwhile get that journalist daughter of yours to do some independent 
> research by googling. There's oodles of stuff if you can be impartial and 
> try and sort the wheat from the chaff.

I have passed the request along, but work and being a single parent of 12 
and 4 year old daughters keeps her pretty busy.
```

```
> been at this lark before in other NewsGroups where your byline got 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Not that it really matters, but a quick search on GOOGLE shows it was I who 
defended Judson first.

Alastair agreed (two days later) that people should be able to post what 
they like but drew the line at my statement I would defend to the death the 
right of people to express themselves. I then posted explaining my position 
further to Alastair.

The exchanges were instigated by a post on the 9th January, 2002, from 
someone calling himself "rational_one" (sadly not borne out by his posts...) 
to the thread "Religion and COBOL" which was later changed by me to "The 
ghost of Patrick Henry".

http://groups.google.co.nz/group/comp.lang.cobol/browse_frm/thread/5f23c78eb2bbd731/dd9ab717320c0813?q=dashwood+defend+tag&rnum=1#dd9ab717320c0813
```

```
trouble to make it possible for any human who so desires to have an intimate

reasons, even if He tried to explain it to us. Also, none of us have any 
experience wielding omniscience or omnipotence. We can only speculate about 
what it might be like. How could we even know what God might like or 
dislike, or desire, apart from Him telling us? :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Omnipotent being (WAS: Making money from Java)

_(reply depth: 32)_
